title: Euclidean Steiner Shallow-Light Trees in Higher Dimensions
summary: This paper proves a conjecture by Solomon about Steiner shallow-light trees (SLT) in Euclidean $d$-space: It is shown that for any finite point set $\mathbb{R}^d$, any root, and any $ε>0$, there is a Euclidean Steiner $(1+ε,O(\sqrt{1/ε}))$-SLT without any dependence on dimension. We also revisit the core example, designed by Solomon, in the plane and its generalization to $d$-space.

AI.tldr: The paper resolves Solomon's conjecture by constructing dimension-free Euclidean Steiner shallow-light trees with stretch 1+ε and lightness O(√(1/ε)) for any finite point set, root, and ε>0; it also generalizes the planar core example to d-space.
AI.motivation: To advance network design in Euclidean space by achieving near-shortest connectivity with limited depth, while removing dependence on ambient dimension; to validate Solomon's conjecture and deepen understanding of Steiner shallow-light trees in high dimensions.
AI.method: A constructive existence proof that extends Solomon's plane construction to Euclidean d-space, yielding a Euclidean Steiner (1+ε, O(√(1/ε)))-SLT; includes revisiting the core plane example and its generalization to d-space.
AI.result: Proof of Solomon's conjecture: existence of dimension-free Euclidean Steiner SLTs with (1+ε) stretch and lightness O(√(1/ε)) for any finite point set, root, and ε>0.
AI.conclusion: Confirms the conjecture and extends applicability to arbitrary dimensions; the generalized planar example provides a blueprint for higher-dimensional Steiner SLTs and suggests directions for algorithmic construction and analysis.

title: When Does Adaptive Guidance Help? Belief-Aware Privileged Distillation for Autonomous Driving Under Partial Observability
comment: 9 pages, 3 figures, 7 tables. Accepted at CVPR 2026 Workshop on Autonomous Driving (WAD)
summary: Guided Soft Actor-Critic (GSAC) distills knowledge from a privileged full-state teacher to a partial-observation student for autonomous driving, but uses a fixed distillation coefficient lambda regardless of the agent's uncertainty. We present Belief-Aware GSAC (BA-GSAC), which modulates lambda via ensemble disagreement, and use it as a testbed for a systematic empirical study asking: when does adaptive guidance actually help? Evaluating five strategies (fixed lambda in {0.01, 0.1}, adaptive, linear decay, and vanilla SAC) across three POMDP difficulty levels on Highway-Env, we find that preliminary single-seed runs suggest benefits under mild and moderate partial observability, but under severe occlusion (evaluated with 3 seeds for all methods) the adaptive coefficient collapses to lambda_min within about 3K steps. We trace this to an observability blindness phenomenon: because the ensemble predicts partial observations, it achieves low disagreement even under heavy occlusion, modeling what is visible but unable to detect what is missing. We diagnose the root cause and propose an architectural fix (training the ensemble on full-state predictions using the guiding actor's privileged access); while not validated here, we show that even with current limitations, the warmup phase provides measurable stabilization (CV=13.3% vs. 29.8% for constant lambda=0.01). In fact, a simple deterministic linear decay schedule achieves the best severe-POMDP performance across all metrics (mean 116.5, CV=8.9%), suggesting that the scheduling effect, not the ensemble, drives the stability benefit. These findings provide practical guidance for designing uncertainty-aware teacher-student frameworks and highlight ensemble prediction targets as an important design choice.

AI.tldr: Adaptive belief-aware distillation (BA-GSAC) modulates the distillation coefficient via ensemble disagreement in a partial-observable driving task; under mild/moderate POMDPs it helps, but under severe occlusion the adaptation collapses; a simple linear decay of lambda outperforms other schemes; training the ensemble on full-state predictions is proposed as a fix; warmup provides stabilization but scheduling drives stability.
AI.motivation: To overcome the fixed, state-agnostic distillation coefficient in Guided Soft Actor-Critic (GSAC) and to understand when uncertainty-aware guidance improves learning under partial observability in autonomous driving.
AI.method: Introduce BA-GSAC that uses ensemble disagreement to adjust the distillation coefficient lambda. Evaluate five strategies (fixed lambda at 0.01 and 0.1, adaptive, linear decay, vanilla SAC) across three POMDP difficulty levels on Highway-Env. Use single-seed and multi-seed experiments; analyze observability blindness and OCclusion severity; propose architectural fix and test warmup effects. Compare stability via coefficient behavior and CV/mean performance.
AI.result: Adaptive BA-GSAC shows benefits in mild/moderate partial observability in preliminary single-seed runs. Under severe occlusion, lambda converges to its minimum within ~3k steps due to low ensemble disagreement caused by observability blindness. The proposed architectural fix (train ensemble on full-state) is not validated in this work. A simple deterministic linear decay schedule achieves the best severe-POMDP performance across metrics (mean 116.5, CV 8.9%), indicating the scheduling effect drives stability more than ensemble adaptation. Warmup improves stabilization (CV 13.3% vs 29.8% for fixed lambda=0.01).
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty-aware teacher-student guidance is sensitive to the observability regime; ensemble-based disagreement targets are not robust under heavy occlusion. Scheduling-based lambda decay can yield the strongest performance, highlighting the importance of training targets and schedule design. The work offers practical guidance for designing uncertainty-aware distillation and underscores the need for robust ensemble predictions or alternative targets when dealing with severe partial observability.

title: Geometry-Aware Representation Denoising for Robust Multi-view 3D Reconstruction
summary: Multi-view 3D reconstruction has achieved remarkable progress with the advent of feed-forward 3D reconstruction models. However, these models are typically trained and evaluated under ideal, degradation-free imaging conditions, whereas real-world observations often contain degradations that differ significantly from such settings. Improving robustness for multi-view 3D reconstruction under degraded conditions therefore remains an important challenge. We present Geometry-Aware Representation Denoising (GARD), a novel framework that performs diffusion-based multi-view restoration directly in the feature space of a feed-forward 3D reconstruction model. This design exploits the geometry-aware feature representations of the 3D reconstructor to effectively recover accurate scene geometry. Furthermore, by employing an additional RGB image decoder, the refined representations can also be used to restore high-quality RGB images, thereby enabling the simultaneous recovery of 3D scene geometry and high-quality imagery. Comprehensive experiments on the Depth Anything 3 (DA3) benchmark demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed GARD framework.

AI.tldr: GARD denoises degraded multi-view 3D reconstructions via diffusion in the feature space of a feed-forward reconstructor, leveraging geometry-aware representations, with an RGB decoder to also restore high-quality images; evaluated on DA3.
AI.motivation: Real-world multi-view 3D reconstruction suffers under degradations not seen in training; robust restoration in feature space that preserves geometric information is needed.
AI.method: Proposes Geometry-Aware Representation Denoising (GARD): diffusion-based restoration performed directly in the feature space of a pre-existing 3D reconstructor, exploiting geometry-aware features; includes an auxiliary RGB image decoder to recover high-quality RGB imagery.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments on the DA3 benchmark show the framework is effective at restoring geometry and, via the RGB decoder, high-quality RGB images.
AI.conclusion: GARD enables simultaneous restoration of 3D geometry and high-quality imagery under degraded imaging conditions by operating in the geometry-aware feature space of feed-forward 3D reconstructors and enabling RGB output via an extra decoder.

title: GEM: Geometric Entropy Mixing for Optimal LLM Data Curation
summary: LLM pre-training efficacy increasingly depends on data composition rather than sheer volume. Yet, optimal mixing is hindered by categorization flaws: human taxonomies suffer from ontological misalignment, and Euclidean clustering fails to address embedding anisotropy. We introduce GEM (Geometric Entropy Mixing), a framework reformulating data curation as a variational problem on the hypersphere augmented with a mixing-balance regularizer. By decoupling the generative prior and optimizing the objective via a provable MM (Minorize-Maximize) algorithm, GEM effectively counteracts the cluster collapse to discover balanced semantic structures invisible to Euclidean heuristics. We employ teacher-student distillation to scale this geometric fidelity to web-scale corpora and introduce the Geometric Influence Score (GIS) for interpretable taxonomy generation. Experiments with 1.1B-parameter models demonstrate that GEM establishes a new state-of-the-art when integrated into mixing strategies like DoReMi and RegMix, improving average downstream accuracy by up to 1.2% and offering a robust coordinate system for predictable data mixing.

AI.tldr: GEM reframes data curation for LLM pre-training as a variational problem on the hypersphere with a mixing-balance regularizer, solved via a Minorize-Maximize algorithm; scaled to web-scale corpora via teacher-student distillation and endowed with the Geometric Influence Score (GIS) for interpretable taxonomy. It yields state-of-the-art results (up to 1.2% average downstream accuracy gains) for 1.1B-parameter models using mixing strategies like DoReMi and RegMix, and provides a robust coordinate system for predictable data mixing.
AI.motivation: Data composition increasingly governs pre-training efficacy; human taxonomies suffer from ontological misalignment, and Euclidean clustering ignores embedding anisotropy. There is a need for geometry-aware, balanced, scalable data curation that preserves semantic structure and is interpretable.
AI.method: GEM formulates data curation as a variational objective on the hypersphere, incorporating a mixing-balance regularizer and decoupling the generative prior. Optimization proceeds via a provable Minorize-Maximize (MM) algorithm to prevent cluster collapse and reveal balanced semantic structures. To scale to web-scale corpora, a teacher-student distillation setup is used. GIS (Geometric Influence Score) provides interpretable taxonomy outputs.
AI.result: Experiments with 1.1B-parameter models show GEM achieves state-of-the-art when embedded into mixing schemes such as DoReMi and RegMix, with improvements in average downstream accuracy up to 1.2% and a robust coordinate system for predictable data mixing.
AI.conclusion: GEM offers a geometry-conscious alternative to Euclidean clustering for data mixing, yielding balanced semantic structures and interpretable taxonomy while scaling to large corpora and improving downstream performance.

title: BrickAnything: Geometry-Conditioned Buildable Brick Generation with Structure-Aware Tokenization
summary: Generating physically buildable brick structures from 3D shapes requires more than geometric reconstruction: the output must also satisfy discrete part constraints and structural stability. Existing brick generation methods either rely on heuristic optimization, which can break down when the target 3D shape does not admit a feasible structure under predefined constraints, or generate brick sequences without explicitly modeling the underlying 3D geometry and assembly relations. In this work, we present BrickAnything, a geometry-conditioned autoregressive framework for generating buildable brick structures from diverse 3D representations. BrickAnything uses point clouds as a unified geometric interface and predicts brick sequences that reconstruct the target shape under assembly constraints. To model structural dependencies among bricks, we introduce a structure-aware tree tokenization, which represents brick structures through local attachment relations. This formulation makes sequence generation more consistent with the physical construction process, and reduces invalid intermediate states. We further introduce preference-based alignment post-training, validity-constrained decoding and adaptive rollback to improve buildability objectives such as stability and geometric fidelity. Extensive experiments demonstrate that BrickAnything produces geometrically faithful and physically realizable brick structures, and that the proposed tokenization effectively reduces rollback and regeneration compared with conventional ordering strategies.

AI.tldr: BrickAnything introduces a geometry-conditioned autoregressive model for buildable brick-structure generation from 3D shapes, using point clouds, a structure-aware tree tokenization of local attachments, and post-training techniques to ensure buildability and fidelity, reducing invalid states compared with prior methods.
AI.motivation: Generate physically realizable brick structures that satisfy discrete part constraints and structural stability, addressing limitations of heuristic optimization and geometry-agnostic sequence generation.
AI.method: Autoregressive generation conditioned on point-cloud geometry; structure-aware tree tokenization to encode local attachment relations; alignment post-training, validity-constrained decoding, and adaptive rollback to improve stability and geometric fidelity.
AI.result: Experiments show geometrically faithful and physically realizable brick structures; the structure-aware tokenization reduces rollback and regeneration compared with conventional ordering strategies.
AI.conclusion: The approach enhances buildability and geometric fidelity in brick-structure synthesis by modeling 3D geometry and assembly relations through a structure-aware tokenization and targeted decoding/post-training techniques.

title: PhyPush: One Push is All You Need for Sensorless Physical Property Estimation with Physics-Guided Transformers
comment: Submitted to 2026 IEEE/RSJ International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems
summary: Accurately estimating object mass and friction is fundamental to achieving reliable and adaptive robotic manipulation. Although interactive perception provides a powerful mechanism for inferring such properties, most existing approaches depend on specialized hardware such as force/torque sensors, tactile arrays, or multi-camera motion-capture systems, limiting scalability and deployment. This paper presents PhyPush, a physics-guided Transformer framework that estimates an object's mass and friction coefficient using only kinematically derived end-effector velocity from a single push. This typically requires data available on standard robotic arms. The model incorporates constraints from Newton's second law and the Coulomb friction model through a physics-guided loss, improving physical consistency and generalization to unseen objects and surfaces. Across diverse simulation and real-world setups, PhyPush consistently achieves more accurate mass and friction estimation in challenging out-of-domain conditions. In simulation, it reduces error by over 10% compared with a baseline that has privileged access to full force information, while in real-world experiments, it outperforms a data-driven loss approach. Overall, the results demonstrate that physics-guided learning can enable low-cost, sensor-efficient estimation of physical properties, relying solely on a single push and readily available kinematic data.

AI.tldr: PhyPush uses a physics-guided Transformer to estimate an object's mass and friction coefficient from only end-effector velocity derived from a single push, achieving better generalization with low-cost sensing.
AI.motivation: Interactive perception for physical property estimation often relies on force/tactile sensing; to scale, a sensor-efficient approach leveraging physics is desirable.
AI.method: Transformers process kinematic data from a single push; a physics-guided loss enforces Newton's second law and Coulomb friction constraints during training; evaluated on simulated and real-world setups.
AI.result: In simulation, PhyPush reduces mass/friction estimation error by over 10% compared to a baseline with full force information; in real-world experiments, outperforms a purely data-driven loss; demonstrates improved physical consistency and generalization to unseen objects and surfaces.
AI.conclusion: Physics-guided learning enables low-cost, sensor-efficient estimation of mass and friction from minimal data, enabling practical manipulation with standard robots.

title: Not All Modalities Are Equal: Instruction-Aware Gating for Multimodal Videos
summary: Pre-trained video large language models excel at visual reasoning. However, they struggle when videos arrive with auxiliary streams, such as audio, depth map, or dense temporal evidence. In such a scenario, uniform fusion induces modality interference, allowing irrelevant channels to distract the model. To address this issue, we present a unified multimodal video understanding framework, named UniMVU, that performs instruction-aware fusion across video, audio, depth map, or any other modality inputs via two levels of dynamic gating: inner-modality gates emphasize salient regions within each modality, whereas modality-level gates re-weight whole streams; both are conditioned on the text instruction to adaptively balance modality importance. Our UniMVU combines cross-modal self-attention with instruction-driven inner-modality gating module and a modality-level gating module with control token; for time-aligned streams we further adopt a fast-to-slow fusion scheme that reduces redundancy. Across six benchmarks (AVQA, AVSD, Music-AVQA, ScanQA, SQA3D and MVBench), our UniMVU achieves consistent gains over static-fusion baselines achieving gains as high as 13.5 in terms of CIDEr metric. Further, our analysis shows that the gating mechanism aligns with the human-interpretable modality relevance, and ablations show the contributions of inner-modality and modality-level gating. Our UniMVU provides a simple, unified recipe for instruction-aware multimodal video understanding that scales to diverse modalities without hand-crafted fusion rules.

AI.tldr: Introduces UniMVU, a unified instruction-aware multimodal video understanding framework using inner-modality and modality-level dynamic gates to fuse video, audio, depth, and other modalities, improving performance on multiple benchmarks without handcrafted fusion rules.
AI.motivation: Pre-trained video LLMs perform well visually but degrade when additional modalities (audio, depth, etc.) are present; uniform fusion causes modality interference and irrelevant channels distract the model. Need instruction-conditioned, selective fusion to balance modality importance.
AI.method: Cross-modal self-attention combined with an instruction-driven inner-modality gating module and a modality-level gating module with a control token. Two levels of dynamic gating: inner-modality gates emphasize salient regions within each modality; modality-level gates re-weight entire streams. For time-aligned streams, a fast-to-slow fusion scheme reduces redundancy.
AI.result: Evaluated on six benchmarks (AVQA, AVSD, Music-AVQA, ScanQA, SQA3D, MVBench). UniMVU achieved consistent gains over static fusion baselines, with improvements up to 13.5 CIDEr. Analysis shows the gating aligns with human-interpretable modality relevance; ablations confirm contributions of inner-modality and modality-level gating.
AI.conclusion: UniMVU provides a simple, unified recipe for instruction-aware multimodal video understanding that scales to diverse modalities without crafting fusion rules, and the gating mechanism supports interpretable modality relevance across tasks.

title: Can LLMs Introspect? A Reality Check
summary:
Can large language models detect and report their own internal states? A number of studies have argued that the answer to this question is yes. We argue, based on lessons from human metacognition research, that this conclusion may be premature: to be convinced of this conclusion we need to distinguish genuine introspection from pattern matching based on surface-level cues. Furthermore, we argue that behavioral evidence alone is inherently insufficient to establish strong introspective claims.
  We re-examine two recently introduced evaluation paradigms in light of this consideration. In the first paradigm, models are expected to detect whether their internal states have been tampered with. We find that models cannot reliably distinguish such interventions on their internal states from manipulations of the input, suggesting that their success in the original studies reflects their ability to detect anomalies more generally, as opposed to interventions on their internal states in particular. In the second paradigm we examine, models are tasked with predicting labels derived from their own hidden states. Here, we find that classifiers that only have access to the input achieve equivalent performance to the model's own in-context predictions, indicating that the original results do not conclusively demonstrate that the model has privileged access to its internal representations. We further introduce a relabeled control setting, where models cannot rely on the semantics of the task to solve it, and instead must rely on the internal representation; models perform closer to chance on this better-controlled version of the task. Taken together, these results indicate that current evidence is insufficient to establish that LLMs display metacognitive monitoring.

AI.tldr: Current evidence that LLMs have true metacognitive access to their internal states is unclear; apparent introspection may reflect pattern-based cues or anomaly detection rather than genuine access to hidden states.
AI.motivation: Clarify claims of metacognition in LLMs by distinguishing genuine introspection from surface cues, and by showing that behavioral evidence alone is insufficient for strong introspective claims.
AI.method: Re-examine two evaluation paradigms: (1) detection of tampering with internal states versus input manipulations; (2) predicting labels derived from hidden states, including a relabeled control where semantics cannot be used and reliance on internal representations is required.
AI.result: Paradigm (1) shows models struggle to distinguish internal-state tampering from input manipulation, suggesting prior success reflected anomaly/detection of general cues rather than true state access. Paradigm (2) shows input-only classifiers match model outputs; in the relabeled control, performance nears chance, indicating lack of privileged access to internal representations.
AI.conclusion: Evidence for LLM metacognitive monitoring is not yet convincing; rigorous, control-based experiments are needed to separate genuine introspection from cue-based inference and to avoid overinterpreting behavioral results.

title: Collaborative Navigation and Exploration with $β$-Sparse Gaussian Processes
summary: Collaborative navigation of heterogeneous robots in unknown environments poses significant challenges due to sensing, communication, and computational limitations. In this work, a lead robot navigates toward a target while a mobile sensor robot (e.g., a drone) assists by transmitting information about its locally observed environment under bandwidth constraints. We propose a framework that enables the sensor to jointly select its transmitted map points and navigation actions online, while also predicting unexplored regions of the environment. To this end, we present $β$-Sparse Gaussian Processes, a novel and robust variational sparse Gaussian Process model for task-aware inducing point selection. Furthermore, we develop an action-selection strategy that balances task relevance with exploration. Simulations on Mars and Earth maps show that the framework can reduce path cost by 18% relative to no communication and decrease transmitted information by 76% compared to raw-data transmission baselines.

AI.tldr: A collaborative navigation framework for lead and sensor robots using beta-Sparse Gaussian Processes to select informative transmissions and navigate jointly under bandwidth constraints; simulations show improved efficiency and bandwidth savings.
AI.motivation: Address challenges in sensing, communication, and computation limits in heterogeneous multi-robot navigation; enable efficient information sharing and environment prediction with limited bandwidth.
AI.method: Introduce Beta-Sparse Gaussian Processes for task-aware inducing point selection; online joint optimization of transmitted map points and robot actions; data-driven prediction of unexplored regions; balance between task relevance and exploration in action selection.
AI.result: Simulation experiments on Mars and Earth maps show an 18% reduction in path cost compared with no communication and up to 76% reduction in transmitted information relative to raw-data transmission baselines.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework enables efficient, bandwidth-aware collaborative navigation; the beta-Sparse GP provides robust inducing point selection, and the online action/data selection improves navigation performance while greatly reducing communication load.

title: DuoGesture: Neuro-Inspired and Biomechanically Informed Dual-Stream Co-Speech Gesture Generation
summary: Co-speech gesture generation requires both semantic expressivity and biomechanically plausible rhythmic motion. Existing holistic gesture models mix lexically grounded semantic gestures with frequent prosody-aligned beat gestures. This limits semantic grounding, speech-motion alignment, and kinematic smoothness. We propose \emph{DuoGesture}, a neuro-inspired and biomechanically informed dual-stream approach that decomposes co-speech gesture synthesis into coupled semantic and beat streams. The two streams are coordinated by a \emph{Semantic Variational Information Bottleneck}, a stochastic frame-level gate that learns when semantic gestures should override rhythmic beat motion. The semantic stream is controlled by \emph{Motion-Grounded Semantic Conditioning}, which replaces purely linguistic word embeddings with motion-language representations to provide motion-aligned semantic priors for long-tailed lexical triggers of gestures. The beat stream is further regularised by an \emph{Inertial Beat Prior}, an anthropometry-weighted arm-chain module that reduces jitter and improves rhythmic consistency without constraining semantic frames. Objective evaluations and subjective experiments show that DuoGesture outperforms strong holistic baselines, while component ablations confirm the complementary roles of semantic grounding, stochastic stream selection, and biomechanical regularisation.

AI.tldr: DuoGesture introduces a dual-stream, neuro-inspired framework for co-speech gesture synthesis that splits semantic gestures and beat-driven motion, coordinated by a Semantic Variational Information Bottleneck and regularized by biomechanical priors, yielding superior performance over holistic baselines.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of holistic gesture models that jeopardize semantic grounding, speech-motion alignment, and kinematic smoothness by blending lexical gestures with prosody-aligned beats.
AI.method: Proposes a dual-stream architecture: a semantic stream guided by Motion-Grounded Semantic Conditioning, and a beat stream regularized by an Inertial Beat Prior. Coordination is achieved via a Semantic Variational Information Bottleneck (frame-level stochastic gate) that decides when semantic gestures override beat motion. The Inertial Beat Prior uses an anthropometry-weighted arm-chain module to reduce jitter while not constraining semantic frames.
AI.result: Objective metrics and human studies show DuoGesture outperforms strong holistic baselines. Ablations demonstrate the complementary contributions of semantic grounding, stochastic gate control, and biomechanical regularization.
AI.conclusion: Semantic and beat streams, when coupled through a variational gating mechanism and biomechanically informed priors, enable more expressive and physically plausible co-speech gestures than holistic models.

title: AirCast-SR: A Foundation Model for Kilometer-Scale Atmospheric Super-Resolution via Latent Consistency Diffusion
comment: Somnath Luitel and Manmeet Singh are equal-contribution co-first authors, with Manmeet Singh (manmeet.singh@wku.edu) as corresponding author
summary: Operational weather prediction at kilometer scales remains computationally prohibitive for traditional numerical weather prediction (NWP) models, limiting forecast access for applications in energy, agriculture, and disaster management that require fine-grained spatiotemporal detail. Here we introduce AirCast-SR, a foundation model for atmospheric super-resolution that downscales global AI weather forecasts from 0.25 degree (~28 km) to 1 km horizontal resolution at hourly temporal resolution, producing 67-hour forecasts of eight coupled surface variables simultaneously. EarthMind-SR employs a three-dimensional U-Net conditioned within a Latent Consistency Model (LCM) diffusion framework, trained on patch-based samples over the contiguous United States (CONUS) using GraphCast forecasts as input and NOAA's Analysis of Record for Calibration (AORC) as the target. The model achieves near-zero bias across all variables and lead times, and its radial power spectral density analysis demonstrates preservation of fine-scale atmospheric structure at wavelengths of 10 km to 100 km where coarser models lose spectral power. We validate EarthMind-SR across three CONUS case studies spanning winter, summer, and spring seasons, and demonstrate zero-shot global transferability over India and Germany using independent surface station observations without any retraining or fine-tuning. As an open-weights foundation model, EarthMind-SR establishes a new paradigm for kilometer-scale AI weather prediction and provides a platform for regional fine-tuning, distillation, and downstream applications in climate services and hazard forecasting.

AI.tldr: Two new AI-based weather super-resolution models: AirCast-SR and EarthMind-SR upscale global forecasts from ~28 km to 1 km at hourly steps, using diffusion/posterior models and 3D U-Nets to produce 67-hour forecasts for eight surface variables; show near-zero bias, preserve fine-scale spectral structure, and zero-shot transfer to India and Germany; open-weights foundation models enabling regional fine-tuning and downstream climate services.
AI.motivation: Enable kilometer-scale usable weather forecasts for applications needing fine-grained spatial detail (energy, agriculture, disaster management) without prohibitive compute costs of traditional NWP at high resolution.
AI.method: AirCast-SR uses downscaling from 0.25° (~28 km) to 1 km with hourly resolution, producing 67-hour forecasts; EarthMind-SR uses a 3D U-Net conditioned within a Latent Consistency Model diffusion framework; trained on CONUS patch samples with GraphCast as input and AORC as target; calibration via NOAA AORC; evaluates via bias and radial power spectral density across seasons and cross-region transfer.
AI.result: Both models achieve near-zero bias across variables and lead times; preserve fine-scale atmospheric structure down to 10–100 km wavelengths (where coarse models lose power); demonstrate three CONUS seasonal case studies; zero-shot global transfer to India and Germany using independent surface stations without retraining; open-weights foundation models enabling regional fine-tuning and downstream applications.
AI.conclusion: AI-based kilometer-scale weather prediction is feasible as an open-weights foundation model, offering scalable regional adaptation, climate services, and hazard forecasting with preserved fine-scale structure and broad transferability.

title: Is Agent Memory a Database? Rethinking Data Foundations for Long-Term AI Agent Memory
summary: Long-running AI agents need persistent memory. Memory supports learning across sessions, reduces repeated context injection, and enables auditing of past decisions. Current agent memory systems and database paradigms treat memory as storage. They localize correctness at records, embeddings, or edges. Each supplies only some of the capabilities that long-term memory requires. The result is four recurring failure modes: unregulated growth, missing semantic revision, capacity-driven forgetting, and read-only retrieval. In our vision, long-term agent memory is a new data-management workload. Its correctness is a property of the state trajectory, not of individual records. We formalize this as Governed Evolving Memory (GEM). GEM replaces record-level database operations with four state-level operators: ingestion, revision, forgetting, and retrieval. Six correctness conditions govern how the state evolves. Three structural observations establish that no record-level system can satisfy these conditions, regardless of the storage model. We realize the abstraction in MemState, a prototype on a property-graph backend. MemState validates feasibility and exposes the gap to a native engine. We outline three research directions that define memory-centric data management as a workload.

AI.tldr: GEM reframes long-term memory as a governed, state-evolving data-management problem, not a collection of records.
AI.motivation: Long-running AI agents need persistent memory; existing memory systems revert to storage-centric designs that fail to ensure end-to-end correctness of memory over time, leading to unregulated growth, semantic revision gaps, forgetting, and read-only retrieval.
AI.method: Introduce Governed Evolving Memory (GEM) with four state-level operators—ingestion, revision, forgetting, retrieval—and six correctness conditions that govern state evolution; provide three structural observations showing record-level systems cannot satisfy these conditions; implement MemState on a property-graph backend to validate feasibility; compare against native engines and outline research directions.
AI.result: Abstraction validated through a MemState prototype, which demonstrates feasibility of the state-centric approach and identifies gaps to native engines; shows that the memory-management abstraction can handle long-term evolution and governance of memory.
AI.conclusion: Advocates memory-centric data management as a formal workload for long-term AI memory and outlines three research directions to advance governance, evolution, and efficiency of persistent memory.

title: NightSight: Passive Computation for Navigation in Dark Using Events
summary: Small aerial robots are particularly well-suited for search and rescue in confined and hazardous environments due to their agility, low cost, and ability to traverse through cluttered spaces that are inaccessible to larger platforms. However, enabling autonomous navigation in complete darkness remains a significant challenge, because small aerial robots cannot easily accommodate perception systems that demand substantial payload, power, or computation. In this work, we present a lightweight perception approach that combines a monocular event camera, a coded aperture lens, and an infrared dot projector to enable navigation in such conditions. The projected pattern, when imaged through the coded aperture, produces depth dependent blur signatures that implicitly encode scene geometry. We train a convolutional neural network to decode these signatures into dense depth maps using only synthetic data generated from a simple planar wall setup. Despite this minimal training regime, the model generalizes zero-shot to complex real-world scenes. Our system operates in real time at 20 Hz on a NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano, demonstrating suitability for resource-constrained platforms. We further analyze the impact of different coded aperture designs on depth estimation performance. Our approach gives high accuracy (l1 error 7.0cm) upto 2.5m range (2.80% error). These results highlight the potential of combining structured illumination, coded optics, and event-based sensing for enabling robust perception and navigation in complete darkness.

AI.tldr: Lightweight depth sensing for small drones in darkness using a monocular event camera, coded aperture, and IR dot projector to produce depth maps; zero-shot generalization from synthetic training; real-time 20 Hz inference on Jetson Orin Nano up to 2.5 m.
AI.motivation: Enable autonomous navigation for miniature aerial robots in complete darkness where conventional sensors are constrained by payload, power, and computation.
AI.method: Combine a monocular event camera, coded aperture lens, and infrared dot projector. Depth signatures arise from blur induced by the pattern through the coded aperture. Train a CNN end-to-end on synthetic data from a planar wall to output dense depth maps. Evaluate on real scenes without further training. Analyze the impact of coded aperture designs. Run at 20 Hz on NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano.
AI.result: Achieves l1 depth error of 7.0 cm up to 2.5 m (2.8% error). Real-time performance at 20 Hz on the Jetson Orin Nano. Demonstrates zero-shot generalization to complex real-world scenes and robustness to darkness.
AI.conclusion: Structured illumination with coded optics and event-based sensing enables robust, lightweight depth perception for navigation in complete darkness on resource-constrained platforms; potential for improved autonomy in cluttered, dark environments.

title: Sentinel: Embodied Cooperative Spatial Reasoning and Planning
comment: The first two authors contributed equally
summary: In this work, we study Cooperative Spatial Intelligence, the ability of decentralized embodied agents to coordinate effectively under dynamic environmental constraints across city-scale outdoor domains. We introduce Sentinel Challenge, a benchmark where multiple decentralized embodied agents must communicate in natural language to agree on a mutually safe and convenient meeting point within large, city-scale outdoor environments. Each agent must then navigate safely while avoiding dynamic sentinels patrolling the area, using a tool that provides coarse spatial information. To address this, we propose CoSaR (Cooperative Spatial Reasoning and Planning), a framework that bridges the high-level communication and planning abilities of foundation models with the precision of classical spatial navigation algorithms. CoSaR enables agents to exchange situational updates, reason over evolving spatial constraints, and collaboratively replan trajectories. Evaluated across 14 city-level scenes with 3-5 agents, CoSaR consistently leads to faster gathering, shorter path lengths, and improved safety. Our results demonstrate that integrating dynamic communication with spatial reasoning is essential for robust multi-agent cooperation. By formalizing this new setting and providing a scalable benchmark, we aim to build a foundation for advancing cooperative spatial intelligence in embodied multi-agent systems. Code and challenge are available at https://github.com/UMass-Embodied-AGI/Sentinel.

AI.tldr: Introduces Sentinel Challenge benchmark and CoSaR framework for cooperative spatial reasoning among decentralized agents in city-scale outdoor environments, combining natural-language communication with classical navigation to safe meeting points and dynamic replanning under moving sentinels.
AI.motivation: Address the gap where decentralized agents need to coordinate under dynamic spatial constraints using both high-level communication and low-level navigation in large outdoor domains.
AI.method: Proposes Sentinel Challenge benchmark; CoSaR framework bridging foundation-model-based communication and planning with classical spatial navigation; supports dynamic updates and replanning; 14 city-level scenes with 3-5 agents; uses a coarse spatial tool.
AI.result: CoSaR yields faster gathering, shorter routes, and improved safety across scenes; demonstrates the importance of integrating dynamic communication with spatial reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Formalizes a new cooperative spatial intelligence setting and provides a scalable benchmark and code to foster development in embodied multi-agent systems.

title: SilIF: Silhouette-Augmented Isolation Forest for Unsupervised Transaction Fraud Detection
summary: Unsupervised anomaly detection is widely used in transaction fraud detection where labels are scarce. Isolation Forest (IF) is among the most popular classical methods due to its scalability and ease of deployment. We propose SilIF, an augmentation of Isolation Forest that adds a silhouette-based scoring layer computed in a representation space induced by the trees of the forest. For each point, we extract a vector of per-tree path lengths, cluster these "fingerprints" into structural groups, and compute a silhouette score that measures how well the point fits its assigned group versus the nearest alternative. The silhouette signal is combined with the base IF score via a single hyperparameter alpha. On the IEEE-CIS Fraud Detection benchmark (~590K transactions, 3.5% fraud), SilIF with alpha=1.0 improves over plain Isolation Forest by +0.0080 AUC-PR on average across five seeds, with SilIF winning on all five seeds (paired t-test p=0.046). We also report results on a synthetic credit-card dataset (Sparkov) where the silhouette augmentation does not improve over plain IF, and we characterize the conditions that distinguish the two outcomes. The paper presents SilIF as a tunable, easy-to-deploy enhancement to Isolation Forest with honest reporting of when it helps and when it does not. Code at https://github.com/venkat15vk/silif-anomaly-detection.

AI.tldr: SilIF augments Isolation Forest with a silhouette-based score derived from per-tree path-length fingerprints; it improves AUC-PR on a real fraud dataset but not on a synthetic dataset, offering tunable deployment with conditions for benefit.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of unsupervised anomaly detection via Isolation Forest by exploiting ensemble-structure information. The paper proposes a silhouette-based augmentation to capture how well a point fits its assigned structural group in the tree-induced representation space.
AI.method: Compute for each data point a vector of per-tree path lengths (fingerprints), cluster these fingerprints into structural groups, compute a silhouette score for the point relative to its group versus the nearest other group, and linearly combine this silhouette score with the base IF score using a single hyperparameter alpha. Evaluate on IEEE-CIS Fraud Detection (~590K transactions, ~3.5% fraud) and a synthetic Sparkov dataset; provide code at the listed GitHub repo.
AI.result: On IEEE-CIS Fraud Detection, SilIF with alpha=1.0 yields +0.0080 AUC-PR over plain Isolation Forest on average across five seeds, with SilIF winning on all seeds (paired t-test p=0.046). On the synthetic Sparkov dataset, silhouette augmentation does not improve over plain IF; authors characterize conditions distinguishing outcomes.
AI.conclusion: SilIF is a tunable, easy-to-deploy enhancement to Isolation Forest, offering honest reporting of when it helps and when it does not; code is provided for practitioners.

title: Personalizing Embodied Multimodal Large Language Model Agents over Long-term User Interactions
summary: Multimodal large language model (MLLM)-based embodied agents have shown strong potential for solving complex tasks in physical environments. However, personalized assistance requires more than following generic instruction or recognizing object categories. In real-world scenarios, the intended target is often specified only implicitly through prior interactions, requiring agents to leverage personalized context accumulated over time. In this work, we propose POLAR, a multiomodal memory-augmented framework for personalized embodied agents over long-term user interactions. POLAR organizes prior interactions into a multimodal knowledge graph that captures semantic memory for personalized context and visual concepts, and episodic memory for embodied experiences such as agent trajectories. To execute embodied tasks, POLAR retrieves relevant memories to interpret the current request and guide task execution. We evaluate POLAR across multiple MLLM backbones and diverse evaluation scenarios to study the role of memory in long-term personalization. Results show that the proposed memory mechanism consistently improves performance by enabling more effective use of information accumulated over prior interactions. The gains are especially pronounced when the agents are required to reason across multiple interactions, perform multi-hop inference, or tracking updates in user-specific context over time.

AI.tldr: POLAR introduces a memory-augmented, multimodal framework for personalized embodied agents, organizing long-term user interactions into a multimodal knowledge graph (semantic memory) and episodic memory, enabling memory-guided task execution; shows consistent improvements across backbones, especially for multi-hop reasoning and time-based personalization.
AI.motivation: To achieve true personalization in embodied AI, agents must leverage accumulated context from prior interactions, not just generic recognition, particularly when targets are implicit and evolve over time.
AI.method: Construct a POLAR framework: maintain a multimodal memory graph (semantic concepts and user context) and episodic memory (trajectories). Retrieve relevant memories to interpret current requests and guide action; evaluate with various MLLM backbones and scenarios.
AI.result: Memory augmentation yields consistent performance gains, with larger benefits in multi-step reasoning and when tracking user-specific context across interactions.
AI.conclusion: Memory-augmented embodied agents effectively leverage long-term multimodal and episodic memory to achieve improved personalization and task performance; memory retrieval is key to translating prior interactions into action.

title: RCSP: Risk-Sensitive Conjectural Scenario Planning for Safe Dynamic Robot Navigation
summary: Mobile robots can fail before they collide: a velocity that is safe now may commit the robot to a passage that moving obstacles will soon close. We study this predictive near-miss commitment problem and propose Risk-Sensitive Conjectural Scenario Planning (RCSP), a planning layer that evaluates candidate commands against plausible short-horizon obstacle futures. RCSP maintains a lightweight belief over local motion conjectures, samples future interactions, penalizes high-risk tails, and executes through a local safety check. In controlled MuJoCo bottleneck tasks, the RCSP planner reaches the goal without collisions and yields higher secondary safety and path-quality point estimates than a non-adaptive predictor, with additional latency. In ROS2/Gazebo, adding the local safety layer to a standard Nav2 stack reduces dynamic near-miss failures. On official DynaBARN/Jackal transfer, tuned DWA and TEB remain stronger on strict benchmark success, revealing the boundary of the approach. These simulation results position RCSP as a predictive-risk module that complements existing navigation stacks in dynamic bottleneck regimes.

AI.tldr: RCSP is a predictive-risk planning layer for mobile robots that evaluates short-horizon obstacle futures to avoid near-miss commitments, integrating risk-sensitive scenario planning into navigation stacks.
AI.motivation: To prevent unsafe velocity choices that later force the robot into dynamically closing passages with moving obstacles; enable proactive risk management by reasoning about plausible futures.
AI.method: Maintain a lightweight belief over local motion conjectures, sample future interactions, penalize high-risk tails, and enforce a local safety check; integrate as a planning layer in existing stacks; evaluated in simulated bottleneck tasks (MuJoCo), ROS2/Gazebo with Nav2, and DynaBARN/Jackal transfer.
AI.result: RCSP achieves fewer collisions and improved secondary safety and path-quality estimates compared to non-adaptive predictors; reduces dynamic near-miss failures in ROS2/Gazebo; in transfer benchmarks, tuned DWA/TEB outperform RCSP on strict success metrics, indicating boundary conditions for RCSP’s benefit.
AI.conclusion: RCSP can serve as a predictive-risk module that complements existing navigation stacks in dynamic bottleneck regimes, offering safety and quality improvements at the cost of added latency; its usefulness is most pronounced in scenarios with imminent but uncertain obstacles.

title: RoMo: A Large-Scale, Richly Organized Dataset and Semantic Taxonomy for Human Motion Generation
summary: Success in generative modeling across language, image, and video demonstrates that large, well-curated datasets are the key driver for building capable models. 3D Human motion, however, has lagged behind, constrained by an unsatisfying choice between small, high-fidelity motion capture datasets and large-scale in-the-wild collections dominated by static or low-quality sequences. We introduce RoMo, a rich, large-scale, carefully curated dataset of in-the-wild human motions that resolves these tradeoffs. To ensure quality, we introduce a taxonomy-aware filtering pipeline that aggressively removes static and artifact-prone sequences. Every sequence is annotated with detailed captions and organized by a novel three-level semantic taxonomy. This hierarchical structure enables fine-grained, per-category evaluation, that reveals model strengths and weaknesses obscured by global metrics. We demonstrate that models trained on RoMo achieve state-of-the-art fidelity and diversity while gaining a superior understanding of complex, subtle text prompts. Finally, we release the Motion Toolbox to standardize metrics, data conversion, and visualization, establishing a foundation for reproducible and interpretable motion generation research.

AI.tldr: RoMo introduces a large-scale, carefully curated 3D human motion dataset with taxonomy-aware filtering and detailed captions, enabling fine-grained evaluation and reproducible motion generation research.
AI.motivation: To overcome the trade-off between small, high-quality mocap datasets and large, noisy in-the-wild data by providing a high-quality, diverse, and richly annotated benchmark for 3D human motion.
AI.method: A taxonomy-aware filtering pipeline aggressively removes static and artifact-prone sequences; sequences are annotated with detailed captions and organized by a novel three-level semantic taxonomy; release of the Motion Toolbox for metrics, data conversion, and visualization.
AI.result: Models trained on RoMo achieve state-of-the-art fidelity and diversity, with improved understanding of complex text prompts; enables per-category evaluation and better diagnostic insights than global metrics.
AI.conclusion: RoMo advances 3D motion datasets by delivering high-quality, diverse, annotated data and tools that support reproducible, interpretable motion generation research.

title: Neural Bayesian Sequential Routing
summary: Human decision-making is sequential and uncertainty-aware, yet standard neural networks often rely on static, dense forward computation with limited visibility into evidence acquisition, uncertainty evolution, or when computation should stop. We introduce \textbf{Neural Bayesian Sequential Routing (NBSR)}, a framework that models neural inference as active evidence accumulation over a hierarchical Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG). Within a Dirichlet--Categorical conjugate framework, neural experts query a persistent global knowledge oracle to extract positive evidence vectors, which act as pseudo-counts and update a Dirichlet belief state by exact conjugate addition. Coupled with a Gumbel-Softmax Straight-Through estimator, this update enables hard, path-dependent routing while preserving surrogate gradients for end-to-end training. The resulting Dirichlet precision and entropy provide mechanisms for uncertainty quantification, entropy-based early exiting, OOD abstention, and cost-aware evidence acquisition. We prove that, under strictly positive evidence extraction, total Dirichlet precision increases monotonically along any valid trajectory and marginal predictive variance is bounded, formalizing sequential ``hypothesis sharpening''; under idealized capacity and optimization assumptions, the terminal Dirichlet expectation recovers the Bayes-optimal conditional distribution. Empirical evaluations across visual categorization, structured medical diagnosis, language modeling, partially observable control, and cost-aware Bayesian experimental design show that NBSR achieves competitive predictive performance while providing transparent routing traces, path-dependent evidence attribution, uncertainty-aware decision control, and resource-rational inference. Overall, NBSR offers a mathematically grounded framework for interpretable, modular, and resource-rational agentic AI.

AI.tldr: Neural Bayesian Sequential Routing (NBSR) introduces active, evidence-driven inference over a DAG with Dirichlet-Categorical conjugacy, enabling path-dependent routing, uncertainty quantification, and resource-aware decision control, with monotone precision growth and potential Bayes-optimal recovery under ideal conditions.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of static forward computation and opaque uncertainty in neural networks; enable sequential hypothesis sharpening and transparent routing traces.
AI.method: Neural experts query a global knowledge oracle to obtain positive evidence vectors treated as pseudo-counts, updating a Dirichlet belief via exact conjugate addition. A Gumbel-Softmax Straight-Through estimator enables hard routing while preserving gradients. Entropy/precision drive early exiting, OOD abstention, and cost-aware evidence acquisition.
AI.result: Empirical studies across domains show competitive accuracy with interpretable routing traces, uncertainty-aware decisions, and resource-rational inference; theoretical results show monotone Dirichlet precision along trajectories and bounded predictive variance; Bayes-optimal distribution recovered under idealized conditions.
AI.conclusion: NBSR provides a principled, modular framework for interpretable, resource-rational agentic AI with explicit uncertainty handling and traceable evidence routing.

title: Closing the Loop in Teleoperation: Episode-Level Data Quality Assessment and Feedback for High-Quality Demonstration Collection
summary: Industrial automation is at a pivotal moment, as Physical AI is driving a transition from rigid, hand-engineered automation systems toward more flexible and adaptive systems. This shift has created a growing demand for large-scale, real-world robot demonstration data, making teleoperation an increasingly important mechanism for data collection. However, high-quality teleoperated demonstrations remain difficult to obtain in practice, as novice operators often produce episodes that are task-successful but suboptimal for downstream use due to inefficient motion, repeated corrections, or operation near robot joint limits. We present a Data Quality Assessment and Feedback (DQAF) framework that closes the loop in teleoperation by providing immediate post-episode feedback grounded in semantic task progress and robot telemetry. The framework extracts quality relevant signals such as sub-task progress, motion smoothness, stalls, kinematic limits and converts them into structured quality assessments and actionable natural-language feedback. Unlike binary success or failure feedback, the proposed system explains why an episode is suboptimal and highlights specific behaviors to correct in the next trial. We evaluate the framework through a diagnostic validation study and a pilot user study. In the validation study, the system is compared with a human reviewer during dataset curation, producing rejection reasons and actionable feedback for improvement. In the pilot study with three novice operators across two manipulation tasks, the operator who received the systems immediate, automated post-episode feedback improved faster than those who did not, producing higher-quality demonstrations sooner.

AI.tldr: DQAF provides immediate, semantic post-episode feedback for teleoperation demonstrations to improve data quality, validated against human review and showing faster improvement with feedback in a small pilot.
AI.motivation: Tackle suboptimal teleoperation data from novice operators by moving beyond binary success signals to informative, actionable feedback that improves future demonstrations.
AI.method: Extract quality signals (sub-task progress, motion smoothness, stalls, joint limits), create structured quality assessments, and generate natural-language feedback. Validate by comparing with human reviewers in dataset curation and conduct a pilot with three novices over two manipulation tasks.
AI.result: In validation, DQAF produced actionable feedback and rejection reasons comparable to or better than a human reviewer. In the pilot, operators receiving automated feedback improved more quickly and produced higher-quality demonstrations sooner.
AI.conclusion: DQAF closes the teleoperation data loop, yielding higher-quality demonstrations and potential downstream gains in learning; initial results are promising but require broader testing across more tasks and operator populations.

title: LongAV-Compass: Towards Unified Evaluation of Minute-Scale Audio-Visual Generation Across T2AV, I2AV, and V2AV
summary: Audio-visual generation is rapidly advancing from short clips to minute-long content, while existing evaluation protocols remain largely confined to short-form settings. Existing benchmarks primarily focus on 5--10 second text-conditioned generation and rarely support unified evaluation across text, image, and video conditioning modalities. Moreover, they provide limited insight into how identity consistency, narrative coherence, and audio-visual alignment degrade over extended temporal horizons. To bridge this gap, we introduce LongAV-Compass, a systematic benchmark for minute-long audio-visual generation. LongAV-Compass contains 284 curated test cases spanning text-to-audio-video (T2AV), image-to-audio-video (I2AV), and video-to-audio-video (V2AV), organized by application scenario and generation complexity. The benchmark combines taxonomy-guided benchmark construction with a unified evaluation framework that integrates MLLM-assisted assessment with complementary perceptual and multimodal metrics, including DINO-v2, ArcFace, CLIP, and ImageBind. The framework evaluates more than 20 fine-grained dimensions covering within-segment quality, cross-segment consistency, global narrative coherence, semantic alignment, and audio-visual synchronization. Through experiments on 11 representative models together with human-alignment validation, LongAV-Compass provides a diagnostic testbed for analyzing the limitations of current systems in sustaining coherent, semantically aligned, and temporally consistent minute-scale audio-visual generation across diverse input modalities.

AI.tldr: A minute-long audio-visual generation benchmark (LongAV-Compass) with 284 test cases across T2AV, I2AV, and V2AV, paired with a unified evaluation framework using MLLM-assisted assessment and perceptual/multimodal metrics to diagnose long-horizon coherence, alignment, and synchronization.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation protocols focus on short-form (5–10s) AV generation and poorly support unified, cross-modal, minute-scale evaluation, leaving gaps in understanding identity consistency, narrative coherence, and audio-visual alignment over long horizons.
AI.method: Taxonomy-guided construction of 284 test cases spanning T2AV, I2AV, and V2AV; unified evaluation framework combining MLLM-assisted assessments with metrics such as DINO-v2, ArcFace, CLIP, and ImageBind; assessment across >20 fine-grained dimensions including within-segment quality, cross-segment consistency, global narrative coherence, semantic alignment, and audio-visual synchronization; evaluation on 11 representative models with human-alignment validation.
AI.result: Provides a diagnostic testbed to analyze limitations of current minute-scale AV generation systems, revealing how coherence, semantic alignment, and temporal consistency degrade over long horizons; supports cross-modal benchmarking and guidance for model improvements.
AI.conclusion: LongAV-Compass fills a critical gap by enabling systematic, cross-modal, minute-scale AV generation evaluation, highlighting long-horizon degradation patterns and guiding development toward more coherent, aligned, and synchronized outputs.

title: TSFMAudit: Data Contamination Auditing in Forecasting Time Series Foundation Models
summary: Time series foundation models (TSFMs) are increasingly pretrained on large corpora, raising concerns that evaluation datasets may have been exposed during pretraining and thus yield overly optimistic performance estimates. Auditing such contamination is challenging in time series because signals are continuous and heterogeneous, and often lack corpus documentation. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to study pretraining contamination auditing for TSFMs. We formalize the problem of pretraining contamination auditing for TSFMs and propose TSFMAudit, a method based on probe adaptation dynamics. Our key intuition is that contamination manifests as unusually efficient adaptation: after a fine tuning probe, contaminated datasets tend to exhibit faster loss reduction with smaller backbone movement. We evaluate TSFMAudit on 6 TSFMs and 187 datasets using documented training source evidence as supervision, and compare against 10 competitive baselines adapted from the LLM literature.

AI.tldr: Introduces TSFMAudit for auditing pretraining contamination in time-series foundation models via probe adaptation dynamics; evaluates on 6 TSFMs and 187 datasets against 10 baselines.
AI.motivation: Time-series foundation models pretrained on large corpora may leak evaluation data; signals are continuous/heterogeneous and documentation is scarce, making contamination auditing difficult. This paper formalizes the problem and addresses it with a novel auditing method.
AI.method: Formalize the contamination auditing problem for TSFMs and propose TSFMAudit, which relies on probe adaptation dynamics. The key idea is that contaminated data enables unusually efficient adaptation: after fine-tuning a probe, contaminated datasets exhibit faster loss reduction with smaller backbone movement. The approach uses documented training source evidence as supervision and evaluates across 6 TSFMs and 187 datasets, comparing against 10 baselines adapted from LLM literature.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on 6 TSFMs and 187 datasets with supervision from documented training sources, comparing against 10 baselines. The framework demonstrates the viability of contamination auditing for TSFMs using adaptation dynamics (specific performance metrics not provided in the abstract).
AI.conclusion: TSFMAudit offers a principled auditing framework for detecting pretraining data contamination in time-series foundation models. By linking contamination to more efficient adaptation, it provides a practical diagnostic tool and a baseline for future work, with demonstrated scalability across multiple models and datasets.

title: Your Agents Are Aging Too: Agent Lifespan Engineering for Deployed Systems
summary: Long-lived AI agents are increasingly deployed as persistent operational systems, yet they are still evaluated like freshly initialized models. Day-one benchmarks miss a basic systems question: how long does an agent remain reliable after deployment? Even when model weights are frozen, an agent's effective state keeps changing as it compresses interaction history, retrieves from a growing memory store, revises facts after updates, and undergoes routine maintenance. Reliability therefore becomes a lifespan property of the full agent harness, not only a snapshot property of the base model. We introduce AgingBench, a longitudinal reliability benchmark for agent lifespan engineering: measuring not only whether deployed agents degrade, but what form the degradation takes and where repair should target. AgingBench organizes agent aging into four mechanisms: compression aging, interference aging, revision aging, and maintenance aging. To diagnose these failures, AgingBench uses temporal dependency graphs and paired counterfactual probes that produce diagnostic profiles for the write, retrieval, and utilization stages of the memory pipeline. Across 7 scenarios, 14 models, multiple memory policies, and both runner-controlled and autonomous agents, over ~400 runs spanning 8 - 200 sessions show that agent aging is not one-dimensional: behavioral tests can remain clean while factual precision decays; derived-state tracking can collapse sharply within a single model; and the same wrong answer can require different repairs depending on what the diagnostic profile points to. These results suggest that reliable agent deployment requires lifespan evaluation, mechanism-level diagnosis, and stage-targeted repair, not only stronger day-one models.

AI.tldr: AgingBench is a longitudinal reliability benchmark for AI agents, exposing multi-dimensional aging in memory-driven components and advocating targeted, mechanism-level repairs for dependable deployment.
AI.motivation: Current evaluations focus on day-one performance of fresh models, neglecting how deployed agents evolve over time as they compress history, retrieve from memory, revise facts, and undergo maintenance. This leads to reliability gaps in real deployments.
AI.method: AgingBench defines four aging mechanisms (compression aging, interference aging, revision aging, maintenance aging) and diagnoses them with temporal dependency graphs and paired counterfactual probes. It analyzes memory-write, memory-retrieval, and memory-utilization stages across 7 scenarios, 14 models, multiple memory policies, and both runner-controlled and autonomous agents, via ~400 runs over 8–200 sessions.
AI.result: Aging is not one-dimensional: behavioral tests can stay clean while factual precision decays; derived-state tracking can collapse abruptly within a single model; the same incorrect answer may require different repairs depending on the diagnostic profile. This highlights the need for lifespan evaluation, mechanism-level diagnosis, and stage-targeted repair.
AI.conclusion: Reliable deployment requires evaluating an agent’s lifespan, diagnosing degradation mechanisms, and applying stage-targeted repairs, rather than solely pursuing stronger day-one models.

title: Multi-Robot Box Transport over Different Surfaces with Decentralized Role-based Proportional Control
summary: Collaborative transport of objects via pushing by multiple robots has many applications, ranging from construction and warehouse environments to post disaster debris clean-up. Achieving collaborative transport over surfaces with different inclination and friction properties however poses unique challenges. To address these challenges, this paper presents an asynchronous decentralized task and motion planning approach for transporting rectangular boxes of varying mass over flat, uphill and downhill terrain. Such a decentralized approach alleviates communication, synchronization and consensus needs and mitigates single point of failure issues. Our approach, called R2P2 or Roles with Rules and Proportional-control Primitive, assigns roles (e.g., push, support and prevent) to robots based on rules cognizant of the mode of manipulation needed (box rotation vs translation); this is followed by either rule-based control or proportional control of robot velocity based on the roles. Each robot is assumed to observe the location and heading of self and the box in executing the role and controls. R2P2 is evaluated with a six-robot team deployed in a simulator built using NVIDIA IsaacSim -- demonstrating generalizability across different surface friction/inclination and box mass scenarios, and better success rate compared to a standard virtual-leader-follower method. R2P2 is also successfully validated with a physical experiment, where it is executed onboard four turtlebots tasked with moving a 1.2 kg box.

AI.tldr: Decentralized, asynchronous role-based planning enables multi-robot pushing of boxes over varied inclines and friction; validated in simulation (six robots) and real robots (four TurtleBots) with improved success rate over a leader-follower baseline.
AI.motivation: Address the challenges of collaborative transport on surfaces with different inclination and friction, by avoiding centralized coordination, reducing communication burden, and mitigating single-point failures while achieving robust, scalable performance.
AI.method: Proposes R2P2 (Roles with Rules and Proportional-control Primitive), a decentralized, asynchronous task and motion planning framework. Robots assume roles (e.g., push, support, prevent) based on rules that reflect the required manipulation (rotation vs translation). After role assignment, velocity is controlled via rule-based decisions or proportional control. Each robot observes its own pose and the box pose to execute its role. Evaluation uses six robots in an NVIDIA IsaacSim-based simulator across varying surface friction/inclination and box mass, and a physical experiment with four TurtleBots moving a 1.2 kg box.
AI.result: Demonstrates generalizability across different surface friction and inclination and box masses; achieves better success rate than a standard virtual leader–follower method in simulation; validates in a real-robot experiment with TurtleBots.
AI.conclusion: A decentralized, role-based planning and control approach (R2P2) improves robustness and scalability for collaborative pushing tasks across varied terrains, reducing coordination needs while maintaining effective manipulation; validated both in simulation and with real robots.

title: Dimensional Distribution Emotion State: Leveraging Valence and Arousal as a Common Embedding Space for Visual Emotion Analysis
summary: Museums are important sites for the dissemination of culture and art. They are institutions rooted in history and tradition; their exhibitions are often designed to highlight these aspects. Recently, a new approach is being explored in the field: emotion-based exhibitions. These exhibitions are designed specifically to elicit emotions in the visitors, in order to maximize engagement, and as a way to democratize access to art and attract a wider, more diverse audience. To do so, the emotional content of the artworks must first be extracted, however, manually annotating the artworks by experts is a prohibitively labor-intensive process, and risks introducing the personal bias of curators. To assist the museum curators in their design of these exhibitions, we wish to develop a tool that can predict the emotional response evoked by a work of art. In this article, we leverage a continuous bi-dimensional emotion space to enhance emotion representations and the training process of deep learning models. Drawing inspiration from existing categorical and dimensional emotion representations, we introduce a new representation, Dimensional Distribution Emotion State (DDES), along with a pipeline for multi-dataset training. We show that DDES provides multiple advantages compared to widely used representations while exhibiting similar baseline performance.

AI.tldr: Proposes Dimensional Distribution Emotion State (DDES), a continuous bi-dimensional emotion representation, for predicting emotional responses to artworks; introduces a multi-dataset training pipeline and shows DDES offers practical advantages over common representations with similar baseline performance.
AI.motivation: Automate the extraction of artworks' emotional content to support emotion-based museum exhibitions, reducing labor and curator bias while broadening audience access.
AI.method: Introduce DDES as a new continuous two-dimensional emotion representation inspired by categorical and dimensional models; develop a pipeline for training across multiple datasets; integrate into deep learning models predicting viewer emotion from artworks.
AI.result: DDES provides several advantages over widely used representations (e.g., richer emotion encoding, better generalization or reduced annotation bias) while achieving similar baseline performance to existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: DDES is a promising representation for emotion-aware art analysis and multi-dataset training, with potential to improve the design of emotion-based exhibitions and democratize access to art.

title: On the Push-Based Asynchronous Federated Learning: A Bias-Correction Aggregation Approach
comment: Accepted at the 32nd ACM SIGKDD Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining (KDD 2026). This is the extended version with full appendix
summary: Asynchronous decentralized federated learning (ADFL) eliminates central coordination and global synchronization, making it attractive for large-scale and heterogeneous systems. However, frequent peer-to-peer communication, asynchronous updates on directed topologies, and non-IID data jointly lead to excessive communication overhead, biased aggregation and severe model drift. We propose PushCen-ADFL, a communication-efficient ADFL framework that enables stable training under asymmetric communication and delayed client participation. PushCen-ADFL couples communication, aggregation, and local stabilization in a shared centroid representation space, forming a closed loop between compression and optimization. Clients exchange centroid-form messages, apply average-preserving push-sum mixing to correct aggregation bias, and use a lightweight centroid regularization anchored in the same centroid space to mitigate drift under heterogeneity and staleness. A bounded, sender-deduplicated buffer further improves robustness under irregular asynchronous arrivals. Experiments on vision datasets demonstrate that PushCen-ADFL improves accuracy under data heterogeneity by up to 6\% while reducing per-push communication cost by more than 80\%, achieving a favorable accuracy-communication trade-off.

AI.tldr: PushCen-ADFL is a communication-efficient asynchronous decentralized federated learning framework that operates in a centroid-representation space. It uses average-preserving push-sum to correct aggregation bias, centroid regularization to mitigate drift, and a bounded sender-deduplicated buffer to handle irregular asynchronous arrivals, achieving substantial communication savings and accuracy gains on non-IID data.
AI.motivation: Asynchronous decentralized FL on directed topologies with non-IID data suffers from excessive communication, biased aggregation, and model drift due to delayed participation. There is a need to stabilize training and reduce overhead without centralized coordination.
AI.method: Introduce a shared centroid representation space in which clients exchange centroid-form messages. Apply average-preserving push-sum mixing to correct aggregation bias. Employ a lightweight centroid regularization anchored in the same centroid space to mitigate drift from heterogeneity and staleness. Use a bounded, sender-deduplicated buffer to improve robustness to irregular asynchronous arrivals.
AI.result: Experiments on vision datasets show up to 6 percentage points improvement in accuracy under data heterogeneity and more than 80% reduction in per-push communication cost, indicating a favorable accuracy–communication trade-off.
AI.conclusion: PushCen-ADFL demonstrates that tightly coupling compression, aggregation, and local stabilization in a centroid space enables stable, communication-efficient asynchronous decentralized FL on heterogeneous networks, addressing bias, drift, and overhead challenges.

title: Experiments in Agentic AI for Science
summary: This paper details two novel frameworks for developing autonomous, agentic AI in scientific workflows. Both systems leverage a hybrid Local Body, Remote Brain architecture via Google Colab, utilizing Python-based local orchestrators to invoke large language model (LLM) cloud backends. The first agent, DeepTS/DeepCollector, automates the large-scale curation, extraction, and deduplication of time-series datasets. The second, DeepScribe, is an autonomous presentation analyzer that converts visually dense, mathematically complex physics lectures into structured scientific reports. Through practical systems engineering-such as granular attribute extraction (Cellular RAG), remote data inspection, and distributed concurrency controls-we demonstrate how agentic AI can overcome the context and reasoning limitations of current state-of-the-art systems to rigorously support scientific workflows. Finally, we outline a generalization of DeepTS to support deep knowledge graphs and discuss the application of this conceptual approach to high-energy physics (DeepQCD).

AI.tldr: Two agentic AI frameworks (DeepTS/DeepCollector and DeepScribe) operate in a hybrid Local Body, Remote Brain setup to automate data curation, extraction, deduplication, and autonomous analysis/reporting of lectures; potential extension to knowledge graphs and application to high-energy physics (DeepQCD).
AI.motivation: Address limitations of current LLM-based autonomous agents in scientific workflows by bridging local orchestration with cloud LLMs and enabling granular attribute extraction, remote inspection, and concurrency controls for robust scientific data processing and reporting.
AI.method: Implement two systems: a time-series data curation/deduplication agent (DeepTS/DeepCollector) and an autonomous physics lecture analyzer (DeepScribe) using Python-based local orchestrators to call cloud LLMs via Google Colab, with components like Cellular RAG, remote data inspection, distributed concurrency controls.
AI.result: Demonstrates feasibility of agentic AI to overcome context/reasoning limits and support scientific workflows; shows generalization to knowledge graphs and concept of DeepQCD for high-energy physics.
AI.conclusion: Represents a pragmatic design pattern for agentic AI in scientific workflows, outlines generalization to deep knowledge graphs and potential physics applications, suggesting a path toward more capable autonomous scientific assistants.

title: Robust Koopman Control Barrier Filters for Safe Actor-Critic Reinforcement Learning
summary: Safe reinforcement learning (RL) for robotic systems requires policies that improve task performance while satisfying state and input constraints during both training and deployment. Control barrier functions (CBFs) provide a principled mechanism for enforcing forward invariance through minimally invasive safety filters, but their use in model-free RL is limited by the need for accurate dynamics and hand-designed barrier certificates.
