We propose Robust Koopman-CBF SAC, a safety-filtered actor--critic framework that learns a finite-dimensional Koopman predictor from data, constructs affine CBF constraints in the lifted space, and enforces them through a quadratic-program safety layer. To account for finite-dimensional Koopman approximation error, the CBF condition is tightened using a projected residual margin estimated from held-out rollout data. The critic is trained on the executed safe action, while the actor is regularized toward the Koopman-CBF feasible set, reducing dependence on the filter over training. Across safe-control benchmarks, the method achieves zero constraint violations on CartPole stabilization and tracking while matching or exceeding unconstrained SAC returns. On high-dimensional Safety Gymnasium locomotion tasks, the method reduces violations in some settings but also exposes important limitations of first-order velocity barriers and linear EDMD models, motivating high-order and multi-step Koopman-CBF extensions. These results suggest that robust Koopman-CBF filters are a promising bridge between model-free RL and certifiable safety, while clarifying the structural conditions under which such filters remain effective. All code is available at \href{https://github.com/DhruvKushwaha/Koopman-CBF-Soft-Actor-Critic}{Github Repository}.

AI.tldr: A model-free RL safety-filter framework combining Koopman dynamics with CBF-based safety via a QP; robustly accounts for Koopman error; achieves zero constraint violations in CartPole; shows partial success in Safety Gym locomotion; highlights need for higher-order extensions.
AI.motivation: Need for safe RL where constraints hold during training and deployment; limit of conventional CBFs requiring accurate dynamics and hand-crafted barriers; desire to leverage data-driven Koopman models with a safety layer in SAC.
AI.method: Learn finite-dimensional Koopman predictor from data; construct affine CBF constraints in lifted space; enforce them through a quadratic-program safety layer; tighten CBF condition using projected residual margin estimated from held-out rollout data to account for model error; critic trained on executed safe action; actor regularized toward the Koopman-CBF feasible set to reduce dependence on the filter during training.
AI.result: Zero constraint violations on CartPole stabilization and tracking; matches or exceeds unconstrained SAC returns; on high-dimensional Safety Gym locomotion tasks, reductions in violations in some settings but reveals limitations of first-order velocity barriers and linear EDMD models; motivates high-order and multi-step Koopman-CBF extensions.
AI.conclusion: Robust Koopman-CBF filters are a promising bridge between model-free RL and certifiable safety, clarifying structural conditions under which such filters remain effective; code is available in the linked repository.

title: Frequency-Guided Fusion For RGB-Thermal Semantic Segmentation
comment: 9 pages, 7 figures, To be Presented at Perception Beyond the Visible Spectrum workshop series (IEEE PBVS) at CVPR, 2026
summary: Semantic segmentation in complex environments such as urban driving scenes remains challenging under adverse lighting conditions, where RGB images alone provide insufficient information. RGB-Thermal fusion leverages the complementary strengths of visible and infrared imagery to improve scene understanding; however, effectively integrating these heterogeneous modalities at varying levels of feature abstraction remains an open problem. In this paper, we propose a multi-modal fusion architecture built upon dual ConvNeXt V2 backbones that employs stage-wise, modality-adaptive fusion strategies. For early-stage features, we introduce a Frequency-Based Fusion Module that decomposes infrared features into low- and high-frequency components via Gaussian filtering, applies dual-branch spatial attention to selectively emphasize thermal patterns and fine-grained boundaries, and integrates them with RGB features through a confidence-gated residual mechanism. For late-stage features, we design a semantic fusion module with cross-modal attention and multi-scale depthwise convolutions to capture semantic correspondences across modalities. The fused features are decoded via a PANet-style bidirectional decoder with deep supervision. Experiments on MFNet and PST900 demonstrate that our lightest variant achieves 61.73\% and 86.24\% mIoU, respectively, with only 35.43M parameters, outperforming recent methods while using substantially fewer parameters and lower computational cost. Code is available at https://github.com/ismailemrecntz/VISIBLE-INFRARED-SENSOR-FUSION

AI.tldr: A lightweight, dual-ConvNeXt V2 RGB-T fusion network with stage-wise modality-adaptive fusion, featuring early Frequency-Based Fusion and late semantic fusion, achieving strong mIoU on MFNet and PST900 with 35.43M parameters.
AI.motivation: RGB-Thermal fusion for semantic segmentation in challenging lighting; need effective, multi-level integration of heterogeneous modalities with low computational cost.
AI.method: Dual ConvNeXt V2 backbones; early-stage Frequency-Based Fusion Module decomposes infrared into low/high frequencies via Gaussian filtering, uses dual-branch spatial attention, and fuses with RGB through a confidence-gated residual; late-stage semantic fusion with cross-modal attention and multi-scale depthwise convolutions; PANet-style bidirectional decoder with deep supervision.
AI.result: On MFNet and PST900, the lightest variant reaches 61.73% and 86.24% mIoU with 35.43M parameters, outperforming recent methods while using substantially fewer parameters and lower computation.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates effective, parameter-efficient RGB-T fusion through multi-stage fusion and attention mechanisms, with public code available.

title: Planning Neural Dynamics with Lie Group Embedding through Supervised Projective Manifold Learning
summary: We propose Lie group embedded dynamical neural networks (LieEDNN) and the corresponding learning algorithms based on gradient descent and metric projection on smooth manifold, where we treat Lie group as an intrinsic representation for continuous symmetry of manifold geometry. Thereby we achieve learnable and stable dynamics on the underlying manifold for general Lie group, and we are able to utilize the powerful representation capability of Lie group such as SO(3) and SE(3) to solve real world engineering problems in areas such as robotics, graphics, and control. Two core challenges are: (i) General Lie groups are incompatible with addition arithmetic, which is necessary for neural network interactions. (ii) The dynamics evolve in the nonlinear representation space of special algebra rather than the normal Euclidean space, which violates the paradigm of common neural ODEs. To address these two challenges, we firstly introduce adjoint Lie group action on the Lie algebra, which induces a linear mapping and transfer to the block-wise structure of weight matrices, such that addition could operate on the Lie algebra as a vector space. Then we parameterize the Lie algebra and the adjoint action as linear transformation so that the architecture is aligned with neural network perceptrons. Explicitly, this embedding appears as block-wise manifold constraints on weights, and we develop algorithms to learn the equilibrium with stability guarantees of the temporal neural network dynamics. Experiments are implemented on a specific Lie group SE(3), with the application scenario of telescopic manipulators.

AI.tldr: Lie group embedded dynamical neural networks (LieEDNN) enable gradient-descent learning on Lie groups by using adjoint actions to map to Lie algebra, allowing addition and linearization, with block-wise weight constraints to obtain stable, manifold-aware neural dynamics; demonstrated on SE(3) for telescopic manipulators.
AI.motivation: Standard neural ODEs and Euclidean neural networks struggle with non-Euclidean symmetry and manifold constraints of Lie groups. Exploiting Lie group representations (e.g., SO(3), SE(3)) promises more accurate, stable dynamics for applications in robotics, graphics, and control.
AI.method: Propose Lie group embedded dynamical neural networks with gradient descent and metric projection on smooth manifolds. Introduce adjoint Lie group action on the Lie algebra to obtain a linear mapping and block-wise weight structure, enabling addition in the Lie algebra. Parameterize the Lie algebra and adjoint action as linear transformations to align with perceptrons. Impose block-wise manifold constraints on weights and develop algorithms to learn equilibria with stability guarantees for temporal neural dynamics.
AI.result: Empirical validation on SE(3) in the context of telescopic manipulators. The approach demonstrates feasibility of the LieEDNN framework and yields learnable, stable dynamics on the Lie group manifold, with experiments showcasing practical applicability.
AI.conclusion: The framework bridges non-Euclidean Lie group geometry with neural dynamics by embedding Lie groups into a linear Lie algebra via adjoint actions, enabling stable, symmetry-aware learning and broad applicability to robotics, graphics, and control.

title: Anchor: Mitigating Artifact Drift in Agent Benchmark Generation
comment: Accepted to RLEval '26 (Workshop at ACM Conference on AI and Agentic Systems 2026)
summary: AI agents are beginning to complete valuable, long-horizon business operations tasks, but training and evaluation environments for enterprise work still struggle to balance realism, verifiability, and scale. Environment and task creation frequently suffers from a failure mode we call artifact drift: when instructions, environments, oracles, and verifiers are created by loosely coupled processes, they frequently disagree on what a task requires, producing environments that are unsolvable, reward-hackable, or inconsistent. We introduce Anchor, a task-generation pipeline that formalizes domain experts' specifications of business workflows into constraint optimization programs. From a single parametric specification, the pipeline jointly produces a natural-language instruction, environment configuration, solver-certified ground-truth solution, and state-based verifier. With Anchor, altering parameters yields new tasks with controlled difficulty and known optimal solutions, producing harness-agnostic environments whose rewards depend solely on end-state business correctness. We apply Anchor to produce ERP-Bench: a benchmark of 300 long-horizon tasks spanning procurement and manufacturing workflows in a production-grade ERP system. We find that generation parameters predict realized difficulty, and that frontier models satisfy explicit task constraints in 26.1% of trials but reach a fully optimal solution in only 17.4% of trials. Overall, we show that Anchor and ERP-Bench offer a concrete recipe for building auditable evaluation environments for economically valuable agent work. We release the task generator and ERP-Bench dataset at erpbench.ai

AI.tldr: Anchor is a parameterized task-generation pipeline that converts domain expert workflows into auditable, end-to-end AI evaluation environments; applied to ERP-Bench with 300 long-horizon tasks; results show partial constraint satisfaction and limited optimality; dataset released.
AI.motivation: Tackle artifact drift and misalignment between instructions, environments, oracles, and verifiers in enterprise task benchmarks, by creating realistic, verifiable, scalable environments with known ground-truth solutions.
AI.method: Formalize domain-specifications as constraint- optimization programs; from a single parametric spec jointly generate a natural-language instruction, environment configuration, solver-certified ground-truth solution, and state-based verifier; enable controlled-difficulty task generation and harness-agnostic evaluation; applied to ERP workflows to build ERP-Bench (300 tasks in production-grade ERP).
AI.result: Generation parameters correlate with realized task difficulty; frontier models satisfy explicit task constraints in 26.1% of trials and achieve fully optimal solutions in 17.4% of trials.
AI.conclusion: Anchor plus ERP-Bench provide a concrete, auditable recipe for constructing economically meaningful agent evaluation environments; the authors release the task generator and ERP-Bench dataset at erpbench.ai.

title: Heterogeneous AAV Logistics Task Allocation: A Reinforcement Learning Enhanced Overlapping Coalition Formation Game Approach
summary: In dynamic urban logistics, the stochastic emergence of time-sensitive tasks poses a significant optimality challenge for heterogeneous AAVs logistics task allocation. To address this problem, a reinforcement learning enhanced overlapping coalition formation game approach is proposed. A dynamic task allocation model is established, where global optimality is mathematically quantified by a generalized logistics cost coupling service quality and resource consumption. To deal with the time-varying task sets induced by stochastic order arrivals, a transformer-based soft actor-critic network is designed. By leveraging multi-head self-attention to encode variable-length logistics states and capture task-wise spatiotemporal dependencies, the learned policy adaptively guides coalition updates, replacing heuristic rules in the overlapping coalition formation game. On this basis, heterogeneous AAVs can form more efficient overlapping coalitions for dynamic logistics tasks. The resulting coalition formation process is proven to constitute an exact potential game, which guarantees convergence to a Nash-stable equilibrium within a finite number of iterations. Numerical simulations demonstrate that the proposed algorithm effectively improves the optimality of task allocation under the generalized logistics cost criterion. In a scenario with 32 AAVs and 80 tasks, our algorithm achieves a 39.76% cost reduction compared with the heuristic OCF baseline. Indoor flight experiments further validate its practicality.

AI.tldr: RL-enhanced overlapping coalition formation for dynamic AAV task allocation with transformer SAC; proves exact potential game and Nash-stable convergence; achieves substantial cost savings in simulated and indoor tests.
AI.motivation: Address stochastic emergence of time-sensitive tasks in dynamic urban logistics with heterogeneous AAVs; existing heuristics underperform; seek adaptive, provably convergent coalition formation to improve global cost optimality.
AI.method: Model: generalized logistics cost combining service quality and resource consumption; dynamic task set due to stochastic orders. Method: transform-based soft actor-critic (SAC) with multi-head self-attention to encode variable-length logistics states and spatiotemporal task dependencies; policy guides overlapping coalition updates (OCF) rather than heuristic rules; establishes OCF as an exact potential game with Nash-stable convergence in finite iterations.
AI.result: Simulation with 32 AAVs and 80 tasks shows ~39.76% cost reduction versus heuristic OCF baseline. Indoor flight experiments validate practicality and convergence.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Transformer-SAC OCF framework provides a scalable, model-driven approach for dynamic, heterogeneous AAV task allocation with convergence guarantees and demonstrated cost efficiency; supports real-time coalition formation under stochastic demand; future work could address scalability, robustness, and real-world deployment considerations.

title: VesselSim: learning 3D blood vessel segmentation without expert annotations
comment: This preprint has not undergone peer review or any post-submission improvements or corrections. The Version of Record of this contribution will be published as part of the MICCAI 2026 proceedings in October
summary: Blood vessel segmentation is a core task in medical image analysis for the care of vascular diseases and surgical planning, yet the challenges of providing expert vascular annotations pose a major obstacle for the progress of related deep learning techniques. To address this, we propose VesselSim, a two-stage framework for universal 3D blood vessel segmentation that eliminates the need for real annotated data during training. First, we introduce a stochastic, geometry-driven vascular simulation framework that models recursive branching, curvature-controlled growth, and collision-aware topology, followed by domain-randomized intensity synthesis to generate 16,500 anatomically plausible 3D angiographic volumes. Second, a 3D U-Net is trained solely on this synthetic data. To bridge the domain gap from synthetic to real images at inference time, we introduce a test-time adaptation strategy via a self-supervised mask reconstruction decoder, enabling adaptation to unseen clinical scans without prior domain knowledge. We evaluate VesselSim in a zero-shot setting on multiple real-world datasets spanning MR and CT across several anatomical regions, including the brain and kidneys. Despite being trained exclusively on synthetic data, VesselSim achieves performance competitive with state-of-the-art vascular segmentation foundation models. These findings suggest that learning vessel geometry from synthetic tubular structures is effective for robust cross-domain generalization, substantially reducing the reliance on acquired medical imaging data and more importantly, expert annotations.

AI.tldr: A synthetic-data–driven, zero-shot 3D vascular segmentation framework (VesselSim) that uses a stochastic vascular simulator and domain-randomized intensity to train a 3D U-Net, with test-time self-supervised adaptation, achieving competitive cross-domain performance without real annotations.
AI.motivation: Expert vascular annotations are scarce and costly; need robust cross-domain segmentation across MR/CT and anatomical regions without labeled data.
AI.method: Stage 1: stochastic geometry-based vascular simulation (recursive branching, curvature-controlled growth, collision-aware topology) to generate 16,500 synthetic 3D volumes with domain randomization of intensities. Stage 2: train 3D U-Net on synthetic data. Test-time adaptation via self-supervised mask reconstruction decoder to adapt to unseen clinical scans without domain knowledge.
AI.result: Zero-shot evaluation on multiple real-world MR/CT datasets across brain and kidney; VesselSim performance competitive with state-of-the-art vascular segmentation foundation models, despite no real training data.
AI.conclusion: Learning vessel geometry from synthetic tubular structures enables robust cross-domain generalization and substantially reduces reliance on annotated medical imaging data.

title: When Rule Violations Are Rare: Chimera Training for Logical Anomaly Detection
summary: Many practical anomalies are not merely rare inputs, but violations of semantic constraints: objects co-occur in structured ways, actions imply preconditions, and events satisfy temporal or relational regularities. We study anomaly detection in this setting, where constraints are given as logical rules over learned visual concepts, but real rule violations are rare or absent during training. We propose a neural rule evaluator that compiles each constraint into a directed acyclic graph and learns feature-aware subtree MLP gates for its internal logical operators. Each gate maps child features and edge-level negations to a parent representation and a rule-satisfaction probability, with intermediate supervision obtained from exact Boolean propagation over ground-truth concept labels. The key difficulty is that same-image training data often provide insufficient coverage of informative truth configurations and also allow shortcut solutions. To address this, we introduce chimera training: an operand-level counterfactual construction at the feature level. Instead of mixing input images, we concatenate subtree features from different samples; each operand keeps the hard truth label of the sample it came from, and the chimera target is obtained by applying the node's logical operator to those inherited labels. This supplies supervised logical counterexamples without requiring real anomalous images. Across CLEVRER, OpenImages, and VidOR, the resulting evaluator improves rule-level anomaly AUROC over independent-events and same-image semantic-training baselines, especially for compositional and relational rules. The method yields both scalar anomaly scores and rule-level attributions.

AI.tldr: Proposes a neural rule evaluator for detecting anomalies under semantic constraints by compiling rules into DAGs with feature-aware MLP gates and using chimera training to generate counterfactual supervision; achieves improved rule-level anomaly detection and interpretable attributions across datasets.
AI.motivation: Anomalies arise from violations of structured, semantically constrained relationships; real rule violations are rare during training, so models must learn from limited coverage and avoid shortcuts.
AI.method: Each constraint is compiled into a DAG; gates map child features and edge-level negations to a parent representation and a rule-satisfaction probability. Intermediate supervision uses exact Boolean propagation on ground-truth labels. Chimera training concatenates subtree features from different samples, preserving each operand's true label, with the target obtained by applying the operator to those labels.
AI.result: On CLEVRER, OpenImages, and VidOR, the method improves rule-level anomaly AUROC over independent-events and same-image semantic-training baselines, particularly for compositional/relational rules; provides scalar anomaly scores and rule-level attributions.
AI.conclusion: Counterfactual feature-level supervision via chimera training mitigates coverage gaps and shortcut solutions, yielding accurate, interpretable rule-based anomaly detection across diverse datasets.

title: OmniToM: Benchmarking Theory of Mind in LLMs via Explicit Belief Modeling
summary: Theory of Mind (ToM), the ability to infer others' knowledge, intentions, and emotions, is commonly evaluated in large language models (LLMs) using end-point question answering, where performance is judged solely by the final answer to a social reasoning query. This paradigm obscures whether the model actually constructs the underlying mental-state representations required for robust reasoning, particularly in scenarios involving divergent, evolving, or mistaken beliefs. In order to address this research gap, we introduce OmniToM, a benchmark that directly evaluates these representations by requiring explicit modeling of belief structures for all relevant actors within a narrative. These structures are composed of belief propositions: minimal statements of what an actor takes to be true about the world or another actor's mental state, allowing knowledge, intentions, emotions, and false beliefs to be analyzed in a common format. Models are evaluated in two stages: Stage 1: Belief Extraction, which extracts from the story the beliefs relevant to its social dynamics, and Stage 2: Belief Labeling, which assigns each belief a seven-dimensional schema label covering recursive order, truth status, knowledge access, explicitness, content type, mental source, and context. Built from 895 stories from the existing ToMBench story corpus and augmented with 22,343 labeled belief propositions, OmniToM uses a human-calibrated LLM-assisted annotation pipeline. Across diverse models in zero-shot evaluation, OmniToM reveals an actor-specific belief-tracking bottleneck: current LLMs struggle with the knowledge-access and representational decisions required to transform narrative facts into actors' beliefs and shared mental states.

AI.tldr: OmniToM: a two-stage benchmark to directly evaluate Theory of Mind beliefs in LLMs by extracting and labeling beliefs about world and mental states; reveals bottlenecks in knowledge access and belief representation across models.
AI.motivation: End-point question answering for ToM masks whether models truly form underlying mental-state representations; need explicit, interpretable belief structures to capture divergent, evolving, or mistaken beliefs.
AI.method: Stage 1 Belief Extraction identifies beliefs relevant to social dynamics from narratives. Stage 2 Belief Labeling assigns each belief a seven-dimensional schema (recursive order, truth status, knowledge access, explicitness, content type, mental source, context). Built from 895 ToMBench stories with 22,343 labeled propositions; annotated via a human-calibrated LLM-assisted pipeline.
AI.result: Zero-shot evaluation across diverse models shows an actor-specific belief-tracking bottleneck; current LLMs struggle with transforming narrative facts into actors' beliefs and shared mental states due to knowledge-access and representational decisions.
AI.conclusion: OmniToM provides a concrete framework and dataset to study belief representations in ToM, revealing gaps in current LLM capabilities and guiding future modeling toward explicit belief-structure reasoning.

title: Efficient On-policy Visual-RL via Stochastic Decoupled Policy Gradient
summary: We present the stochastic decoupled policy gradient (SDPG), a lightweight visual reinforcement learning (RL) method that trains diverse visuomotor control policies end-to-end within a few hours on a single NVIDIA RTX 4080 GPU. SDPG estimates policy gradients via random perturbations of trajectory rollouts, requiring orders of magnitude fewer batch-rendered environments and substantially reducing compute and memory overhead. On visual MuJoCo benchmarks, SDPG consistently outperforms baseline methods in training time, memory usage, and rewards. Finally, to support future research, we introduce a suite of realistic visual robotics benchmarks spanning dexterous manipulation, challenging locomotion, and demonstrate effective sim-to-real transfer on physical hardware.

AI.tldr: SDPG is a lightweight, sample-efficient visual RL method that uses stochastic decoupled policy gradients via random trajectory perturbations, enabling end-to-end visuomotor policies trained in hours on a single GPU.
AI.motivation: To reduce training time, memory usage, and environment requirements for visual reinforcement learning while enabling end-to-end visuomotor control and sim-to-real transfer.
AI.method: SDPG estimates policy gradients through random perturbations of trajectory rollouts (a stochastic decoupled gradient approach), enabling training with orders of magnitude fewer batch-rendered environments and lower compute/memory overhead on visual MuJoCo tasks.
AI.result: On visual MuJoCo benchmarks, SDPG outperforms baselines in training time, memory usage, and rewards; it includes a benchmark suite of realistic visual robotics tasks and demonstrates effective sim-to-real transfer on hardware.
AI.conclusion: SDPG provides a practical, efficient approach for visual RL with broad applicability, supported by a new visual robotics benchmark suite for future research.

title: Benchmarking Convolutional, Transformer, Hybrid, and Vision Language Models for Multi Disease Retinal Screening
comment: 12 pages, 3 figures, accepted at ICMHI 2026, 10th International Conference on Medical and Health Informatics, Kyoto, Japan. To appear in ACM Conference Proceedings
summary: Modern deep learning offers powerful tools for automated retinal screening, but it remains unclear how different visual model families compare in realistic multi-disease settings and under domain shift. In this work, we benchmark twelve architectures across four model families: convolutional neural networks, vision transformers, hybrid CNN-transformer backbones, and vision-language models, using the Retinal Fundus Multi-disease Image Dataset (RFMiD). We evaluate two tasks: binary screening for any retinal disease and multi-label classification across 28 disease classes. Using standardized training, calibration, and evaluation protocols, we report AUC, F1, precision, recall, and sensitivity at a clinically relevant operating point with specificity near 80%. On RFMiD, all architectures perform well on binary screening, with AUC above 84%, but attention-based models perform best. SwinTiny and the hybrid CoAtNet0 and MaxViTTiny models achieve the strongest binary screening results and improve macro and micro F1 in the multi-label setting. Vision-language models, including CLIP ViT-B/16 and SigLIP-Base384, are competitive with CNN baselines but do not surpass the best transformer and hybrid backbones. In external validation on Messidor-2 for referable diabetic retinopathy, AUC ranges from 66.8% to 84.7%, with hybrid and transformer models again showing strong performance. These results provide a reproducible reference for model selection in multi-disease retinal screening and guide future automated screening tools for clinical deployment.

AI.tldr: Benchmark study of 12 architectures (CNNs, transformers, hybrids, and vision-language models) across RFMiD for binary and multi-label retinal disease tasks; transformers/hybrids excel, vision-language competitive but not superior; external Messidor-2 validation shows strong but variable AUC; provides reference for model selection and deployment guidance.
AI.motivation: To systematically compare diverse visual model families in realistic multi-disease retinal screening and under domain shift, enabling informed model selection for clinical deployment.
AI.method: Standardized training, calibration, and evaluation across four model families (CNNs, vision transformers, hybrid CNN-transformer backbones, vision-language models) on RFMiD for two tasks: binary screening (any disease) and multi-label (28 diseases). Metrics: AUC, F1, precision, recall, and sensitivity at a fixed specificity (~80%). External validation on Messidor-2 for referable diabetic retinopathy.
AI.result: On RFMiD, AUC >84% for binary screening across architectures; attention-based models perform best. SwinTiny, CoAtNet0, and MaxViTTiny yield strongest binary results and improve macro/micro F1 in multi-label. Vision-language models (CLIP ViT-B/16, SigLIP-Base384) are competitive with CNNs but don’t beat best transformer/hybrid backbones. External Messidor-2 validation shows AUC 66.8–84.7%, with hybrids/transformers again strong. The work provides a reproducible reference for model selection in multi-disease retinal screening and guidance for clinical deployment.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid and transformer architectures offer the strongest overall performance for multi-disease retinal screening, with attention-based models leading on binary tasks and certain hybrids excelling in multi-label performance; vision-language models are competitive but not superior. The study establishes a reproducible framework and benchmarks to guide model choice and deployment in real-world screening settings.

title: JobBench: Aligning Agent Work With Human Will
summary: Current benchmarks for occupational AI agents are scoped primarily by economic values, telling a replacement story. We introduce JobBench, which evaluates AI agents on the workflows that experts identify as high-priority for delegation, empowering humans based on their needs instead of replacing them with GDP value. JobBench covers 130 agentic tasks across 35 occupations. Each task is packaged as a workspace of heterogeneous reference files, requiring the agent to reason through the cluttered information streams of real professional work. Outputs are graded by a fact-anchored chain of rubrics, averaging 35.6 binary criteria per task. We evaluate 36 models; the strongest, Claude Opus~4.7 under Claude Code, reaches only 45.9 %. We hope JobBench shifts the community's target labour-market effect from replacement to enhancement: building agents that do what humans actually want delegated, not only what is most economically valuable.

AI.tldr: JobBench presents a workflow- and task-based benchmark for occupational AI agents, shifting evaluation from replacement/value metrics to human augmentation across 130 tasks in 35 occupations; despite 36 models tested, best model reaches 45.9% according to a 35.6-binary-criterion rubric, underscoring substantial room for improvement.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks emphasize economic replacement (GDP/value) rather than aligning AI capabilities with real professional needs. The work argues for evaluating AI on tasks humans actually delegate to augment their work, not merely on cost/replace potential.
AI.method: Provide 130 agentic tasks across 35 occupations, packaged as workspaces containing heterogeneous reference files. Agents must reason through cluttered information streams typical of professional work. Outputs are graded using a fact-anchored rubric consisting of about 35.6 binary criteria per task, enabling fine-grained evaluation across tasks.
AI.result: Evaluation of 36 models; the top performer (Claude Opus 4.7 under Claude Code) achieves 45.9% on the rubric. This indicates substantial gaps between current system capabilities and human-aligned delegation tasks.
AI.conclusion: JobBench may redirect AI research toward enhancement-oriented objectives, incentivizing the development of agents that perform delegated tasks matching human needs rather than focusing solely on economic replacement or GDP gains.

title: Provably Safe Motion Planning Under Unknown Disturbances
summary: We present a provably safe sampling-based motion planning algorithm for robotic systems affected by random disturbances of unknown distribution. We consider systems with linear or linearizable dynamics evolving in workspace with arbitrary-shaped obstacles subject to state and control constraints. Safety requirements are formulated as chance-constraints. Our approach leverages data from trajectories of the system to learn a Wasserstein ambiguity tube, i.e., a sequence of ambiguity sets, which contains the trajectory of the system's state distribution with high confidence. This ambiguity tube is then used in a probabilistically complete algorithm to grow a sampling-based motion planning tree that respects the constraints of the problem. We show that learning several lower-dimensional ambiguity tubes instead of a single high-dimensional one effectively reduces the conservatism and boosts scalability. Additionally, we design an efficient bandit-based validity checker that remarkably increases the empirical performance of our approach without sacrificing probabilistic completeness. Case studies show our algorithm finds valid plans in cluttered environments under strict safety thresholds, outperforming state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: Provably safe sampling-based motion planning under unknown disturbances using Wasserstein ambiguity tubes and a bandit-based validity checker; achieves probabilistic completeness with reduced conservatism and improved performance.
AI.motivation: Ensure safety and feasibility for robotic motion planning in the presence of stochastic disturbances with unknown distribution, while maintaining scalability and probabilistic completeness under chance constraints.
AI.method: Learn a sequence of Wasserstein-ambiguous tubes from trajectory data that bound the state distribution with high confidence; use lower-dimensional ambiguity sets to reduce conservatism; integrate these tubes into a probabilistically complete sampling-based planner to satisfy chance constraints; employ a bandit-based validity checker to efficiently prune/validate samples and improve empirical performance.
AI.result: Learning multiple low-dimensional ambiguity tubes reduces conservatism and improves scalability compared to a single high-dimensional tube; empirical results show better performance than state-of-the-art methods in cluttered environments with tight safety thresholds, while maintaining probabilistic completeness.
AI.conclusion: Representing disturbances with a family of low-dimensional Wasserstein ambiguity tubes, learned from data, enables scalable, provably safe planning. A bandit-based validity checker further enhances practical performance without sacrificing guarantees.

title: A multifractal-based masked auto-encoder: an application to medical images
summary: Masked autoencoders (MAE) have shown great promise in medical image classification. However, the random masking strategy employed by traditional MAEs may overlook critical areas in medical images, where even subtle changes can indicate disease. To address this limitation, we propose a novel approach that utilizes a multifractal measure (Renyi entropy) to optimize the masking strategy. Our method, termed Multifractal-Optimized Masked Autoencoder (MO-MAE), employs a multifractal analysis to identify regions of high complexity and information content. By focusing the masking process on these areas, MO-MAE ensures that the model learns to reconstruct the most diagnostically relevant features. This approach is particularly beneficial for medical imaging, where fine-grained inspection of tissue structures is crucial for accurate diagnosis. We evaluate MO-MAE on several medical datasets covering various diseases, including MedMNIST and COVID-CT. Our results demonstrate that MO-MAE achieves promising performance, surpassing other basiline and state-of-the-art models. The proposed method also adds minimum computational overhead as the computation of the proposed measure is straightforward. Our findings suggest that the multifractal-optimized masking strategy enhances the model's ability to capture and reconstruct complex tissue structures, leading to more accurate and efficient medical image representation. The proposed MO-MAE framework offers a promising direction for improving the accuracy and efficiency of deep learning models in medical image analysis, potentially advancing the field of computer-aided diagnosis.

AI.tldr: MO-MAE uses Renyi entropy-based multifractal analysis to guide masking in masked autoencoders for medical images, focusing on high-information regions to improve reconstruction and classification; evaluates on MedMNIST and COVID-CT with improved performance and low overhead.
AI.motivation: Medical images require sensitive detection of subtle, diagnostically relevant details. Random masking in MAEs may miss these regions, reducing learning efficiency. A multifractal, information-rich masking strategy aims to preserve and emphasize critical structures during reconstruction.
AI.method: Compute Renyi-entropy-based multifractal measures to identify high-complexity regions in images. Use these regions to guide the masking pattern in masked autoencoder training (MO-MAE), concentrating masking on information-rich areas to force the model to reconstruct them. Evaluate on multiple medical datasets (e.g., MedMNIST, COVID-CT) and compare against baselines and state-of-the-art models.
AI.result: Reported to achieve promising performance, surpassing baselines and state-of-the-art models, with minimal computational overhead due to straightforward computation of the multifractal measure.
AI.conclusion: Multifractal-optimized masking in MAEs enhances the model’s ability to capture and reconstruct complex tissue structures, improving accuracy and efficiency for medical image analysis and potentially aiding computer-aided diagnosis.

title: InfoQuant: Shaping Activation Distributions for Low-Bit LLM Quantization
summary: Low-bit activation quantization remains a major bottleneck in efficient large language model (LLM) deployment. The difficulty is not only that activations contain outliers, but that their distributions are often poorly matched to a low-bit uniform quantizer. Existing post-training quantization (PTQ) methods suppress peaks, balance channels, or minimize reconstruction error, yet they rarely specify what activation distribution is actually easy to discretize. As a result, activations may appear numerically smoother while still incurring large quantization error because the quantization range remains wide or most values collapse into a few levels near the mean. We recast activation transformation as quantizer-facing distribution design and analyze quantization error from an information-theoretic perspective. Our analysis shows that quantization-friendly activations should jointly have a smaller numerical range and sufficient dispersion within that range. Guided by this analysis, we propose InfoQuant, a train-free method that employs Peak Suppression Orthogonal Transformation (PSOT) to shape activations into more quantization-friendly distributions. We further introduce adaptive outlier-token selection to improve the robustness of PSOT during optimization. Across multiple LLM families, InfoQuant consistently outperforms prior PTQ and end-to-end training baselines. Under W4A4KV4, it preserves 97% of floating-point accuracy on average and reduces the LLaMA-2 13B performance gap by 42% over the previous state of the art. Code is available at [https://github.com/LLIKKE/InfoQuant](https://github.com/LLIKKE/InfoQuant)

AI.tldr: InfoQuant is a train-free method that reshapes activation distributions to be quantization-friendly via Peak Suppression Orthogonal Transformation (PSOT) and adaptive outlier-token selection, achieving strong PTQ performance on LLMs and reducing the accuracy gap to FP32.
AI.motivation: Low-bit activation quantization in LLMs is hampered by outliers and activation distributions that do not align well with low-bit uniform quantizers. Existing PTQ methods suppress peaks or minimize reconstruction error but lack guidance on which activation distributions are easiest to discretize, leading to suboptimal quantization ranges and collapsed values.
AI.method: Recast activation transformation as quantizer-facing distribution design and analyze quantization error from an information-theoretic perspective. Propose InfoQuant, a train-free approach using Peak Suppression Orthogonal Transformation (PSOT) to sculpt activations into quantization-friendly distributions, with adaptive outlier-token selection to improve robustness.
AI.result: InfoQuant consistently outperforms prior PTQ and end-to-end training baselines across multiple LLM families. Under W4A4KV4, it preserves 97% of floating-point accuracy on average and reduces the LLaMA-2 13B performance gap by 42% relative to the previous state of the art.
AI.conclusion: Diseases aside, InfoQuant demonstrates that shaping activation distributions with PSOT enables robust, train-free low-bit quantization for LLMs, delivering substantial accuracy retention and tighter performance gaps compared to prior PTQ methods.

title: Managing Uncertainty in LLM-Generated Procedural Knowledge for Virtual Laboratory Planning
summary: Educational virtual laboratories can make experimental training more scala-ble, adaptive, and accessible, especially when students have limited access to physical laboratory facilities. However, authoring new simulated laboratory procedures remains costly: educators must describe new equipment, define how instruments and materials interact, and specify valid procedural flows that can be executed or assessed inside the virtual environment. Large lan-guage models can assist in this authoring process by generating detailed ex-perimental procedures, but their output should not be treated as directly exe-cutable plans. They may omit necessary actions, arrange steps in the wrong order, or produce instructions that are logically incorrect or incompatible with the laboratory equipment. This paper presents a prototype framework for managing uncertainty in LLM-generated procedural knowledge for virtu-al laboratory planning. The framework aims to reduce procedural uncertainty by using structured domain representations and uncertain LLM-generated state-transition samples to extract candidate procedural rules, transform them into explicit and inspectable constraints, and use them to repair uncertain procedural steps. Although the motivating domain refers to educational vir-tual laboratories, the underlying problem is more general: managing uncer-tain procedural knowledge for action planning in structured interactive envi-ronments. We illustrate the approach in a virtual laboratory domain involving laboratory instruments, containers, tools, and material-transfer actions.

AI.tldr: Prototype framework to manage uncertainty in LLM-generated procedural knowledge for virtual labs by extracting candidate rules from uncertain state-transition samples, converting them into constraints, and repairing steps within a structured domain.
AI.motivation: Educational virtual labs enable scalable, adaptive training but require costly authoring of procedures; LLMs can help generate procedures, yet outputs are not reliably executable and may be incomplete or incorrect, necessitating uncertainty management for planning.
AI.method: Leverage structured domain representations and uncertain LLM-derived state-transition samples to extract candidate procedural rules; transform them into explicit constraints; use these constraints to repair or constrain uncertain procedural steps; demonstrate in a virtual laboratory domain with instruments, containers, tools, and material-transfer actions.
AI.result: Prototype framework is presented and illustrated in a virtual laboratory domain; approach is generalizable to other structured interactive environments; the abstract does not report empirical evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Structured constraint-based repair of LLM-generated procedural knowledge can improve reliability of action planning in virtual labs and other structured interactive domains; the approach supports extracting, constraining, and refining procedural rules from probabilistic outputs.

title: Enabling Extensible Embodied Capabilities with Tools
summary: Most existing embodied intelligence methods formulate perception, reasoning, planning, and control within a unified parameterized policy. Yet these capabilities are inherently hierarchical and heterogeneous, making them difficult to reliably learn and modularize within a single model. We propose a capability externalization approach that decouples heterogeneous capabilities into independently optimized tools, dynamically invoked at inference time. To this end, we introduce Embodied Tool Protocol (ETP), a standardized protocol for embodied tool registration, discovery, invocation, and execution, and curate 100+ validated tools spanning perception, cognition, reasoning, and execution as the tool base. Building on this, we construct EmbodiedToolBench to evaluate both whether tool augmentation improves embodied performance and how well current models use tools across tool-necessity recognition, tool selection, tool execution, and tool-chain composition. Experiments across simulation and real-world platforms confirm that capability externalization consistently improves embodied performance (avg. gain 31% on EB-ALFRED and 36% on EB-Navigation), yet reveal a clear boundary: gains are substantial for cognition and perception but are limited for execution-type capabilities. Moreover, our analysis reveals that knowing when, which, and how to invoke tools remains a persistent challenge across all models, thereby highlighting embodied tool competence as a critical direction for future research.

AI.tldr: Externalizes capabilities as independently optimized tools via Embodied Tool Protocol (ETP); tool bank across perception, cognition, reasoning, and execution; Empirical gains shown for embodied tasks, but gains are heterogeneous and execution-type capabilities lag; tool-usage decision remains challenging.
AI.motivation: Embodied agents relying on a single monolithic policy struggle with hierarchical, heterogeneous capabilities. Capabilities (perception, cognition, planning, control) are more reliably learned and modularized when externalized as discrete tools.
AI.method: Introduce Embodied Tool Protocol (ETP) for tool registration, discovery, invocation, and execution. Curate 100+ validated tools spanning perception, cognition, reasoning, and execution. Build EmbodiedToolBench to assess (i) tool augmentation on embodied performance and (ii) tool-usage capability: tool-necessity recognition, tool selection, tool execution, and tool-chain composition. Evaluate on simulation and real-world platforms EB-ALFRED and EB-Navigation.
AI.result: Tool augmentation improves embodied performance (average gains: 31% on EB-ALFRED; 36% on EB-Navigation). Gains are substantial for cognition and perception, but limited for execution-type capabilities. There is a persistent challenge in determining when, which, and how to invoke tools across models.
AI.conclusion: Capability externalization via an embodied tool ecosystem is a promising research direction for modular, improved embodied AI. However, tool competence—particularly tool-usage reasoning and invocation policy—remains a critical bottleneck that future work should address.

title: Evi-Steer: Learning to Steer Biomedical Vision-Language Models through Efficient and Generalizable Evidential Tuning
comment: MICCAI 2026 Early Accept; Project Page: https://tahakoleilat.github.io/Evi-Steer
summary: Parameter-efficient adaptation of vision-language foundation models is crucial for precise multimodal understanding of biomedical images, yet existing methods remain deterministic and often struggle under domain shift or ambiguous image-text alignment. This limitation is particularly critical in the clinic, where models should remain robust in low-data regimes and domain shifts. We present Evi-Steer, an evidential cross-modal low-dimensional steering framework for BiomedCLIP that enables uncertainty-aware parameter-efficient fine-tuning while updating only 0.11% of total model parameters. Our approach performs lightweight low-dimensional token updates in both vision and text encoders while simultaneously estimating epistemic uncertainty. These uncertainty estimates update gate residuals, allowing the model to adapt conservatively when evidence is weak. Furthermore, we introduce cross-modal confidence fusion based on Dempster-Shafer theory, enabling visual adaptation to be conditioned on textual confidence and suppressing conflicting or uncertain cross-modal updates. We conduct a comprehensive evaluation on 15 biomedical imaging datasets spanning 8 organs and 8 imaging modalities under few-shot learning and domain generalization settings. Evi-Steer consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods under few-shot learning and domain shift settings, demonstrating a practical and robust pathway for deploying vision-language models in real-world clinical settings. Code is available at https://github.com/HealthX-Lab/Evi-Steer.

AI.tldr: Evi-Steer enables uncertainty-aware, parameter-efficient cross-modal fine-tuning for BiomedCLIP using low-dimensional updates and Dempster-Shafer fusion, showing strong few-shot and generalization performance across biomedical datasets.
AI.motivation: Robust, parameter-efficient adaptation of vision-language foundation models in biomedical imaging under domain shift and limited data; existing methods are often deterministic and brittle to misalignment.
AI.method: Evi-Steer employs lightweight low-dimensional token updates in both vision and text encoders, jointly estimates epistemic uncertainty, and uses gate residuals to adapt conservatively. Cross-modal confidence fusion via Dempster-Shafer theory conditions visual updates on textual confidence and suppresses uncertain or conflicting updates.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art methods under few-shot learning and domain generalization across 15 biomedical imaging datasets spanning 8 organs and 8 modalities; demonstrates robustness suitable for clinical deployment; code available at the provided URL.
AI.conclusion: Provides a practical, uncertainty-aware pathway for deploying vision-language models in biomedicine by combining parameter-efficient tuning with evidence-based cross-modal fusion and robust performance under data scarcity and domain shifts.

title: GAC: Noise-Aware Adaptive Mixing for Hybrid SFT-RL Post-Training
summary: Hybrid post-training usually combines supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning, but fixed mixing schedules cannot adapt when the relative noise of the two signals changes over time. We propose GAC, a noise-aware controller that derives an adaptive mixing weight from online estimates of gradient variance and disagreement between the two training signals. The method adds smoothing, prior guidance, and bounded updates while reusing existing training tensors. Experiments on math, code, science, and logic benchmarks show that GAC consistently improves hybrid post-training over strong fixed and rule-based baselines, with larger gains at larger model scales and less than 1% training overhead.

AI.tldr: GAC is a noise-aware adaptive mixer for hybrid post-training that adjusts the blend of supervised fine-tuning and RL using online gradient statistics; it yields consistent gains with minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: Hybrid post-training with fixed mixing cannot cope with time-varying noise between signals; adaptive balancing is needed to maximize efficiency and performance.
AI.method: GAC computes an adaptive mixing weight from online estimates of gradient variance and disagreement between the supervised and RL signals, adds smoothing, prior guidance, and bounded updates, and reuses existing training tensors.
AI.result: Across math, code, science, and logic benchmarks, GAC improves over strong fixed and rule-based baselines, with larger gains for larger models and training overhead under 1%.
AI.conclusion: GAC provides an effective, lightweight, noise-aware mechanism for adaptive mixing in hybrid post-training, scalable across domains and model sizes.

title: ScientistOne: Towards Human-Level Autonomous Research via Chain-of-Evidence
summary: Autonomous research agents produce competitive solutions and professional-looking manuscripts, yet their outputs contain verifiability failures undetectable by surface-level evaluation: fabricated citations, unreproducible scores, and method descriptions that diverge from the implementation. We address this through three contributions. First, Chain-of-Evidence (CoE), a verifiability framework requiring every claim to be traceable to its evidence source. Second, ScientistOne, an end-to-end autonomous research system that maintains evidence chains by construction throughout literature review, solution discovery, and paper writing. Third, CoE Audit, a post-hoc audit whose four integrity checks -- score verification, specification violation, reference verification, and method-code alignment -- apply uniformly to all systems. Across 75 papers spanning five systems and five frontier research tasks, every baseline exhibits at least one systematic failure mode: hallucinated reference rates reach 21%, score verification passes in as few as 42% of papers, and method-code alignment ranges from 20% to 80%. ScientistOne achieves zero hallucinated references (0/337), perfect score verification (12/12), and the highest method-code alignment (14/15), while matching or exceeding human expert performance on all five tasks. ScientistOne further generalizes to six additional tasks spanning medical imaging, fine-grained recognition, 3D perception, and language modeling, achieving state-of-the-art on Parameter Golf and gold medals on MLE-Bench tasks where baselines fail entirely.

AI.tldr: Autonomous research agents suffer verifiability failures in citations, scores, and methods; a three-pronged framework (CoE, ScientistOne, CoE Audit) enforces traceable evidence and robust evaluation, achieving strong results across many tasks and domains.
AI.motivation: To address verifiability gaps in autonomous scientific writing and solution discovery, where fabricated references and misaligned methods undermine trust.
AI.method: Introduce Chain-of-Evidence (CoE) framework; develop ScientistOne system maintaining evidence chains across literature review, solution discovery, and paper writing; implement CoE Audit with four checks (score verification, specification violation, reference verification, method-code alignment) and apply to all systems.
AI.result: Across 75 papers in five systems and five tasks, baselines exhibit at least one failure; hallucinated references up to 21%; score verification success as low as 42%. ScientistOne achieves 0/337 hallucinated references, 12/12 score verifications, high method-code alignment (14/15), and matches/exceeds human performance on five tasks; generalizes to six additional tasks including medical imaging, fine-grained recognition, 3D perception, and language modeling, with state-of-the-art on Parameter Golf and MLE-Bench medals where baselines fail.
AI.conclusion: A verifiability-centric design (CoE) and its automated audit (CoE Audit) enable reliable, reproducible autonomous research outputs and broad generalization across domains, addressing fundamental trust issues in AI-assisted scholarship.

title: HyperSim: A Holistic Sim-To-Real Framework For Robust Robotic Manipulation
summary: Scaling data volume and diversity is critical for generalizing embodied intelligence. While synthetic data generation offers a scalable alternative to expensive physical data acquisition, transferring robotic manipulation policies from simulation to the real world (sim-to-real) remains a formidable challenge due to the domain gap. This paper presents HyperSim, a holistic framework spanning from synthetic data generation to policy training and seamless real-world deployment. To systematically bridge the sim-to-real gap, HyperSim is realized through three core pillars: high-fidelity environment synthesis, adversarial trajectory generation, and sim-and-real co-training. Collectively, these modules address domain discrepancies by enhancing visual fidelity, expanding data coverage, and enforcing domain-invariant representations. We rigorously validate HyperSim through a large-scale empirical study involving 400 real-world task executions across two representative manipulation models. Assessed across three fine-grained metrics, our complete pipeline achieves remarkable sim-to-real success rates of 80% and 95% with ACT and π_{0}, respectively. Furthermore, policies trained on our adversarial trajectories exhibit significantly enhanced robustness against dynamic uncertainties, achieving a 35% higher completion rate under physical perturbations.

AI.tldr: HyperSim delivers a holistic sim-to-real pipeline for robotic manipulation using high-fidelity environment synthesis, adversarial trajectory generation, and sim-and-real co-training, achieving strong transfer and robustness.
AI.motivation: Bridge the sim-to-real gap and scale data diversity for embodied manipulation, enabling policies trained in synthetic data to generalize in the real world.
AI.method: Three pillars: (1) high-fidelity environment synthesis to improve visual fidelity and realism; (2) adversarial trajectory generation to broaden coverage and challenge policies; (3) sim-and-real co-training to learn domain-invariant representations and align sim and real policies; validated on 400 real-world executions across two manipulation models; metric set includes three fine-grained measures.
AI.result: Achieves sim-to-real success rates of 80% (ACT) and 95% (π0); 400 real-world tasks; 35% higher completion under dynamic perturbations when using adversarial trajectories.
AI.conclusion: A holistic pipeline addressing fidelity, coverage, and invariance yields robust sim-to-real transfer; potential for broader application, with future work needed for ablations, generalization to more tasks/models, and analysis of each pillar's contribution.

title: CNNs, Transformers, Hybrid, and Vision Language Models for Skin Cancer Detection
comment: 13 pages, 3 figures, accepted at ICPRAI 2026, The Fifth International Conference on Pattern Recognition and Artificial Intelligence. To appear in Lecture Notes in Computer Science
summary: Skin cancer is a common and fast rising malignancy worldwide. Early detection is critical for improving outcomes. Deep learning models trained on dermoscopic and clinical images can support automated and fast triage. However, many studies evaluate only a limited set of architectures. Experimental setups also vary across studies. In this paper, we present a unified evaluation of twelve deep learning models for binary skin cancer detection on the PAD-UFES-20 dataset. The models span four families: convolutional neural networks (CNN), vision transformers (ViT), hybrid convolution transformer backbones, and vision language models (VLM). Performance is assessed using AUC, the maximum F1 score with its precision and recall, and sensitivity at 80% specificity, reflecting screening oriented requirements. Our results show that well tuned CNNs already provide strong baselines, but transformer based families consistently improve discrimination. Hybrid models (MaxViT Tiny, CoAtNet0) and a SigLIP based VLM achieve the best overall trade off between ranking performance and clinically relevant operating points, while CLIP based model offers high precision. The full codebase for all experiments is publicly released. Together, these findings offer practical guidance on which model families are most suitable for real world deployment in skin cancer screening and establish a reproducible reference point for future work on PAD-UFES-20.

AI.tldr: Unified evaluation of 12 DL models on PAD-UFES-20 for binary skin cancer detection shows CNNs strong baselines; transformer-based models improve discrimination; hybrid models (MaxViT Tiny, CoAtNet0) and a SigLIP-based VLM offer the best overall trade-offs; CLIP-based model provides high precision; codebase released; establishes a reproducible reference for PAD-UFES-20.
AI.motivation: To provide a fair, cross-family comparison on a standardized dataset to guide deployment in skin cancer screening and to establish a reproducible reference for future work.
AI.method: Evaluate twelve models across four families (CNN, ViT, hybrid convolution-transformer backbones, and vision-language models) on the PAD-UFES-20 dataset. Performance is reported using AUC, the maximum F1 score with its precision and recall, and sensitivity at 80% specificity to align with screening contexts. The full codebase is publicly released.
AI.result: CNNs offer strong baselines; transformer-based families consistently improve discrimination; hybrid models (MaxViT Tiny, CoAtNet0) and SigLIP-based VLM achieve the best overall trade-off between ranking performance and clinically relevant operating points; CLIP-based model provides high precision; code released publicly.
AI.conclusion: The findings deliver practical guidance on selecting model families for real-world skin cancer screening and establish a reproducible reference point for future work on PAD-UFES-20.

title: Max-Window Scale Estimation for Near-Lossless HiF8 W8A8 Quantization-Aware Training
summary: Quantization-aware training (QAT) with low-bit floating-point formats enables efficient LLM deployment, yet introduces subtle failure modes invisible to standard training metrics. We present a systematic study of HiF8 W8A8 QAT for OpenPangu-Embedded-1B through the lens of Delayed Tensor Scaling (DTS). Across eight controlled experiments, we identify and disentangle two orthogonal failure modes: (i)amax saturation, where delayed scale estimates silently corrupt knowledge-sensitive representations via forward-pass clipping, and (ii)catastrophic forgetting, where an aggressive learning rate overwrites pretrained commonsense knowledge independently of quantization. Neither is detectable from training loss alone. We address amax saturation with a conservative max-algorithm DTS strategy over a 64-step history window, and mitigate forgetting via a 500-step BF16 warmup followed by QAT at lr=10^{-5}. Both fixes are necessary and sufficient: our final configuration achieves 0.43% MMLU drop, 0.58% HellaSwag drop, and 0.22% ARC-Challenge drop versus a matched BF16 baseline, with a training loss APE of only 0.11% over 10,000 steps.

AI.tldr: HiFi8 W8A8 QAT for OpenPangu-Embedded-1B analyzed under DTS; identifies two failure modes (amax saturation and catastrophic forgetting); proposes DTS max-algorithm and BF16 warmup; achieves small accuracy drops vs BF16 baseline and low training loss APE.
AI.motivation: Quantization-aware training with low-bit float formats introduces subtle failure modes not captured by training loss; need robust evaluation and fixes for reliable LLM deployment.
AI.method: Eight controlled experiments analyzing two failure modes; adopt conservative max-algorithm DTS with 64-step history; BF16 warmup 500 steps, then QAT with lr=1e-5; evaluate on MMLU, HellaSwag, ARC-Challenge; compare to BF16 baseline; measure training loss APE.
AI.result: Two orthogonal failure modes identified: amax saturation and catastrophic forgetting; fixes necessary and sufficient: 0.43% MMLU drop, 0.58% HellaSwag drop, 0.22% ARC-Challenge drop relative to BF16 baseline; training loss APE 0.11% over 10k steps.
AI.conclusion: Careful DTS strategy and warmup scheme enable robust QAT for low-bit formats; potential generalization; interplay between quantization and knowledge retention matters; standard training metrics insufficient.

title: Automatic Layer Selection for Hallucination Detection
summary: Recent studies on hallucination detection have shown that hallucination-related signals are more strongly encoded in intermediate layers than in the final layer of large language models (LLMs). Although a growing body of work has sought to exploit this property for hallucination detection, how to automate the selection of high-performing layers remains underexplored, and principled methods for this purpose are still lacking. To address this gap, we first propose several hypotheses for why such signals emerge in intermediate layers and evaluate corresponding criteria for automatic layer selection across diverse LLM architectures, scales, and tasks, covering both question answering and summarization hallucination detection benchmarks. However, we find that none of these criteria consistently delivers satisfactory performance. We therefore propose a new selection criterion, First Effective Peak of Intrinsic Dimension (FEPoID), which consistently identify optimal or near-optimal layers and outperforms both the aforementioned criteria and existing hallucination detection baselines. FEPoID is training-free and incurs negligible computational overhead. In addition, we study the generation behaviors of LLMs and introduce a simple yet effective truncation strategy, which further amplifies hallucination-related signals and substantially improves overall detection performance. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/DesoloYw/Automatic-Layer-Selection-for-Hallucination-Detection.git

AI.tldr: A novel, training-free criterion (FEPoID) automatically selects optimal intermediate layers for hallucination detection in LLMs, outperforming prior criteria and baselines; a simple truncation strategy further enhances detection. Code released.
AI.motivation: Hallucination signals are predominantly encoded in intermediate layers of LLMs, but there is no robust, principled method to automatically select high-performing layers across architectures, scales, and tasks (QA and summarization).
AI.method: The study formulates hypotheses about why intermediate layers harbor strong hallucination signals and empirically evaluates several layer-selection criteria across diverse LLMs and tasks. When existing criteria fail to generalize, it introduces First Effective Peak of Intrinsic Dimension (FEPoID), a training-free metric for identifying optimal layers. Additionally, it analyzes generation behavior and proposes a truncation strategy to magnify hallucination signals and boost detection performance.
AI.result: FEPoID consistently identifies optimal or near-optimal layers and outperforms previous criteria and baselines. The truncation strategy substantially improves detection performance. The approach is training-free and incurs negligible overhead; code is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: FEPoID provides a robust, efficient method for automatic layer selection in hallucination detection across LLMs, with broad applicability to QA and summarization tasks, and is complemented by a simple truncation technique to further boost performance; code is released.

title: L-Learning : A Lyapunov-Based Approach Leveraging Lagrangian Mechanics for Efficient and Stable Robot Tracking
summary: This paper presents L-Learning, a novel data-driven control framework for robotics that integrates Lyapunov stability theory with Lagrangian mechanics to enhance trajectory tracking performance. While traditional control methods often suffer from performance degradation in dynamic and uncertain environments, data-driven approaches, while more adaptable, are frequently limited by high sample complexity and a lack of rigorous stability guarantees. L-Learning mitigates these challenges by explicitly learning the system's energy function from data, thereby optimizing performance while ensuring closed-loop stability intrinsically. Characterized by superior control accuracy, theoretical stability guarantees, and high sample efficiency, L-Learning represents a promising solution for practical robotic applications.

AI.tldr: L-Learning is a data-driven control framework for robotics that combines Lyapunov stability theory with Lagrangian mechanics to learn an energy function from data, aiming for stable trajectory tracking, improved performance, and high sample efficiency.
AI.motivation: Traditional model-based controllers degrade in dynamic/uncertain environments, while generic data-driven methods excel in adaptability but often lack rigorous stability guarantees and sample efficiency. An energy-based learning approach could provide stability guarantees while leveraging data.
AI.method: The framework explicitly learns the system's energy function (likely a Lagrangian/Hamiltonian form) from data and enforces closed-loop stability via Lyapunov stability constraints, integrating energy-based modeling with data-driven learning.
AI.result: Claims superior tracking accuracy, theoretical stability guarantees, and high sample efficiency in robotic tasks, suggesting practical viability.
AI.conclusion: L-Learning presents a promising direction by marrying energy-based dynamics with data-driven learning to maintain stability while improving performance in robotics.

title: Sleep-stage efficient classification using a lightweight self-supervised model
summary: Accurate classification of sleep stages is crucial for diagnosing sleep disorders and automating this process can significantly enhance clinical assessments. This study aims to explore the use of a self-supervised model (more specifically, an adapted version of mulEEG) combined with a Linear SVM classifier to improve sleep stage classification. \textbf{Methods:} The mulEEG model, which learns electroencephalogram signal representations in a self-supervised manner, was simplified here by replacing ResNet-50 with 1D-convolutions used as time series encoder by a ResNet-18 backbone. Two other adaptations were conducted: the first one evaluated different configurations of the model and data volume for training, while the second tested the effectiveness of time series features, spectrogram features, and their concatenation as inputs to a Linear SVM classifier. \textbf{Results:} The results showed that reducing the volume of data offered a better cost-benefit ratio compared to simplifying the model. Using the concatenated features with ResNet-18 also outperformed the linear evaluations of the original mulEEG model, achieving higher classification performance. \textbf{Conclusions:} Simplifying the mulEEG model to extract features and pairing it with a robust classifier leads to more efficient and accurate sleep stage classification. This approach holds promise for improving clinical sleep assessments and can be extended to other biological signal classification tasks.

AI.tldr: Self-supervised mulEEG with a 1D-Conv/ResNet-18 encoder, paired with a Linear SVM using concatenated time-series and spectrogram features, yields efficient and accurate sleep stage classification; data-volume reduction outperforms further model simplification.
AI.motivation: Improve sleep stage classification by learning robust EEG representations via self-supervision while reducing data and model complexity; assess whether a simpler encoder and a linear classifier can match or surpass the original mulEEG setup.
AI.method: Adapted self-supervised mulEEG using a 1D-convolution time-series encoder with a ResNet-18 backbone. Investigated: (i) various training data volumes/configurations; (ii) inputs to a Linear SVM comprising time-series features, spectrogram features, and their concatenation.
AI.result: Reducing data volume offered a better cost-benefit than reducing model complexity. Concatenated time-series and spectrogram features with ResNet-18 achieved higher sleep-stage classification performance than linear evaluations of the original mulEEG.
AI.conclusion: A simplified feature extractor + robust linear classifier can deliver efficient and accurate sleep-stage classification, with potential applicability to other biological signal tasks.

title: HRVConformer: Neonatal Hypoxic-Ischemic Encephalopathy Classification from the Heart Rate signals
comment: Paper submitted to Journal of Engineering Applications of Artifical Intelligence
summary: This paper presents the HRVConformer, a novel deep learning architecture for the classification of hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy (HIE) using the instantaneous heart rate (HR) signal. Unlike conventional approaches that rely on handcrafted features, HRVConformer directly processes raw HR signals in an end-to-end manner, capturing both local and long-range dependencies through a hybrid Convolution-Transformer framework. By integrating convolutional layers for local feature extraction and Transformer-based attention mechanisms for global context modelling, the architecture effectively enhances signal representation and classification performance. The model was trained using supervised learning on a large HR dataset consisting of 1,573 one-hour epochs, including 259 one-hour expert-annotated epochs and a substantial set of weakly labelled data. A 314-hour validation set provided a robust performance estimation, while an independent 215-hour dataset with expert annotations was reserved for final testing. HR signals were extracted from electrocardiogram (ECG) recordings using an improved Pan-Tompkins algorithm, which significantly enhanced both signal quality and data availability. Experimental results demonstrate that the HRVConformer achieves an AUC of 83.23\% and accuracy of 74.56\% on the test set. These results surpass the performance of the Transformer, ResNet50 and fully convolutional networks baselines, highlighting the advantages of integrating convolutional and Transformer-based components for HR-based HIE classification. The proposed method provides a promising step toward a more accurate and automated assessment of HIE using HR signals. The code is available at: https://github.com/syu-kylin/HRVConformer.

AI.tldr: End-to-end HR signal classifier for hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy using a hybrid CNN-Transformer (HRVConformer).
AI.motivation: Relying on handcrafted HR features may miss informative patterns; end-to-end processing of raw HR signals with local and global dependency modeling can improve HIE classification; leverage large weak-label data and expert-annotated data with robust validation/test sets.
AI.method: Hybrid Conformer architecture that fuses convolutional layers for local feature extraction with Transformer-based attention for global context, applied to raw instantaneous HR signals derived from ECG; training on a dataset of 1,573 one-hour epochs (259 expert-annotated), with 314-hour validation and 215-hour expert-annotated test set; uses an improved Pan-Tompkins HR extraction; evaluation against Transformer, ResNet50, and FCN baselines; code released.
AI.result: Test AUC 83.23% and accuracy 74.56%, outperforming Transformer, ResNet50, and FCN baselines.
AI.conclusion: HRVConformer demonstrates that combining local-feature extraction with global-context modeling on raw HR signals yields superior performance for HIE classification and holds promise for automated, data-efficient HR-based clinical assessment; code is publicly available.

title: Exploiting Local Dynamics Regularity for Reusable Skills in Offline Hierarchical RL
summary: Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning (HRL) promises to solve long-horizon Reinforcement Learning (RL) tasks more efficiently than non-hierarchical counterparts by discovering and reusing temporally-extended skills. However, obtaining skills that are actually reusable remains an open challenge. Towards this end, we focus on abstractions that exploit the intuition of local dynamics: local transitions in different global contexts require similar kinds of action sequences. By aligning these contexts with the action sequences they require, we are able to learn which skills to reuse and where to reuse them. In principle, this information should benefit many HRL algorithms, where high-level policies have to reason about the low-level skills they use. The resulting algorithm CARL (Contrastive Action-based Representations for Reusable Local Control) shows both qualitative clustering of meaningful skills in complex humanoid environments and improved downstream performance on the OGBench benchmark when integrated with HIQL.

AI.tldr: CARL introduces a contrastive, action-based representation learning framework to identify reusable local skills by aligning local dynamics across global contexts, improving HRL reuse and downstream performance when combined with HIQL on humanoid tasks and OGBench.
AI.motivation: Reusable skills in hierarchical RL remain challenging. The authors hypothesize that local transitions in different global contexts share similar action sequences, so aligning contexts with required action sequences can reveal reusable skills and where to apply them.
AI.method: Propose CARL (Contrastive Action-based Representations for Reusable Local Control). Learn action-based representations via contrastive learning to cluster and align local dynamic contexts by matching state-action sequences to their effect across contexts. Use this alignment to guide skill reuse in HRL. Demonstrate qualitative clustering of skills in humanoid tasks and evaluate downstream performance on the OGBench benchmark when CARL is integrated with HIQL.
AI.result: CARL yields interpretable clusters of meaningful local skills in complex humanoid environments and improves performance on OGBench when integrated with HIQL, indicating effective discovery and reuse of local skills within HRL.
AI.conclusion: Aligning local dynamics with corresponding action sequences enables more effective discovery and reuse of skills in HRL. CARL provides a general mechanism that can benefit HRL algorithms by clarifying where and which skills to reuse, with demonstrated gains on standard benchmarks when paired with HIQL.

title: On the Generalization Capabilities, Design Choices and Limitations of Keypoint Imitation Learning
comment: This version was submitted to IROS 2026
