AI.motivation: Evaluate whether mature object pose/shape estimation, when used with antipodal grasp sampling, can outperform end-to-end grasp synthesis, under realistic constraints (7-DoF grasps, single-view RGB(-D)), and assess limits in clutter and runtime.
AI.method: Empirical comparison: 1 end-to-end grasp synthesis method vs 3 modular methods that first estimate pose/shape for all scene objects and then synthesize antipodal grasps; two pose/shape backbones (encoder-decoder and diffusion); inputs include single-view RGB(-D); evaluation includes failure modes and runtimes; exploration of language-conditioned grasps via vision-language models with RGB-D input.
AI.result: Modular methods consistently outperform the end-to-end method across experiments; capable of generating plentiful grasps even for small objects where end-to-end fails; effectiveness depends on pose/shape estimation accuracy; partial degradation in clutter; run-time analysis provided; demonstrated language-conditioned grasps with vision-language models achieving comparable performance to LERF-TOGO baseline.
AI.conclusion: Modular pose-and-shape-guided grasping with antipodal sampling is more mature and robust than end-to-end approaches under the tested settings; future work includes improving pose/shape estimation in clutter and integrating vision-language conditioning to enhance grasp selection.

title: VisualNeedle: Benchmarking Active Visual Search in Information-Dense Scenes
summary: Frontier multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have been reported to achieve over 90% accuracy on fine-grained perception benchmarks. However, such scores do not necessarily imply faithful use of visual evidence. Prior studies have identified three shortcuts that inflate benchmark performance. First, linguistic priors and lexical cues in questions often enable models to infer plausible answers without seeing the image. Second, coarse global semantics from the visual encoder can bypass fine-grained local details. Third, in some ``think-with-images'' benchmarks, corrupting the intermediate images returned by visual tools barely affects the final answer. These findings suggest that higher input resolution or larger question pools alone do not elicit genuine active visual search. To address this, we introduce VisualNeedle, a challenging, information-dense, and fine-grained benchmark for scenes where critical evidence is spatially constrained to minute regions and not discernible at a glance. We further propose a counterfactual crop-black setting, which replaces crops returned by tools with black images of the same size, to test whether tool-enabled performance truly relies on intermediate visual evidence. We evaluate 9 promninent MLLMs across three settings: no-tool, standard tool-enabled, and crop-black. No-tool accuracy stays below 20\%, and the best tool-enabled model reaches only 56.01\%, still trailing the 63.00% human majority-vote accuracy. These results reveal persistent limitations in fine-grained visual search, while the crop-black ablation confirms that success on VisualNeedle hinges on genuine intermediate visual evidence.

AI.tldr: VisualNeedle reveals persistent gaps in fine-grained visual search for frontier multimodal LLMs; tool-enabled performance still lags behind human baselines, and crop-black ablations show reliance on intermediate visual evidence.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between high benchmark scores and faithful visual reasoning by creating a dense, spatially constrained dataset and a crop-black diagnostic to test reliance on visual evidence.
AI.method: Introduce VisualNeedle benchmark; propose crop-black setting; evaluate 9 prominent MLLMs across three conditions (no-tool, standard tool-enabled, crop-black) on fine-grained visual tasks.
AI.result: No-tool accuracy <20%; best tool-enabled accuracy 56.01%; human majority-vote accuracy 63.00%; crop-black ablation confirms performance depends on genuine intermediate visual evidence.
AI.conclusion: Current MLLMs still struggle with fine-grained visual search; tool-enabled gains exist but do not reach human performance, and crop-black diagnostics reveal reliance on actual visual evidence rather than priors or coarse semantics.

title: Unified Neural Scaling Laws
summary: We present a functional form (that we refer to as a Unified Neural Scaling Law (UNSL)) that accurately models and extrapolates the scaling behaviors of deep neural networks as multiple dimensions all vary simultaneously (i.e. how the evaluation metric of interest varies as one simultaneously varies the number of model parameters, training dataset size, number of training steps, number of inference steps, amount of compute, and various hyperparameters) for various architectures and for each of various tasks within a varied set of upstream and downstream tasks. This set includes large-scale vision, language, math, and reinforcement learning. When compared to other functional forms for neural scaling, this functional form yields extrapolations of scaling behavior that are considerably more accurate on this set.

AI.tldr: A Unified Neural Scaling Law (UNSL) models multi-dimensional scaling of neural networks across parameters, data, steps, compute, and hyperparameters, across architectures and tasks (vision, language, math, RL), yielding more accurate extrapolations than prior scaling forms.
AI.motivation: Existing neural scaling laws typically address scaling along a single or limited number of dimensions. A unified, multi-dimensional scaling law is needed to accurately predict performance when multiple factors vary simultaneously across diverse tasks and architectures.
AI.method: Propose a functional form named UNSL that generalizes scaling across multiple dimensions. Fit and validate it on a broad set of architectures and tasks (vision, language, math, reinforcement learning), and compare its extrapolations to those of other neural scaling laws.
AI.result: UNSL provides more accurate extrapolations of scaling behavior than prior forms across the studied set, enabling better predictions when several factors (parameters, data, steps, compute, hyperparameters) vary concurrently.
AI.conclusion: A unified, multi-dimensional scaling law improves predictive accuracy of neural scaling across diverse domains and settings, supporting more reliable cross-domain extrapolation and informing decisions on model/data/hyperparameter configurations.

title: MedGuideX: Internalizing Decision Logic from Executable Guidelines into Large Language Models for Clinical Reasoning
summary: Clinical practice guidelines (CPGs) encode evidence-based decision logic that clinicians apply by evaluating patient variables, conditional criteria, and recommendation rules. However, existing methods often use CPGs as free-text training data or retrieval sources, underutilizing their procedural decision structure. To better exploit this structure, we introduce a guideline-derived training pipeline that transforms CPG recommendations into executable clinical decision logic and uses it to generate factual and counterfactual question-answering data. Theses data teach models both guideline-supported decisions and how decisions change under different patient conditions. Post-training a medical LLM on the generated data yields MedGuideX. Across four clinical reasoning benchmarks, MedGuideX achieves a 10.28% relative improvement in average accuracy. Physician evaluation further shows that MedGuideX better recovers clinician authored reasoning steps and produces physician-preferred rationales in faithfulness, validity, completeness, and clarity. Overall, our results show that executable decision logic from CPGs can be transformed into scalable supervision for building reliable medical LLMs.

AI.tldr: Guideline-to-decision-logic pipeline converts CPG recommendations into executable logic to generate factual/counterfactual QA data for LLM training; MedGuideX shows improved clinical reasoning accuracy and physician-aligned rationales.
AI.motivation: CPGs contain procedural decision structures that are underutilized when treated as free text, limiting their value for training reliable medical LLMs.
AI.method: Translate CPG recommendations into executable decision logic, generate factual and counterfactual QA pairs, train a medical LLM on this data, and evaluate on four clinical-reasoning benchmarks with physician assessments.
AI.result: MedGuideX achieves a 10.28% relative improvement in average accuracy across benchmarks; physicians prefer its rationales and find them faithful, valid, complete, and clear.
AI.conclusion: Executable decision logic derived from CPGs can provide scalable, faithful supervision for building reliable medical LLMs.

title: Trust, Geometry, and Rules: A Credibility-Aware Reinforcement Learning Framework for Safe USV Navigation under Uncertainty
summary: Autonomous navigation of Unmanned Surface Vehicles (USVs) that is safe and compliant with the International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea (COLREGs) remains a formidable challenge in dynamic maritime environments, particularly when perception systems exhibit miscalibrated uncertainty. Existing Reinforcement Learning (RL)-based methods often falter because state-estimation errors induce unreliable belief states that mislead the value function, while discrete traffic rules introduce discontinuity in the learning objective. To address these challenges, we propose a framework integrating credibility-aware learning, geometric safety shielding, and continuous rule-aware embedding. First, Credibility-Weighted Value Learning (CW-VL) introduces a dynamic trust factor derived from the discrepancy between filter-estimated covariance and empirical error statistics to modulate the critic's heteroscedastic loss, preventing policy overfitting to noisy samples. Second, the Covariance-Inflated Velocity Obstacle (CI-VO) maps position-estimation uncertainty into set-wise angular margins, forming a conservative geometric shield that overrides hazardous exploratory actions. Third, Risk-Aware COLREGs Duty Embedding relaxes binary encounter duties into continuous rule-aware signals, providing smooth sector-transition information and suppressing oscillation from sparse rule rewards. Simulated encounter studies demonstrate improved training robustness against perceptual inconsistency and superior collision avoidance and COLREGs compliance over baselines.

AI.tldr: A credibility-aware reinforcement learning framework for autonomous USV navigation under COLREGs, introducing three components—CW-VL, CI-VO, and Risk-Aware COLREGs Duty Embedding—that improve robustness to perceptual uncertainty and COLREGs compliance in simulation.
AI.motivation: Perception-driven state estimation errors destabilize RL policies; discrete COLREGs rules create learning discontinuities and sparse rewards; a unified framework that (i) models credibility, (ii) provides uncertainty-aware safety shielding, and (iii) offers continuous rule signaling is needed for safe, compliant USV navigation.
AI.method: 1) CW-VL: derives a dynamic trust factor from the discrepancy between filter covariance and empirical error to modulate the critic’s heteroscedastic loss, mitigating overfitting to noisy samples. 2) CI-VO: translates position-estimation uncertainty into set-wise angular margins (inflated velocity obstacles) to form a conservative geometric shield that can override hazardous actions. 3) Risk-Aware COLREGs Duty Embedding: replaces binary encounter duties with continuous signals to provide smooth sector-transition information and reduce oscillations from sparse rewards.
AI.result: Simulated encounter studies show improved training robustness against perceptual inconsistency and superior collision avoidance and COLREGs compliance compared with baselines.
AI.conclusion: Integrating credibility-aware learning, geometric safety shielding, and continuous rule-aware embedding yields safer, more robust autonomous USV navigation under perceptual uncertainty, with simulation evidence of improved performance over traditional RL baselines.

title: Multi-Modal Building Inspection via Perceiver IO Fusion of Satellite and Street-Level Imagery
summary: We present a multi-modal classification framework that fuses satellite and street-level imagery through a Perceiver IO architecture operating on spatial patch tokens from a shared DINOv2 backbone. The design naturally handles a variable number of street-level views per building without padding or fixed-size pooling, and jointly predicts multi-label roof element and roof material classes. We construct a large-scale dataset of 32,135 buildings (61,672 segments) spanning ten countries, pairing satellite images with up to eight street-level views per segment and evaluating four masking strategies for isolating the target building. We propose an RGB-M masking strategy that appends the building footprint mask as a fourth input channel, providing a soft spatial prior that outperforms hard cropping across both modalities. The Perceiver IO fusion model improves over all other fusion strategies and yields substantial per-class gains for attributes visible from street level (e.g., +11.3 AP for slate, +1.3 AP for dormers), though the satellite-only baseline retains a slight advantage in macro-averaged mAP for classes that are predominantly visible from above. These results establish a scalable, flexible architecture for multi-modal building inspection that can accommodate heterogeneous inputs and multiple output tasks.

AI.tldr: A scalable multi-modal fusion framework for building analysis that combines satellite and up to eight street-level views using a Perceiver IO on a shared DINOv2 backbone, with an RGB-M masking strategy, evaluated on a large, diverse dataset; shows improved attribute detection from street view but satellite view remains strong for above-ground features.
AI.motivation: Enable robust, flexible multi-modal building inspection by fusing heterogeneous satellite and street-level imagery without fixed-size padding, supporting variable input counts and multi-label outputs.
AI.method: Fuse patch tokens from DINOv2 features via Perceiver IO; compare multiple masking strategies (including RGB-M) for targeting the building; construct a large dataset with satellite plus up to eight street views across 10 countries; train multi-label roof element and roof material classifiers; evaluate per-class gains.
AI.result: Perceiver IO fusion outperforms other fusion schemes; notable gains for street-visible attributes (e.g., slate +11.3 AP, dormers +1.3 AP); satellite-only baseline still leads in macro mAP on features mainly seen from above; RGB-M masking provides soft spatial priors and surpasses hard cropping; dataset size and diversity demonstrated.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a scalable, flexible multi-modal building inspection architecture capable of handling heterogeneous inputs and multiple output tasks, with strong per-class improvements for street-visible attributes and robust performance across modalities.

title: Quantized Keys Steal Attention: Bias Correction for KV-Cache Compression in Video Diffusion
comment: Variants of this manuscript were accepted to the ICML 2026 workshops SCALE and F2S
summary: Chunk-wise autoregressive video diffusion models rely on a KV cache of previously generated chunks to avoid redundant computation, but this cache quickly becomes a memory bottleneck as videos grow longer. Methods that quantize the KV cache to low bitwidths reduce memory pressure but degrade video quality. We show that a key driver of this degradation is a systematic bias in attention weights: due to the convexity of the exponential in softmax attention, quantization noise inflates the contribution of cached keys, a phenomenon we call the Jensen bias. This effect causes quantized keys to steal attention mass from the unquantized current chunk. We derive a per-attention-score correction that removes this bias in expectation, computed on the fly from the quantization step sizes of the cached keys and the query norm. Using a second-order Taylor approximation, the additional computational overhead is negligible, and no additional memory is needed alongside the cache. Evaluated on MAGI-1, SkyReels-V2, and HY-WorldPlay at INT2 quantization, our correction recovers most of the quality lost to aggressive quantization, reaching near-BF16 video quality, and can outperform INT4 quantization while using 50% less memory.

AI.tldr: Per-attention-score correction removes Jensen bias in quantized KV caches for chunk-wise autoregressive video diffusion, enabling INT2 quantization with near-BF16 quality and 50% memory savings.
AI.motivation: KV caches in long-video diffusion cause memory bottlenecks when using chunk-wise generation. Quantizing the KV cache reduces memory but introduces bias in attention weights (Jensen bias) that degrades quality.
AI.method: Identify Jensen bias from the convex exponential in softmax, derive a per-attention-score correction computed on-the-fly from quantization step sizes and the query norm. Use a second-order Taylor expansion to keep overhead negligible, requiring no extra memory beyond the cache. Apply correction during attention computation for cached keys.
AI.result: On MAGI-1, SkyReels-V2, and HY-WorldPlay with INT2 quantization, the correction recovers most quality lost to aggressive quantization, achieving near-BF16 video quality and outperforming INT4 while using ~50% less memory.
AI.conclusion: The bias-correction approach enables memory-efficient, high-quality chunk-wise video generation with quantized KV caches, balancing memory savings and perceptual quality for long videos.

title: AGORA: Adapter-Grounded Observation-Action Retention for Inference-Free Prompt Compression in LLM Agents
summary: The token-level extractive compressors widely used for general LM context are structurally inappropriate for LLM agents: across 17 (env, backbone, method) cells spanning two independent token-level method families, every cell collapses to mean reward <= 0.05 despite 1.3-13.3x realized compression. We name and characterize this failure mode as action-grammar destruction -- the tokens carrying action semantics (identifiers, brackets, action verbs) are exactly those self-information ranks lowest, so a general-purpose compressor reliably removes them and the environment rejects the residual. The diagnosis points to step-granularity compression. We introduce AGORA, an inference-free step-level compressor combining a structural prompt parser, an always-keep floor for format- and recency-critical content, and a 125M-parameter relevance scorer trained on counterfactual next-action-change labels (~2ms/step, zero per-step LLM toll). Across the compared inference-free and LLM-based methods, AGORA is the only one retaining >= 75% uncompressed performance in 8 of 9 cells (with the lone exception at 73%); a four-way component ablation isolates the structural floor as the dominant quality lever and the learned scorer as the source of 1.0-11.5x adaptive end-to-end compression from a single fixed keep ratio.

AI.tldr: AGORA: a step-level, inference-free compressor that preserves action semantics in LLM agent workflows, outperforming token-level methods across 17 env/backbone/method cells, achieving 75%+ uncompressed performance in 8/9 cells and up to 11.5x compression.
AI.motivation: Token-level extractive compressors destroy action-bearing tokens (identifiers, brackets, action verbs) in LLM agent contexts due to step-granularity, causing action semantics to be lost and environments to reject residual prompts; need a structure-aware, low-latency compression approach.
AI.method: AGORA combines a structural prompt parser to identify critical content, an always-keep floor for format- and recency-critical content, and a 125M-parameter relevance scorer trained on counterfactual next-action-change labels; operates inference-free (~2 ms/step, zero per-step LLM toll) and applies across 17 env/backbone/method cells; includes four-way ablation to assess component contributions.
AI.result: AGORA is the sole method among compared inference-free and LLM-based baselines to retain >=75% uncompressed performance in 8 of 9 cells (73% in the remaining). It achieves 1.0–11.5× adaptive end-to-end compression with a fixed keep ratio, with the structural floor identified as the dominant quality lever and the learned scorer as the source of adaptive compression.
AI.conclusion: Step-level, structure-aware compression successfully mitigates action-grammar destruction that plagues token-level methods. AGORA provides robust, low-cost compression for LLM agent pipelines and demonstrates the efficacy of combining a structural parser, a hard content floor, and a learned relevance scorer.

title: Towards Shared Embodied Intelligence in Humanoid Robots through Optimization Development and Testing of the Human Aware ergoCub Robot
summary: Collaboration is central to human behavior, enabling tasks beyond individual capability. This ability arises from coordinating actions through internal representations of others, a concept known as shared intelligence. Additionally, humans are characterized by physical bodies and cognitive abilities that are optimized in response to their environment, a phenomenon referred to as embodied cognition. Designing humanoid robots that collaborate safely and effectively with people requires unifying these principles. Here we propose an architecture that integrates shared intelligence and embodied cognition to enable robots to physically collaborate with humans, where robot hardware and control are optimized for human metrics, using representations of the human body and motion intelligence. The ultimate goal is to achieve a form of shared embodied intelligence. Specifically, our architecture optimizes robot hardware and physical intelligence parameters with respect to human ergonomic metrics. This is accomplished by modeling human-robot interaction as a function of hardware configurations and embedding human models into the robot's physical intelligence. As a concrete implementation, we present the humanoid robot ergoCub, whose morphology and control have been optimized for collaborative tasks with humans. Our approach provides a framework for designing humanoid robots that prioritize human ergonomics at both the hardware and physical intelligence levels, with applications in industrial and assistive robotics.

AI.tldr: Integrating shared intelligence and embodied cognition to design humanoid robots optimized for human ergonomics in collaboration, exemplified by ergoCub; a framework that tunes hardware and physical intelligence against human metrics for safe, effective human-robot task sharing.
AI.motivation: To enable safe and effective human-robot collaboration by harmonizing cognitive representations of others (shared intelligence) with physically embodied agents (embodied cognition), optimizing design for human ergonomic metrics.
AI.method: Proposes an architecture that embeds human models into the robot’s physical intelligence and optimizes hardware configurations and control with respect to human ergonomic metrics; uses a function of hardware configurations to model human-robot interaction; concrete implementation of this approach is the ergoCub humanoid with morphology and control tuned for collaborative tasks.
AI.result: Introduction of ergoCub as a concrete embodiment of the framework, with morphology and control optimized for joint human-robot tasks; demonstration of a pathway to prioritize human ergonomics at both hardware and physical intelligence levels.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers a practical approach to create humanoid robots with shared embodied intelligence, emphasizing human ergonomics in hardware design and physical intelligence, with applications in industrial and assistive robotics.

title: Detail Consistent Stage-Wise Distillation for Efficient 3D MRI Segmentation
summary: Deploying high-performing 3D medical image segmenters (e.g., nnU-Net) is often limited by memory footprint and inference latency. Compression is therefore necessary, but compact 3D encoders tend to lose fine structural cues (small lesions and sharp boundaries) as downsampling repeats across multi-resolution stages. We propose Detail Consistent Distillation (DCD), a stage-wise distillation framework that preserves structural detail across scales by aligning teacher-student features in a wavelet-decomposed representation. At each encoder stage, DCD distills directional detail components in the wavelet domain while leaving the coarse approximation comparatively unconstrained, avoiding over-regularization of global semantics. DCD is used only during training and introduces no inference-time overhead. Experiments on the BraTS 2024 and ISLES 2022 benchmarks demonstrate that our approach achieves superior performance in MRI segmentation using 3D multi-modal data. Code and implementation details for DCD are publicly available at https://github.com/ClinicaAlpha/DCD-3D-MedSeg.

AI.tldr: DCD improves 3D medical image segmentation by training a compact encoder with stage-wise wavelet-domain distillation of detail components, preserving small lesions and sharp boundaries without increasing inference cost.
AI.motivation: Compact 3D encoders suffer detail loss (small lesions, sharp boundaries) due to repeated downsampling; preserving structural cues without extra inference burden is essential for accurate MRI segmentation.
AI.method: At each encoder stage, apply 3D wavelet decomposition to feature maps and distill directional high-frequency detail components from a teacher to a student; leave the coarse low-frequency approximation unconstrained to avoid over-regularizing global semantics; training-time only with no inference overhead; employs a teacher-student framework with wavelet-domain losses.
AI.result: Reportedly achieves superior MRI segmentation performance on BraTS 2024 and ISLES 2022 using multi-modal data; code and implementation details are publicly available.
AI.conclusion: Stage-wise, wavelet-domain distillation preserves structural detail during training for compact 3D encoders, improving segmentation accuracy without runtime costs and potentially generalizing to other 3D medical imaging tasks.

title: Scaling World-Model Reinforcement Learning Through Diffusion Policy Optimization
summary: Model-based reinforcement learning (RL) can be effectively supported at scale through the use of world models. However, in practice, scaling such approaches remains fundamentally limited. A commonly recognized challenge is model bias and error compounding, which degrade long-horizon predictions. Beyond these issues, we identify a more critical yet underexplored bottleneck: a structural misalignment between search and value learning in existing world model approaches. In particular, policy improvement often relies on value functions induced by a separate, non-search policy, resulting in training inconsistency and ultimately suboptimal learning. To address this limitation, we propose Model-Based Diffusion Policy Optimization (MBDPO) in world models, a framework that unifies search and policy optimization through diffusion policy representations, thereby unlocking the potential of world models for scalable policy learning. Instead of constructing an explicit planner over a learned world model, we reformulate policy optimization as a diffusion process over searched trajectories in latent world models. In this view, we extract an implicit energy function from the collected dataset that anchors the policy, enabling MBDPO to refine the score field for policy optimization while mitigating misalignment. We evaluate MBDPO across a wide range of settings, including multi-task offline pretraining, online learning, and offline-to-online fine-tuning. In the offline regime, we further investigate its scaling behavior by pretraining on large-scale datasets, observing consistent and monotonic performance gains with increasing model capacity.

AI.tldr: MBDPO unifies search and policy optimization in world-model RL by using diffusion policy representations over latent, searched trajectories, mitigating training misalignment and enabling scalable policy learning; it extracts an implicit energy from data to anchor the policy and shows monotonic scaling with model capacity across offline/online regimes.
AI.motivation: World-model RL suffers from model bias, error accumulation, and particularly a misalignment between search (planning) and value learning (policy improvement). This misalignment leads to inconsistent training and suboptimal long-horizon performance. A unified approach is needed to align optimization objectives.
AI.method: Introduce Model-Based Diffusion Policy Optimization (MBDPO). Replace explicit planners with diffusion processes over searched trajectories in latent world models. Derive an implicit energy function from the dataset to anchor the policy and refine the score field guiding policy optimization, thereby aligning search with value learning.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across offline multi-task pretraining, online learning, and offline-to-online fine-tuning. In offline settings, scaling analyses show monotonic performance gains with increasing model capacity, indicating robustness and scalability of the approach across tasks and regimes.
AI.conclusion: MBDPO provides a principled way to unify search and policy learning in world-model RL, mitigating misalignment and enhancing scalability. The diffusion-based policy optimization leverages latent trajectories and data-derived energy to stabilize and improve policy learning, with demonstrated gains across offline/online scenarios and scaling with model capacity.

title: TPS-Drive: Task-Guided Representation Purification for VLM-based Autonomous Driving
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) provide a promising foundation for autonomous driving planning, yet bridging semantic reasoning and precise 3D spatial forecasting remains a critical challenge. Existing representation strategies generally follow two paths: text-aligned methods flatten continuous spatial states into symbols, which compromises geometric structure and induces "spatial hallucinations"; dense visual methods preserve spatial topology but overwhelm standard tokenizers with redundant background textures, leading to "representation interference". To address these limitations, we introduce TPS-Drive, a novel framework centered on Task-Guided Representation Purification that empowers VLMs to Think in Purified Space. At its core, an Agent-Centric Tokenizer utilizes a task-guided vector quantization mechanism supervised by a frozen 3D detection head, which explicitly reallocates limited codebook capacity from pervasive static backgrounds to critical dynamic agents and effectively isolates spatial redundancy. Leveraging this purified spatial vocabulary, TPS-Drive employs a decoupled reasoning pipeline that sequentially performs scene understanding, future forecasting, and action generation. The framework is optimized via a progressive three-stage training paradigm, culminating in reward-driven refinement that surpasses pure imitation learning. Extensive experiments validate our approach: TPS-Drive achieves accurate agent spatial state forecasting and reduces collision rates in open-loop nuScenes evaluations, while establishing new safety records on the rigorous closed-loop NAVSIMv1 and NAVSIMv2 benchmarks.

AI.tldr: TPS-Drive introduces Task-Guided Representation Purification for Vision-Language Models in autonomous driving, leveraging an Agent-Centric Tokenizer to reallocate codebook capacity from static backgrounds to dynamic agents, enabling a purified spatial vocabulary. It uses a decoupled, three-stage reasoning pipeline (scene understanding, future forecasting, action generation) with progressive training and reward-driven refinement. Demonstrates improved open-loop forecasting, reduced collisions, and new safety records on NAVSIMv1/v2 benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Bridging semantic reasoning and precise 3D spatial forecasting in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) for autonomous driving remains challenging due to either geometric information loss in text-aligned methods or representation interference from background textures in dense visual methods. A purified, agent-focused spatial representation aims to retain geometry while suppressing background noise, enabling reliable planning.
AI.method: TPS-Drive framework with Task-Guided Representation Purification. An Agent-Centric Tokenizer performs task-guided vector quantization, supervised by a frozen 3D detection head, reallocating limited codebook capacity from static background to dynamic agents to isolate spatial redundancy. Uses a purified spatial vocabulary enabling a decoupled reasoning pipeline that sequentially performs scene understanding, future forecasting, and action generation. Trained with a progressive three-stage scheme and reward-driven refinement that surpasses imitation learning.
AI.result: Empirical results show accurate agent spatial state forecasting and reduced collision rates in open-loop nuScenes evaluations. In closed-loop settings (NAVSIMv1 and NAVSIMv2), TPS-Drive achieves new safety records, demonstrating improved planning reliability and safety performance.
AI.conclusion: The proposed purification mechanism and decoupled reasoning pipeline effectively address spatial hallucinations and representation interference in VLM-based autonomous driving. The approach yields stronger performance in both open-loop forecasting and closed-loop safety benchmarks, with a staged training paradigm and reward-driven refinement enhancing final policy quality.

title: Zero-Shot Object Re-Identification in Egocentric Kitchen Videos via Multi-Stage SAM3 Feature Fusion
summary: Object re-identification (ReID) in egocentric kitchen videos is challenging due to rapid viewpoint changes, frequent occlusions, cluttered scenes, and large intra-class appearance variations. Objects may leave and re-enter the field of view, and the large diversity of instances with limited annotations makes supervised ReID difficult to scale, motivating zero-shot approaches. We study zero-shot object ReID on the EPIC-Kitchens benchmark, where the goal is to match active food and kitchen-tool instances across frames using only pre-trained visual features. We first evaluate five state-of-the-art feature extractors, including Vision-Language Models (VLMs) - CLIP, DINOv2, DreamSim, I-JEPA, and SAM3 - and show that zero-shot methods fail, with the best baseline achieving only 45.3% mAP. We then propose an Enhanced SAM3 ReID Pipeline, a zero-shot multi-stage method built around SAM3 segmentation as the core component. Stage 1 uses SAM3 to suppress background clutter. Stage 2 fuses embeddings from SAM3, DINOv2, and CLIP into a single L2-normalized descriptor. Stage 3 augments cosine similarity with mask-shape IoU for geometric consistency, and Stage 4 applies k-reciprocal re-ranking. The full pipeline improves performance by 7.5% mAP to 52.8%.

AI.tldr: Zero-shot object ReID in egocentric kitchens remains challenging; a multi-stage SAM3-based pipeline improves mAP from 45.3% to 52.8% on EPIC-Kitchens.
AI.motivation: Motivated by the difficulty of scaling supervised ReID in highly dynamic, cluttered egocentric kitchen scenes with large intra-class variation and objects entering/exiting the field of view; seeks zero-shot capability.
AI.method: A four-stage Enhanced SAM3 ReID Pipeline built around SAM3 segmentation: Stage 1 uses SAM3 to suppress background clutter; Stage 2 fuses embeddings from SAM3, DINOv2, and CLIP into a single L2-normalized descriptor; Stage 3 augments cosine similarity with mask-shape IoU for geometric consistency; Stage 4 applies k-reciprocal re-ranking.
AI.result: The full pipeline yields a 7.5 percentage point improvement in mAP, achieving 52.8% from a baseline of 45.3% mAP for zero-shot ReID on EPIC-Kitchens.
AI.conclusion: A zero-shot, multi-stage pipeline leveraging segmentation and multi-modal embeddings with geometric and ranking enhancements significantly improves ReID performance in egocentric kitchen videos; demonstrates the viability of SAM3-based segmentation as a core component for zero-shot ReID, though gaps to supervised methods may remain.

title: Two-Parameter Flows for Learning Population Dynamics of Physical Systems
summary: This work addresses the problem of learning the dynamics of high-dimensional probability densities over time using unlabeled samples, without assuming access to trajectory information. We introduce two-parameter flows that learn only sampling-time transports from a base distribution to each marginal and then extract a physics-time velocity by regressing on coupled synthetic trajectories. We prove that the resulting physics-time dynamics are unique and inherit regularity from the sampling-time transports. Because we can build on standard, well-developed conditional flow matching techniques for learning the base-to-marginal transports, our approach scales to high dimensions and avoids per-step optimal-transport couplings, while allowing admissible non-gradient dynamics that can naturally explain rotational or circulating physics phenomena.

AI.tldr: A two-parameter flow framework learns the time evolution of high-dimensional densities from unlabeled samples by learning sampling-time transports to marginals and regressing to obtain a physics-time velocity; it guarantees uniqueness and regularity, scales to high dimensions, and supports non-gradient dynamics for rotational phenomena.
AI.motivation: To infer dynamical evolution of probability densities over time when trajectory data is unavailable, particularly in high dimensions, while avoiding per-step optimal-transport couplings and enabling non-gradient (e.g., rotational) dynamics.
AI.method: Introduce two-parameter flows that separate sampling-time transports (base distribution to each marginal) from physics-time velocity. Learn base-to-marginal transports via conditional flow matching; generate coupled synthetic trajectories; regress to recover a velocity field in physics-time. Prove uniqueness and regularity of the resulting dynamics and leverage standard conditional flow matching for scalability.
AI.result: The framework yields a unique physics-time dynamics with regularity inherited from the sampling-time transports. It scales to high dimensions, avoids per-step OT couplings, and can represent non-gradient dynamics such as rotational phenomena.
AI.conclusion: The proposed two-parameter flow approach provides a scalable, theoretically grounded method to learn time-evolving densities from unlabeled samples, combining transport learning with regression to a physics-time velocity, and enabling non-gradient dynamics relevant for complex physical systems.

title: Tail-Aware HiFloat4: W4A4 Post-Training Quantization for Wan2.2
summary: This report describes Tail-Aware HiFloat4, our submission to the low-bit text-to-video generation quantization challenge. Our method adapts the public ViDiT-Q post-training quantization pipeline to Wan2.2 under the HiFloat4 numerical format. We quantize the main linear layers in both Wan2.2 transformer modules with W4A4 HiFloat4 fake quantization, keep numerically sensitive boundary modules in high precision, and introduce an activation-tail-aware percentile calibration module for channel-mask construction. Together with compact PTQ-state restoration, this design reduces the influence of rare calibration outliers while keeping the runtime HiFloat4 arithmetic and sampling pipeline unchanged.

AI.tldr: Tail-Aware HiFloat4 PTQ adapts ViDiT-Q to Wan2.2 for low-bit text-to-video generation; quantizes main linear layers with W4A4 HiFloat4 fake quantization, preserves numerically sensitive boundaries in high precision, and uses activation-tail-aware percentile calibration for channel-mask construction, with PTQ-state restoration to mitigate rare calibration outliers while keeping the runtime HiFloat4 pipeline intact.
AI.motivation: Enable accurate, end-to-end low-bit quantization of transformer-based text-to-video models by addressing calibration outliers and numerical sensitivity, while maintaining the HiFloat4 arithmetic path.
AI.method: Adapt ViDiT-Q post-training quantization to Wan2.2. Quantize main linear layers in Wan2.2 transformer modules with W4A4 HiFloat4 fake quantization; keep numerically sensitive boundary modules in high precision; introduce activation-tail-aware percentile calibration to construct channel masks; apply compact PTQ-state restoration to stabilize calibration; preserve the existing runtime HiFloat4 arithmetic and sampling pipeline.
AI.result: The design reduces the influence of rare calibration outliers and maintains the runtime HiFloat4 arithmetic and sampling pipeline, enabling effective low-bit quantization without altering the inference path.
AI.conclusion: Tail-Aware HiFloat4 PTQ provides an effective, end-to-end approach for low-bit quantization of text-to-video models between ViDiT-Q and Wan2.2, balancing accuracy, stability, and runtime compatibility.

title: Learning to Balance Motor Thermal Safety and Quadrupedal Locomotion Performance with Residual Policy
summary: Motor thermal management is often overlooked in the context of electrically-actuated robots, particularly legged robots, but motor overheating is a key factor that limits long-duration locomotion especially under payload conditions. This paper integrates a whole-body thermal model of a quadruped robot into the reinforcement learning pipeline to update motor temperatures, and proposes a two-stage training framework for motor thermal management. In this framework, a nominal policy is first pre-trained as a locomotion baseline capable of traversing diverse terrains. A residual policy is then trained on top of the nominal policy to provide corrective actions based on the robot's thermal state, ensuring high performance under low-temperature conditions and preventing motor overheating under high-temperature conditions. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed policy achieves an effective balance between motor thermal safety and locomotion performance. Real-world experiments on a Unitree A1 quadruped robot further validate the approach: under a 3 kg payload, the robot achieves stable locomotion across multiple terrains for over 13 minutes, while the nominal policy alone leads to motor overheating in about 5 minutes.

AI.tldr: A two-stage reinforcement learning framework for motor thermal management in quadrupeds: pre-train a nominal locomotion policy, then train a residual policy that uses the thermal state to correct actions, balancing safety and performance. Validated in simulation and on a Unitree A1 with 3 kg payload, achieving 13+ minutes of stable locomotion versus ~5 minutes for the nominal policy alone.
AI.motivation: Motor overheating constrains long-duration locomotion in electrically-actuated legged robots, especially under payloads; integrating a thermal-aware control loop into RL can sustain performance while preventing overheating.
AI.method: Develop a whole-body thermal model and embed it in an RL pipeline. Use a two-stage training: (1) pre-train a nominal locomotion policy for diverse terrains; (2) train a residual policy on top of the nominal to adjust actions based on thermal state, ensuring safe operation under high temperatures while maintaining performance at low temperatures.
AI.result: Simulation shows the residual policy achieves a balance between motor thermal safety and locomotion performance. Real-world validation on a Unitree A1 shows stable locomotion across multiple terrains for over 13 minutes under a 3 kg payload, whereas the nominal policy overheats in about 5 minutes.
AI.conclusion: The two-stage thermal-aware RL framework effectively extends mission duration and maintains locomotion performance under thermal constraints, demonstrating practical viability for payload-bearing quadrupeds.

title: OmniGF: A Dual-Branch Vision-Language Framework for Unified Gaze Following
summary: Understanding human gaze behavior is essential for complex scene comprehension and human-computer interaction. Traditional gaze following models are typically restricted to pure spatial localization, lacking the high-level capacity to reason about semantic targets or complex social contexts. Furthermore, these models often process individuals sequentially, requiring redundant computations over the same scene image for multi-person inference. While recent Vision-Language Models (VLMs) offer the exceptional semantic reasoning needed to address gaze-related semantic tasks, their reliance on discrete text generation inherently limits precision in continuous spatial tasks like gaze localization. To bridge this gap, we propose OmniGF, a unified vision-language framework that adapts foundational VLMs for highly scalable multi-person gaze reasoning. The model adopts a dual-branch decoding strategy: a structured language branch generates discrete reasoning states, while a continuous spatial branch directly taps into the VLM's dense hidden states. Supervising these extracted representations with high-resolution gaze target heatmaps effectively overcomes the spatial bottleneck of text-only coordinate generation. Furthermore, to explicitly ground the model in multi-person scenes, we augment the input with head embeddings encoded from cropped head images, providing fine-grained appearance and orientation cues for all individuals simultaneously. By modeling all individuals and leveraging the strong semantic capability of VLMs, OmniGF seamlessly integrates precise spatial gaze target estimation, semantic gaze prediction, and complex social gaze reasoning. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our framework establishes new state-of-the-art performance across multiple standard benchmarks. Code is available at https://github.com/cvlab-stonybrook/omnigf.

AI.tldr: OmniGF is a unified vision-language gaze reasoning framework that combines discrete reasoning via a language branch with continuous spatial decoding, enabling scalable multi-person gaze localization and semantic prediction grounded by head embeddings; it achieves state-of-the-art results on gaze benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Understanding gaze behavior requires high-level semantic reasoning and efficient multi-person handling. Traditional models are either spatially crude or inefficient for multi-person inference and text-only outputs limit spatial precision. A unified VLM-based approach can address these gaps by combining semantic reasoning with precise localization.
AI.method: A dual-branch decoding architecture: (1) a structured language branch that emits discrete reasoning states, and (2) a continuous spatial branch that taps into the VLM's dense hidden states to produce precise gaze localization. Supervising both branches with high-resolution gaze heatmaps overcomes spatial bottlenecks of text-only coordinates. The model additionally ingests head embeddings derived from cropped head images to ground all individuals in the scene, supplying appearance and orientation cues for multi-person grounding. End-to-end training integrates spatial, semantic, and social gaze reasoning.
AI.result: The approach establishes new state-of-the-art performance across standard gaze benchmarks, demonstrating improvements in both spatial precision and semantic gaze reasoning for multi-person scenes. Code is released at the provided repository.
AI.conclusion: OmniGF effectively unifies spatial gaze localization, semantic prediction, and social reasoning in multi-person contexts by leveraging a dual-branch decoding strategy and head-grounded VLM features, yielding superior performance over prior methods.

title: Stateful Inference for Low-Latency Multi-Agent Tool Calling
summary:
Multi-agent tool calling is becoming the dominant interaction pattern for LLM-based systems, yet existing inference frameworks treat each tool call as an independent request, re-processing the entire conversation from scratch even though 85-95% of the prompt is unchanged from the previous turn.
  We present a stateful inference architecture that converts the $O(n_t)$ per-turn cost of conventional serving into an $O(Δ_t)$ delta-only cost: a persistent KV cache lives across turns and advances by ingesting only the new tokens, while a radix prefix cache extends this across interleaved multi-agent traffic and a prompt-lookup speculative decoder accelerates structured output. Against vLLM and SGLang on novel, fully-generated workloads, the reference implementation is $2.1\times$ faster per turn on a 6-turn agentic workflow and $4.2\times$ on the median turn of a 35-turn one, halving end-to-end wall time. The advantage comes from stateful reuse and speculation, not caching.

AI.tldr: A stateful inference architecture for LLM tool-calling achieves delta-based costs and significant speedups by persisting a KV cache, a radix prefix cache, and a prompt-lookup speculative decoder, outperforming vLLM and SGLang on novel workloads.
AI.motivation: To reduce per-turn computation in multi-agent, tool-using LLM systems by avoiding re-processing largely unchanged prompts and by efficiently handling interleaved traffic and structured outputs.
AI.method: Introduce a persistent KV cache across turns that ingests only new tokens (Δ_t), a radix prefix cache to extend across interleaved multi-agent traffic, and a prompt-lookup speculative decoder to accelerate structured output. Evaluate against vLLM and SGLang on novel, fully-generated workloads, measuring per-turn speed and end-to-end wall time in multi-turn workflows.
AI.result: Reference implementation achieves 2.1× faster per turn on a 6-turn agentic workflow and 4.2× on the median turn of a 35-turn workflow, effectively halving end-to-end wall time. Gains attributed to stateful reuse and speculation rather than caching alone.
AI.conclusion: Stateful reuse plus speculative decoding yields substantial efficiency gains for multi-agent LLM tool-calling, addressing the O(n_t) per-turn cost of conventional serving. The results suggest good performance improvements in realistic, multi-agent settings, contingent on effective state management and speculative accuracy.

title: UnityMAS-O: A General RL Optimization Framework for LLM-Based Multi-Agent Systems
summary:
LLM-based multi-agent systems decompose complex tasks into interacting roles, but most remain manually orchestrated by prompts, tools, and control rules, while agents are rarely optimized through a unified reinforcement learning interface. Existing RL post-training frameworks mainly target single-policy optimization and lack abstractions for user-defined multi-agent workflows, structured interaction, role-specific credit assignment, and configurable parameter sharing.
  We present UnityMAS-O, a general RL optimization framework for LLM-based multi-agent systems. UnityMAS-O treats the complete workflow as the optimization unit, rather than a single response or policy trajectory. It represents workflows through four first-class objects: logical agent roles, graph trajectories, user-defined rewards, and agent--model mappings. This decouples logical agents from physical model parameters, supporting full sharing, full separation, and partial sharing, with rewards assigned at role, turn, and trajectory levels.
  UnityMAS-O extends verl with a Ray-based star-topology runtime. A central controller executes workflows, invokes tools, records structured trajectories, and assembles rewards; model-local worker groups handle rollout, buffering, advantage computation, and distributed PPO-style updates. Users can define agents, workflows, model mappings, and rewards without rewriting the optimization infrastructure.
  We instantiate UnityMAS-O on retrieval-augmented QA, iterative agentic search, and reflective code generation. Across Natural Questions, HotpotQA, and held-out code tasks, multi-agent RL improves manually specified workflows after optimization, with especially large gains for smaller models and strict code all-passed metrics. These results show that UnityMAS-O can serve as a reusable substrate for converting diverse LLM-based multi-agent workflows into trainable multi-agent RL systems.

AI.tldr: A general RL optimization framework (UnityMAS-O) for LLM-based multi-agent systems, decoupling agents from model parameters, enabling multi-agent workflows with role/trajectory rewards and distributed PPO-style updates; demonstrated on QA, agentic search, and code generation with improvements over manual workflows.
AI.motivation: LLM-based multi-agent systems often rely on manual orchestration via prompts, tools, and control rules. Existing RL post-training frameworks mainly optimize single policies and lack abstractions for multi-agent workflows, role-specific credit assignment, and flexible parameter sharing.
AI.method: UnityMAS-O treats the entire workflow as the optimization unit. It defines four first-class objects—logical agent roles, graph trajectories, user-defined rewards, and agent–model mappings—to decouple logic from parameters and support full/partial model sharing. It extends Verl with a Ray-based star-topology runtime: a central controller orchestrates workflows and rewards, while model-local worker groups perform rollout, buffering, advantage computation, and distributed PPO-style updates. Users specify agents, workflows, mappings, and rewards without altering the underlying optimization system.
AI.result: Across retrieval-augmented QA, iterative agentic search, and reflective code generation tasks (Natural Questions, HotpotQA, and held-out code tasks), multi-agent RL improves over manually specified workflows, with larger gains for smaller models and strict all-passed metrics, indicating UnityMAS-O’s effectiveness as a reusable substrate for trainable multi-agent RL in diverse LLM-based workflows.
AI.conclusion: UnityMAS-O provides a reusable, scalable substrate that converts diverse LLM-based multi-agent workflows into trainable multi-agent RL systems by abstracting workflow units, decoupling logic from models, and enabling distributed, PPO-style optimization.

title: VR-DAgger: Immersive VR for Dexterous Data Collection and Uncertainty-Guided On-Policy Correction
summary: Learning from demonstrations is effective for robotic manipulation, but collecting sufficient task-specific data remains a major bottleneck. Under distribution shift, small errors compound, performance degrades, and expert time is often spent on redundant, low-value corrections instead of the few critical failure cases.

AI.tldr: LfD is effective for robotic manipulation but data collection is a bottleneck; under distribution shift, small errors compound, performance degrades, and expert time is spent on low-value corrections instead of addressing critical failures.
AI.motivation: Improve data efficiency and robustness of imitation learning for robotic manipulation by mitigating data bottlenecks and distribution-shift-induced degradation.
AI.method: Not specified in the excerpt; the abstract discusses the problem and implications but does not detail a proposed method.
AI.result: Not specified in the excerpt; no empirical results are described in the provided text.
AI.conclusion: The abstract highlights the need for data-efficient and failure-focused strategies to counter distribution shift and reduce costly expert corrections.

title: The Rescue Effect: Spatio-Semantic Early Exit Bypasses Quantization Collapse in CLIP
summary: Deploying Vision-Language Models on resource-constrained hardware typically requires INT8 quantization, but in joint-embedding architectures such as CLIP this introduces a failure mode distinct from quantized CNN classifiers: activation noise accumulated across transformer blocks perturbs the direction of the multimodal embedding, eroding the cosine alignment on which zero-shot retrieval depends. We characterize this as Quantization-Induced Representation Collapse (QIRC) and quantify it on INT8 CLIP ViT-B/32, where the layer-wise noise-to-signal ratio grows from below 10% in shallow blocks to 52% at Layer 11. We propose LRA-EE (Layer-wise Representation-Aware Early Exit), which bypasses noise-saturated deep layers via Spatio-Semantic Aggregation (replacing the immature shallow [CLS] with a global patch-token average), a learned multi-feature gate (confidence, top-2 margin, spatial-activation variance), and Layer-adaptive Confidence Thresholding calibrated to each layer's Information-to-Noise Ratio. On ImageNet-1K zero-shot classification, LRA-EE reduces FLOPs by 13.4% and improves Top-1 accuracy by +2.44%p (58.72% -> 61.16%) over the INT8 baseline. A four-quadrant decomposition isolates the Rescue Effect: 9.5% of samples are correctly classified at shallow exits but lost to noise at full depth, against only 7.1% suffering the inverse.

AI.tldr: Quantization-induced activation noise in joint-embedding models like CLIP degrades cosine alignment; propose Layer-wise Representation-Aware Early Exit (LRA-EE) with Spatio-Semantic Aggregation, a learned multi-feature gate, and layer-adaptive thresholds to skip noisy deep layers. Achieves 13.4% FLOPs reduction and +2.44pp Top-1 on ImageNet-1K zero-shot; four-quadrant rescue analysis shows partial early-classification stability under noise.
AI.motivation: In resource-constrained hardware, INT8 quantization introduces activation noise that accumulates across transformer blocks, perturbing the embedding direction and eroding cosine alignment essential for zero-shot retrieval. There is a need to defend joint-embedding representations against quantization-induced collapse.
AI.method: 1) Layer-wise early exits with Spatio-Semantic Aggregation: replace immature shallow CLS token with global patch-token average. 2) Learned multi-feature gate: uses confidence, top-2 margin, and spatial-activation variance to decide exits. 3) Layer-adaptive Confidence Thresholding calibrated to each layer's Information-to-Noise Ratio to balance accuracy and FLOPs. 4) Four-quadrant decomposition to analyze which samples benefit from shallow exits (rescue effect).
AI.result: On ImageNet-1K zero-shot classification, LRA-EE reduces FLOPs by 13.4% and improves Top-1 accuracy from 58.72% to 61.16% when compared to the INT8 baseline.
AI.conclusion: LRA-EE effectively mitigates Quantization-Induced Representation Collapse (QIRC) in INT8 CLIP by enabling layer-wise adaptive early exits guided by information-to-noise considerations and a robust Spatio-Semantic aggregation strategy, achieving simultaneous efficiency and accuracy gains. The four-quadrant analysis highlights that a non-trivial portion of samples can be rescued at shallow exits, while a minority still degrade with depth.

title: Dynamic Link Prediction with Temporally Enhanced Signed Graph Neural Networks
summary: Temporal signed networks (TSNs) model the time evolution of cooperative and adversarial relationships that arise in applications such as social media analysis, trust and reputation systems, and financial transaction networks. While graph neural networks (GNNs) perform well for static or unsigned link prediction, effective learning in temporal signed graphs remains challenging due to the interaction of signed relations, evolving structure, and balance-theoretic constraints. To address this gap, we propose a \emph{modular} temporal enhancement framework for signed GNNs that integrates historical context into otherwise static architectures. The framework introduces a Historical Context Integration Module (HCIM) that combines learnable recency-aware temporal weighting, LSTM-based embedding trajectory modeling, and multi-head temporal attention to capture both short- and long-term signed interaction dynamics. Historical information is fused with current node representations using either global or node-adaptive weighting, allowing the architecture-agnostic framework to accommodate heterogeneous temporal behaviors. We instantiate the approach on the Self-Explainable Signed Graph Transformer (SE-SGformer), preserving interpretability while extending it with temporal awareness. Experiments on real-world and synthetic TSNs, including Bitcoin OTC, Bitcoin Alpha, Reddit, and small-world network models, demonstrate consistent and statistically significant improvements over the static baseline.

AI.tldr: A modular temporal enhancement framework (HCIM) for signed GNNs that injects historical context into static architectures to better model temporal signed networks, instantiated on SE-SGformer, showing improved performance on real and synthetic TSNs.
AI.motivation: Temporal signed graphs require integrating evolving structure, sign constraints, and long- and short-term interaction dynamics; existing static or unsigned GNNs underperform on TSN tasks.
AI.method: Historical Context Integration Module (HCIM) combining recency-weighted temporal signals, LSTM-based embedding trajectory modeling, and multi-head temporal attention; flexible fusion with current node representations via global or node-adaptive weighting; architecture-agnostic; instantiated on Self-Explainable Signed Graph Transformer (SE-SGformer).
AI.result: Experiments on real-world and synthetic TSNs (Bitcoin OTC, Bitcoin Alpha, Reddit, small-world models) show consistent and statistically significant improvements over the static baseline.
AI.conclusion: The HCIM-based modular temporal enhancement framework effectively captures short- and long-term signed interaction dynamics, preserves interpretability in SE-SGformer while adding temporal awareness, and generalizes to heterogeneous temporal behaviors across TSNs.

title: Completion vs Optimality: Policy Gradient in Long-Horizon Cumulative-Damage Problems
summary: Long-horizon decision problems with cumulative damage couple locally attractive actions to globally adverse outcomes. We identify two orthogonal failure modes for policy-gradient methods on this class and propose a decomposition that separates them: \emph{completion} (reaching the terminal horizon rather than exiting via an implicit terminal constraint) and \emph{optimality} (matching the dynamic-programming reference given completion). Under PPO with a linear soft penalty, granting horizon access alone reduces the completion rate: the penalty's equilibrium drives the dominant-activity share to zero, while action-space restriction combined with horizon access achieves completion but leaves an optimality gap ($ΔM_{\text{final}} = 0.271$) that we trace to first-phase greedy commitment at the damage origin. We derive four testable predictions and evaluate them in two separately calibrated environments that share the same abstract structure but differ in domain, horizon, activity set, and calibration data: a 49-step bricklayer career and a 20-season NBA power-forward career. All four predictions replicate qualitatively. The horizon-invariance prediction is met at three of four tested horizons, with the exception at $H = 15$ consistent with the $H^*$ boundary ($H^* \in [6, 14]$ under the NBA parameters).

AI.tldr: Identifies two orthogonal failure modes for policy-gradient optimization in long-horizon cumulative-damage tasks: completion (reaching terminal horizon) and optimality (matching a dynamic-programming reference given completion). Proposes a decomposition, analyzes PPO with a linear soft penalty, and validates four predictions in two calibrated domains (bricklayer career, NBA career). Horizon access alone reduces completion; with horizon access plus action-space restriction completion is achieved but leaves an optimality gap ΔM_final ≈ 0.271. Four predictions replicate qualitatively; horizon-invariance holds for 3 of 4 horizons, with H* boundary in NBA parameters explaining the anomaly.
AI.motivation: To diagnose why policy-gradient methods struggle on long-horizon, cumulative-damage decision problems and to separate failure modes into completion and optimality for clearer diagnosis and potential remedy.
AI.method: Decompose policy-gradient failure into two orthogonal pieces: completion and optimality. Derive four testable predictions. Evaluate them in two separately calibrated environments sharing the same abstract structure but differing in domain, horizon, action set, and calibration data: a 49-step bricklayer career and a 20-season NBA power-forward career. Use PPO with a linear soft penalty and analyze horizon-access effects, penalty-driven equilibrium, and the impact of action-space restriction on completion and optimality. Quantify optimality gap ΔM_final and relate it to early-dix greedy commitment at the damage origin.
AI.result: The decomposition isolates two failure modes and yields four testable predictions. Horizon access with a linear soft penalty reduces completion because the penalty’s equilibrium drives down dominant activity. Adding horizon access with action-space restriction achieves completion but induces an optimality gap ΔM_final ≈ 0.271, attributed to first-phase greedy commitment at the damage origin. All four predictions are qualitatively replicated in both environments. Horizon-invariance is observed at three of four horizons; the mismatch at H=15 aligns with an H* boundary estimated as [6,14] under NBA parameters.
AI.conclusion: A principled decomposition into completion and optimality clarifies why policy-gradient methods fail in long-horizon damage problems and provides testable predictions that hold across domains. The results suggest that mere horizon access is insufficient; achieving true optimality requires addressing the dynamic-programming reference and avoiding early greedy commitments. The NBA bricklayer results support a boundary effect (H*) governing horizon-invariance.

title: TCBiRRT: Rapid Motion Planning for Tightly Coupled Dual-arm Space Manipulator Using Task-space Random Expansion
summary: Planning the motion path for a tightly coupled dual-arm space manipulator under closed-chain constraints is a fundamental yet challenging problem in on-orbit assembly of large-scale space structures. The closed-chain constraints significantly reduce the feasible configuration space, making it difficult for existing planners to efficiently generate collision-free motions, especially in cluttered environments. To address this issue, this paper proposes a task-space constrained bidirectional rapidly-exploring random tree algorithm, termed TCBiRRT. Unlike conventional methods that operate in the high-dimensional configuration space, the proposed approach performs random sampling and node expansion directly in the task space defined by the manipulated object pose. A task-space node expansion strategy is developed to generate candidate object motions, which are then mapped to continuous joint paths using a path inverse kinematics algorithm. The method is further integrated with a bidirectional RRT framework and a regrasp mechanism to efficiently connect two random trees. Extensive simulations are conducted in representative on-orbit assembly scenarios with varying levels of environmental complexity. The results demonstrate that TCBiRRT achieves significantly higher success rates and orders-of-magnitude improvements in planning time compared to state-of-the-art planners. The proposed method provides an efficient and robust solution for motion planning of tightly coupled dual-arm space manipulators.

AI.tldr: Task-space constrained bidirectional RRT (TCBiRRT) for dual-arm space manipulators under closed-chain constraints; samples in task space, maps to joint paths via IK, uses bidirectional RRT and regrasp to improve planning; shows large gains in success rate and planning time in on-orbit assembly.
AI.motivation: Close-chain constraints drastically reduce feasible configurations and collision-free motions; existing high-dimensional planners struggle in cluttered environments; need efficient, robust planning for on-orbit assembly with tightly coupled arms.
AI.method: TCBiRRT performs sampling and node expansion directly in task space defined by the manipulated object's pose; develops a task-space node expansion strategy to generate candidate object motions; applies path inverse kinematics (PIK) to map to continuous joint trajectories; integrates with bidirectional RRT and a regrasp mechanism to connect two trees efficiently.
AI.result: Extensive simulations across representative on-orbit assembly scenarios with varying environmental complexity; TCBiRRT achieves higher success rates and orders-of-magnitude faster planning times compared to state-of-the-art planners.
AI.conclusion: Provides an efficient, robust approach for motion planning of tightly coupled dual-arm space manipulators under closed-chain constraints, beneficial for on-orbit assembly in cluttered environments.

title: HydraPrompt: An Adaptive and Asymmetric Framework of Vision-Language Models for Synthetic Image Detection
summary: The rapid evolution of generative models has precipitated a proliferation of fabricated content, posing significant challenges to existing Synthetic Image Detection (SID) methods. Capitalizing on advancements in vision-language models (e.g., CLIP), recent attempts have leveraged learnable textual prompts to identify synthetic images. However, they still leverage static prompt as a fixed boundary for real and fake images, failing to adapt to the varying types of forgery that emerge during inference. To overcome this issue, we propose **HydraPrompt**, an asymmetric prompting framework that dynamically adjusts the category centers by aligning with fine-grained image cues. Specifically, we propose an Asymmetric Prompt Adapter (**APA**): (1) for authentic category, we introduce a single set of prompts to capture the consistent representative patterns, which serves as a unified anchor for real content. While (2) for fake category, we construct sample-adaptive prompts that specialize in capturing diverse cues from different samples, enabling adaptive modeling of forgery image variations. To increase pronounced discriminability within different synthetic images, we further introduce a Conditional Supervised Contrastive (**CSC**) objective, which compacts the authentic representations while capturing fine-grained forgery clues. Extensive experiments on popular SID benchmarks demonstrate the state-of-the-art performance of our framework.

AI.tldr: HydraPrompt uses asymmetric prompting to align category centers with fine-grained image cues for SID: a unified authentic prompt (APA) anchors real content, and sample-adaptive fake prompts capture diverse forgery cues; a Conditional Supervised Contrastive (CSC) objective tightens authentic representations while emphasizing forgery details, achieving state-of-the-art SID performance.
AI.motivation: Static prompts inadequately bound real vs. fake content across evolving forgery types. There is a need for adaptive, sample-specific prompts that can align with diverse forgery cues while maintaining a stable real-content anchor.
AI.method: Asymmetric Prompt Adapter (APA): authentic category uses a single, stable prompt set as a universal anchor; fake category uses sample-adaptive prompts tailored to each image to capture diverse forgery cues. CSC: a supervised contrastive loss that pulls authentic representations together while separating fine-grained forgery cues. The framework leverages vision-language modelling (e.g., CLIP) for alignment.
AI.result: Experiments on popular SID benchmarks report state-of-the-art performance, validating the effectiveness of adaptive prompting and CSC in improving discrimination between authentic and synthetic images.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, asymmetric prompting with sample-adaptive cues and CSC improves robustness to evolving forgery techniques and enhances discriminability in SID tasks.

title: Classification and detection of multiple UAVs using rational Gaussian wavelet neural networks
summary: The detection of unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) is important for the protection of civilian and military infrastructure. In this paper we propose a cost effective UAV detection system using sound signals obtained from microphones. The recorded signals are passed through a signal processing pipeline which employs interpretable adaptive feature extractors using so-called rational Gaussian wavelets. These adaptive wavelet transformations are embedded into and trained together with an underlying small neural network which detects and classifies UAVs based on the obtained features. This leads to a physically interpretable machine learning algorithm that in addition to classifying UAVs is also capable of detecting UAV swarms. We demonstrate our results using data collected in indoor studio and noisy outdoor environments. We conclude that the proposed method outperforms traditional machine learning approaches for detecting and classifying single UAVs as well as drone swarms, while retaining a high degree of interpretability. Our implementation of the proposed methods is made publicly available for reproducibility.

AI.tldr: Cost-effective, interpretable UAV detection from microphone data using adaptive rational Gaussian wavelets embedded in a small neural network; capable of single-UAV and swarm detection; results show improved performance and high interpretability; code 공개.
AI.motivation: UAVs threaten critical infrastructure; require robust detection in varied environments with low false positives, while maintaining interpretability for trust and debugging.
AI.method: A signal processing pipeline on microphone signals using interpretable adaptive feature extractors based on rational Gaussian wavelets. These adaptive wavelet transforms are trained jointly with a small neural network to perform UAV detection and classification, including swarm detection. Evaluated in indoor studio and noisy outdoor settings; results indicate superior performance to traditional ML; implementation is publicly available.
AI.result: The proposed method outperforms traditional machine learning approaches for single UAV detection and swarm detection while preserving interpretability; reproducible due to public code release.
AI.conclusion: The approach delivers a physically interpretable, effective UAV detection system suitable for challenging environments and swarm scenarios, with open-source resources enabling reproducibility and further research.

title: MemFail: Stress-Testing Failure Modes of LLM Memory Systems
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents increasingly rely on external memory systems to remain consistent across long-horizon interactions, but little empirical work has been done to understand the specific failure modes and design choices that these systems present. Existing benchmarks report aggregate question-answering accuracy and treat memory systems as black boxes, making it impossible to attribute an incorrect answer to a particular failure mode of the system. We introduce MemFail, a diagnostic benchmark that isolates the failure modes of modern LLM memory systems. We begin by formalizing memory systems as the composition of three canonical operations -- summarization, storage, and retrieval -- and identify the potential failure modes induced by each. Based on these hypothesized failure modes, we construct five datasets spanning four tasks, each adversarially designed to test a specific operation of a memory system. Using these datasets, we evaluate four state-of-the-art memory systems on MemFail and demonstrate how MemFail can be used to empirically understand the tradeoffs induced by differences in memory system architectures.

AI.tldr: MemFail is a diagnostic benchmark that isolates failure modes of LLM memory systems by formalizing memory as three operations (summarization, storage, retrieval), and uses adversarial datasets across four tasks to test five operation-specific failure modes in four state-of-the-art memory systems.
AI.motivation: To move beyond aggregate QA accuracy and attribute errors to concrete memory-system components, enabling principled design and comparison of LLM memory architectures for long-horizon interactions.
AI.method: Formalize memory as summarization, storage, retrieval. Identify potential failure modes for each, design five adversarial datasets targeting specific operations, and evaluate four memory systems across four tasks to reveal architectural tradeoffs.
AI.result: MemFail exposes distinct, operation-specific failure modes and demonstrates that different memory architectures trade off in predictable ways; provides a diagnostic framework for memory-system evaluation.
AI.conclusion: MemFail offers a principled, modular benchmarking framework to diagnose, compare, and guide the design of LLM memory systems, facilitating targeted improvements and empirical understanding of architecture tradeoffs.

title: Towards Drone-based Mapping of Volcanic Gases using Gas Tomography
summary: Volcanoes emit large amounts of CO2, directly influencing human lives. Mapping volcanic gas emissions helps to forecast eruptions and understand the impact of volcanoes on climate and the environment. Drone-based gas sensing significantly reduces risks in volcanic monitoring but faces technical limitations when measuring gas, as rotor downwash disperses the gas plume before detection. Gas Tomography using remote gas sensing addresses this challenge. At the Salinelle dei Cappuccini mud volcanoes, we demonstrate that while drone-mounted in-situ sensors failed to detect CO2 emissions due to aerodynamic disturbance, open-path sensing successfully enabled remote gas distribution mapping. We present a novel model-based gas tomographic reconstruction approach that incorporates a Lagrangian model to compensate for wind-induced advection. The resulting gas distribution maps align with manually collected in-situ measurements, confirming that model-based gas tomography effectively overcomes downwash limitations and enables accurate mapping of volcanic emissions.

AI.tldr: Model-based gas tomography with wind-advection compensation enables remote open-path mapping of volcanic CO2 emissions using drone-based sensing, overcoming rotor downwash; validated at Salinelle dei Cappuccini mud volcanoes against in-situ measurements.
AI.motivation: Volcanoes emit CO2 that impacts climate and human health; accurate mapping of emissions is crucial for eruption forecasting and environmental impact assessment. Drone-based sensing offers risk reduction but suffers from aerodynamic downwash that distorts measurements, necessitating robust reconstruction methods.
AI.method: Develop a model-based gas tomographic reconstruction that incorporates a Lagrangian wind-advection model to correct for wind-driven plume transport in open-path sensing data. Apply the method to Salinelle dei Cappuccini mud volcanoes, comparing drone-based open-path estimates with manually collected in-situ measurements.
AI.result: The open-path gas distribution maps produced by the model-based tomography align with manually collected in-situ CO2 measurements, demonstrating that the wind-advection compensation overcomes downwash-related distortions and enables accurate, remote mapping of volcanic emissions.
AI.conclusion: Model-based gas tomography with a Lagrangian advection correction effectively mitigates rotor downwash limitations in drone-based volcanic gas sensing, enabling reliable, non-invasive mapping of CO2 emissions.

title: Rethinking Weakly-supervised Video Temporal Grounding From a Game Perspective
summary: This paper addresses the challenging task of weakly-supervised video temporal grounding.
