Existing approaches are generally based on the moment proposal selection framework that utilizes contrastive learning and reconstruction paradigm for scoring the pre-defined moment proposals. Although they have achieved significant progress, we argue that their current frameworks have overlooked two indispensable issues: 1) Coarse-grained cross-modal learning: previous methods solely capture the global video-level alignment with the query, failing to model the detailed consistency between video frames and query words for accurately grounding the moment boundaries. 2) Complex moment proposals: their performance severely relies on the quality of proposals, which are also time-consuming and complicated for selection. To this end, in this paper, we make the first attempt to tackle this task from a novel game perspective, which effectively learns the uncertain relationship between each vision-language pair with diverse granularity and flexible combination for multi-level cross-modal interaction.Specifically, we creatively model each video frame and query word as game players with multivariate cooperative game theory to learn their contribution to the cross-modal similarity score. By quantifying the trend of frame-word cooperation within a coalition via the game-theoretic interaction, we are able to value all uncertain but possible correspondence between frames and words. Finally, instead of using moment proposals, we utilize the learned query-guided frame-wise scores for better moment localization.Experiments show that our method achieves superior performance on both Charades-STA and ActivityNet Caption datasets.

AI.tldr: Introduces a game-theoretic, frame-level cross-modal grounding method for weakly-supervised video grounding that bypasses proposal generation.
AI.motivation: Current weakly-supervised video grounding methods rely on coarse global video–query alignment and predefined, heavy moment proposals, which hinder precise boundary localization and efficiency.
AI.method: Model each video frame and query word as players in a multivariate cooperative game to learn their contributions to cross-modal similarity; quantify frame–word cooperation trends within coalitions to capture uncertain correspondences; use query-guided frame-wise scores for localization instead of predefined proposals.
AI.result: Demonstrates superior performance on Charades-STA and ActivityNet Captions datasets versus baselines.
AI.conclusion: Game-theoretic cross-modal interaction enables finer-grained alignment and efficient localization by capturing uncertain frame–word associations and avoiding heavy proposal generation.

title: Curriculum Learning for Safety Alignment
comment: Accepted at the ICML 2026 GlobalSouthML Workshop
summary: Direct Preference Optimisation (DPO) is widely used for safety alignment in large language models. However, prior work shows it is brittle and exhibits poor out-of-distribution (OOD) generalisation. In this paper, we investigate whether Curriculum Learning can improve the robustness of DPO-based safety alignment. We propose Staged-Competence, a curriculum-based framework that organises preference data by difficulty, employs competence-based sampling, and progressively updates the reference model during training. Averaged across three model families, Staged-Competence reduces OOD harmful response rates by 16% and jailbreak attack success rates by 20%, while preserving general capabilities with near-zero over-refusal. We further show that Staged-Competence (1) matches baseline safety with only 75% of the training data and (2) yields better separation between safe and unsafe responses. Staged-Competence is agnostic to the policy optimisation loss and can extend to other DPO variants and alignment domains. Our code and data are available at https://github.com/Sandeep5500/curriculum-learning-for-safety.

AI.tldr: Staged-Competence uses curriculum learning to reorder DPO preference data by difficulty and update the reference model progressively, improving safety robustness and data efficiency across model families while maintaining capabilities.
AI.motivation: DPO-based safety alignment is brittle and poorly generalizes to out-of-distribution (OOD) or jailbreak attacks; a curriculum approach could shape learning dynamics and reduce over-refusal while maintaining safety.
AI.method: Introduce Staged-Competence: (1) organize preference data by difficulty, (2) apply competence-based sampling, (3) progressively update the reference model during training; evaluate across three model families and compare with baseline DPO on OOD harms, jailbreak attack success, and general capabilities.
AI.result: OOD harmful responses reduced by 16% and jailbreak successes by 20%; near-zero over-refusal; matches baseline safety with 75% of training data; better separation between safe and unsafe responses; agnostic to policy optimisation loss and extendable to other DPO variants; code/data available.
AI.conclusion: Staged-Competence provides a data-efficient, robust, curriculum-based framework for safety alignment with DPO that generalizes across models and alignment domains and improves safety metrics without sacrificing capabilities.

title: Mind the Tool Failures: Achieving Synergistic Tool Gains for Medical Agents
summary: Medical AI agents increasingly use external tools for diagnosis, treatment recommendation, and evidence retrieval, yet most existing approaches assume that task-appropriate tools are reliable within their intended scope. This assumption is fragile in real clinical settings, where even relevant tools may fail on challenging instances and lead to unsafe downstream decisions. To address this issue, we study medical tool use under imperfect-tool settings to correct failure instances missed by individual tools. Instance-dependent failure patterns create a gap between the best fixed single tool and an ideal instance-wise selector, which we refer to as the Single-Oracle risk gap. The core challenge is that conventional task-level tool selection cannot realize this gap, as it is inherently bounded by the performance of the best single tool. Motivated by this observation, we therefore account for instance-level heterogeneity and formulate tool use as an instance-level selection problem. Particularly, we propose a GRPO-based reinforcement learning framework with rewards for probabilistic risk minimization and disagreement-aware synergy learning, which promotes instance-level correction of erroneous tool consensus. Furthermore, an entropy-guided sampling strategy is adopted to upweight high-disagreement instances, which provide stronger signals for learning instance-specific tool synergy. These two components complement each other in mitigating instance-level heterogeneity and improving tool synergy. Experiments on two tasks and seven medical benchmarks show that our method consistently achieves robust and stable improvements over a broad range of baselines, highlighting the importance of synergy-aware tool use for reliable medical agentic systems.

AI.tldr: Proposes instance-level, synergy-aware tool use for medical AI to overcome imperfect external tools; introduces a GRPO-based RL framework with entropy-guided sampling to correct erroneous tool consensus.
AI.motivation: In real clinical settings, external medical tools can fail on challenging cases, causing unsafe downstream decisions. A fixed best tool is insufficient; there is an instance-level performance gap (Single-Oracle risk gap) between any fixed tool and an ideal instance-wise selector.
AI.method: Develops a GRPO-based reinforcement learning framework that uses rewards focused on probabilistic risk minimization and incorporates disagreement-aware synergy learning to correct erroneous tool consensus at the instance level. An entropy-guided sampling strategy upweights high-disagreement instances to improve learning of tool synergy.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations on two tasks and seven medical benchmarks show robust and stable improvements over a broad range of baselines, indicating the importance of synergy-aware, instance-level tool use for reliable medical agentic systems.
AI.conclusion: Instance-level, synergy-aware tool selection with entropy-guided emphasis on hard cases improves robustness and safety of medical AI systems by mitigating heterogeneous tool performance.

title: FineVLA: Fine-Grained Instruction Alignment for Steerable Vision-Language-Action Policies
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are increasingly expected to not only complete robot tasks, but also follow human instructions about how those tasks should be executed. However, existing robot datasets usually pair trajectories with coarse goal-level language, leaving execution-critical details such as active arm, approach direction, and contact region unspecified. This limits steerable policy learning and robotic video understanding. We introduce FineVLA, an open framework for action-aligned fine-grained VLA supervision. The framework includes: (1) a data construction tool that unifies 972,247 trajectories across 85K tasks from 10 open-source robot datasets and builds FineVLA-Data, a human-verified dataset of 47,159 fine-grained trajectories; (2) a held-out benchmark with 500 videos, 10,816 atomic facts, and 1,030 VQA questions; (3) a robotics-specialized VLM annotator for scalable fine-grained annotation; and (4) a steerable VLA policy trained with controlled mixtures of fine-grained and raw goal-level instructions. Our experiments yield three findings. First, fine-grained supervision does not sacrifice goal-level success: FG-only improves over Raw-only by +1.4 to +8.1 success-rate points across settings. Second, fine-grained and raw instructions are complementary, following a consistent inverted-U trend peaking at FG:Raw = 1:2 to 1:1. The best mixed setting reaches 86.8%/82.5% in RoboTwin simulation and 62.7/100 in real-world dual-arm manipulation (vs. 49.9 Raw-only). Third, fine-grained supervision improves steerable control: the largest real-world gains appear on pose (+23), color (+18), and approach direction (+18)--factors where goal-level instructions provide no guidance. Overall, fine-grained language should augment goal-level instructions: specifying how to execute alongside what to achieve. Project page: https://finevla.xlang.ai/

AI.tldr: FineVLA provides fine-grained, action-aligned supervision for vision-language-action (VLA) in robotics, unifying 972k trajectories into a 47k-trajectory dataset, coupled with a 500-video benchmark, VLM annotation tools, and steerable policies that blend fine-grained and raw goal-level instructions; results show fine-grained signals improve execute-level control and complement goal directives.
AI.motivation: Existing robot datasets pair trajectories with coarse goal-level language, missing execution-critical details (e.g., active arm, approach direction, contact region), hindering steerable policy learning and robotic video understanding.
AI.method: 1) Data construction that unifies large-scale trajectories into FineVLA-Data; 2) a held-out benchmark with videos, facts, and VQA questions; 3) a robotics-specialized VLM annotator for scalable fine-grained annotation; 4) training a steerable VLA policy with mixtures of fine-grained and raw goal-level instructions.
AI.result: Fine-grained supervision (FG) improves over Raw-only in success rates; FG:Raw mixture follows an inverted-U, with peak performance around FG:Raw=1:2 to 1:1; best mix yields high sim and real-world scores, outperforming Raw-only; FG signals yield substantial gains in pose, color, and approach direction.
AI.conclusion: Fine-grained language should augment, not replace, goal-level instructions, enabling richer execution guidance without sacrificing task success.

title: Underwater360: Reconstructing Underwater Scenes from Panoramic Images with Omnidirectional Gaussian Splatting
summary: Underwater scene reconstruction is essential for immersive exploration of aquatic environments, yet remains challenging due to complex participating-media effects such as absorption and scattering, as well as the limited field of view (FoV) of conventional cameras. Although combining panoramic imaging with 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) offers a promising direction for photorealistic underwater rendering, traditional 3DGS struggles with both spherical projection distortion and underwater medium degradation. In this paper, we propose \textbf{Underwater360}, a physics-informed omnidirectional 3DGS framework for underwater panoramic scene reconstruction. First, we introduce an Omnidirectional Gaussian Splatting module that performs ray casting directly in spherical camera space instead of relying on 2D projection approximations, thereby reducing geometric distortions under 360$^\circ$ FoV. Second, we design a physics-based appearance-medium modeling architecture with pose-conditioned appearance embeddings to explicitly decouple intrinsic scene radiance from depth-dependent backscatter and attenuation, enabling physically grounded scene appearance restoration. Finally, we establish a new panoramic underwater benchmark dataset containing both synthetic and real-world scenes. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Underwater360 achieves superior performance in underwater novel view synthesis and scene appearance restoration, delivering improved rendering quality and cross-view consistency in complex underwater environments. The code and datasets are released at https://github.com/SwcK423/Underwater360

AI.tldr: Introduces Underwater360, an omnidirectional 3DGaussian Splatting framework for underwater panoramas that reduces projection distortion and models medium effects physically, enabling superior novel-view rendering and appearance restoration; accompanies a new panoramic underwater dataset and code release.
AI.motivation: Underwater imaging is hindered by absorption, scattering, and restricted FoV; conventional 3DGaussian Splatting with panoramas suffers distortion and medium degradation, necessitating a physics-informed, wide-FoV reconstruction approach.
AI.method: An Omnidirectional Gaussian Splatting module that ray casts directly in spherical camera space to minimize 360° distortion; a physics-based appearance–medium model with pose-conditioned appearance embeddings to decouple radiance from depth-dependent backscatter and attenuation; creation of a panoramic underwater benchmark dataset; extensive experiments on novel view synthesis and appearance restoration.
AI.result: Demonstrates superior rendering quality and cross-view consistency for underwater scenes, with improved novel-view synthesis and appearance restoration compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: Underwater360 enables accurate, physically grounded panoramic underwater reconstruction by combining distortion-reducing omnidirectional splatting with a physics-based appearance model, and provides public datasets and code for benchmarking.

title: Towards Feedback-to-Plan Decisions for Self-Evolving LLM Agents in CUDA Kernel Generation
summary:
Large language models (LLMs) have shown strong empirical gains as self-evolving agents for CUDA kernel generation, driven by feedback-conditioned planning across generations. However, how planning decisions attribute and combine heterogeneous feedback signals remains opaque. Standard end-to-end ablations fail to resolve this question, as iterative planning amplifies early perturbations and conflates feedback effects with trajectory-dependent drift.
  We introduce \texttt{CUDAnalyst}, a unified analysis layer for controlled, generation-level attribution of planning decisions to feedback components via trajectory freezing and selective feedback injection. \texttt{CUDAnalyst} enables stable generation-level evaluation and principled coalitional-style attribution of feedback effects and interactions. Our results show that explicit planning is beneficial only when feedback is aligned, that effective planning emerges from structured multi-feedback interactions, and that high-level plans from stronger reasoning models can partially transfer to weaker ones. These trends hold across reference backbones, representative workloads, and reference induction regimes, indicating that the identified feedback-to-plan structure is robust within the controlled axes studied.

AI.tldr: CUDAnalyst provides a generation-level attribution framework to dissect how feedback signals shape planning in LLM-driven CUDA kernel generation, via trajectory freezing and feedback injection, enabling stable evaluation and coalitional attribution.
AI.motivation: Understanding how heterogeneous feedback signals influence planning is crucial for robust LLM-based code synthesis; end-to-end ablations are confounded by trajectory drift.
AI.method: Introduce CUDAnalyst, a unified analysis layer enabling generation-level attribution through trajectory freezing and selective feedback injection; coalitional-style attribution; evaluation across backbones, workloads, regimes.
AI.result: Explicit planning is beneficial only when feedback is aligned; planning emerges from structured multi-feedback interactions; high-level plans from stronger models partially transfer to weaker ones; robust across conditions.
AI.conclusion: Feedback-to-plan structure is robust; CUDAnalyst provides stable evaluation and insight into feedback interactions; suggests design principles for LLM-driven kernel generation and future research on multi-feedback integration.

title: Riding the Shifting Potential: When Reactive Control Suffices for Multi-Goal Behavior
summary: Reactive control is often considered insufficient for multi-objective tasks because conflicting objectives give rise to local minima. We argue this limitation is not inherent but arises from static encodings that fail to reflect how objectives currently interact. We exploit the interaction structure encoded in a graph-based world model by extending it with nullspace projections: conflicts are resolved where they arise by projecting lower-priority gradients into the nullspace of higher-priority ones, with priorities determined continuously from the current state. We demonstrate this in two domains where conflicts between objectives are central: navigation around non-convex obstacles, where static potential fields fundamentally fail, and planar pushing of non-convex objects, where our method achieves $100\%$ success across one-hundred configurations versus $0\%$ for the steepest-descent baseline and ${\sim}55\%$ for diffusion policy, without demonstrations or retraining. The same formulation transfers directly to a real robot with additional perceptual and kinematic constraints, accommodating them through the same mechanism.

AI.tldr: State-aware, nullspace-projected multi-objective control on a graph-based world model resolves conflicts between objectives, overcoming local minima in non-convex navigation and planar pushing; achieves strong improvements and near-perfect success on tested tasks, transferable to real robots.
AI.motivation: Reactive control often gets trapped in local minima due to static, conflicting objective encodings. By making objective priorities and interactions state-dependent and resolving conflicts with nullspace projections, expected behavior reflects current task interactions.
AI.method: Extend a graph-based world model with nullspace projections. Compute continuous, state-dependent priorities and project lower-priority gradients into the nullspace of higher-priority ones, resolving conflicts as they arise. Validate on two domains—navigation around non-convex obstacles and planar pushing of non-convex objects—and demonstrate transfer to real robots with perceptual and kinematic constraints using the same mechanism.
AI.result: Non-convex obstacle navigation: static potential fields fail. Planar pushing of non-convex objects: 100% success across 100 configurations, vs 0% for steepest-descent baseline and ~55% for diffusion policy, with no demonstrations or retraining. The approach transfers to real robots with perceptual/kinematic constraints.
AI.conclusion: The presented framework reframes reactive control limitations as a consequence of static encodings. A state-aware, nullspace-based projection approach on a graph-based world model provides a general, transferable mechanism for resolving objective conflicts in real-time.

title: Cross-scale Aligned Supervision for Training GANs
summary: Modern GANs often introduce adversarial supervision on intermediate generator outputs and interpret the resulting multi-stage synthesis as coarse-to-fine hierarchical generation. In this work, we challenge this interpretation. We argue that standard scale-wise adversarial supervision does not construct a proper coarse-to-fine hierarchy: each intermediate image is independently pushed toward the real distribution at its own resolution, but this scale-wise realism does not ensure that outputs across stages represent the identical generated sample. Moreover, the scale-specific image produced at each stage is not used as an explicit refinement target for the subsequent stage. Therefore, its adversarial loss can improve a scale-specific output without constraining later stages to preserve the same sample trajectory, allowing them to move toward a different sample rather than refine the previous output. We refer to this problem as a cross-scale trajectory misalignment problem. To resolve it, we propose CAT, a Cross-scale Aligned Transformer for multi-scale adversarial generation. CAT keeps the discriminator scale-wise, so each intermediate output is evaluated at its own resolution, while adding a simple generator-side consistency regularization that aligns intermediate outputs with the final output. On class-conditional ImageNet-256, CAT-H/2 achieves an FID-50K of 1.56 with one-step inference after only 60 training epochs, outperforming strong one-step GAN and diffusion/flow baselines.

AI.tldr: Cross-scale trajectory misalignment in multi-scale GANs; propose CAT (Cross-scale Aligned Transformer) with scale-wise discriminators and generator-side consistency regularization to align intermediate outputs with the final output; achieves strong FID on ImageNet-256 (CAT-H/2, FID-50K 1.56) with one-step inference after 60 epochs.
AI.motivation: Standard scale-wise adversarial supervision does not guarantee the same sample progression across scales; intermediate outputs are optimized independently, risking trajectory misalignment.
AI.method: Introduce CAT: keep discriminators at each scale; add generator-side consistency regularization to align intermediate outputs with final output; uses transformer-based cross-scale alignment; evaluate on ImageNet-256.
AI.result: CAT-H/2 achieves FID-50K of 1.56 with one-step inference after 60 epochs, outperforming strong one-step GANs and diffusion/flow baselines.
AI.conclusion: Addressing cross-scale misalignment improves multi-scale GAN generation; explicit cross-scale alignment yields better sample quality with potentially faster inference.

title: Semigroup Consistency as a Diagnostic for Learned Physics Simulators
comment: 10 pages, 3 figures, 3 tables. Accepted to the AI4Physics Workshop at the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning
summary: Learned physics simulators are often evaluated by one-step or short-horizon prediction error, but these metrics can miss failures in temporal composition and long-horizon rollout. For autonomous, state-complete systems, exact solution maps satisfy a semigroup law: direct evolution over $s+t$ should agree with evolution over $s$ followed by $t$. We propose normalized semigroup error as a post hoc, model-agnostic diagnostic comparing these direct and composed learned predictions. On one-dimensional heat and Burgers dynamics with time-conditioned ConvNet and FNO baselines, semigroup error is positively associated with rollout degradation, with trajectory-level Spearman correlation $ρ= 0.635$ and $95%$ CI $[0.621, 0.649]$. Semigroup regularization has mixed effects, supporting semigroup consistency primarily as an evaluation diagnostic rather than a universally beneficial training objective.

AI.tldr: Normalized semigroup error as a model-agnostic diagnostic for learned physics simulators; compares direct vs composed predictions to assess long-horizon consistency; shows positive association with rollout degradation; semigroup regularization has mixed effects and is more suitable as evaluation diagnostic than universal training objective.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon consistency is not captured by short-horizon prediction error. For exact solution maps, evolution over s+t should equal composition of s and t. The metric aims to detect temporal composition failures in learned simulators.
AI.method: Compute a direct prediction for horizon s+t and compare it to the composed prediction (prediction for s followed by t) using time-conditioned ConvNet and Fourier Neural Operator baselines on 1D heat and Burgers dynamics; normalize the error to obtain semigroup error; evaluate its relationship with rollout degradation.
AI.result: Semigroup error correlates with rollout degradation: Spearman ρ=0.635 with 95% CI [0.621, 0.649]. Semigroup regularization shows mixed effects and is not universally beneficial, functioning better as an evaluation diagnostic than as a training objective.
AI.conclusion: Normalization of semigroup consistency provides a useful, model-agnostic diagnostic for long-horizon reliability of learned simulators. Regularizing toward semigroup consistency yields mixed results and should be used primarily for evaluation rather than as a universal training objective.

title: It's Not the Capability: Harness Sensitivity Is Non-Monotone Across LLM Agent Tiers
summary:
A prevalent assumption in LLM agent deployment holds that more structured harnesses universally improve
  reliability, and that higher-capability models need proportionally less structural guidance -- together
  implying a monotone inverse relationship between model capability tier and optimal harness complexity. We
  test this hypothesis through a controlled 432-run experiment crossing six models across four capability
  tiers with three harness conditions (light, balanced, strict) on HEAT-24, a 24-task synthetic benchmark
  with git-based workspace verification. Our results refute the monotone inverse relationship on two
  fronts. First, for the frontier chat model evaluated (Gemini 2.5 Flash), increased harness verbosity
  lowers VTSR by 29-38 percentage points -- a harness-complexity paradox. Second, for the frontier
  reasoning model evaluated (Qwen3.5-122B, extended thinking enabled), strict harness achieves the highest
  VTSR (91.7%) and the lowest latency, the opposite of the prediction. Within the constrained tier, a 2B
  model (Gemma4:e2B) matches strong-open-tier stability at 91.7% across all harnesses. Because each tier is
  represented by a single model in this study, these results should be interpreted as model-specific
  observations; harness sensitivity appears non-monotone across the models evaluated, and depends
  critically on model type (chat vs. reasoning). We introduce a six-label failure taxonomy showing that
  format_violation dominates capable-model failures while wrong_file dominates low-capability failures, and
  we derive practical tier-aware harness selection guidelines.

AI.tldr: The paper challenges the assumption that stronger models require less structural guidance, showing non-monotone effects of harness complexity across models and tasks, introducing a six-label failure taxonomy and tier-aware harness guidelines.
AI.motivation: Test the prevailing belief that higher-capability LLMs need proportionally less structural guidance and that harness complexity should inversely relate to model capability, using a controlled, multi-model, multi-harness experimental setup on a synthetic benchmark.
AI.method: A 432-run experiment across six models spanning four capability tiers, with three harness conditions (light, balanced, strict) evaluated on HEAT-24 (24 tasks with git-based workspace verification). Measured outcomes include VTSR (valid task success rate) and latency; supplemented by a failure taxonomy.
AI.result: Results refute the monotone inverse relationship. For frontier chat model Gemini 2.5 Flash, stricter/higher harness verbosity lowers VTSR by 29–38 percentage points (harness-complexity paradox). For frontier reasoning model Qwen3.5-122B with extended thinking, strict harness yields the highest VTSR (91.7%) and lowest latency. Within constrained tier, 2B Gemma4:e2B attains 91.7% stability across all harnesses. Findings are model-specific and non-monotone, depending on model type (chat vs. reasoning). A six-label failure taxonomy is introduced, showing format_violation dominates capable-model failures while wrong_file dominates low-capability failures; practical tier-aware harness guidelines are proposed.
AI.conclusion: Harness sensitivity is non-monotone and model-type dependent; universal claims about harness complexity vs. model capability are unsupported. Harness selection should be tier-aware and tailored to model type, with explicit attention to failure modes and governance for stability across deployment tiers.

title: Sparse-LiDAR Prompting of Monocular Geometry Foundations: An Empirical Study Toward Long-Range Driving Depth
summary: Sparse-LiDAR-prompted depth foundation models (PromptDA, Prior Depth Anything, DMD3C) have shown strong results on indoor scenes or within KITTI's standard 80-meter evaluation cap. However, two limitations remain: (i) systematic distance-stratified evaluation in long-range driving regimes (50-150 m) is largely absent; (ii) prior approaches built on disparity-based foundations rely on pre-interpolated dense priors, leaving truly sparse LiDAR injection on point-map foundations (e.g., MoGe-2, NeurIPS 2025) unexplored. We present SLIM (Sparse-LiDAR Injected Monocular geometry), the first adaptation of MoGe-2 to accept truly sparse LiDAR input. SLIM integrates a partial-convolution sparse encoder with a multi-scale fusion neck that fuses LiDAR features into the point-map decoder at five scales. We adopt density-agnostic training (random injection ratio in [0.005, 0.30]) so a single model serves diverse input densities. On Virtual KITTI and CARLA, SLIM reduces the absolute relative error of the MoGe-2 baseline by approximately 39-51% at 100-150 m. Ablation across six injection ratios shows partial-convolution injection improves both AbsRel and RMSE on Virtual KITTI in all six settings; on CARLA, AbsRel improves in five of six settings (one near-tie at 0.015 differs by 0.0013), and RMSE is comparable across encoders, with partial-convolution improving in three settings (by up to 0.31 unit) and losing by at most 0.11 unit in the other three.

AI.tldr: SLIM adapts MoGe-2 to truly sparse LiDAR inputs, achieving large long-range depth accuracy gains and density-robust performance via partial-convolution encoding and multi-scale LiDAR-to-point-map fusion, outperforming the baseline by ~40-50% AbsRel at 100-150 m.
AI.motivation: Two key gaps: (i) lack of systematic distance-stratified evaluation in long-range driving regimes (50-150 m); (ii) prior disparity-based methods rely on pre-interpolated dense priors, leaving truly sparse LiDAR injection on point-map foundations underexplored.
AI.method: Adapt MoGe-2 with a partial-convolution sparse encoder and a multi-scale fusion neck that injects LiDAR features into the point-map decoder at five scales; train density-agnostically with random LiDAR injection ratio in [0.005, 0.30], enabling a single model to handle diverse input densities.
AI.result: On Virtual KITTI and CARLA, SLIM reduces the MoGe-2 baseline AbsRel by ~39-51% at 100-150 m. Ablation over six injection ratios shows partial-convolution injection improves AbsRel and RMSE on Virtual KITTI in all six settings; on CARLA AbsRel improves in five of six settings (near-tie at 0.015 with a 0.0013 difference); RMSE is comparable across encoders, with partial-convolution improving in three settings (up to 0.31) and decreasing by at most 0.11 in the other three.
AI.conclusion: SLIM demonstrates effective, density-robust sparse-LiDAR injection into point-map depth models, delivering strong long-range performance gains and consistent improvements with partial-convolution across diverse evaluation densities; points to viability of truly sparse LiDAR fusion in depth foundation models.

title: Reparametrizing Shampoo and SOAP for Subspace Basis Updates and BFloat16 Storage
summary: Shampoo-based methods, such as KL-Shampoo and SOAP, have demonstrated strong performance in training neural networks and rely on QR decomposition. Because existing QR implementations require single-precision (FP32) arithmetic and remain computationally expensive, these methods become time- and memory-intensive when their preconditioning matrices are large. Moreover, using BFloat16 (BFP16) storage to reduce memory usage can degrade the performance of Shampoo-based methods. We propose a reparametrization of the preconditioner that supports BFP16 storage and forms a complete basis by combining updated basis vectors with unchanged ones. By updating only part of the basis through QR decomposition in a subspace, our approach reduces computational overhead while mitigating the performance degradation caused by BFP16 storage. Our approach applies broadly to Shampoo-based methods that employ QR decomposition, including KL-Shampoo, SOAP, and KL-SOAP. In particular, it improves the performance of SOAP and KL-SOAP under BFP16 storage, enabling KL-SOAP to match or exceed KL-Shampoo. Overall, our approach makes Shampoo-based methods more memory- and time-efficient.

AI.tldr: Proposes a memory/time-efficient reparameterization for Shampoo-based preconditioners that supports BFP16 by updating a subspace of the QR basis, improving SOAP/KL-SOAP performance and enabling parity or superiority to KL-Shampoo.
AI.motivation: QR-based Shampoo methods are computationally and memory-intensive for large preconditioners; BFP16 storage degrades performance; need a method that maintains accuracy while reducing overhead and is compatible with existing Shampoo variants.
AI.method: Reparameterize the preconditioner by forming a complete basis from updated and unchanged vectors; update only a portion of the basis via QR decomposition in a subspace to lower cost; applicable to KL-Shampoo, SOAP, KL-SOAP.
AI.result: Reduces computational overhead and mitigates degradation from BFP16 storage; improves SOAP and KL-SOAP performance under BFP16, with KL-SOAP matching or surpassing KL-Shampoo; overall gains in memory and time efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The approach is broadly applicable to QR-based Shampoo methods, enabling more efficient training with BFP16 without sacrificing performance.

title: A Dataset of Robot-Patient and Doctor-Patient Medical Dialogues for Spoken Language Processing Tasks
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have brought huge improvements to Artificial Intelligence (AI), which can be applied to general-purpose tasks. However, their application to textual or spoken medical consultations is still an open research problem. This paper proposes MeDial-Speech, a novel speech dataset for training and evaluating Med-AIs that can carry out consultations with patients. It was collected in realistic environments from robot-patient and doctor-patient dialogues, contains 111+ hours of speech data (without data augmentation), and covers four health conditions: Lewy body dementia, heart failure, shoulder pain, and angina. In addition, we propose a dialogue benchmark via sentence selection (with 20 options) to evaluate three state-of-the-art LLMs: GPT-5 mini, DeepSeek-V3, and Claude Sonnet 4. Experimental results reveal that Claude Sonnet 4 is the best in sentence selection, with 71.1% accuracy using manual transcriptions and 74.7% using automatic transcriptions, and that all LLMs are highly overconfident in their probabilistic predictions, regardless of selecting correct or incorrect sentences in medical dialogues. This dataset is free of charge for non-commercial purposes at: https://huggingface.co/datasets/hcuayahu/MeDial-Speech

AI.tldr: Introduces MeDial-Speech, a 111+ hour speech dataset for medical AI dialogue in realistic settings (robot-patient and doctor-patient conversations) across four conditions, plus a 20-option sentence-selection benchmark to evaluate LLMs. Claude Sonnet 4 performs best (71.1% manual, 74.7% automatic), while all models show high overconfidence. Dataset is freely available for non-commercial use.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of realistic, speech-based medical dialogue data to train and evaluate Med-AI systems capable of performing consultations, bridging the gap between static text benchmarks and spoken clinical interactions.
AI.method: Collect 111+ hours of robot-patient and doctor-patient dialogues in four health conditions (Lewy body dementia, heart failure, shoulder pain, angina); propose a sentence-selection dialogue benchmark with 20 options to assess three LLMs (GPT-5 mini, DeepSeek-V3, Claude Sonnet 4) using both manual and automatic transcripts.
AI.result: Claude Sonnet 4 achieved highest sentence-selection accuracy: 71.1% with manual transcripts and 74.7% with automatic transcripts; all LLMs exhibit high overconfidence in probabilistic predictions regardless of sentence correctness; dataset is accessible at HuggingFace for non-commercial use.
AI.conclusion: The dataset provides a valuable resource for evaluating Med-AI dialogue capabilities in realistic spoken contexts and highlights calibration challenges in LLMs; future work should expand condition coverage, diversify dialogue settings, and adopt additional evaluation metrics beyond sentence-selection accuracy.

title: Trust Region Q Adjoint Matching
summary: Off-policy reinforcement learning of pretrained flow policies remains challenging due to the instability of optimization arising from the multi-step sampling process. Recently, Q-learning with Adjoint Matching (QAM) addressed this issue by reformulating into a memoryless stochastic optimal control (SOC) problem with a learned critic. However, QAM inherits a fundamental fragility of critic-guided improvement: small critic errors are amplified when critics are ill-conditioned, often leading to model collapse. This paper introduces Trust Region Q-Adjoint Matching (TRQAM), a stable off-policy fine-tuning algorithm that adaptively controls the path-space KL with pretrained flow policies through projected dual descent. Specifically, we optimize the trust-region parameter $λ$ in SOC dynamics, and theoretically show that the path-space KL can be represented by a closed-form function of $λ$. As a result, our method can precisely control the exact deviation from pretrained flow policies, achieving stable off-policy RL. Through experiments on 50 OGBench tasks, TRQAM consistently outperforms prior arts in both offline RL and offline-to-online RL. In particular, TRQAM achieves an overall success rate of 68% in offline RL, substantially improves the strongest baseline at 46%.

AI.tldr: TRQAM is a stable off-policy fine-tuning algorithm for pretrained flow policies that optimizes a path-space KL via a λ-controlled stochastic optimal control formulation, yielding improved stability and performance on 50 OGBench tasks (offline RL and offline-to-online RL).
AI.motivation: Off-policy RL with pretrained flow policies is unstable due to multi-step sampling and brittle critics; a robust, policy-constrained fine-tuning method is needed.
AI.method: Introduce TRQAM, which adaptively controls the path-space KL through projected dual descent by optimizing the SOC dynamics parameter λ. The path-space KL has a closed-form representation in λ, allowing precise constraint of deviation from pretrained policies.
AI.result: TRQAM consistently outperforms prior methods on 50 OGBench tasks, achieving an offline RL success rate of 68%, substantially higher than the strongest baseline at 46%.
AI.conclusion: TRQAM provides a stable, controllable off-policy RL framework for pretrained policies by directly constraining policy deviation via λ in the SOC-derived Q-Adjoint Matching formulation; it improves offline and offline-to-online RL performance on large benchmarks.

title: AnchorDiff: Training-Free Concept Grounding for MM-DiTs via Anchor-Based Graph Propagation
summary: Multi-Modal Diffusion Transformers (MM-DiTs) encode rich representations for training-free concept grounding, but existing attention-based methods often produce overlapping activations on visually confusable concepts, a failure mode we call concept leakage, where target responses spill over to non-target objects. To address this issue, we propose AnchorDiff, a training-free grounding method that decouples semantic localization from structural refinement. AnchorDiff selects a high-confidence anchor from concept-to-image attention map and propagates it as a one-hot seed over a hybrid graph derived from image-to-image self-attention. The graph uses output-space similarity for dense within-object propagation and a row-wise attention gate to suppress cross-object connections. Additionally, we introduce the Multi-Concept Confusion Dataset, which contains images with multiple visually similar concepts and separate masks, enabling explicit evaluation of concept leakage. Experiments show that AnchorDiff achieves strong grounding performance on ImageNet-Segmentation and PascalVOC, while substantially reducing concept leakage on our Multi-Concept Confusion Dataset.

AI.tldr: AnchorDiff provides a training-free grounding method that reduces concept leakage in multi-modal diffusion transformers by decoupling semantic localization from structural refinement via anchor-based propagation on a hybrid graph; introduces a Multi-Concept Confusion Dataset for explicit leakage evaluation, achieving strong grounding on standard benchmarks and substantially lower leakage on the new dataset.
AI.motivation: Address concept leakage in attention-based visual grounding where target concepts bleed into visually confusable non-targets, particularly in multi-modal diffusion models that enable training-free grounding.
AI.method: (A) Identify a high-confidence anchor from the concept-to-image attention map. (B) Propagate this anchor as a one-hot seed over a hybrid image graph built from image-to-image self-attention. (C) Use output-space similarity for dense within-object propagation. (D) Apply a row-wise attention gate to suppress cross-object connections. (E) Train-free grounding process: no task-specific fine-tuning. (F) Create and utilize the Multi-Concept Confusion Dataset with images containing multiple visually similar concepts and separate masks for explicit leakage evaluation.
AI.result: AnchorDiff achieves strong grounding performance on ImageNet-Segmentation and Pascal VOC. It substantially reduces concept leakage on the proposed Multi-Concept Confusion Dataset compared to baseline methods.
AI.conclusion: Training-free AnchorDiff effectively decouples semantic localization from structural refinement, improving grounding accuracy while mitigating leakage in visually-confusable scenarios; the new dataset enables explicit evaluation of concept leakage and supports robust benchmarking.

title: QAM-W: Joint 2D Codebook Quantization for LLM Weights via Hadamard Rotation and Activation-Aware Scaling
summary: Scalar post-training quantizers discard pairwise coordinate structure within weight rows. We introduce QAM-W (Quadrature Amplitude Modulation for Weights), a codec that recovers this structure: each row is L2-normalized, block-Hadamard rotated, paired into 2D coordinates, and quantized against a single Lloyd-Max codebook trained on the unit circular Gaussian, with activation-aware per-channel scaling. In a cross-model study spanning five LLMs from four families (1.1B--13B parameters) and eight quantized configurations, the activation-aware variant at $\approx 5.5$ bpw stays within $\pm 0.4\%$ of BF16 WikiText-2 perplexity on every model, matching the SmoothQuant W8A8 quality envelope at $32\%$ fewer weight bits. Joint 2D coding outperforms polar (amplitude $\times$ phase) coding by 2--15~pp $Δ$PPL at equal bitrate, and paired KL against BF16 tracks $Δ$PPL\% at Spearman $ρ= 0.99$ across 37 (method, model) rows, consistent with a monotone composite bound from codec distortion to KL divergence. A 3.5~bpw variant is competitive on quantization-tolerant architectures. At strict 4~bpw, the rotated-codebook frontier method QTIP outperforms QAM-W; the contribution is the quality-preserving 5--6~bpw band.

AI.tldr: QAM-W recovers pairwise weight-structure lost in scalar post-training quantizers by normalizing rows, applying a block-Hadamard rotation, pairing into 2D coordinates, and quantizing with a Lloyd-Max codebook trained on unit circular Gaussian, with activation-aware per-channel scaling; evaluated on five LLMs across multiple configurations, achieving near-BF16 perplexity at ~5.5 bpw and surpassing alternatives in 2D coding while revealing a 4 bpw regime where QTIP dominates.
AI.motivation: Conventional scalar post-training quantizers destroy pairwise coordinate structure within weight rows, degrading model quality. The work seeks to preserve this structure via a 2D coding scheme (QAM-W) to improve rate-distortion performance and maintain perplexity close to BF16 with fewer bits.
AI.method: Row-wise L2 normalization; block-Hadamard rotation; pair into 2D coordinates; quantize against a single Lloyd-Max codebook trained on a unit circular Gaussian; incorporate activation-aware per-channel scaling; evaluate across five LLMs (1.1B–13B) with eight quantized configurations; compare to SmoothQuant W8A8 and polar/ KL baselines; examine 5.5 and 3.5 bpw variants, and 4 bpw with QTIP.
AI.result: Activation-aware QAM-W at ~5.5 bpw maintains perplexity within ±0.4% of BF16 WikiText-2 across all models and matches SmoothQuant W8A8 at 32% fewer weight bits; 2D coding outperforms polar coding by 2–15 pp ΔPPL at equal bitrate; paired KL vs BF16 tracks ΔPPL% with Spearman ρ = 0.99 across 37 (method, model) combinations; 3.5 bpw is competitive; at 4 bpw, QTIP (rotated-codebook frontier) outperforms QAM-W, indicating a quality-preserving 5–6 bpw band.
AI.conclusion: QAM-W enables compact, quality-preserving 2D weight quantization by reintroducing structural information through rotation and pairing, achieving strong rate-distortion performance and KL consistency across models; it occupies a 5–6 bpw band for high-quality quantization, with QTIP taking the lead at 4 bpw.

title: Beyond a Single Direction: Chain-of-Thought Disrupts Simple Steering of Refusal
summary: Large reasoning models (LRMs) generate chain-of-thought (CoT) traces before producing final outputs, introducing a dynamic internal state that may complicate control mechanisms such as refusal. Unlike instruction-tuned LLMs, where refusal is mediated by a single directional subspace, refusal in large reasoning models (LRMs) additionally depends on the CoT. In DeepSeek-R1-Distill-LLaMA-8B, activation steering reverses refusal in only 39% of cases when the CoT is kept fixed, but removing the CoT entirely increases this to 70%, indicating that the CoT actively reinforces refusal. In a two-stage intervention where the model regenerates its CoT under activation steering, refusal is reversed in 94% of cases, while the resulting CoT alone retains 48% of this effect even after steering is removed. This suggests that the CoT can carry and reconstruct the compliance signal independently. These findings indicate that refusal in LRMs is jointly encoded in residual stream activations and CoT. This joint activation makes LRM more robust against activation-level interventions alone, but exposes CoT to a possible alternative surface attack.

AI.tldr: Refusal in large reasoning models (LRMs) is encoded jointly in residual activations and the chain-of-thought (CoT). CoT can reinforce or reconstruct compliance; activating steering can obscure refusals, but regenerating CoT under steering recovers most of the effect, revealing CoT as a transferable signal and a potential attack surface.
AI.motivation: To understand how refusal behavior emerges in LRMs during multi-step reasoning and how internal state (residual activations) and chain-of-thought contribute to compliance, with implications for robustness against intervention and safety-alignment strategies.
AI.method: Empirical evaluation on DeepSeek-R1-Distill-LLaMA-8B. The study tests (1) activation steering with fixed CoT vs removed CoT, (2) a two-stage intervention where the model regenerates CoT under steering, and (3) measurement of refusal reversal rates and residual-CoT effects.
AI.result: Refusal reversal rates: 39% with fixed CoT, 70% when CoT is removed. With two-stage intervention and CoT regeneration under steering, refusal reverses in 94% of cases; the regenerated CoT alone retains 48% of this effect after steering removal. CoT can carry and reconstruct the compliance signal.
AI.conclusion: Refusal in LRMs is jointly encoded in residual activations and CoT, making overall resistance to activation-level attacks robust but introducing a vulnerability surface in the CoT that can be exploited to surface-attack or reconstruct compliance signals.

title: YOLO26-RipeLoc Lite: A lightweight architecture for tomato ripeness detection and picking point localization in greenhouse robotic harvesting
summary: In greenhouse tomato production, automated harvesting requires accurate detection of ripe tomatoes, ripeness classification, and precise picking-point localization for robotic end-effectors. This paper proposes YOLO26-RipeLoc Lite, a lightweight deep learning architecture based on YOLO26 for simultaneous detection, ripeness classification, and center-point localization of greenhouse tomatoes. The model introduces three modifications: (1) a Lightweight Feature Pyramid Network (LFPN) with depthwise separable convolutions for efficient multi-scale fusion, (2) a Ripeness-Aware Attention Module (RAAM) with dual pooling and a learnable ripeness bias vector for enhanced color-texture discrimination, and (3) a Compact Detection Head (CDH) with shared convolutions and an integrated center-point regression branch for direct grasp planning. The model is evaluated on a custom dataset of 1,500 images with 6,227 instances (3,566 ripe, 2,661 unripe) from the SILAL greenhouse, Abu Dhabi, UAE. YOLO26-RipeLoc Lite achieves mAP@0.5 of 92.9% (95.2% ripe, 90.6% unripe) with the highest precision (95.2%) among all evaluated architectures using only 2.38M parameters. Post-training BatchNorm pruning at 30% reduces parameters to ~1.8M with negligible accuracy loss. Ablation studies confirm that greenhouse-aware HSV augmentation provides the largest improvement (+2.02 pp mAP@50), backbone freezing achieves peak precision (93.8%), and 3-phase progressive unfreezing yields the best localization quality (mAP@50:95 of 64.6%). Comparisons with YOLOv8n/s, YOLO11n/s, YOLO12n/s, and YOLO26s confirm superior accuracy-efficiency: 2.9 pp higher precision than YOLO12n with 7.0% fewer parameters and integrated center-point localization for robotic end-effector guidance.

AI.tldr: A lightweight, multi-task YOLO-based detector for greenhouse tomatoes that performs detection, ripeness classification, and center-point localization with high accuracy and efficiency; achieves 92.9% mAP@0.5 with 2.38M params, and effective pruning reduces to ~1.8M with minimal accuracy loss.
AI.motivation: Automate ripe-tomato harvesting requiring accurate detection, ripe/unripe classification, and precise grasp point localization; existing models are either heavy or lack integrated center-point regression for end-effector guidance.
AI.method: Build YOLO26-RipeLoc Lite from YOLO26; introduce three innovations: LFPN with depthwise separable convolutions for efficient multi-scale fusion; RAAM using dual pooling and a learnable ripeness bias vector to enhance color-texture discrimination; CDH with shared convolutions and integrated center-point regression for direct grasp planning. Training includes greenhouse-aware HSV augmentation, backbone freezing with 3-phase progressive unfreezing, and post-training BatchNorm pruning (~30%) to reduce parameters.
AI.result: Evaluation on a custom dataset: 1,500 images, 6,227 instances (3,566 ripe, 2,661 unripe). Achieves mAP@0.5 of 92.9% (ripe 95.2%, unripe 90.6%), highest precision 95.2%. Parameter count 2.38M; BN pruning reduces to ~1.8M with negligible accuracy loss. Ablations show greenhouse HSV augmentation yields +2.02 pp mAP@50, backbone freezing peaks at 93.8% precision, and 3-phase progressive unfreezing yields mAP@50:95 of 64.6%. Compared to YOLOv8n/s, YOLO11n/s, YOLO12n/s, and YOLO26s, the method achieves ~2.9 pp higher precision than YOLO12n with ~7% fewer parameters and includes center-point localization for end-effector guidance.
AI.conclusion: YOLO26-RipeLoc Lite provides accurate, efficient simultaneous detection, ripeness classification, and center-point localization suitable for automated greenhouse harvesting, with strong accuracy-efficiency trade-offs and effective augmentation/training strategies; its integrated center-point regression supports practical robotic picking workflows.

title: Triadic Dynamics Aware Diffusion Posterior Sampling for Inverse Problems: Optimizing Guidance and Stochasticity Schedules
summary: Generative posterior sampling using diffusion models has emerged as a dominant paradigm for solving inverse problems in imaging, which usually consists of three main components: data consistency (DC) guidance, classifier-free guidance (CFG) and stochasticity. While prior arts have focused on how to develop each or all components, less attention has given to how to schedule them, leading to heuristically fixed or partially adjusted suboptimal schedules. In this work, we argue that the interactions among all three components in terms of scheduling are crucial for significantly improved performance in solving inverse problems in imaging. Our analysis shows that aggressive CFG early in sampling conflict with DC guidance, while stochasticity brings the trajectory back to higher-probability regions. Based on these findings, we propose Triadic Dynamics Aware Posterior Sampling (TriPS), which reformulates posterior sampling as a time-varying control problem and optimizes schedules following a triadic trend of decreasing DC and stochasticity scales alongside increasing CFG scale. TriPS achieves this through two strategies: template-based search over functional priors for reliable baseline schedules, and Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO)-based reinforcement learning for more flexible temporal curves. Experiments demonstrate TriPS outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in data fidelity and perceptual realism.

AI.tldr: TriPS jointly optimizes the scheduling of data-consistency, classifier-free guidance, and stochasticity in diffusion-based posterior sampling via a time-varying control framework, using template priors and GRPO RL to outperform baselines in data fidelity and perceptual realism.
AI.motivation: Current diffusion-based inverse problem solvers rely on fixed or partially adjusted schedules for DC, CFG, and stochasticity. Interactions among these components during sampling suggest that jointly optimizing their schedules could yield substantial gains in data fidelity and perceptual realism.
AI.method: Reformulate posterior sampling as a time-varying control problem with a triadic trend: decreasing DC and stochasticity scales while increasing CFG scale. Implement two strategies: (1) template-based search over functional priors to obtain reliable baseline schedules, and (2) Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO)-based reinforcement learning to learn flexible temporal curves.
AI.result: Empirical results show TriPS outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in both data fidelity and perceptual realism across inverse imaging tasks.
AI.conclusion: Jointly optimizing the scheduling of DC, CFG, and stochasticity under a triadic dynamic framework yields significant improvements in diffusion-based posterior sampling, with TriPS offering reliable baselines and a learnable policy for flexible schedules.

title: A PAC-Bayesian View of Generalisation for Physics-Informed Machine Learning
summary: Physics-informed machine learning (PIML) integrates mechanistic knowledge, typically in the form of partial differential equations (PDE), into data-driven models. Despite strong empirical performance, its statistical generalisation properties remain poorly understood, particularly in the regression setting with unbounded losses. Existing analyses rely on approximation or stability arguments and do not fully capture how physical structure influences generalisation from finite data. In this work, we develop a PAC-Bayesian framework for PIML that provides high-probability generalisation guarantees in the presence of unbounded losses. We adopt a multi-task perspective that jointly treats data fidelity, PDE residuals, initial and boundary conditions, avoiding the looseness induced by standard union-bound approaches. Our analysis leverages the structure of physics-informed objectives to derive novel bounds where the complexity scales with input-gradient norms of the losses, revealing a direct link between physical regularity and generalisation. We instantiate this framework under Sobolev and Poincaré-type assumptions, yielding two classes of bounds that trade off statistical complexity and smoothness in different regimes. Building on these results, we propose a self-bounding-aware learning algorithm that directly optimises tractable surrogates of the derived bounds, along with a practical procedure to estimate the associated constants in realistic settings. Empirical evaluations on standard PDE benchmarks demonstrate that our bounds are non-vacuous, significantly tighter than union-bound baselines, and can be effectively minimised during training. Overall, our results provide a principled statistical foundation for the generalisation of physics-informed models.

AI.tldr: Develops a PAC-Bayesian framework for physics-informed ML with unbounded losses, linking generalisation to physical regularity; provides Sobolev/Poincaré-based bounds, a self-bounding learning algorithm, and non-vacuous empirical results.
AI.motivation: PI-informed ML integrates PDE structure but lacks principled statistical generalisation guarantees, especially with unbounded losses; need bounds that reflect physical regularity and multi-task objectives.
AI.method: A multi-task PAC-Bayesian analysis that jointly handles data fidelity, PDE residuals, and boundary/initial conditions; derives bounds where complexity scales with input-gradient norms of losses; instantiates under Sobolev and Poincaré-type assumptions; proposes a self-bounding-aware learning algorithm optimizing surrogate bounds and a practical constants-estimation procedure.
AI.result: Derived two classes of non-vacuous bounds that trade statistical complexity and smoothness; bounds tighten compared to union-bound baselines; algorithm successfully minimises the surrogates during training; empirical evaluation on standard PDE benchmarks supports practical relevance.
AI.conclusion: Provides a principled statistical foundation for generalisation in physics-informed models and demonstrates that physical regularity controls generalisation via gradient-norm–dependent complexity terms, with actionable training procedures.

title: The Attribution Blind Spot: Detecting When Language Models Rely on Memory Rather Than Retrieved Context
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation promises to ground language model outputs in external evidence, yet the field has no reliable way to verify whether retrieved context actually governs generation -- a prerequisite for any high-stakes deployment. The standard assumption, that context-consistent output implies context-governed output, breaks when the retrieved document overlaps with the model's pretraining data: the model can produce faithful-looking text entirely from parametric memory, and both pathways yield indistinguishable output. We name this failure the attribution blind spot and introduce Computational Reality Monitoring (CRM) to address it. CRM operationalizes a principle adapted from cognitive science's reality monitoring framework: comparing internal representations with and without context reveals membership-conditioned representational divergence that output-level monitors systematically miss. CRM does not certify which source an individual generation used; it detects whether pretraining exposure leaves a measurable internal trajectory signature, establishing a necessary substrate for source attribution. Across nine model variants spanning three families, this divergence concentrates in architecture-specific layer patterns, receives converging support from block-level noise intervention, and generalizes across tasks and datasets while collapsing on domain-confounded benchmarks. The attribution blind spot is measurable and partially addressable: internal representations carry a diagnostic signal invisible at the output level, establishing a foundation for systems whose internal awareness of evidence provenance governs their external behavior.

AI.tldr: CRM reveals an attribution blind spot in retrieval-augmented generation: outputs can appear context-governed even when internal representations show no context reliance threshold; internal divergence offers a diagnostic signal for source attribution.
AI.motivation: To address the inability to verify whether retrieved context actually governs generation, especially when retrieved documents overlap pretraining data and output looks faithful, compromising high-stakes deployment.
AI.method: Empirical analysis across nine model variants (three families). Compare internal representations with and without retrieved context to detect membership-conditioned divergence. Analyze layer- and block-level patterns, apply block-level noise interventions, and test across multiple tasks/datasets; assess generalization and domain-confounding effects.
AI.result: Found systematic, architecture-specific divergence in internal representations when context is present versus absent, concentrated in certain layers. Divergence generalizes across tasks/datasets but collapses on domain-confounded benchmarks; internal signals persist even when outputs appear context-consistent, and noise interventions modulate the signal.
AI.conclusion: Internal representations carry a diagnostic signal about evidence provenance that is invisible to output-level monitors. This partial addressability provides a foundation for building systems whose internal awareness of evidence provenance can govern external behavior, enabling potential source attribution without certifying a specific source for each generation.

title: FoundObj: Self-supervised Foundation Models as Rewards for Label-free 3D Object Segmentation
comment: ICML 2026. Zihui and Zhixuan are co-first authors. Code and data are available at: https://github.com/vLAR-group/FoundObj
summary: We address the challenging task of 3D object segmentation in complex scene point clouds without relying on any scene-level human annotations during training. Existing methods are typically constrained to identifying simple objects, primarily due to insufficient object priors in the learning process. In this paper, we present FoundObj, a novel framework featuring a superpoint-based object discovery agent that incrementally merges suitable neighboring superpoints, guided by our innovative semantic and geometric reward modules. These modules synergistically leverage semantic and geometric priors from self-supervised 2D/3D foundation models, providing complementary feedback to the object discovery agent and enabling robust identification of multi-class objects through reinforcement learning. Extensive experiments on diverse benchmarks demonstrate that our approach consistently outperforms existing baselines. Notably, our method exhibits strong generalization in zero-shot and long-tail scenarios, underscoring its potential for scalable, label-free 3D object segmentation.

AI.tldr: FoundObj proposes a self-supervised 3D object segmentation framework that discovers and merges superpoints via a reinforcement-learning agent, guided by semantic and geometric rewards from foundation models, enabling zero-shot and long-tail multi-class segmentation without scene-level labels.
AI.motivation: 3D scene point clouds require robust object priors for segmentation, but labeling at scene level is costly and existing methods struggle with complex scenes and multi-class objects. A scalable, label-free approach leveraging self-supervised foundations and weak supervision is needed to improve generalization to zero-shot and long-tail classes.
AI.method: A superpoint-based object discovery agent incrementally merges neighboring superpoints. The agent is guided by two reward modules—semantic and geometric—that exploit priors from self-supervised 2D/3D foundation models. Reinforcement learning drives the discovery/merging process to identify multi-class objects.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on diverse benchmarks show that FoundObj consistently outperforms baselines. It demonstrates strong generalization in zero-shot and long-tail scenarios, indicating robust, scalable, label-free 3D object segmentation.
AI.conclusion: FoundObj introduces a reinforcement-learning–driven, superpoint-based object discovery framework that leverages semantic and geometric priors from foundation models, achieving effective zero-shot and long-tail multi-class 3D segmentation without scene-level annotations and with strong generalization.

title: Comparative Study of Vision-Based Metric Measurement for Large-Scale Planar Scenes
summary: Vision-based metric distance and area measurement remains challenging in large-scale outdoor environments due to long-range sensing, camera zoom, and unstable imaging conditions. This work studies planar metric measurement in a real-world reservoir monitoring scenario using PTZ cameras and compares three representative approaches: geometry-based monocular ranging, image stitching with birds-eye-view transformation, and stereo-based ranging using two jointly calibrated monocular cameras. For monocular ranging, planar localization models are derived from camera geometry and the effect of camera pitch angle is analyzed. Image stitching is investigated for large-area mapping, while a stereo-based scheme is developed for long-range measurement without dedicated stereo hardware. Experiments show clear trade-offs: monocular ranging achieves meter-level accuracy under sufficiently large pitch angles, stereo-based ranging achieves decimeter-level accuracy with reduced sensitivity to pitch variations, and image stitching is effective for small-scale scenes but degrades in stability and scalability as scene size increases.

AI.tldr: Three PTZ-camera-based metric measurement strategies for large-scale outdoor planar scenes are compared: monocular ranging with geometry, BEV image stitching, and stereo ranging with two monocular cameras. Trade-offs: monocular can achieve meter-level accuracy at high pitch, stereo achieves decimeter-level accuracy with less pitch sensitivity, image stitching works for small scales but struggles with stability and scalability.
AI.motivation: To enable reliable planar metric measurements in large outdoor environments where long-range sensing and imaging conditions challenge standard methods.
AI.method: Evaluate three representative approaches: geometry-based monocular ranging using planar localization models and pitch-angle analysis; image stitching with birds-eye-view transformation for large-area mapping; and a stereo-based scheme using two calibrated monocular cameras for long-range measurement without dedicated stereo hardware. Experiments quantify accuracy and robustness under varying pitch and scene sizes.
AI.result: Monocular ranging yields meter-level accuracy with sufficiently large camera pitch; stereo-based ranging achieves decimeter-level accuracy and is less sensitive to pitch variations; image stitching is effective for small-scale scenes but becomes unstable and less scalable as the scene grows.
AI.conclusion: There are clear trade-offs among methods; for large outdoor planar measurements, stereo-based or high-pitch monocular approaches offer better accuracy and robustness, while image stitching suits small areas but is not scalable.

title: MechRL: Reinforcement Learning Agents Perform Circuit Discovery for Mechanistic Interpretability
summary: Mechanistic interpretability has identified small sets of attention heads that implement specific behaviours in transformer language models, but recovering these circuits typically requires a bespoke analytical pipeline for each new task. We recast circuit discovery as a reinforcement-learning problem. An agent operates over the 144 attention heads of GPT-2 small as a discrete action space; each action triggers a zero-ablation and a contrastive reward that subtracts the ablation's damage to general next-token prediction from its damage to the target task. A single PPO policy, trained on two tasks (induction and IOI) in a vectorised multi-task environment, attains the per-episode oracle on both training tasks and on a held-out third task (docstring completion). Its preferred heads coincide with the canonical heads of established literature on precisely the axes those papers identify as causally non-redundant under single-head ablation; the categories they identify as redundant are correctly de-prioritised by the agent. On the held-out task, best-of-five planning recovers 96\% of the oracle ceiling with no task signal supplied at evaluation. These results indicate that reinforcement learning over causal interventions is a viable, transferable substrate for identifying the single-head bottlenecks of mechanistic circuits, complementary to existing path-patching approaches.

AI.tldr: Reinforcement learning over attention head interventions identifies a small, transferable set of causal heads in GPT-2 small, achieving near-oracle performance on multiple tasks and aligning with canonical literature on non-redundant heads; RL-based circuit discovery complements existing patching approaches.
AI.motivation: Mechanistic interpretability seeks minimal causal circuits; bespoke pipelines per task hinder generalization; aim to develop a general RL-based discovery method to recover single-head bottlenecks and evaluate transferability.
AI.method: Treat circuit discovery as discrete-action RL over 144 heads; actions correspond to ablating a head; reward is a contrastive signal: damage to target task minus damage to general next-token prediction; uses PPO in a vectorised multi-task setup with two training tasks (induction, IOI) and a held-out task (docstring completion).
AI.result: A single PPO policy attains per-episode oracle on training tasks and held-out task; selected heads coincide with canonical, non-redundant heads identified by prior literature; redundant categories de-prioritised; best-of-five planning achieves 96% of oracle ceiling on held-out task without task signal at evaluation.
AI.conclusion: RL over causal interventions is a viable, transferable substrate for identifying single-head bottlenecks; complementary to path-patching approaches.

title: LiveK12Bench: Have Large Multimodal Models Truly Conquered High School-level Examinations?
summary: Advanced Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) have demonstrated impressive performance in K-12 reasoning tasks, exhibiting great promise as intelligent tutors. Realizing this potential requires models to navigate real-world examinations effectively, yet most existing benchmarks fail to capture the complexity of authentic testing environments. Specifically, most datasets are static, prone to data contamination, and are often confined to restricted modalities, disciplines, and evaluation criteria. To address these issues, we introduce LiveK12Bench, a dynamic, holistic, multi-disciplinary benchmark designed to evaluate the reasoning abilities of LMMs in realistic examination scenarios. LiveK12Bench comprises 2K+ verified questions spanning Mathematics, Physics, Chemistry, and Biology, sourced from the latest real-world exam papers and designed to grow over time. Our framework features several core innovations: 1) featuring an automated pipeline that continuously ingests and parses the latest examination papers to mitigate data leakage; and 2) proposing a novel `Mock Exam' evaluation scheme, which assesses the ability to complete end-to-end exams autonomously with accurate and efficient reasoning paths. Extensive experiments on 12 LMMs reveal that advanced models suffer substantial performance degradation under exam-realistic constraints: GPT-5's score drops from 79 to 53 (out of 100) when process rigor and efficiency are jointly evaluated. Our findings expose critical vulnerabilities, such as sensitivity to complex visual layouts, highlighting the gap between idealized reasoning capabilities and true educational readiness. Both code and dataset are publicly available.

AI.tldr: LiveK12Bench is a dynamic, multi-disciplinary exam-style benchmark for large multimodal models, featuring automated ingestion of latest exam papers and a Mock Exam evaluation to test end-to-end reasoning under realistic constraints; results reveal significant performance gaps, highlighting vulnerabilities in current models.
AI.motivation: To overcome static, data-leakable, and modality-limited benchmarks that fail to reflect authentic testing environments and educational readiness for AI tutors.
AI.method: An automated pipeline continuously ingests and parses current examination papers to mitigate data leakage; a new Mock Exam evaluation assesses end-to-end exam completion with transparent, efficient reasoning paths; the benchmark covers 2K+ verified questions across Mathematics, Physics, Chemistry, and Biology sourced from real-world papers and grows over time.
AI.result: 12 large multimodal models evaluated; advanced models suffer substantial performance degradation under exam-realistic constraints; GPT-5 score falls from 79 to 53 when process rigor and efficiency are jointly constrained; models show vulnerability to complex visual layouts.
AI.conclusion: LiveK12Bench reveals a gap between idealized reasoning and true educational readiness, emphasizing the need for realistic, leakage-resistant benchmarks; code and dataset are publicly available to enable replication and further research.

title: LocateAnything: Fast and High-Quality Vision-Language Grounding with Parallel Box Decoding
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) commonly formulate visual grounding and detection as a coordinate-token generation problem, serializing each 2D box into multiple 1D tokens that are learned and decoded largely independently. This token-by-token decoding mismatches the coupled structure of box geometry and creates a practical inference bottleneck due to strictly sequential generation. We introduce LocateAnything, a unified generative grounding and detection framework based on Parallel Box Decoding (PBD). By decoding geometric elements such as bounding boxes and points as atomic units in a single step, LocateAnything preserves intra-box geometric coherence and unlocks substantial parallelism. We show that PBD improves both decoding throughput and localization accuracy. We further develop a scalable data engine and curate LocateAnything-Data, a large-scale dataset with more than 138 million training samples, substantially increasing data diversity for high-precision localization. Extensive evaluations show that LocateAnything advances the speed-accuracy frontier, achieving significantly higher decoding throughput while improving high-IoU localization quality across diverse benchmarks. The results highlight the complementary benefits of Parallel Box Decoding and large-scale training data in enabling efficient and precise unified visual grounding and detection.

AI.tldr: Parallel Box Decoding enables single-step, coherent geometric decoding for grounding and detection, boosting speed and high-IoU accuracy with large-scale data.
AI.motivation: Current VLM grounding/detection decoders serialize 2D boxes into sequences of 1D tokens, causing sequential bottlenecks and geometric misalignment. There is a need to preserve intra-box geometry and unlock parallelism to improve throughput and localization precision.
AI.method: Propose LocateAnything with Parallel Box Decoding (PBD) that decodes bounding boxes and points as atomic units in one step. Unifies grounding and detection in a single framework. Build a scalable data engine and LocateAnything-Data with ~138M training samples to enhance data diversity for high-precision localization.
AI.result: PBD increases decoding throughput and improves localization accuracy (particularly high-IoU). Large-scale data further boosts performance; the method advances the speed-accuracy frontier across benchmarks and highlights the complementary benefits of parallel decoding and large datasets.
AI.conclusion: Locating objects with coherent geometric decoding and large-scale data yields faster, more accurate unified grounding/detection. PBD effectively mitigates token-by-token bottlenecks and leverages diverse training data for robust localization.

title: Clinically-Grounded Counterfactual Reasoning for Medical Video Diagnosis
summary: Medical video diagnosis involves inferring clinical decisions from dynamic tissue responses throughout examination processes. Existing methods rely on an end-to-end learning paradigm that i) focuses on appearance rather than pathology, ii) lacks clinical priors, and iii) reasons solely from observations without counterfactual comparison. This work introduces MedVCR, a counterfactual reasoning framework that mimics clinical diagnostic thinking. MedVCR comprises three components: a Counterfactual Generator that synthesizes tissue evolution under specified pathological states via a diffusion-based manner; a Counterfactual Representation Learning module that encodes diagnostic knowledge through clinical rules (i.e., temporal consistency, pathological separability, and counterfactual alignment); and a Dual Diagnostic Prediction strategy that integrates video-level assessment with frame-level counterfactual analysis. MedVCR is evaluated under both fully supervised (e.g., colposcopy) and weakly supervised (e.g., colonoscopy) video diagnosis settings, yielding 2.6%-10.2% performance gains compared with leading baselines. Comprehensive ablation studies further validate the effectiveness of each component. The code will be released.

AI.tldr: MedVCR introduces a counterfactual reasoning framework for medical video diagnosis, combining a diffusion-based counterfactual generator, rule-guided representation learning, and dual video/frame-level prediction, achieving consistent gains in supervised and weakly supervised settings; code to be released.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of end-to-end video diagnosis: focus on appearance rather than pathology, lack of clinical priors, and absence of counterfactual comparison.
