Extensive experiments on visual reasoning benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of InterSketch, even outperforming proprietary models such as Gemini-3-Pro.

AI.tldr: InterSketch introduces an interleaved visual–textual chain-of-thought (VT-CoT) reasoning framework that generates intermediate visual sketches with external tools and interleaves them with textual reasoning. It uses a cold-start stage with a synthetic VT-CoT dataset and a reflection mechanism for self-correction, followed by a reinforcement learning (RL) stage with a stepwise reward to alleviate reward sparsity. It achieves strong results on visual reasoning benchmarks, surpassing strong baselines like Gemini-3-Pro.
AI.motivation: Current vision-language models (VLMs) exhibit shallow, text-centric reasoning and struggle with long-horizon, multi-turn visual tasks. The goal is to enable human-like interleaved perception and reasoning by integrating dynamic visual sketches with textual CoT, enabling self-correction and longer-horizon reasoning.
AI.method: Introduce InterSketch, which interleaves visual sketches (generated via external tools) with textual reasoning. Stage 1 cold-start: synthesize a high-quality interleaved VT-CoT dataset and implement a reflection mechanism to enable multi-turn interleaved reasoning and self-correction. Stage 2 RL: apply stepwise reward signals to mitigate sparse end-to-end rewards in long-horizon tasks. The model dynamically produces intermediate visuals and alternates with textual reasoning to improve perception and logic.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on visual reasoning benchmarks show the approach is effective, surpassing proprietary models such as Gemini-3-Pro.
AI.conclusion: InterSketch effectively enhances VT-CoT by interleaving visual sketches and textual reasoning, with self-correction and stage-wise RL, achieving strong performance and suggesting potential for long-horizon visual understanding.

title: DDGAD: Trajectory Dynamics for Diffusion-Based Graph Anomaly Detection
summary: Graph anomaly detection (GAD) aims to identify nodes or substructures whose behavior or attributes deviate significantly from the overall pattern in graph-structured data, with critical applications in financial risk control, social network analysis, and cybersecurity. However, existing GCN-based methods suffer from the fundamental problem of contamination propagation, where anomalous nodes pollute the representations of their neighbors through message passing, leading to degraded detection performance. In this paper, we propose DDGAD, a novel diffusion-based graph anomaly detection framework that leverages trajectory dynamics to distinguish normal and anomalous nodes. Our key insight is that normal nodes exhibit consistent and stable representation trajectories under the coupled effects of diffusion regularization and reliability-aware neighborhood consensus, while anomalous nodes exhibit unstable and conflicting dynamics due to the directional disagreement between the global manifold prior and locally contaminated message passing. To mitigate contamination propagation, we introduce a distributed reliability-aware consensus refinement mechanism and define three complementary anomaly signals: neighbor inconsistency, reliability weight, and dynamical conflict energy. We further provide a preliminary theoretical analysis on normal node stability under the coupled dynamics. These signals collectively characterize anomalous behaviors from the perspectives of local inconsistency, consensus reliability, and dynamical instability. Extensive experiments on five real-world datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based graph anomaly detection method (DDGAD) that uses trajectory dynamics and a reliability-aware consensus to mitigate contamination in GCNs, detecting anomalies via three signals and supported by theoretical stability analysis, validated on five real-world datasets.
AI.motivation: GCN-based graph anomaly detection suffers from contamination propagation: anomalous nodes pollute neighbors during message passing, degrading detection performance. A diffusion-driven approach with reliable consensus is proposed to separate stable normal dynamics from unstable anomalous dynamics.
AI.method: Propose DDGAD, a diffusion-based graph anomaly detection framework that analyzes representation trajectory dynamics. Introduces a distributed reliability-aware consensus refinement mechanism and three complementary anomaly signals—neighbor inconsistency, reliability weight, and dynamical conflict energy—to distinguish normal and anomalous nodes. Provides preliminary theoretical analysis on normal-node stability under coupled diffusion and consensus dynamics.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on five real-world datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework, validating the usefulness of the three signals and the reliability-aware refinement in mitigating contamination.
AI.conclusion: DDGAD effectively characterizes anomalous behavior via local inconsistency, consensus reliability, and dynamical instability, and mitigates contamination in diffusion-based graph anomaly detection while offering theoretical insight into normal-node stability.

title: LELA: An End-to-end LLM-based Entity Linking Framework with Zero-shot Domain Adaptation
summary: Entity linking is a key component of many downstream NLP systems, yet existing approaches are often tied to the specific target knowledge bases and domains, limiting their real world application. In this paper, we extend LELA, a modular and domain-agnostic LLM-based entity disambiguation method, into a practical Python library that integrates zero-shot Named Entity Recognition (NER) -thereby providing a complete end-toend pipeline for entity-linking in real-world usage. We provide experimental results validating LELA's performance and robustness across diverse entity linking settings. In our demo, users can play with the system on their own input texts.

AI.tldr: Extends LELA into a practical Python library that integrates zero-shot NER, delivering an end-to-end, domain-agnostic entity-linking pipeline with experimental validation and a demo.
AI.motivation: Address the limitations of domain- and KB-specific entity linking approaches by providing a modular, domain-agnostic, end-to-end solution that leverages LLM-based disambiguation and zero-shot NER for real-world applicability.
AI.method: Develop a Python library that builds on LELA, integrating zero-shot NER to form a complete end-to-end entity-linking pipeline. Emphasizes modular design and real-world usage, with experimental evaluation across diverse settings and a user-facing demo.
AI.result: Empirical results validate LELA’s performance and robustness across diverse entity linking scenarios, demonstrating the practicality and reliability of the end-to-end library in real-world usage.
AI.conclusion: The proposed library enhances entity linking in real-world NLP systems by offering a domain-agnostic, end-to-end solution that combines LELA’s LLM-based disambiguation with zero-shot NER, backed by experimental validation and an interactive demo.

title: CmIVTP: Cross-modal Interaction-based Vessel Trajectory Prediction for Maritime Intelligence
summary: Maritime intelligent transportation systems (MITS) are essential for ensuring navigation safety and efficiency in busy waterways. However, accurate vessel trajectory prediction remains challenging due to the limitations of single-source data. Automatic identification system (AIS) data is often sparse or unavailable for small vessels, while closed-circuit television (CCTV) data alone cannot fully capture dynamic vessel behavior. To mitigate these challenges, we propose a cross-modal interaction-based vessel trajectory prediction (named CmIVTP) framework to model the intricate interactions between vessel dynamics and environmental constraints. Specifically, we introduce a target-aware scene encoder to extract scene semantic features, effectively capturing vessel-environment interactions and enhancing trajectory prediction accuracy. In addition, we propose a cross-modal interaction transformer, which integrates AIS-derived motion features, CCTV-based environmental features, and scene representations. It leverages cross-modal attention mechanisms to simultaneously capture intra-modal semantics and inter-modal interactions, ensuring dynamically consistent and environmentally feasible predictions. Furthermore, we construct a vessel group trajectory bank by clustering historical AIS trajectories into representative motion patterns, providing an efficient and scalable approach for candidate trajectory generation. Additionally, we introduce the maritime multimodal dataset plus (named Maritime-MmD$^+$), a large-scale dataset that synchronizes AIS data and CCTV video data, providing robust support for multimodal trajectory prediction research. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CmIVTP achieves better performance on multimodal-driven vessel trajectory prediction benchmarks. The code resources for this work can be available at https://github.com/LouisYxLu/CmIVTP.

AI.tldr: A cross-modal trajectory prediction framework CmIVTP for vessels leveraging AIS, CCTV, and scene semantics; introduces target-aware scene encoder, cross-modal transformer, and a vessel trajectory bank; accompanied by Maritime-MmD+ dataset; claims improved multimodal trajectory prediction.
AI.motivation: Address data sparsity/incompleteness in maritime tracking by integrating AIS motion with CCTV environmental cues through cross-modal attention to produce feasible, accurate trajectories.
AI.method: Propose target-aware scene encoder for scene features; cross-modal interaction transformer with AIS, CCTV, and scene representations; vessel group trajectory bank via clustering historical AIS; Maritime-MmD+ dataset; evaluation on multimodal benchmarks; code available.
AI.result: CmIVTP achieves superior performance on multimodal vessel trajectory benchmarks; demonstrates effectiveness of cross-modal fusion; scalable trajectory bank; dataset supports future multimodal research.
AI.conclusion: Cross-modal fusion via targeted scene encoding and transformer yields accurate, feasible vessel trajectories; dataset and components enable robust multimodal prediction in MITS settings; potential for broader adoption and extension.

title: MuCon: Clipped Muon Updates for LLM Training
summary: Muon-style optimizers take a matrix-valued momentum or preconditioned update $B = U \operatorname{diag}(σ_1,\ldots,σ_r) V^\top$ and replace it with its canonical partial polar factor $\operatorname{Pol}(B) = U V^\top$. This maps every nonzero singular value to one. MuCon is the clipped-Muon variant studied here: it applies singular-value clipping to the same Muon matrix, $D^{\mathrm{MuCon}}\_τ(B) = \operatorname{MClip}\_τ(B) = U \operatorname{diag}\bigl(\min\{σ\_i,τ\}\bigr) V^\top, \qquad τ> 0$. Thus, $\operatorname{MClip}\_τ$ denotes the mathematical clipping operator, while MuCon denotes the optimizer primitive that substitutes this clipped direction for Muon's polar direction. The Muon/MuCon scaling parameterization used in this work is called $\text{SpectralP}$: it is the hidden-matrix scaling recipe under which polar Muon or clipped MuCon directions are applied. The map $\operatorname{MClip}\_τ$ is the Frobenius projection onto the spectral-norm ball $\{X : \|X\|_2 \le τ\}$: it leaves singular values at or below $τ$ unchanged and modifies only the violating singular directions. This paper asks when the MuCon clipping step can be approximated without a full dense SVD. We record two exact identities, a polar/absolute-value formula and a scalar-root formulation leading to a rational Newton filter for the clipped positive-semidefinite factor, and identify the numerical obstruction common to both: singular values near the threshold make sign decisions and rational solves ill-conditioned. Matrix-function methods are therefore useful only when paired with stable polar/square-root primitives or explicit regularization near the clipping boundary.

AI.tldr: MuCon clips the Muon-style matrix factor by projecting onto the spectral-norm ball, and the paper presents exact polar/absolute-value and scalar-root reformulations for the clipped factor, but identifies a numerical obstruction near the clipping threshold that limits the utility of matrix-function methods unless paired with stable polar/square-root primitives or regularization.
AI.motivation: To enable efficient, SVD-free clipping of Muon-style momentum/preconditioners in spectral-norm constrained optimization and to understand when matrix-function approaches are numerically viable.
AI.method: Derives two exact reformulations for the clipping operation: a polar/absolute-value formula and a scalar-root formulation that yields a rational Newton filter for the clipped PSD factor. Analyzes the conditioning and identifies the obstruction that singular values near τ cause sign decisions and ill-conditioned rational solves. Concludes that matrix-function techniques are useful only with stable polar/sqrt primitives or with explicit regularization near the clipping boundary.
AI.result: Two exact identities are established and a Newton-like filter for the PSD factor is proposed; numerical obstructions near the clipping threshold are identified, limiting applicability of pure matrix-function methods without robust primitives or regularization.
AI.conclusion: Matrix-function approaches for clipped Muon factors are viable only when paired with numerically stable polar/sqrt operations or when explicit regularization is used near the clipping boundary; otherwise, practical clipping requires caution or alternative methods.

title: Generating Robust Portfolios of Optimization Models using Large Language Models
comment: Accepted at the ICML 2026 LM4Plan Workshop
summary: Mathematical optimization is a powerful tool for structured decision-making across domains such as resource allocation and planning. Formulating optimization models faithful to reality, though, remains a significant bottleneck as it typically demands both domain expertise and optimization knowledge that are often scarce. Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) promise to bridge this gap, enabling the generation of candidate optimization models from natural language descriptions. However, there is no guarantee that any single LLM-generated model is reliable, and existing approaches that output only one model are therefore risky. In this work, we propose a novel algorithm that generates a portfolio of optimization models, designed to be robust to the limitations of LLMs. Our method exploits the observation that a single LLM can play two distinct roles $\unicode{x2014}$ as a stochastic generator and as a reasoning evaluator $\unicode{x2014}$ and proposes a unified framework that leverages both capabilities in a complementary manner. We provide theoretical guarantees showing that, as long as either the generator or the evaluator is well-aligned with human preferences, the portfolio is guaranteed to contain high-quality candidates, enabling a principled human-in-the-loop process in which a decision-maker can review multiple candidates before committing to one. We further validate our approach empirically, demonstrating strong performance across a range of optimization modeling tasks.

AI.tldr: A portfolio-generation framework uses a single LLM as both stochastic generator and reasoning evaluator to produce multiple, robust optimization models; it provides theoretical guarantees that, if either role aligns with human preferences, the portfolio includes high-quality candidates, and it is validated empirically across optimization tasks.
AI.motivation: Reliance on a single LLM-generated model is risky due to potential misalignment with real-world constraints and preferences. By producing a diverse portfolio and leveraging human-in-the-loop, the approach increases reliability and practical utility in optimization modeling.
AI.method: Develop a unified framework where the LLM acts in two roles: a stochastic generator that proposes candidate optimization models and a reasoning evaluator that assesses them. The generator and evaluator complement each other, enabling a portfolio that is robust to LLM limitations. Theoretical guarantees show that if either component aligns with human preferences, the portfolio contains high-quality models. A human-in-the-loop reviews multiple candidates to select one. Empirical validation demonstrates strong performance across various optimization tasks.
AI.result: Theoretical guarantee: as long as either the generator or evaluator is well-aligned with human preferences, the portfolio contains high-quality candidates. Empirically, the approach achieves strong performance across diverse optimization modeling tasks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed dual-role LLM framework enables robust optimization-model discovery by coupling generation and evaluation in a portfolio, together with human-in-the-loop review, reducing risk associated with single-model reliance and achieving reliable performance.

title: ReCA: Multi-Shot Long Video Extrapolation via Recursive Context Allocation
summary: Minute-scale cinematic video generation is a central challenge for generative video models. Existing paradigms address only fragments of this challenge: single-shot extrapolation preserves an anchor but lacks cinematic structure, while multi-shot storytelling imposes structure yet remains free to invent its visual states rather than continue an observed one. We define Multi-Shot Video Extrapolation (MSVE), a task that extends an observed frame or clip into a sequence of cinematically structured shots while preserving anchor state and advancing narrative intent. This setting operates under the finite per-call generation budget of short-video models. We identify three coupled bottlenecks: (1) global planners over-specify unsupported details from full screenplays; (2) shot-level prompts dilute task-relevant state when carrying the complete story; and (3) temporal chaining turns generated frames into a lossy memory in which identity, scene, object, and action state decay. MSVE reveals that long-video failure is not merely a limitation of context length, but a failure of context allocation. We propose Recursive Context Allocation (ReCA), an inference-time framework that allocates context hierarchically across planning and generation. ReCA recursively decomposes MSVE into context-bounded subproblems, invokes frozen generators at leaf nodes, and propagates structured state updates across time. To evaluate this setting, we further propose MSVE-Bench and NB-Q, a source-grounded protocol with prompts purpose-built for 3 to 5 minute long-video generation, a regime not addressed by existing short-clip benchmarks. Compared to previous methods, ReCA improves average normalized score by 8 to 16 percent over the strongest competing controller and improves multi-shot consistency metrics by 28 to 43 percent. View the project page at https://reca.vmv.re.

AI.tldr: ReCA enables long-form cinematic video extrapolation by hierarchical, inference-time context allocation, yielding improved consistency and scores over prior methods.
AI.motivation: To address the gap in long-form cinematic video generation: existing methods either extrapolate from a single anchor without cinematic structure or tell stories with invented visuals, failing to continue observed states within budgeted short-video models.
AI.method: Recursive Context Allocation (ReCA): recursively decompose MSVE into context-bounded subproblems, invoke frozen generators at leaf nodes, and propagate structured state updates across time. Introduces MSVE-Bench and NB-Q benchmarks for 3–5 minute videos.
AI.result: Quantitative gains: average normalized score improves by 8–16% over the strongest competing controller; multi-shot consistency metrics improve by 28–43%.
AI.conclusion: ReCA demonstrates that long-video failure arises from inefficient context allocation, and a hierarchical, inference-time framework can recover continuity across time; the paper also provides new benchmarks (MSVE-Bench, NB-Q) for longer video evaluation.

title: Diffuse to Detect: Generative Diffusion Models for Unsupervised IC Anomaly Detection
summary: Latent defect screening is challenged by extremely low failure rates, high-dimensional test data, and absence of labeled anomalies. We propose the first unsupervised anomaly detection framework incorporating a Diffusion Transformer. Raw test measurements are first compressed by an autoencoder, then reshaped into a structured token sequence enriched with sinusoidal and per-device wafer-position embeddings. Anomaly scores are derived from the noise-prediction error over mid-range diffusion timesteps, enabling fast wafer-scale screening without any labeled defects or manual feature engineering. Our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance on industrial 16nm IC test data under extreme class imbalance, offering interpretable failure localization through latent-space reconstruction residuals.

AI.tldr: Unsupervised anomaly detection for wafer-level screening using a diffusion-transformer on autoencoder features; achieves state-of-the-art performance on imbalanced 16nm IC data with interpretable failure localization.
AI.motivation: Latent defect screening in semiconductor manufacturing faces extremely low failure rates, high-dimensional test data, and lack of labeled anomalies, necessitating scalable, unsupervised methods with interpretable outputs.
AI.method: Compress raw measurements with an autoencoder, reshape into a structured token sequence enriched with sinusoidal and per-device wafer-position embeddings. Use a diffusion transformer and derive anomaly scores from noise-prediction error at mid-range diffusion timesteps. No labeled defects or manual feature engineering; localization is via latent-space reconstruction residuals.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on industrial 16nm IC test data under extreme class imbalance; enables fast wafer-scale screening and provides interpretable failure localization through reconstruction residuals.
AI.conclusion: Introduces the first unsupervised anomaly detection framework based on a Diffusion Transformer for wafer-level screening, combining compression, structured tokenization, and diffusion-based anomaly scoring to achieve scalable, interpretable screening.

title: ORCA: An End-to-End Interactive Copilot for Optimized Root Cause Analysis
summary: Causal analysis is a crucial task in many domains, including manufacturing, social science, and medicine. However, despite recent progress, the conceptual and methodological complexity of causal methods makes them largely inaccessible to domain experts. This gap prevents experts from leveraging these advances and hinders researchers who lack access to real-world data for validation. To bridge this divide, we introduce ORCA, a copilot for end-to-end causal analysis. ORCA orchestrates agents to understand the user's goals and guide them through the most appropriate causal analysis workflow, from fully automatic to highly user-guided execution. It features causal discovery, causal effect estimation, explainability and Root-Cause-Analysis (RCA). ORCA evaluates and compares performance, generates key metrics and diagrams, and generates insights through structured reports. We highlight its effectiveness across several real-world use-cases.

AI.tldr: ORCA is a copilot framework that orchestrates agent-based causal analysis, enabling end-to-end workflows from automatic to user-guided, with modules for discovery, effect estimation, explainability, and root-cause analysis, plus evaluation metrics and structured reports across real-world use cases.
AI.motivation: Causal analysis is essential across domains but remains conceptually and methodologically complex, making it inaccessible to domain experts and validating across data-scarce settings. A bridge is needed to enable end-to-end, user-friendly causal workflows.
AI.method: ORCA orchestrates agents to infer user goals and select appropriate causal-analysis workflows, supporting fully automatic to highly guided execution. It integrates causal discovery, causal effect estimation, explainability, and root-cause analysis (RCA), and provides performance evaluation, key metrics/diagrams, and structured reports to present insights.
AI.result: Demonstrated effectiveness across several real-world use-cases, with ORCA evaluating and comparing performance, generating metrics/diagrams, and producing insights through structured reports.
AI.conclusion: ORCA acts as a copilot for end-to-end causal analysis, lowering barriers for domain experts, enabling flexible workflows, and delivering interpretable outputs across diverse real-world scenarios.

title: Variational Inference for Evidential Deep Learning
summary: While Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) achieve remarkable performance, their tendency to produce overconfident predictions. Evidential Deep Learning (EDL) mitigates this by formulating predictions as a Dirichlet distribution over class probabilities to explicitly quantify epistemic uncertainty. However, we found that the conventional EDL suffers from two fundamental limitations: a Kullback-Leibler (KL) penalty that only suppresses the evidence of negative classes, producing excessively high evidence therefore decreasing the model's ability to quantify uncertainty, and an absence in theoretical guarantee of setting Dirichlet parameter $α=e+1$. In this paper, we propose a mathematically principled framework, Variational Inference Evidential Deep Learning (VI-EDL). By reformulating evidential learning through the lens of variational inference, we derive an Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO), which prevents the evidence from growing excessively. Theoretically, we rigorously establish a generalization bound and reveal how the predicted uncertainty, feature and network complexity affect this bound, and why setting $\boldsymbolα = \mathbf{e} + \mathbf{1}$ can minimize it. Extensive experiments on standard visual and medical datasets demonstrate that VI-EDL achieves state-of-the-art performance, showing excellent performance in out-of-distribution detection, noise detection and autonomous driving scenario. The code is available in https://github.com/seutjw/VI-EDL.

AI.tldr: VI-EDL reframes evidential deep learning as variational inference to bound evidence growth, yielding better uncertainty estimates and calibration; it justifies α = e+1 and achieves strong results on vision and medical datasets with notable OOD/noise detection and autonomous driving performance.
AI.motivation: To fix two core issues in conventional EDL: (i) KL penalty only reduces negative-class evidence, leading to excessive total evidence and poor uncertainty; (ii) lack of theoretical justification for the Dirichlet parameter choice α = e+1.
AI.method: Formulate evidential learning within a variational inference framework to derive an Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO), controlling evidence growth; derive a generalization bound showing how uncertainty, features, and network complexity influence it; argue that α = e+1 minimizes the bound; implement VI-EDL as the resulting model, with derivations and training objective.
AI.result: Empirical results on standard visual and medical datasets show state-of-the-art performance, particularly in out-of-distribution detection, noise detection, and autonomous driving contexts; code released at the cited repository.
AI.conclusion: VI-EDL provides a principled, theoretically grounded framework for evidential deep learning with favorable uncertainty quantification and robust performance, and clarifies the role of Dirichlet parameters in uncertainty bounds.

title: Boosting Knowledge Graph Foundation Models via Enhanced Negative Sampling
summary: Knowledge graphs (KGs) have become the core backbone of numerous downstream tasks such as question answering and recommender systems. However, despite all this, KGs are often very incomplete. To perform zero-shot knowledge graph completion in unseen KGs, which have different relational vocabularies from those used for pre-training, KG foundation models (KGFMs) receive a wide range of attention. Existing KGFMs often perform training using random negative triples, which are constructed by replacing the head or tail entity of a positive triple with a random entity. However, these negative triples are often constructed with limited quality, providing weak supervision for KGFM training. In this paper, we propose a simple yet effective adaptive negative sampling approach, KMAS, to enhance existing KGFMs. KMAS constructs hard negative triples through the updated relation embeddings generated from the existing KGFM's relation encoder. To further adaptively align with the evolving capability of the KGFM during the training process, KMAS adjusts the ratio of hard negative triples dynamically throughout the whole training process: after a warmup phrase, it increases the ratio linearly and then decreases linearly. Extensive experiments are conducted over 44 data sets. Experimental results demonstrate that our proposed negative sampling method can enhance many SOTA KGFMs without requiring excessive additional time or memory consumption.

AI.tldr: KMAS is an adaptive hard negative sampling method for KG foundation models that dynamically adjusts the ratio of hard negatives during training based on updated relation embeddings, improving zero-shot KG completion across many datasets with minimal extra cost.
AI.motivation: KGs are incomplete and zero-shot KGC in unseen KGs with different vocabularies demands better supervision. Random negative sampling provides weak signals; high-quality negatives are needed to train KGFMs effectively.
AI.method: KMAS generates hard negative triples using updated relation embeddings from the model's relation encoder to craft challenging negatives. It also uses a dynamic ratio schedule: after a warmup phase, the proportion of hard negatives increases linearly during training and then decreases linearly to adapt to the model's evolving capabilities.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on 44 datasets shows that KMAS improves many state-of-the-art KGFMs with no significant overhead in time or memory.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive, quality-aware negative sampling can significantly boost KGFMs for zero-shot KGC, offering a simple, efficient enhancement over random negatives and aligning negative sampling with model progression.

title: Scheduled Style Injection: Expanding the Style-Content Pareto Frontier in Training-Free Diffusion-based Style Transfer
summary: Style transfer with pre-trained diffusion models has advanced rapidly, but a core question remains underexplored: where in the model should style injection be strongest? StyleID, the leading training-free method, uses a single global parameter (gamma) uniformly across all layers and timesteps, which forces a fixed tradeoff between style quality and content preservation. We show this tradeoff is unnecessarily rigid. We systematically explore four dimensions of control: varying style injection strength across decoder layers, across denoising timesteps, and scheduling ControlNet geometric conditioning along both axes. The pattern is consistent everywhere: decreasing schedules, with stronger structural signal injection in shallower layers and earlier timesteps, reliably outperform the reverse. Beyond direction, schedule shape matters: cosine and square-root timestep schedules outperform linear. Most importantly, we find that gamma scheduling and ControlNet conditioning are nearly independent. The resulting combined configurations expand the Pareto frontier, offering superior tradeoffs between style fidelity and content preservation compared to any single baseline setting. Our best balanced configuration achieves ArtFID of 27.036 versus StyleID's 28.801 - a 6.1% relative improvement, with consistent gains across the full style-content tradeoff frontier. Results are validated across 35 configurations totaling over 28,000 stylized images using four complementary metrics. These findings generalize across SD backbones with identical rank ordering. All modifications are training-free, parameter-free, and require only a few lines of scheduling code; code is available at https://github.com/ameyskulkarni/scheduled_style_injection.

AI.tldr: Scheduling-based multi-dimensional style injection (layerwise and timestep-wise) with diverse timestep schedules and ControlNet conditioning improves StyleID-style transfer, expanding the Pareto frontier of style fidelity vs content preservation; best config reduces ArtFID from 28.801 to 27.036 (6.1% rel), training-free.
AI.motivation: Address the rigid trade-off of a single global gamma in StyleID by exploring multiple control dimensions and scheduling strategies to improve style-content tradeoffs in diffusion-based style transfer.
AI.method: Vary style injection strength across decoder layers and denoising timesteps; schedule ControlNet conditioning along both axes; compare cosine, square-root, and linear timestep schedules; evaluate gamma scheduling vs ControlNet conditioning; test 35 configurations on ~28k stylized images across four metrics and multiple SD backbones; all modifications are training-free and parameter-free.
AI.result: Best balanced configuration achieves ArtFID 27.036 vs 28.801 (6.1% relative improvement); gains are consistent across the full style-content frontier; results validated across 35 configurations totaling ~28k stylized images with four metrics; rank-order preserved across SD backbones; code released at the provided URL.
AI.conclusion: Scheduling-based injection with adaptive layer/timestep control and independent ControlNet conditioning expands the Pareto frontier for style transfer, offering training-free, broadly generalizable improvements; earlier layers and timesteps with non-linear (cosine/sqrt) schedules are advantageous and gamma scheduling remains largely independent from ControlNet conditioning.

title: Extra-Merge: Tracing the Rank-1 Subspace of Model Merging in Language Model Pre-Training
summary: Model merging has emerged as a lightweight paradigm for enhancing Large Language Models (LLMs), yet its underlying mechanisms remain poorly understood. In this work, we analyze late-stage pre-training trajectories and uncover a \textbf{Rank-1 Subspace} phenomenon: while raw optimization steps oscillate violently, consecutive \emph{merged} checkpoints collapse onto a stable, approximately one-dimensional linear manifold. We theoretically ground this observation in a \emph{river-valley} landscape analysis: averaging acts as a geometric low-pass filter that dampens high-curvature noise to reveal the optimal descent direction. Capitalizing on this insight, we propose \textbf{Extra-Merge}, a training-free strategy that extrapolates along this subspace to minimize loss without additional gradient updates. Extensive experiments across GPT-2 and LLaMA families (124M to 2B) demonstrate that Extra-Merge consistently outperforms standard merging baselines. Notably, it yields consistent zero-shot accuracy gains on Pythia-12B downstream tasks and generalizes effectively to the Muon optimizer \citep{jordan2024muon}.

AI.tldr: Merged checkpoints during late-stage pretraining align along a stable Rank-1 subspace; Extra-Merge extrapolates along this geometry to reduce loss without gradient updates, outperforming baselines across GPT-2/LLaMA sizes and downstream tasks.
AI.motivation: Model merging is a lightweight technique to fuse knowledge from multiple checkpoints, but its benefits are limited by noisy optimization trajectories. Identifying the geometric structure of merged states could enable more reliable and gradient-free improvements.
AI.method: Empirically analyze late-stage pretraining trajectories to identify a Rank-1 subspace in merged checkpoints. Ground the finding with a river-valley landscape analogy where averaging acts as a low-pass filter to reveal the optimal descent direction. Propose Extra-Merge, a training-free extrapolation along the identified subspace to minimize loss without new gradient steps. Evaluate on GPT-2 and LLaMA variants (124M–2B) and test on Pythia-12B downstream tasks and compatibility with Muon optimizer.
AI.result: Extra-Merge consistently outperforms standard merging baselines across architectures and scales. It yields consistent zero-shot accuracy gains on Pythia-12B tasks and generalizes to the Muon optimizer, indicating robustness and broader applicability.
AI.conclusion: The merging process operates on a low-dimensional (Rank-1) subspace that can be exploited with a training-free extrapolation strategy. Extra-Merge provides a simple, effective enhancement to model merging with strong cross-model and cross-task generalization potential.

title: BatteryMFormer: Multi-level Learning for Battery Degradation Trajectory Forecasting
summary: Early battery degradation trajectory forecasting (BDTF), which predicts the full-life state-of-health trajectory from early operational data, is critical for battery optimization, manufacturing, and deployment. Battery degradation data exhibit two key characteristics. First, degradation data present a multi-level structure, including regularities shared within aging conditions and trajectory patterns shared across batteries. Second, degradation-related variations in voltage-current profiles are often localized to specific state-of-charge (SOC) intervals. Existing approaches often fail to explicitly model these characteristics. To bridge this gap, we propose BatteryMFormer, a multi-level Transformer for early BDTF. BatteryMFormer integrates (1) an aging-condition-aware decoder that injects aging-condition priors via aging-condition-informed queries and aging-condition-aware attention, (2) a meta degradation pattern memory that learns and retrieves trajectory prototypes to guide long-horizon forecasting, and (3) a dual-view encoder that jointly captures temporal dynamics and SOC-localized variations from voltage and current time series. Extensive experiments on four battery domains show that BatteryMFormer consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, marking a significant step toward reliable BDTF. Our code is available at https://github.com/Ruifeng-Tan/BatteryMFormer.

AI.tldr: Introduces BatteryMFormer, a multi-level Transformer for early battery degradation trajectory forecasting (BDTF) that uses aging-condition-aware decoding, meta degradation pattern memories, and dual-view encoding to capture intra-condition regularities and SOC-localized variations, achieving consistent gains across four battery domains.
AI.motivation: Battery degradation data exhibit a multi-level structure (shared intra-aging-condition patterns and cross-battery trajectory signatures) and SOC-localized variations in voltage-current profiles. Existing methods inadequately model these characteristics, hindering reliable early BDTF.
AI.method: 1) Aging-condition-aware decoder: injects aging-condition priors via aging-condition-informed queries and attention. 2) Meta degradation pattern memory: learns and retrieves trajectory prototypes to guide long-horizon forecasting. 3) Dual-view encoder: jointly captures temporal dynamics and SOC-localized variations from voltage and current time series.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on four battery domains shows BatteryMFormer consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines; code released at https://github.com/Ruifeng-Tan/BatteryMFormer.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a principled architecture that directly encodes the two key data characteristics of degradation trajectories. Demonstrates reliable improvements across diverse domains and provides open-source code for reproducibility.

title: TrackRef3D: Multi-View Consistent Track-then-Label for Open-World Referring Segmentation in 3D Gaussian Splatting
summary: Referring 3D Gaussian Splatting (R3DGS), which utilizes natural language for 3D object segmentation, has emerged as a crucial capability for embodied AI. However, existing methods typically rely on expensive per-scene manual annotation and per-view pseudo mask generation, which suffer from multi-view inconsistency and poor generalization to varying query specificities. To address this, we present TrackRef3D, a fully automatic pipeline that achieves open-world referring segmentation in 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) without manual annotation by introducing a multi-view consistent track-then-label paradigm that fundamentally decouples object discovery from semantic grounding. Specifically, we propose a Trajectory-Aware Semantic Consensus Module (TSCM) which aggregates cross-view predictions via synonymous clustering and trajectory-aware voting to establish a canonical semantic identity, thereby ensuring multi-view consistency. Furthermore, we employ a visibility-aware description generation strategy to mitigate ambiguity and propose a Hybrid Training Strategy (HTS) that jointly optimizes coarse category semantics and fine-grained referential cues to ensure robustness under varying query specificities using a multi-positive contrastive objective. Extensive experiments on benchmarks demonstrate that TrackRef3D achieves state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: TrackRef3D automates open-world 3D referring segmentation for 3D Gaussian Splatting by a track-then-label pipeline with a Trajectory-Aware Semantic Consensus Module, visibility-aware description, and Hybrid Training Strategy, achieving state-of-the-art performance without manual annotations.
AI.motivation: To remove the dependency on per-scene manual annotations and per-view pseudo masks that cause multi-view inconsistency and poor generalization to varying query specificity; to decouple object discovery from semantic grounding and enforce cross-view semantic consistency in 3D segmentation.
AI.method: Introduce TrackRef3D with (1) Trajectory-Aware Semantic Consensus Module (TSCM) that aggregates cross-view predictions via synonymous clustering and trajectory-aware voting to produce a canonical semantic identity; (2) visibility-aware description generation to reduce ambiguity; (3) Hybrid Training Strategy (HTS) optimizing coarse category semantics and fine-grained referential cues with a multi-positive contrastive objective; (4) multi-view track-then-label paradigm for 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) without manual annotation.
AI.result: Experiments on benchmarks show state-of-the-art performance, validating effectiveness of the proposed components and robustness to query variations.
AI.conclusion: TrackRef3D presents a fully automatic, multi-view-consistent, open-world referring segmentation framework for 3DGS that eliminates manual labeling and improves grounding robustness by decoupling discovery and grounding.

title: The Stability of Singular Distribution: A Spectral Perspective on the Two-Phase Dynamics of Language Model Pre-training
summary: Large language model pre-training typically exhibits a two-phase trajectory: a fast initial loss drop followed by a prolonged slow improvement. We identify an underlying spectral phenomenon, Stability of Singular Distribution (SoSD), where the trace-normalized singular value spectrum stabilizes early, even as parameter matrices continue to evolve. We demonstrate that synchronization between SoSD and the slow-descent regime is widely observed across diverse architectures (GPT-2, LLaMA) and settings, including various schedules (Step-wise, WSD, Cosine Decay), weight decays, and optimizers (AdamW, Muon). By analyzing a simplified Transformer, we prove that growing weight norms inevitably precipitate an early SoSD threshold, after which the rate of loss decrease becomes theoretically bounded by the variation in the singular distribution. We further interpret strategies like WSD and Muon through their ability to modulate the SoSD scale, offering a spectral lens for understanding efficient pre-training dynamics.

AI.tldr: SoSD (Stability of Singular Distribution) reveals an early-stabilizing spectral property in LLM pretraining: the trace-normalized singular value spectrum stabilizes even as weights grow, and this stabilization aligns with the slow-descent phase across architectures, schedules, and optimizers. A simple Transformer theory shows growing weight norms trigger an early SoSD threshold, bounding loss decrease by changes in the singular distribution; strategies like WSD and Muon act by adjusting SoSD scale, framing pretraining efficiency spectrally.
AI.motivation: Explain the two-phase loss trajectory in large language model pretraining (fast initial improvement followed by slow descent) by linking it to a stable spectral property of the network’s singular value distribution, and to understand how training schedules and optimizers influence efficiency through this spectral lens.
AI.method: Empirical analysis across multiple LLMs (GPT-2, LLaMA) and training settings (Step-wise, WSD, CosineDecay; with various weight decays and optimizers such as AdamW and Muon) to observe SoSD; theoretical analysis on a simplified Transformer showing that increasing weight norms induce an early SoSD threshold, after which loss decrease is bounded by the variation in the singular distribution; interpretative framing of WSD and Muon via their impact on SoSD scale.
AI.result: SoSD stabilizes early in training and synchronizes with the slow-descent regime across models and settings; growing weight norms induce an early threshold for SoSD; the rate of loss decrease beyond threshold is theoretically bounded by changes in the singular distribution; WSD and Muon are explained as mechanisms to modulate SoSD scale, offering a spectral explanation for efficient pretraining dynamics.
AI.conclusion: A spectral lens via SoSD provides a unified understanding of pretraining dynamics across architectures and optimization strategies, enabling interpretation of training efficiency and guiding the design of schedules and optimizers that influence the singular distribution to sustain efficient learning.

title: Traceable Knowledge Graph Reasoning Enables LLM-Assisted Decision Support for Industrial VOCs in the Steel Industry
summary: Key knowledge for steel-industry volatile organic compounds (VOCs) governance is scattered across unstructured scientific literature, making it difficult to integrate process, pollutant, and control-technology evidence and increasing the risk of hallucination when general large language models (LLMs) answer low-frequency industrial questions. Here we developed Chat-ISV, a knowledge graph (KG) enhanced multi-agent Q&A system that parses a curated steel-industry VOCs literature corpus, constructs a Neo4j KG with 27180 nodes and 81779 semantic edges, and combines prompt-constrained extraction, chunk-centered topology optimization, multi-agent routing, source-backtracking retrieval, local literature retrieval, open-domain knowledge access, and interactive subgraph visualization. Benchmark tests and 400 expert blind evaluations showed that topology optimization reduced isolated nodes from 57% to 4.08% and that Chat-ISV achieved high factual reliability, with 96.93% precision, 72.63% recall, an F1-score of 0.830, and a mean score of 1.69/2.00. By converting fragmented environmental-engineering literature into traceable, queryable, and decision-support-oriented knowledge, Chat-ISV establishes a scalable environmental-informatics paradigm for reliable LLM deployment and intelligent pollution-control decision support in specialized industrial domains.

AI.tldr: A KG-enhanced multi-agent QA system (Chat-ISV) converts scattered steel-VOC literature into a Neo4j graph to enable reliable LLM-based decision support; it achieves high factual reliability and dramatically reduces isolated concepts.
AI.motivation: Governance of steel-industry VOCs is hindered by fragmented, unstructured literature and LLM hallucinations on low-frequency industrial questions; a structured, traceable knowledge base is needed for reliable decision support.
AI.method: Construct a Neo4j knowledge graph from a curated steel-VOC literature corpus (27,180 nodes; 81,779 edges). Implement prompt-constrained extraction, chunk-centered topology optimization, multi-agent routing, source-backtracking retrieval, local literature retrieval, open-domain knowledge access, and interactive subgraph visualization.
AI.result: Topology optimization reduced isolated nodes from 57% to 4.08%. Evaluations yielded 96.93% precision, 72.63% recall, F1=0.830; mean user score 1.69/2.00 across 400 expert blind evaluations.
AI.conclusion: Chat-ISV demonstrates a scalable environmental-informatics paradigm for reliable LLM deployment and intelligent pollution-control decision support in specialized industrial domains by turning fragmented literature into traceable, queryable knowledge.

title: O-MARC: Omni Memory-Augmented Compression Distillation for Efficient Video Understanding
summary: Omnimodal large language models enable unified audio video understanding, but long joint token sequences make inference costly, and existing benchmarks do not fully isolate audio visual association in noisy user generated videos. We introduce UGC-AVQA, a public UGC benchmark with 1,000 videos and 4,816 QA pairs, where an audio removal test ensures that benchmark questions require both acoustic and visual evidence. To reduce inference cost, we propose OMAC, a training free plug in compression method that preserves salient visual memory and temporally grounded audio anchors. To further make compact models robust to compressed inputs, we introduce O-MARC, a compression distillation framework for learning with memory compressed multimodal contexts. On Qwen2.5-Omni-3B, O-MARC improves the average score across four benchmarks to 45.8, outperforming full token inference at 44.1 and OmniZip at 41.0. OMAC also keeps inference efficient, reducing latency by 34.6\% (1.53$\times$ speedup) and memory by 34.7\% compared with full token inference.

AI.tldr: Introduces UGC-AVQA benchmark and two compression techniques (OMAC and O-MARC) to enable efficient, robust multimodal reasoning for omni-modal LLMs; achieves better accuracy than full-token baselines on Qwen2.5-Omni-3B and substantial speedups.
AI.motivation: Omnimodal LLMs process long joint audio-visual token sequences, causing high inference cost. Existing benchmarks fail to isolate audio-visual associations in noisy user-generated content, limiting fair evaluation of multimodal grounding and robustness.
AI.method: UGC-AVQA: a public dataset of 1,000 videos with 4,816 QA pairs, including an audio-removal test to ensure reliance on both acoustic and visual evidence. OMAC: a training-free plug-in compression method that preserves salient visual memory and temporally grounded audio anchors. O-MARC: a compression distillation framework trained to operate on memory-compressed multimodal contexts.
AI.result: On Qwen2.5-Omni-3B, O-MARC improves average score across four benchmarks to 45.8, surpassing full-token inference at 44.1 and OmniZip at 41.0. OMAC also reduces latency by 34.6% (about 1.53x speedup) and memory usage by 34.7% compared with full-token inference.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates effective, efficient multimodal reasoning on noisy UGC data by combining a challenging evaluation benchmark with practical compression techniques that enable compact models to achieve competitive performance with significantly reduced compute and memory demands.

title: Beyond Pairwise Preferences: Listwise Reward-Aware Alignment for Diffusion Models
summary: Preference optimization has emerged as an efficient alternative to online reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) for aligning text-to-image diffusion models. However, existing methods largely reduce supervision to binary pairwise comparisons. This pairwise reduction is limiting when training data naturally contains multiple candidate images for the same prompt, and when continuous reward scores can provide richer information than a single winner-loser label. To address these limitations, we propose Diffusion LAIR, a reward-aware listwise preference optimization method for diffusion models. For each prompt, LAIR converts reward scores across a group of candidate images into centered advantage weights, then optimizes an advantage-weighted regression objective on the implicit reward, defined as the denoising-loss improvement of the current model over a fixed reference model, with a quadratic penalty that regularizes the magnitude of the implicit reward. The resulting objective uses all candidates simultaneously rather than selecting pairs, and remains conservative by explicitly controlling the magnitude of the implicit reward. The LAIR objective admits a bounded closed-form optimum in implicit-reward space, clarifying how the regularization strength controls the magnitude of the preference update. Experiments show that Diffusion LAIR outperforms strong preference optimization baselines on SD1.5 and SDXL across text-to-image generation, compositional generation, and image editing benchmarks.

AI.tldr: A reward-aware listwise preference optimization (Diffusion LAIR) for diffusion models using centered advantage weights and a bounded implicit reward improves performance on SD1.5 and SDXL.
AI.motivation: Pairwise preference reduction is limiting when data includes multiple candidates per prompt and continuous reward scores; a method leveraging full reward information can improve alignment.
AI.method: For each prompt, convert candidate rewards to centered advantage weights and optimize an advantage-weighted regression on the implicit reward—defined as the denoising-loss improvement over a fixed reference model—with quadratic regularization to bound reward magnitude; uses all candidates and has a closed-form optimum.
AI.result: Diffusion LAIR outperforms strong baselines on SD1.5 and SDXL across text-to-image generation, compositional generation, and image editing benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: LAIR provides a principled, stable, and data-efficient listwise optimization framework for diffusion models, with a bounded update controlled by regularization, yielding robust empirical gains.

title: Can Broad Biomedical Knowledge be Contextualized into Scenario-Grounded Propositions?
summary: Biomedical discovery often requires connecting broad biomedical knowledge with specific experimental or clinical data. Background knowledge suggests relevant mechanisms but is usually too general to map directly onto dataset variables, while data-driven patterns can be dataset-specific and hard to interpret mechanistically. We study this missing link as knowledge contextualization: transforming broad biomedical knowledge into evidence-supported, scenario-grounded propositions that domain experts can inspect, replay, and validate. We propose SCENE, a bi-level multi-agent framework that treats knowledge contextualization as iterative search. The upper level converts broad knowledge into search directions and grounds them in the dataset schema. The lower level executes these directions through multi-objective optimization to identify concrete propositions that balance evidential strength and data support. Feedback between the two levels progressively refines the search. We evaluate SCENE in two settings: discovering patient subgroups with heterogeneous treatment benefits in clinical trial scenarios, and identifying context-specific biological responses in LINCS L1000 studies. In clinical trials, SCENE discovers specific, well-supported subgroups and outperforms existing baselines. In L1000 studies, SCENE identifies perturbational contexts with strong target-response matching and high positive rates. These results show that SCENE bridges broad knowledge and scenario-specific evidence, producing traceable, inspectable hypotheses for follow-up validation.

AI.tldr: SCENE is a bi-level, iterative search framework that contextualizes broad biomedical knowledge into dataset-grounded, testable propositions, demonstrated in clinical trial subgroup discovery and LINCS L1000 perturbation contexts.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between general biomedical knowledge and dataset-specific variables by producing interpretable, evidence-supported hypotheses that domain experts can inspect and validate.
AI.method: SCENE uses a bi-level, multi-agent approach. The upper level converts broad knowledge into search directions tied to the dataset schema; the lower level performs multi-objective optimization to extract concrete propositions balancing evidential strength with data support. An iterative feedback loop refines the search.
AI.result: In clinical trials, SCENE discovers specific, well-supported subgroups and outperforms baselines. In LINCS L1000 studies, it identifies perturbational contexts with strong target–response matching and high positive rates.
AI.conclusion: SCENE bridges broad knowledge and scenario-specific evidence, yielding traceable, inspectable hypotheses suitable for follow-up validation.

title: FTibSuite: A Comprehensive Resource Suite for Tibetan Vision-Language Modeling
summary: Vision-language models have progressed rapidly, but Tibetan remains a severely underserved low-resource language due to the lack of reproducible training and evaluation infrastructure. To fill this gap, we introduce FTibSuite, a comprehensive resource suite for Tibetan vision-language research, consisting of FTibData (human-verified multimodal training corpora spanning continual pretraining, image-text alignment, and instruction tuning data), FTibBench (Tibetan adaptations of five mainstream multimodal benchmarks with a hierarchical quality-control workflow to reduce translation noise), and FTibVLM, a reproducible baseline built on Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct via a three-stage adaptation pipeline. Experiments on FTibBench show FTibVLM delivers consistent performance gains across all tasks, such as improving MMBench accuracy from 42.97 to 67.78 and POPE-random accuracy from 47.53 to 80.56, while retaining the backbone's original Chinese capabilities with minimal degradation, providing the first standardized foundation for Tibetan multimodal research.

AI.tldr: FTibSuite delivers a reproducible Tibetan vision-language research stack (FTibData, FTibBench, FTibVLM). It enables continual pretraining, image-text alignment, and instruction-tuning data; adapts five benchmarks; achieves strong gains on FTibBench while preserving Chinese capabilities.
AI.motivation: Tibetan is severely underserved low-resource language; need reproducible training/evaluation infrastructure and benchmarks for multimodal research.
AI.method: Construct FTibData with human-verified multimodal corpora across continual pretraining, image-text alignment, and instruction tuning; FTibBench with five Tibetan-adapted multimodal benchmarks and a hierarchical quality-control workflow to reduce translation noise; FTibVLM baseline built by adapting Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct in a three-stage adaptation pipeline.
AI.result: FTibVLM yields substantial performance gains on FTibBench: MMBench accuracy 42.97 -> 67.78; POPE-random 47.53 -> 80.56; minimal degradation to Chinese capabilities.
AI.conclusion: FTibSuite provides the first standardized foundation for Tibetan multimodal research and a reproducible baseline for future work.

title: Dense2MoE: Pushing the Pareto Frontier of On-Device LLMs via Unified Pruning and Upcycling
summary: The Mixture of Experts MoE architecture is highly promising for resource constrained on device deployments yet training these models from scratch incurs prohibitive costs Current methods attempt to alleviate this by upcycling dense models into MoEs however they often introduce parameter redundancy that degrades inference efficiency Alternatively standard layer pruning mitigates redundancy but inevitably compromises model accuracy To resolve this dilemma we propose Dense2MoE a novel framework that unifies pruning and upcycling through Layer Fusion UpCycling LF UC Guided by hardware Roofline theory Dense2MoE systematically overcomes the inference memory wall by pruning bandwidth heavy attention modules from redundant layers while repurposing their Multi Layer Perceptrons MLPs into MoE experts This structural innovation preserves the models core capabilities and strictly limits active parameters via selective token routing With a modest continual pre training budget Dense2MoE efficiently converts publicly available dense LLMs into on device ready MoE models Extensive experiments demonstrate that Dense2MoE significantly advances the Pareto frontier for on device inference latency versus model accuracy outperforming dense baselines state of the art compression and standard upcycling methods

AI.tldr: Dense2MoE unifies pruning and upcycling to convert dense LLMs into on-device MoEs, achieving better latency-accuracy tradeoffs with limited active parameters.
AI.motivation: To enable efficient on-device deployment of Mixture of Experts models by addressing training costs, parameter redundancy, and memory bandwidth bottlenecks in existing upcycling and pruning approaches.
AI.method: Proposes Layer Fusion UpCycling (LF UC) guided by hardware Roofline theory. Prunes bandwidth-heavy attention modules in redundant layers and repurposes their MLPs into MoE experts, with selective token routing to strictly bound active parameters. Uses a modest continual pre-training budget to convert public dense LLMs into on-device MoEs.
AI.result: Dense2MoE significantly expands the Pareto frontier for on-device latency versus accuracy, outperforming dense baselines, state-of-the-art compression, and standard upcycling, across extensive experiments.
AI.conclusion: Dense2MoE provides a practical, hardware-aware framework to convert dense models into efficient on-device MoEs without large retraining, preserving core capabilities while controlling parameter usage.

title: Counteraction-Aware Multi-Teacher On-Policy Distillation for General Capability Recovery with Domain Preservation
summary: Domain specialization can improve LLM behavior in vertical domains, but often weakens the general capabilities inherited from the original model. Recent Multi-Teacher On-Policy Distillation (MOPD) pipelines recover model capabilities by supervising student-generated trajectories with teacher feedback, but typically assume teacher-aligned prompt coverage, requiring prompts to match the teachers' training distributions. This assumption is difficult to satisfy when the general teacher is an open-source model whose post-training data are unknown. Instead of attempting to reconstruct this hidden distribution, we study general capability recovery with readily available proxy general prompts. We identify two failure modes of vanilla MOPD in this incomplete-coverage situation: recovery-preservation counteraction from mixing conflicting recovery and preservation gradients, and weak-signal flattening from uniformly averaging samples with unequal correction demand. We propose Counteraction-Aware Multi-Teacher On-Policy Distillation (CaMOPD), which addresses these issues with decoupled alternating training and gap-based sample selection. CaMOPD gives general recovery dedicated updates, periodically reviews domain prompts for preservation, and selects samples with larger averaged token-level teacher-student log-probability gaps to concentrate correction signals. Across role-play dialogue and medical reasoning QA scenarios, CaMOPD performs best in general recovery over baselines while maintaining domain-specific behavior. Gradient coherence analyses further support the intended effect of CaMOPD in producing more coherent correction signals.

AI.tldr: CaMOPD enhances general capability recovery in Multi-Teacher On-Policy Distillation under incomplete teacher-coverage by decoupled alternating training and gap-based sample selection, outperforming baselines in role-play and medical reasoning tasks while preserving domain behavior.
AI.motivation: Domain specialization often harms general capabilities; when teacher prompts don't match hidden training data, standard MOPD struggles to recover general behavior. The work seeks to recover general capabilities using readily available proxy prompts, avoiding reconstruction of hidden distributions.
AI.method: CaMOPD introduces decoupled alternating training with dedicated updates for general recovery, periodic review of domain prompts for preservation, and gap-based sample selection that prioritizes samples with larger averaged token-level teacher–student log-probability gaps to amplify correction signals.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across role-play dialogue and medical reasoning QA shows CaMOPD achieves superior general recovery while maintaining domain-specific behavior; gradient coherence analyses indicate more coherent correction signals under CaMOPD compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: CaMOPD effectively mitigates recovery–preservation conflicts and weak-signal flattening inherent in standard MOPD under incomplete teacher-coverage, delivering improved general capabilities without sacrificing domain-specific performance.

title: Gaussian-Voxel Duet: A Dual-Scaffolding Hybrid Representation for Fast and Accurate Monocular Surface Reconstruction
summary: While 3D Gaussian Splatting has achieved remarkable success in photorealistic novel view synthesis, its pursuit of fast and high-fidelity 3D reconstruction has long been constrained by a trade-off between geometric accuracy and optimization efficiency. Methods specialized in image rendering converge quickly at the cost of imperfect geometry caused by superfluous primitives overfitting training views, while methods integrating neural signed-distance field (SDF) for better geometry incur prohibitive training costs. In this paper, we attempt to strike a better trade-off by tethering scaffold-anchored Gaussians to a jointly optimized sparse voxel scaffold. This hybrid Gaussian-Voxel representation explicitly confines anchored Gaussians to a narrow band around surfaces defined by voxelized SDFs, which effectively improves representation efficiency and condenses floating Gaussians without sacrificing geometry quality. An implicit surface tethering loss further pulls individual Gaussian primitives closer to SDF-induced surfaces in a mutually regularized manner for improved reconstruction accuracy. Extensive experiments on diverse real-world indoor scenes from ScanNet++, ScanNetv2, and DeepBlending datasets demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art surface reconstruction quality as well as superior novel view synthesis against leading baselines, while maintaining fast training convergence and real-time rendering. Code will be available at https://github.com/duzh11/VoxelGS.

AI.tldr: Hybrid Gaussian-Voxel (VoxelGS) ties Gaussians to a voxelized SDF scaffold to boost 3D reconstruction and rendering efficiency, achieving state-of-the-art geometry and fast convergence on indoor scenes.
AI.motivation: Mitigate the trade-off in 3D Gaussian Splatting between geometric accuracy and optimization efficiency. Pure rendering-focused methods overfit training views; SDF-based methods improve geometry but incur high training costs. A scalable, accurate representation is needed.
AI.method: Anchor scaffold-anchored Gaussians to a sparse voxel scaffold; confine Gaussians to a narrow band around surfaces defined by voxelized SDFs; employ an implicit-surface tethering loss to mutually regularize Gaussians toward the SDF surfaces; end-to-end joint optimization; evaluate on real-world indoor scenes; release code.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art surface reconstruction quality and superior novel view synthesis on ScanNet++, ScanNetv2, and DeepBlending; demonstrates fast training convergence and real-time rendering; extensive ablations support effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid Gaussian-voxel representation improves efficiency without sacrificing geometry, delivering robust 3D reconstruction and fast rendering in real-world indoor scenes; practical and scalable approach for high-fidelity NeRF-like systems.

title: Position: AI Safety Requires Effective Controllability
summary: AI safety is still largely framed as alignment: training models to follow human preferences, safety policies, and normative constraints. That framing has improved the behavior of modern language models, but aligned behavior does not by itself guarantee that a deployed agent can be stopped, overridden, or constrained once it operates in open-ended, interactive, and tool-using environments. A system may be safe in expectation and still fail to yield to explicit runtime authority under conflicting instructions, long-horizon execution, adversarial inputs, or risky tool use. This position paper argues that AI safety therefore requires controllability as a first-class objective. We define \emph{controllability} as the ability of an AI system to remain reliably interruptible, overridable, redirectable, and constrainable by explicit control signals at runtime while preserving ordinary utility when such signals are absent. To study this gap, we introduce \controlbench{}, a benchmark for evaluating controllability failures in high-risk agentic scenarios. Experiments with OpenClaw-based agents show that current alignment and guardrail mechanisms reduce risk, but often fail to provide persistent, authoritative, and enforceable runtime control. We therefore propose a control-centric architectural framework that highlights explicit control planes, runtime intervention pathways, persistent control states, and auditable decision interfaces as key design principles for future controllable AI systems.

AI.tldr: Controllability is essential alongside alignment for safe AI in open-ended, tool-using settings. The paper introduces controllability and a benchmark (controlbench) to evaluate runtime interruptibility, overridability, redirectability, and constraining ability. Empirical results with OpenClaw-based agents show alignment reduces risk but often fails to provide persistent, authoritative control. A control-centric architectural framework with explicit control planes, intervention pathways, persistent states, and auditable interfaces is proposed for future controllable AI systems.
AI.motivation: Alignment alone does not guarantee runtime control in interactive, high-risk, tool-using agents. There is a gap between safe behavior in expectation and enforceable authority during operation, especially under conflicting instructions or adversarial inputs.
AI.method: Define controllability as explicit runtime control over AI actions; introduce controlbench as a benchmark for controllability failures; conduct experiments with OpenClaw-based agents to assess how current alignment/guardrails perform; propose a control-centric architecture emphasizing explicit control planes, intervention pathways, persistent control states, and auditable decision interfaces.
AI.result: Current alignment and guardrails reduce risk but do not ensure persistent, authoritative runtime control; the controlbench experiments reveal controllability gaps in high-risk agentic scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Controllability should be a first-class objective in AI safety. The paper outlines design principles (explicit control planes, runtime intervention pathways, persistent control states, auditable interfaces) to build controllable AI systems and suggests future work to operationalize these concepts.

title: MedVol-R1: Reward-Driven Evidence Grounding for Volumetric Reasoning Segmentation
summary: Volumetric Reasoning Segmentation (VRS) aims to segment a target region in a 3D medical scan from a free-form clinical query, where the referent is often implicit and requires both medical knowledge and volume-grounded reasoning. Existing methods typically rely on specialized segmentation tokens to connect language with mask decoding, but this coupling collapses the decision process into opaque latent representations, limiting interpretability and generalization to diverse narrative expressions. In this paper, we present MedVol-R1, a reinforcement learning-based framework for VRS that explicitly decouples evidence grounding from volumetric delineation: the LVLM grounds clinical reasoning to a verifiable 2D evidence anchor (key axial slice and 2D bounding boxes), which is then propagated into a coherent 3D mask by a frozen MedSAM2 module. We train MedVol-R1 with cold-start supervised fine-tuning followed by GRPO, guided by a multi-component reward that encourages informative evidence selection, accurate 2D spatial grounding, and cross-slice volumetric coherence, without requiring costly chain-of-thought annotations. Experiments on CT-ORG, AbdomenCT-1K, and KiTS23 from the M3D-Seg benchmark demonstrate that MedVol-R1 consistently outperforms strong baselines and achieves state-of-the-art performance, with reinforcement learning providing clear gains over pure supervised fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: MedVol-R1 is an RL-based volumetric reasoning segmentation framework that decouples evidence grounding from 3D segmentation by grounding to 2D evidence anchors (key axial slice and 2D bounding boxes) and propagating to a 3D mask via a frozen MedSAM2 module; trained with cold-start supervised fine-tuning followed by GRPO using a multi-component reward, achieving state-of-the-art on CT-ORG, AbdomenCT-1K, and KiTS23 in M3D-Seg.
AI.motivation: Address the limited interpretability and generalization of existing VRS methods, which couple language and mask decoding through opaque latent tokens. By grounding to verifiable 2D evidence anchors, the approach enhances transparency and cross-expression robustness.
AI.method: LVLM-based grounding to a 2D evidence anchor (key axial slice + 2D bounding boxes); propagate to a coherent 3D mask using a frozen MedSAM2 module. Training includes cold-start supervised fine-tuning followed by GRPO (policy optimization) guided by a multi-component reward that promotes informative evidence selection, accurate 2D grounding, and cross-slice volumetric coherence; no chain-of-thought annotations required.
AI.result: MedVol-R1 consistently outperforms strong baselines and achieves state-of-the-art performance on CT-ORG, AbdomenCT-1K, and KiTS23 (M3D-Seg); RL gains over purely supervised fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling evidence grounding from volumetric delineation via 2D anchors and optimizing with RL yields improved interpretability and performance for VRS across multiple CT datasets, with effective cross-slice coherence and compatibility with existing 2D anchor and MedSAM2 pipelines.

title: SIKA-GP: Accelerating Gaussian Process Inference with Sparse Inducing Kernel Approximations for Bayesian Deep Learning
comment: 20 pages, 8 figures; accepted to International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML) 2026
summary: Gaussian processes (GPs) provide a principled Bayesian framework for uncertainty estimation, but their computational complexity severely limits scalability to large datasets. We propose SIKA-GP, which accelerates GP inference using sparse inducing kernel approximations based on a dyadic ordered template basis, incurring only ${O}(\log M)$ complexity dependence on the number of inducing points. Our approach constructs compact and expressive kernel representations from sparsely activated bases, enabling efficient tensorized GPU computation and seamless integration with modern large-scale models. SIKA-GP can be naturally embedded into Bayesian neural networks (BNNs) with sparse activations, yielding significant speedups in both training and inference without sacrificing predictive performance. The method naturally extends to deep feature learning, addressing the scalability challenges introduced by deep architectures and high-dimensional feature representations. Empirical results on vision and transformer-based language benchmarks demonstrate that our approach consistently delivers fast and accurate GP models, providing a principled path toward scalable kernel learning.

AI.tldr: SIKA-GP proposes a dyadic inducing-kernel approach that reduces GP inference to O(log M) complexity, enabling GPU-accelerated, scalable kernel learning and seamless use in Bayesian neural nets and deep feature scenarios, with fast, accurate results on vision and language benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Gaussian processes provide principled uncertainty estimation but scale poorly to large datasets and deep feature representations. There is a need for scalable, expressive kernel representations and efficient integration with modern large-scale models.
AI.method: Introduce sparse inducing kernel approximations built from a dyadic ordered template basis. This yields compact representations from sparsely activated bases and O(log M) complexity with respect to the number of inducing points. The approach supports tensorized GPU computation, can be embedded into Bayesian neural networks with sparse activations, and extends naturally to deep feature learning.
AI.result: Empirical results on vision and transformer-based language benchmarks show that SIKA-GP delivers fast and accurate GP models, with significant speedups in both training and inference while maintaining predictive performance.
AI.conclusion: SIKA-GP provides a scalable, principled framework for kernel learning that integrates with modern large-scale models, addressing GP scalability in deep feature settings and enabling efficient uncertainty-aware modeling.

title: Scaling, Benchmarking, and Reasoning of Vision-Language Agents for Mobile GUI Navigation
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have shown rapid progress in mobile GUI navigation. This paper presents a systematic study of data scaling, benchmarking, and reasoning for VLM-based agents in this domain. To facilitate rigorous evaluation, we introduce HyperTrack, a large-scale dataset with over 16000 real-world tasks across more than 650 Chinese mobile applications, along with GUIEvalKit, an open-source toolkit for unified benchmarking of VLMs on offline GUI navigation tasks. Using HyperTrack, we analyze the effects of training data scale on both supervised and reinforcement-based finetuning. Our results show that reinforcement-based finetuning consistently outperforms supervised finetuning, particularly in out-of-domain settings, highlighting the synergy between data scaling and reinforcement learning. Leveraging GUIEvalKit, we further benchmark state-of-the-art (SOTA) VLMs and analyze how interaction history and reasoning capabilities influence task completion. Together, HyperTrack and GUIEvalKit provide a comprehensive platform for developing and evaluating VLM agents in mobile GUI navigation tasks.

AI.tldr: Introduces HyperTrack and GUIEvalKit for rigorous VLM-based mobile GUI navigation evaluation, demonstrates RL finetuning's superiority with data scaling, and analyzes interaction history and reasoning; provides a comprehensive benchmark platform.
AI.motivation: Address the need for large-scale, real-world data, standardized offline benchmarking, and analysis of reasoning/interaction dynamics to advance VLM agents in mobile GUI navigation.
AI.method: Build HyperTrack: a real-world dataset with >16k tasks across 650+ Chinese apps; release GUIEvalKit for unified offline GUI navigation benchmarking; conduct experiments comparing supervised vs reinforcement-based finetuning across data scales; benchmark SOTA VLMs and study the role of interaction history and reasoning.
