AI.result: Reinforcement-based finetuning consistently outperforms supervised finetuning, especially in out-of-domain settings; larger training data scales benefit RL, revealing synergy between data scaling and reinforcement learning; interaction history and reasoning capabilities significantly influence task success; HyperTrack and GUIEvalKit enable robust benchmarking and evaluation.
AI.conclusion: HyperTrack and GUIEvalKit together form a comprehensive platform for developing and evaluating VLM agents in mobile GUI navigation, providing insights into data-scale impacts and RL advantages, and enabling rigorous cross-model comparisons.

title: MSCGC-KAN: Multi-scale Causal Graph Convolution and Kolmogorov-Arnold Feature Mapping for EEG Emotion Recognition
summary: Electroencephalogram (EEG)-based emotion recognition is an important affective computing task, and recent EEG foundation models provide useful generic representations for downstream adaptation. However, under the fine-tuning setting, three limitations remain prominent: insufficient modeling of multi-scale emotional dynamics, inadequate exploitation of inter-channel functional connectivity, and the limited expressive power of simple linear classification heads. To address these issues, this paper proposes a new EEG emotion recognition method, termed MSCGC-KAN, which introduces a structured task head composed of multi-scale causal graph convolution and Kolmogorov--Arnold feature mapping. Built on a pre-trained CBraMod backbone, MSCGC-KAN enhances downstream adaptation by jointly strengthening multi-scale temporal modeling, learnable inter-channel connectivity modeling, and nonlinear discriminative mapping within a compact task-specific head. This design preserves the representation advantage of the foundation model while making the classifier more sensitive to emotion-related spatiotemporal patterns. Extensive experiments are conducted on the public FACED and SEED-VII datasets. The proposed method achieves a balanced accuracy of 60.66\%, a Cohen's Kappa of 0.5525, and a weighted F1-score of 60.40\% on FACED, and obtains 33.27\%, 0.2223, and 33.64\%, respectively, on SEED-VII. Compared with the CBraMod+Linear baseline, the balanced accuracy is improved by 5.91 and 2.03 percentage points on the two datasets, respectively. These results indicate that structured task-head design is an effective way to improve EEG emotion recognition when fine-tuning pre-trained EEG models.

AI.tldr: MSCGC-KAN adds a structured task head with multi-scale causal graph convolution and Kolmogorov–Arnold feature mapping on top of a pre-trained CBraMod backbone for EEG emotion recognition, improving multi-scale temporal modeling, inter-channel connectivity, and nonlinear classification; validated on FACED and SEED-VII with notable gains over CBraMod+Linear.
AI.motivation: To overcome three main fine-tuning limitations of EEG foundation models: (1) insufficient multi-scale emotional dynamics modeling, (2) inadequate exploitation of inter-channel functional connectivity, and (3) limited expressive power of simple linear classification heads.
AI.method: Introduce MSCGC-KAN, a structured task head consisting of multi-scale causal graph convolution and Kolmogorov–Arnold feature mapping, built atop a pre-trained CBraMod backbone, to jointly enhance temporal modeling, learnable inter-channel connectivity, and nonlinear discriminative mapping in a compact head.
AI.result: On FACED: balanced accuracy 60.66%, Cohen's Kappa 0.5525, weighted F1 60.40%. On SEED-VII: balanced accuracy 33.27%, Cohen's Kappa 0.2223, weighted F1 33.64%. Relative to CBraMod+Linear, BA improved by 5.91 and 2.03 pp on FACED and SEED-VII respectively.
AI.conclusion: A structured task-head design can effectively enhance EEG emotion recognition during fine-tuning of pre-trained EEG models by preserving backbone representations while increasing sensitivity to emotion-related spatiotemporal patterns.

title: Open-Weight LLM Fine-Tuning Defenses are Susceptible to Simple Attacks
summary: Recent defenses for safeguarding open-weight large language models (LLMs) are intended to prevent adversarial usage. Underlying these defenses is an assumption that new harmful behavior is learned through fine-tuning rather than elicited by jailbreaking the model. Yet, pretrained LLMs already encode substantial harmful knowledge across many domains, which raises an important question: can an adversary jailbreak safeguarded models, to achieve harmful usage without fine-tuning at all? In this paper, we show that open-weight safeguards are susceptible to simpler strategies that, despite being well known, have not been systematically evaluated against these safeguards. Specifically, we evaluate two low-cost attacks--abliteration and prefilling--that do not rely on gradient-based optimization. Across three harmfulness evaluation benchmarks (BeaverTails, HarmBench, and AdvBench), these attacks increase attack success rates against safeguarded open-weight models from below 10\% to a range of 16%-96%. To mitigate this vulnerability, we introduce abliteration-resistant tuning (ART), which incorporates an abliteration-based objective into training. ART can be layered onto existing defenses and reduces the success rates of abliteration, prefilling, and their combination by 10%-20%. These findings indicate that the attack surface for open-weight models is broader than previously characterized, and that evaluations of safeguarding defenses should incorporate a more diverse set of attack strategies beyond adversarial fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: Non-gradient jailbreaks (abliteration and prefilling) can defeat safeguarded open-weight LLMs, increasing attack success from <10% to 16–96% across three benchmarks. The authors propose Abliteration-Resistant Tuning (ART) to mitigate these attacks, reducing success rates by 10–20%. They argue safeguards must be evaluated against diverse, low-cost attack strategies, not just adversarial fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Challenging the assumption that harmful behavior in safeguarded open-weight LLMs only arises through post-training fine-tuning. Investigates whether simple, non-gradient strategies can elicit harmful outputs from safeguarded models and how to strengthen defenses accordingly.
AI.method: Evaluate two low-cost, non-gradient attacks—abliteration and prefilling—against safeguarded open-weight LLMs. Use three harm-evaluation benchmarks (BeaverTails, HarmBench, AdvBench) to measure attack success. Propose ART by integrating an abliteration-based objective into training, and assess its effectiveness when layered on existing defenses.
AI.result: Abliteration and prefilling dramatically raise attack success: from <10% to 16–96% across benchmarks. ART reduces the success of abliteration, prefilling, and their combination by about 10–20%.
AI.conclusion: Open-weight safeguards are more vulnerable than previously thought; evaluation should include diverse, non-gradient attacks beyond adversarial fine-tuning. ART offers a practical mitigation layered onto existing defenses, but continual reassessment of attack modalities is needed.

title: ICCU: In-Context Continual Unlearning via Pattern-Induced Refusal Rules
summary: Machine unlearning aims to remove the influence of specific data from trained language models. In real-world deployments, unlearning requests often arrive sequentially, which challenges existing fine-tuning-based methods: fine-tuning each request is costly, accumulates utility loss, and may cause cross-request interference. To address these issues, we propose ICCU (In-Context Continual Unlearning), an in-context continual unlearning framework that induces readable refusal rules from unlearning datasets and applies them at inference time either as a filter or via the system prompt, without modifying model parameters. Because rules are accumulated as an order-independent union, ICCU is compositional and free of cross-request interference, and the original forget-set data can be discarded after rule induction. Extensive experiments show that ICCU effectively suppresses target knowledge while preserving utility, scales across sequential requests, and remains robust to paraphrased and cross-lingual queries.

AI.tldr: ICCU enables in-context continual unlearning by inducing readable refusal rules from unlearning data and applying them at inference-time, without changing model parameters, ensuring sequential requests compose without interference.
AI.motivation: In real deployments, unlearning requests arrive one after another. Fine-tuning per request is costly, degrades utility, and risks cross-request interference; a parameter-free, scalable, and interference-free solution is needed.
AI.method: ICCU learns order-independent, readable refusal rules from unlearning datasets and uses them as a filter or system prompt during inference. Rules are accumulated as a union, discard original forget-set data after rule induction, and no model fine-tuning is performed. The approach is robust to paraphrased and cross-lingual queries.
AI.result: Experimental results show that ICCU effectively suppresses target knowledge while preserving model utility, scales across sequential unlearning requests, and is robust to paraphrase and cross-lingual queries.
AI.conclusion: ICCU provides a compositional, interference-free, parameter-free unlearning framework suitable for sequential requests, avoiding cross-request interference and without retraining.

title: DelowlightSplat: Feed-Forward Gaussian Splatting for Lowlight 3D Scene Reconstruction
summary: Novel-view synthesis and 3D reconstruction from sparse posed images are central to robotics and AR/VR. Yet, feed-forward 3D Gaussian reconstruction fails under lowlight due to noise, color shifts, and unreliable correspondence. We propose DelowlightSplat, a lowlight-aware feed-forward Gaussian splatting framework for clean novel-view rendering. We build a controllable multi-view lowlight benchmark by degrading only context views while keeping target views clean. We introduce a lightweight Lowlight Adapter for residual enhancement to improve matchability, and couple it with cost-volume-based multi-view inference to directly predict clean 3D Gaussians. Experiments show that DelowlightSplat significantly outperforms previous feed-forward method and two-stage pipeline under lowlight conditions.

AI.tldr: DelowlightSplat is a lowlight-aware feed-forward Gaussian splatting method for clean novel-view rendering and 3D reconstruction, using a Lowlight Adapter and cost-volume multi-view inference to outperform prior feed-forward and two-stage pipelines under low-light conditions.
AI.motivation: Low-light conditions degrade Gaussian-based 3D reconstruction due to noise, color shifts, and unreliable feature correspondences, prompting the need for a robust, feed-forward, lowlight-aware approach.
AI.method: Introduce DelowlightSplat, a Gaussian splatting framework augmented with a lightweight Lowlight Adapter for residual enhancement. Combine with cost-volume-based multi-view inference to directly predict clean 3D Gaussians. Create a controllable lowlight benchmark by degrading context views while keeping target views clean.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations show DelowlightSplat significantly outperforms existing feed-forward baselines and a two-stage pipeline under low-light scenarios.
AI.conclusion: A lowlight-aware, feed-forward Gaussian splatting approach with residual enhancement and multi-view cost-volume inference improves robustness for novel-view synthesis and 3D reconstruction in challenging lighting, enabling more reliable robotics/AR/VR applications.

title: Recursive Flow Matching
summary: Generative models have emerged as a powerful paradigm for solving physics systems and modeling complex spatiotemporal dynamics. However, achieving high physical accuracy without incurring high computational cost remains a fundamental challenge, as existing approaches face a critical speed-fidelity trade-off. In this work, we introduce Recursive Flow Matching (RecFM), a generative framework for forecasting complex spatiotemporal dynamics. RecFM enforces self-consistency to align trajectories across discretization scales, reducing discretization errors and improving performance across metrics for physics-based tasks. To our knowledge, this is the first method to achieve high-fidelity one- and few-step (2-4 step) dynamic generation for scientific systems with performance comparable to state-of-the-art multi-step solvers. Across challenging scientific benchmarks, RecFM achieves up to a 20$\times$ speedup over leading diffusion-based emulators while improving predictive accuracy. Furthermore, RecFM reduces mean squared error by over 15% compared to vanilla flow matching, offering a scalable and efficient solution for real-time scientific emulation.

AI.tldr: RecFM enables high-fidelity, one- to few-step forecasting for complex spatiotemporal dynamics with large speedups over diffusion-based emulators and improvements over vanilla flow matching.
AI.motivation: Need to resolve the speed–fidelity trade-off in physics-emulator models; reduce discretization error; enable real-time emulation.
AI.method: Recursive Flow Matching enforcing self-consistency across discretization scales; align trajectories across scales; suitable for 1-4 step generation; improved performance vs standard flow matching; faster than diffusion models.
AI.result: Up to 20× speedup over leading diffusion-based emulators; improved predictive accuracy; MSE reduced by >15% vs vanilla flow matching across benchmarks; high fidelity with few steps comparable to multi-step solvers.
AI.conclusion: RecFM offers a scalable, efficient framework for real-time scientific emulation with high fidelity in few-step generation, addressing speed-fidelity trade-off and matching or beating state-of-the-art solvers.

title: StepOPSD: Step-Aware Online Preference Distillation for Agent Reinforcement Learning
summary: Reinforcement learning for multi-turn agents suffers from a credit-assignment mismatch: rewards are sparse and trajectory-level, while success often hinges on a few local decisions. Existing online policy distillation (OPD) provides denser token-level supervision, but typically treats heterogeneous agent trajectories as monolithic strings rather than causal interaction units. We present StepOPSD, a post-rollout preference self-distillation framework that takes the agent step as the unit of credit redistribution. StepOPSD decomposes trajectories into action-centered step segments, rescoring them under hindsight-enriched teacher contexts and converting token-level log-probability gaps into sign-preserving advantage shaping with a normalized per-step credit budget before the GRPO update. Across ALFWorld and Search-QA with Qwen3-1.7B and Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct, StepOPSD attains best or second-best results on subsets most sensitive to local causal errors, including first-place performance on ALFWorld Heat (79.1%), PickTwo (95.0%), Search-QA TriviaQA (61.6%), and tied-best performance on HotpotQA (40.4%). The results further reveal a consistent two-knob law: smaller α_clip acts as a broadly stabilizing local trust region, whereas the optimal global mixing strength λ_mix remains task-dependent. These findings suggest that step-aware distillation is most useful when trajectory-level rewards are weakly aligned with the local action that determines downstream success.

AI.tldr: StepOPSD introduces step-level credit redistribution via post-rollout preference self-distillation, decomposing trajectories into action-centered steps, and using hindsight-enriched contexts to reshape step-level advantages before policy updates. It achieves strong results on multi-turn RL benchmarks, and reveals a two-knob law: smaller per-step clipping (alpha_clip) stabilizes local trust regions, while optimal global mixing (lambda_mix) is task-dependent.
AI.motivation: Reinforcement learning for multi-turn agents suffers from credit-assignment mismatch: sparse, trajectory-level rewards make it hard to attribute success to local decisions. Existing online policy distillation provides token-level supervision but treats heterogeneous trajectories monolithically. There is a need for step-level, causally-aware credit redistribution.
AI.method: StepOPSD is a post-rollout, step-level self-distillation framework. It segments trajectories into action-centered steps, rescoring them with hindsight-enriched teacher contexts. It converts log-probability gaps into sign-preserving advantage shaping, applies a normalized per-step credit budget, and performs a GRPO (presumably gradient-based policy optimization) update.
AI.result: On ALFWorld and Search-QA benchmarks with Qwen3-1.7B and Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct, StepOPSD achieves top or near-top performance on subsets most sensitive to local causal errors, including first place on ALFWorld Heat (79.1%), PickTwo (95.0%), TriviaQA (61.6%), and tied-best on HotpotQA (40.4%). A consistent two-knob law emerges: smaller alpha_clip stabilizes local trust regions, while lambda_mix's optimal value is task-dependent.
AI.conclusion: Step-aware distillation addresses the misalignment between trajectory-level rewards and local action impact, improving robustness to local causal errors. StepOPSD provides a practical approach to densify supervision without discarding trajectory context, and its two-knob law informs hyperparameter selection across tasks.

title: Attenuation-Resilient Alternating Optimization for Laparoscopic Liver Landmark Detection
comment: This paper has been accepted by MICCAI 2026
summary: Liver surface landmark detection is a fundamental prerequisite for anatomical guidance in laparoscopic liver surgery. However, it remains unreliable in practice due to two pervasive challenges: illumination attenuation in underexposed regions and the structural mismatch between pixel-wise localization and continuous curvilinear geometry. To address these limitations, we propose A2ONet, an attenuation-resilient alternating optimization network for robust liver landmark detection. To mitigate illumination attenuation, A2ONet embraces an illumination field compensation (IFC) block that adaptively enhances dark regions while preserving structural consistency. Meanwhile, we introduce a lightweight frequency-orientation selective filter (FOSF) to suppress repetitive texture interference and preserve salient curvilinear cues. Building upon these resilient representations, we design an alternating seg-curve optimization (ASCO) decoder that iteratively couples dense segmentation with explicit curve modeling, enabling mutual guidance to optimize both structural continuity and endpoint localization. Extensive evaluations on L3D-2K, L3D, and P2ILF demonstrate consistent improvements over competitive methods, establishing a more reliable foundation for intraoperative anatomy guidance. Our code will be available at https://github.com/hyperiondk115/A2ONet.

AI.tldr: A2ONet proposes an attenuation-resilient framework for liver landmark detection in laparoscopy, combining illumination field compensation, a frequency-orientation selective filter to reduce texture noise, and an alternating seg-curve decoder to jointly optimize segmentation and curve endpoints, with strong results on multiple benchmarks and open-source code.
AI.motivation: Illumination attenuation in underexposed regions and the mismatch between pixel-wise landmarks and continuous curvilinear liver anatomy hinder reliable landmark detection.
AI.method: An attenuation-resilient alternating optimization network (A2ONet) comprising (1) an illumination field compensation (IFC) block to enhance dark regions while keeping structure, (2) a lightweight frequency-orientation selective filter (FOSF) to suppress repetitive textures and preserve curvilinear cues, and (3) an alternating seg-curve optimization (ASCO) decoder that iteratively couples dense segmentation with explicit curve modeling to improve continuity and endpoint localization.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations on L3D-2K, L3D, and P2ILF show consistent improvements over competitive methods; code will be released at the authors' GitHub.
AI.conclusion: The proposed A2ONet provides a more reliable foundation for intraoperative anatomy guidance by robustly handling illumination variation and structuring landmark geometry, validated on multiple datasets.

title: Aligning Few-Step Generative Models by Amortizing Sample-based Variational Inference
summary: Aligning a few-step generative model is challenging, since existing alignment frameworks typically rely on restrictive assumptions: a tractable likelihood, a specific ODE/SDE solver, or a particular model family. We introduce FAV, Few-step Generative Models Alignment via Sample-based Variational Inference, a general alignment framework that requires only sample access to the generator and the reference distribution. We cast alignment as sampling from a reward-tilted distribution anchored to a reference distribution. We leverage Stein Variational Gradient Descent as a sample-based variational inference scheme and amortize its particle updates into the generator parameters via fixed-point regression. We evaluate FAV on two domains: robotics manipulation and image generator alignment. On generative policy alignment for robotic manipulation, FAV outperforms prevailing policy extraction baselines across 56 offline and 30 offline-to-online RL tasks. For image generator alignment, FAV fine-tunes diverse few-step backbones, including GAN, drifting model, consistency models, and flow maps, scaling from ImageNet-$256$ to 1024$^2$ text-to-image synthesis. Code is available at https://github.com/Jaewoopudding/FAV.

AI.tldr: FAV introduces Few-step Generative Models Alignment via Sample-based Variational Inference, a general, sample-access-only framework that uses Stein Variational Gradient Descent with amortized updates to align few-step generators, showing strong results in robotics manipulation and image generation.
AI.motivation: Existing alignment methods rely on restrictive assumptions (tractable likelihoods, fixed ODE/SDE solvers, or specific model families). A general, sample-based alignment framework is needed that only requires sample access to the generator and the reference distribution.
AI.method: Formulates alignment as sampling from a reward-tilted distribution anchored to a reference distribution. Uses Stein Variational Gradient Descent as a sample-based variational inference method and amortizes particle updates into the generator parameters via fixed-point regression.
AI.result: In robotics manipulation, FAV outperforms policy extraction baselines across 56 offline and 30 offline-to-online RL tasks. In image generation, it fine-tunes diverse few-step backbones (GANs, drifting models, consistency models, and flow maps) from ImageNet-256 to 1024^2 text-to-image synthesis. Code is available at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: FAV provides a general, sample-based alignment framework for few-step generative models that reduces reliance on restrictive assumptions and demonstrates cross-domain effectiveness in both robotics and image generation, highlighting broad applicability to future few-step generative architectures.

title: VitaBench 2.0: Evaluating Personalized and Proactive Agents in Long-Term User Interactions
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have evolved into interactive agents that collaborate with users in real-world tasks. Effective collaboration in such settings increasingly depends on understanding the user beyond what is explicitly stated, as user intent is often reflected in fragmented daily interactions and requires both personalized modeling and proactive interaction. However, existing agent benchmarks primarily evaluate reasoning and tool use, largely overlooking the challenges of inferring and leveraging user preferences in realistic scenarios. To address this gap, we introduce VitaBench 2.0, a benchmark for evaluating personalized and proactive agent behavior in long-term user interactions. In VitaBench 2.0, tasks are organized as temporally ordered sequences for individual users, where preferences are embedded in fragmented and heterogeneous interactions. Successful completion of tasks requires the agent to continuously extract, utilize, and update user preferences from these interactions. We further evaluate proactiveness through tasks that require agents to recognize missing information and actively acquire it from users or environments before making decisions. To support systematic analysis, we provide an extensible memory interface that enables controlled comparison across different memory architectures. We benchmark a diverse set of frontier proprietary and open-source LLMs. Results show that real-world personalization remains highly challenging even for state-of-the-art models, revealing a substantial gap between current capabilities and practical requirements. Extensive analysis further reveals the failure modes and capability bottlenecks of current agents in real-world personalized decision-making, providing insights for future model improvements.

AI.tldr: VitaBench 2.0 provides a memory-enabled benchmark for evaluating personalized and proactive agent behavior in long-term, user-specific tasks; it reveals that state-of-the-art LLMs struggle to infer and act on user preferences in realistic interactions.
AI.motivation: To move beyond isolated reasoning and tool-use benchmarks by evaluating how agents infer, maintain, and use user preferences from fragmented, heterogeneous interactions over time, and to assess proactiveness in acquiring missing information.
AI.method: Design of VitaBench 2.0: tasks are temporally ordered sequences for individual users with preferences embedded in fragmented interactions. Agents must continuously extract, utilize, and update user preferences. Proactiveness is tested via tasks requiring recognition of missing information and active information acquisition from users or environments. An extensible memory interface enables controlled comparison across memory architectures. Evaluation spans frontier proprietary and open-source LLMs.
AI.result: Real-world personalization remains highly challenging; there is a substantial gap between state-of-the-art capabilities and practical requirements for personalized decision-making. Failure modes and capability bottlenecks are analyzed to guide future improvements.
AI.conclusion: VitaBench 2.0 provides a principled framework and dataset for evaluating long-horizon, personalized and proactive agent behavior, highlighting the need for improved user preference modeling, memory architectures, and proactive interaction strategies to close the gap to real-world use.

title: JetViT: Efficient High-Resolution Vision Transformer with Post-Training Attention Search
summary: We introduce JetViT, a novel family of hybrid-architecture Vision Transformer (ViT) models that match the accuracy of state-of-the-art full-attention vision foundation models while achieving substantially higher inference efficiency on high-resolution images. At the core of our approach is Post-Training Attention Search, a post-training acceleration framework that converts pre-trained full-attention ViTs into efficient hybrid-attention variants by identifying and replacing redundant full-attention blocks with linear or window-attention blocks. By inheriting the MLP and attention weights from the base model, Post-Training Attention Search efficiently explores the architectural design space through three key steps: (1) optimizing the linear-attention block design; (2) finding the best combination of linear-attention and window-attention blocks; and (3) identifying and preserving critical full-attention blocks. We evaluate JetViT on two representative high-resolution vision foundation models, DINOv3 and DepthAnythingV2. On the NVIDIA H100 GPU, JetViT achieves up to 1.79x higher throughput and up to 44.81% lower latency without sacrificing accuracy. We will release our code and accelerated ViT models soon.

AI.tldr: JetViT is a post-training framework that converts full-attention ViTs into hybrid-architecture ViTs by replacing redundant blocks with linear or window-attention, achieving higher throughput on high-res images with comparable accuracy.
AI.motivation: Reduce inference cost of high-resolution vision transformers without sacrificing accuracy by lowering the computational burden of full self-attention.
AI.method: Post-Training Attention Search (PTAS): keeps MLP and attention weights from the base ViT; searches design of linear-attention blocks, mixes them with window-attention blocks, and preserves critical full-attention blocks; evaluates on DINOv3 and DepthAnythingV2 using NVIDIA H100 GPUs.
AI.result: Up to 1.79x throughput improvement and up to 44.81% lower latency, with accuracy maintained; demonstrates effective hybrid-architecture ViTs for high-resolution vision foundations; code and models to be released.
AI.conclusion: Post-training architectural search can yield efficient hybrid ViTs that retain accuracy while significantly boosting inference speed on high-resolution images; practical for deployment on modern GPUs; future work includes code release and broader evaluation.

title: Linear and Neural Dueling Bandits with Delayed Feedback
summary:
Contextual dueling bandits form a cornerstone of preference-based decision-making, with critical applications in
  recommender systems and large language model alignment. However, standard algorithms rely on the idealized assumption
  of immediate feedback, a condition frequently violated in real-world scenarios such as prompt optimization. This
  setting introduces a unique theoretical challenge: unlike linear bandits, dueling bandit estimators lack closed-form
  solutions, rendering naive adaptations of standard weighting techniques biased. To address this, we formalize the
  problem of Contextual Dueling Bandits with Stochastic Delayed Feedback and propose two novel algorithms: Linear
  (LDB-DF) and Neural (NDB-DF) Dueling Bandits with Delayed Feedback. Central to our approach is a novel estimator that
  integrates an Inverse Probability Weighting (IPW) mechanism directly into the loss function, ensuring unbiased
  correction for delayed or missing feedback. We provide comprehensive theoretical analysis, establishing an
  O(d*sqrt(T)) regret bound for the linear setting and sub-linear guarantees for the neural setting. Extensive
  experiments on both simulated and real-world datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our propose.

AI.tldr: We propose Linear Dueling Bandits with Delayed Feedback (LDB-DF) and Neural Dueling Bandits with Delayed Feedback (NDB-DF) for contextual dueling bandits with stochastic delayed feedback, introducing an IPW-based estimator integrated into the loss to correct for delay/missing feedback; provides theoretical regret guarantees and empirical validation.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of unbiased estimators and closed-form solutions in contextual dueling bandits under stochastic delays, improving learning efficiency and performance in real-world delayed-feedback settings (e.g., prompt optimization).
AI.method: Two algorithms: LDB-DF (linear) and NDB-DF (neural). Introduce an IPW-based loss formulation that incorporates delay probabilities directly into the optimization. Prove regret bounds: O(d sqrt(T)) for linear; sub-linear for neural. Empirical evaluation on synthetic and real-world datasets.
AI.result: Theoretical guarantees with sublinear regret; empirical results showing effectiveness over baselines in both simulated and real data.
AI.conclusion: IPW-integrated loss enables unbiased correction for delayed feedback in contextual dueling bandits; LDB-DF and NDB-DF achieve favorable regret bounds and demonstrate practical performance on benchmark tasks.

title: Detecting Is Not Resolving: The Monitoring Control Gap in Retrieval Augmented LLMs
summary: Retrieval-augmented LLMs are deployed for tasks where evidence quality determines action safety, yet evaluation protocols assume that single-turn robustness predicts robustness when evidence accumulates across turns. We show this assumption is fundamentally incorrect. Models exhibit a monitoring-control gap: they readily acknowledge contradictory evidence, yet this awareness fails to constrain their final recommendations - detecting epistemic conflict does not imply resolving it safely. Through a multi-turn document accumulation protocol across four model families (1.5B-32B parameters) and over 50,000 turn-level evaluations, we demonstrate that single-turn diagnostics systematically overestimate RAG safety, that contradiction acknowledgement is uncorrelated with safe resolution, a pattern corroborated by targeted human validation, and that no universal prompt fix exists. Converging mechanism evidence - hidden-state probing, attention analysis, and response-strategy taxonomy - points to action selection as the most plausible locus of the deficit: danger-relevant information is internally represented and receives enhanced attention during unsafe generation, yet fails to constrain output behavior. The gap between what models recognize and what they do must be measured and closed before retrieval-augmented systems can be trusted in high-stakes settings.

AI.tldr: Single-turn diagnostics overestimate RAG safety; multi-turn evidence accumulation reveals a monitoring-control gap where recognition of epistemic conflict does not translate into safe action; no universal prompt fix; results span 4 model families and 50k turns; mechanism implicates action selection as bottleneck.
AI.motivation: Evaluate whether single-turn robustness tests for retrieval-augmented LLMs generalize to multi-turn evidence accumulation in high-stakes settings, where evidence quality guides action safety.
AI.method: Multi-turn document accumulation protocol across four model families (1.5B–32B params); >50,000 turn-level evaluations; complementary analyses include hidden-state probing, attention analysis, and response-strategy taxonomy, plus targeted human validation.
AI.result: Findings show that single-turn diagnostics systematically overestimate RAG safety; contradiction acknowledgment is uncorrelated with safe resolution; no universal prompt fix exists; mechanism analyses point to action selection as the locus of the deficit—danger-relevant information is represented and attended to but fails to constrain outputs; need to measure and close the gap for trustworthy retrieval-augmented systems.
AI.conclusion: Measurement and remediation of the recognition-to-action gap are essential before RAG systems can be trusted in high-stakes contexts; results motivate revised evaluation protocols and targeted model-alignment efforts.

title: OmniRetriever: Any-to-Any Audio-Video-Text Retrieval via Fusion-as-Teacher Distillation
summary: Unified multimodal embedding spaces have become the standard interface for cross-modal retrieval and multimodal RAG, and recent audio-video-text (AVT) encoders extend this setting to three modalities. Such encoders can produce a joint (T,V,A) embedding whenever all three modalities are available, but standard pairwise InfoNCE objectives leave this signal unused during training. We close this gap with fusion-as-teacher distillation, which treats a stop-gradient copy of the fused embedding as a teacher signal for the single-modal embeddings, paired with a Tuple-InfoNCE term that supervises the fused embedding directly. We instantiate this objective as OmniRetriever-7B. Across six zero-shot retrieval benchmarks, OmniRetriever-7B surpasses the closed-source Gemini Embedding 2 by 13.3-18.0 R@1 on Clotho and SoundDescs, and reaches the contemporary zero-shot specialist band of open video-text encoders on MSR-VTT and MSVD. To stress-test joint representations, we further release OmniRetriever-Bench, a 12-direction AVT retrieval benchmark totaling 3782 triples; on it OmniRetriever-7B attains AVG-all 34.84, improving over Gemini Embedding 2 by 1.72 and over the best prior open-source AVT method by 8.03.

AI.tldr: OmniRetriever-7B uses fusion-as-teacher distillation with Tuple-InfoNCE to jointly train triple-modal AVT embeddings, achieving state-of-the-art zero-shot AVT retrieval and releasing OmniRetriever-Bench.
AI.motivation: Standard pairwise InfoNCE neglects the joint (T,V,A) signal; there is a need for training objectives that leverage fused embeddings to improve cross-modal representations across three modalities.
AI.method: Train with a stop-gradient fused embedding as a teacher (fusion-as-teacher) and supervise the fused embedding directly with a Tuple-InfoNCE term; instantiated as OmniRetriever-7B.
AI.result: Outperforms Gemini Embedding 2 by 13.3-18.0 R@1 on Clotho and SoundDescs; competitive with open-video-text encoders on MSR-VTT and MSVD; AVG-all 34.84 on OmniRetriever-Bench, +1.72 over Gemini 2, +8.03 over prior best open-source AVT method.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates effectiveness of joint AVT representations via distillation and triple-wise objectives; provides strong AVT retrieval benchmarks and a scalable encoder family.

title: Auditing and Fixing Economic Validity in Tabular Foundation Models for Discrete Choice
comment: 5 pages, 1 table. Accepted at the FMSD Workshop, ICML 2026
summary: Tabular foundation models achieve strong accuracy on choice prediction tasks, but their predictions often violate the economic logic those tasks require: raising a price sometimes increases predicted demand, and implied willingness-to-pay estimates are frequently negative or implausible. We propose a two-stage adapter that embeds foundation model predictions within a utility-maximization framework. In the first stage, we estimate a standard choice model whose parameters are constrained to obey economic theory. In the second stage, we freeze those parameters and train a correction term that incorporates the foundation model's predictions as additional information. The result is a model that inherits the foundation model's accuracy gains while guaranteeing monotonic price-demand relationships under policy perturbation and producing analytically computable trade-off measures. On two transportation datasets, the adapter recovers up to 13 percentage points of accuracy over a standard logit model while maintaining perfect economic consistency, something neither the raw foundation models nor conventional distillation achieve.

AI.tldr: Two-stage adapter integrates foundation-model predictions into a constrained utility-maximizing choice model, yielding accuracy gains and guaranteed economic consistency.
AI.motivation: Tabular foundation models excel in prediction but violate economic logic (e.g., monotone price-demand), prompting a method that preserves theory while leveraging ML gains.
AI.method: Stage 1: estimate a standard choice model with parameters constrained by economic theory. Stage 2: freeze those parameters and train a correction term that uses foundation-model predictions as additional information. The result enforces monotonicity under price changes and provides analytic trade-off measures.
AI.result: On two transportation datasets, the adapter achieves up to 13 percentage-point accuracy gains over a standard logit, while maintaining perfect economic consistency; raw foundation models and standard distillation fail to achieve this combination.
AI.conclusion: The two-stage adapter successfully combines foundation-model accuracy with economic rationality, enabling reliable, interpretable policy analysis and computable trade-offs.

title: Query Symbolically or Retrieve Semantically? A Dataset and Method for Semi-Structured Question Answering
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems for question answering typically retrieve evidence by semantic similarity between the query and document chunks. While effective for unstructured text, this approach is less reliable on semi-structured corpora where answering may require exact filtering, aggregation, or exhaustive retrieval over structured attributes across multiple documents. Symbolic approaches support such operations, but they are often brittle on noisy natural-language corpora. We address this gap with DualGraph, a RAG framework that represents documents through two complementary views: a Textual Knowledge Graph for semantic retrieval and a Symbolic Knowledge Graph for symbolic querying over typed subject--predicate--object triples. Building on these two components, we provide multiple strategies for selecting or combining semantic and symbolic evidence.We also introduce SpecsQA, a benchmark from a commercial shopping website with semi-structured product documents and manually curated questions spanning open-ended and specification-oriented retrieval. Experiments show that DualGraph consistently outperforms state-of-the-art dense-retrieval, GraphRAG, symbolic, and table-oriented baselines across question types.Code and data are available at https://github.com/corneliocristina/DualGraphRAG.

AI.tldr: DualGraph combines textual and symbolic knowledge graphs to improve retrieval in RAG for semi-structured data, with SpecsQA benchmarking and outperformance over baselines.
AI.motivation: RAG struggles with semi-structured corpora that require exact filtering, aggregation, or cross-document reasoning; symbolic approaches are brittle on noisy NL data; a hybrid approach could leverage strengths of both views.
AI.method: Construct two complementary graphs: a Textual Knowledge Graph for semantic retrieval and a Symbolic Knowledge Graph with typed triples (subject-predicate-object). Propose strategies to select and fuse semantic and symbolic evidence for QA; eval on SpecsQA benchmark derived from a shopping domain.
AI.result: DualGraph consistently outperforms state-of-the-art dense retrieval, GraphRAG, symbolic, and table-oriented baselines across various question types.
AI.conclusion: The dual-view framework effectively handles both semantic and symbolic retrieval needs in semi-structured data; code and data are released at the provided GitHub link.

title: Adaptation-Free Heterogeneous Collaborative Perception with Unseen Agent Configurations
comment: 9 pages main paper, 23 pages including references and appendix, 7 figures
summary: Collaborative perception improves 3D object detection by enabling agents to share complementary observations, but most existing methods assume fixed or known collaborator encoder configurations, limiting deployment in practice. In this work, we consider an open-world setting in which auxiliary agents with unseen configurations may appear after deployment, such as different LiDAR beam counts or encoder architectures. To address this challenge, we propose ALF, a collaborative perception framework that enables zero-adaptation collaboration with unseen agent configurations by lifting lightweight box-level messages into ego-compatible auxiliary features. ALF converts auxiliary box-level messages into pseudo-BEV maps and synthesizes ego-compatible latent features by combining object-centric cues with scene context from the ego feature. On V2X-Real, under a zero-shot evaluation across 64 case studies, ALF outperforms the strongest prior baseline by 35.91% in relative mAP@0.7 while requiring only 120 bytes per agent per frame (approximately 9.6 Kbps bandwidth at 10 Hz).

AI.tldr: ALF enables zero-adaptation collaborative perception for open-world agents with unseen configurations by transforming lightweight box messages into ego-compatible auxiliary features, via pseudo-BEV synthesis and object-centric + scene-context fusion, achieving strong zero-shot gains with minimal bandwidth.
AI.motivation: Conventional collaborative perception assumes fixed or known collaborator encoder configurations, which hampers deployment when unseen agents with different LiDAR beams or encoders appear. A zero-adaptation solution is needed for open-world collaboration.
AI.method: ALF lifts loose, box-level messages from auxiliary agents into ego-compatible features by converting them into pseudo-BEV maps and fusing them with ego scene context to synthesize latent representations without requiring retraining on unseen configurations.
AI.result: In zero-shot evaluation on V2X-Real across 64 cases, ALF improves relative mAP@0.7 by 35.91% over the strongest prior baseline, using only about 120 bytes per agent per frame (~9.6 Kbps at 10 Hz).
AI.conclusion: ALF demonstrates effective zero-adaptation collaborative perception for open-world settings, offering strong performance gains with very low communication overhead and without explicit knowledge of collaborator configurations.

title: Beyond Holistic Models: Systematic Component-level Benchmarking of Deep Multivariate Time-Series Forecasting
comment: accepted by KDD 2026 Datasets and Benchmarks Track
summary: While previous research in multivariate time series forecasting has focused on developing complex holistic models, this work advocates for a shift toward a granular, component-level understanding of their impacts. We propose TSCOMP, the first large-scale benchmark that systematically deconstructs deep forecasting methods into their core, fine-grained components--spanning series preprocessing, encoding strategies, network architectures including specific and large time-series models, and optimization methods. Using constrained orthogonal experimental design and extensive evaluations, we conduct multi-view analyses that reveal component effectiveness across different backbones, data characteristics, and their interactions. Beyond providing insights, this benchmark establishes a fine-grained performance corpus comprising over 20,000 model-dataset evaluations, which supports the learning of automated component selection, enabling zero-shot model construction on new datasets. Our experiments demonstrate that the corpus-driven approach, despite its simplicity, consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, validating the soundness of our evaluation design and confirming that systematic component selection surpasses manually designed complex architectures. All code and the performance corpus are publicly available at https://github.com/SUFE-AILAB/TSCOMP.

AI.tldr: TSCOMP introduces a large-scale benchmark for multivariate time-series forecasting by deconstructing models into components, enabling automated component selection and zero-shot construction; a corpus of 20k+ evaluations shows superior performance over complex architectures.
AI.motivation: Move from monolithic, holistic forecasting models to granular, component-level understanding of how preprocessing, encoding, architectures, and optimization choices interact with data characteristics; create a benchmark and performance corpus to study these effects.
AI.method: Decompose forecasting pipelines into four component families (preprocessing, encoding, network architectures including specific and large models, optimization). Use constrained orthogonal experimental design to explore interactions across backbones and datasets; accumulate >20,000 model-dataset evaluations; build publicly available performance corpus and code.
AI.result: Corpus-driven component selection consistently outperforms state-of-the-art holistic models; enables zero-shot model construction on new datasets; validates the evaluation design and utility of systematic component analysis.
AI.conclusion: Granular benchmarking of forecast components is valuable; automated, data-driven component selection can surpass manual complex architectures; TSCOMP provides resources to advance component-aware model design in multivariate time-series forecasting.

title: The Compressive Knowledge Graph Hypothesis: Which Graph Facts Matter for Scientific Hypothesis Generation?
summary: Knowledge graphs (KGs) can provide structured scientific context to language models, but it remains unclear which graph facts actually shape the generated hypotheses. We study KG-guided hypothesis generation for battery materials across Mistral-7B, Llama-3.1-70B, and Gemini 2.5 Flash. We perturb local KGs by varying density, ontology richness, topology, and control structure, and evaluate outputs with both provided-graph and fixed-reference metrics. Across models, KG utility is selective and model-dependent: graph context changes outputs, but no-KG outputs also recover substantial graph content from model priors. Compact top-k subgraphs often approximate full-KG behavior, including when claimed-outcome triples are held out. At the same time, compression is not unique to one semantic ranking rule, random and topology-based subsets can also recover much of the signal. These results support a redundancy-aware Compressive KG hypothesis: useful KG signal is often recoverable from compact, scientifically structured subgraphs rather than requiring the full local graph.

AI.tldr: KG-guided hypothesis generation shows KG signals are compressible; compact subgraphs suffice.
AI.motivation: To identify which KG facts actually influence hypotheses and assess when KG context helps across large language models.
AI.method: Perturb local KGs by varying density, ontology richness, topology, and control structure; evaluate outputs with provided-graph and fixed-reference metrics across Mistral-7B, Llama-3.1-70B, and Gemini 2.5 Flash.
AI.result: KG utility is selective and model-dependent: graph context changes outputs, but no-KG outputs also recover substantial graph content from model priors. Compact top-k subgraphs often approximate full-KG behavior, including when claimed-outcome triples are held out. Compression is not unique to one semantic ranking rule; random and topology-based subsets can also recover much of the signal.
AI.conclusion: Redundancy-aware Compressive KG hypothesis: useful KG signal is often recoverable from compact, scientifically structured subgraphs rather than requiring the full local graph.

title: DV-SFT: Direct Vision Supervision for Fine-Grained Visual Understanding
summary: Multimodal large language models are typically trained end-to-end to predict ground-truth answers, yet supervision signals are applied exclusively to text tokens. Visual tokens, the core carriers of visual information, are optimized only implicitly as part of the context, leading to coarse-grained visual understanding. Prior works attempt to supervise visual inputs but inevitably rely on auxiliary components such as additional decoders or forward passes, because visual tokens lack readily interpretable labels. This limits their practical applicability. In this work, we propose \textbf{D}irect \textbf{V}ision \textbf{S}upervised \textbf{F}ine-\textbf{T}uning (DV-SFT), which constructs explicit, token-level supervision for visual tokens and trains them through the same next-token prediction objective used for text. Specifically, we exploit the direct vision--text correspondence in OCR-related scenarios and automatically label each visual token with the word in its corresponding image patch. DV-SFT treats the MLLM as a black box, requiring no architectural modifications or additional forward passes. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superiority of direct vision supervision. DV-SFT consistently outperforms standard SFT across three in-domain and four out-of-domain benchmarks. Further analyses show that vision supervision effectively enhances fine-grained visual understanding and achieves higher multimodal alignment efficiency.

AI.tldr: DV-SFT applies direct, token-level supervision to visual tokens in multimodal LLMs by automatically labeling each visual patch with the OCR word it contains, training with the same next-token objective, and doing so without architectural changes; it yields state-of-the-art results across domains.
AI.motivation: Visual tokens in multimodal LLMs lack explicit supervision, leading to coarse visual understanding; prior methods rely on auxiliary components and forward passes, reducing practicality.
AI.method: Leverage OCR-aligned direct vision-text correspondences to automatically label each visual token with its corresponding word; train the model with the standard next-token objective, treating the LLM as a black box with no architectural modifications or extra forward passes.
AI.result: DV-SFT consistently outperforms standard SFT across three in-domain and four out-of-domain benchmarks; analysis shows enhanced fine-grained visual understanding and higher multimodal alignment efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Direct vision supervision is a practical, effective approach to improve multimodal LLMs, enabling explicit visual token-level supervision without changing architecture and with broad applicability.

title: Distribution-Aware Conformal Prediction: A Framework for generating efficient prediction intervals for time series
comment: submitted to Journal of Machine Learning Research (JMLR)
summary: We present Distribution-aware Conformal Prediction (DCP), a unified framework integrating probabilistic predictors like Monte Carlo dropout, deep ensembles, and quantile regression with score-agnostic conformal calibration to produce valid and efficient prediction intervals. Leveraging a numerical inversion approach to construct interval bounds, DCP accommodates arbitrary combinations of distribution generating predictors and nonconformity scores. Benchmark analysis on synthetic and real-world time series data demonstrate DCP's ability to adaptively calibrate prediction intervals under varying uncertainty regimes. Crucially, DCP's modular design facilitates plug-and-play experimentation with different predictor-score pairings, quantitatively supported by a newly introduced modified Winkler score that balances validity and efficiency by explicitly penalizing undercoverage. While DCP generalizes and extends existing approaches like Conformalized Quantile Regression and Conformalized Monte Carlo, its modular design allows further extensions, setting a foundation for advancing uncertainty quantification in dynamic environments and high-risk applications.

AI.tldr: DCP unifies probabilistic predictors with conformal calibration via numerical inversion to yield valid, efficient prediction intervals; modular, plug‑and‑play framework; introduces a modified Winkler score; extends conformal methods; demonstrated on time-series data.
AI.motivation: Need robust, finite-sample valid uncertainty quantification that remains efficient across diverse predictors and dynamic uncertainty regimes; bridge probabilistic predictors with score-agnostic conformal calibration.
AI.method: Numerical inversion to construct interval bounds; integrate probabilistic predictors (Monte Carlo dropout, deep ensembles, quantile regression) with score-agnostic conformal calibration; modular design enabling plug-and-play predictor-score pairings; introduce a modified Winkler score that explicitly penalizes undercoverage; benchmark on synthetic and real-world time series.
AI.result: Adaptive, calibrated prediction intervals across varying uncertainty regimes; intervals maintain validity with improved efficiency; modular design supports various predictor-score combinations; benchmark results support dynamic calibration; foundation for further extensions beyond existing methods like Conformalized Quantile Regression and Conformalized Monte Carlo.
AI.conclusion: DCP generalizes and extends existing conformal methods, offering a flexible, extensible framework for uncertainty quantification in dynamic environments and high-risk applications; enables systematic experimentation with predictor=score pairings and paves the way for future enhancements.

title: Learning to Act under Noise: Enhancing Agent Robustness via Noisy Environments
summary: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have facilitated the widespread deployment of LLMs as interactive agents capable of reasoning, planning, and tool use. Despite strong performance on existing benchmarks, such agents often exhibit notable degradation when deployed in real-world settings, where environments are inherently stochastic and imperfect. We argue that this discrepancy arises from a fundamental mismatch between idealized training settings and real-world interaction dynamics, where current paradigms rely on carefully curated task instructions and stable, well-controlled environments. To address this gap, we propose NoisyAgent, an agentic training framework that explicitly incorporates environmental imperfections into the agent learning process. We identify two major sources of interaction noise in real-world scenarios: user noise, which captures ambiguity and variability in user interaction, and tool noise, which reflects failures and anomalies in tool execution. We introduce such perturbations into the training pipeline by modifying user interaction patterns and simulating tool execution results within the training environment. To stabilize training while encouraging agents to handle increasingly challenging imperfections, noise is applied to only a subset of rollouts and progressively increased in difficulty as the model adapts to the current noise level. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach consistently improves agent robustness under noisy and dynamic environments. Our analysis reveals that training under noise conditions also yields performance gains on idealized benchmarks, suggesting that controlled exposure to environmental noise promotes more generalizable reasoning and decision-making behaviors. Our findings highlight the importance of modeling interaction imperfections for bridging the gap between agent training and real-world deployment.

AI.tldr: NoisyAgent trains interactive agents with built-in environmental imperfections to boost robustness in stochastic real-world settings, and surprisingly improves performance on clean benchmarks as well.
AI.motivation: Real-world deployments expose agents to noisy user input and tool execution failures, unlike idealized, curated training environments. Bridging this gap requires training with realistic interaction noise.
AI.method: Introduce two noise sources—user noise and tool noise—into the training loop. Perturb user interactions and simulate tool outcomes within the simulator. Apply noise to only a subset of rollouts and gradually increase difficulty as training progresses.
AI.result: Agents exhibit enhanced robustness under noisy, dynamic environments. Notably, training under noise also improves performance on ideal benchmarks, indicating better generalization from exposed imperfections.
AI.conclusion: Modeling interaction imperfections during training is crucial to align agent capabilities with real-world deployment and can yield generalizable improvements across both noisy and clean settings.

title: Respecting Modality Gap in Post-hoc Out-of-distribution Detection with Pre-trained Vision-Language Models
summary: Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection has emerged as a popular technique to enhance the reliability of machine learning models by identifying unexpected inputs from unknown classes. Recent progress in pre-trained vision-language models (VLMs) has enabled zero-shot OOD detection without access to in-distribution (ID) training data; in this setting, existing methods commonly treat text embeddings of class names as class prototypes. In this paper, we challenge the widely adopted text-as-prototype paradigm by theoretically showing that off-the-shelf textual prototypes are generally misaligned with the optimal visual prototypes, yielding an intrinsic modality gap that cannot be eliminated by prompt engineering alone. To mitigate this gap under the post-hoc constraint, this paper presents an online pseudo-supervised framework that directly learns class prototypes in the visual feature space using unlabeled test-time data streams and soft predictions from the pre-trained VLMs. We provide theoretical guarantees for the convergence of the online optimization procedure. Extensive experiments empirically demonstrate that our method achieves a new state of the art across a variety of OOD detection setups.

AI.tldr: Textual prototypes for zero-shot OOD detection with vision-language models are misaligned with optimal visual prototypes; an online visual-prototype learning method using unlabeled test-time data and VLM soft labels achieves state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: There is an intrinsic modality gap between pre-defined textual class prototypes and visual features extracted by a model. Relying on text prompts as class prototypes and prompt engineering cannot fully bridge this gap, especially under post-hoc constraints and zero-ID-data settings.
AI.method: An online pseudo-supervised framework that learns class prototypes directly in the visual feature space. It uses unlabeled test-time data streams and soft predictions from a pre-trained VLM as pseudo-labels to update visual prototypes, with theoretical convergence guarantees for the online optimization.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows state-of-the-art performance across diverse OOD detection setups, outperforming text-as-prototype baselines and other zero-shot methods.
AI.conclusion: The paper demonstrates the limitations of the text-as-prototype paradigm and offers a practical online, post-hoc approach to learn visual prototypes from unlabeled data, effectively bridging the modality gap and enhancing OOD detection performance.

title: Separate Aggregation of Split Network for Personalized Federated Learning
summary: Federated learning enables collaborative model training without sharing raw data, but its performance can degrade substantially under heterogeneous client data distributions. A single global model often cannot satisfy diverse client requirements, so personalized federated learning has therefore been explored to improve client specific performance while preserving global generalization. Existing PFL methods often face a fundamental tradeoff in which stronger global sharing can undermine local specialization, whereas stronger local adaptation can lead to overfitting under limited data, label imbalance, and missing class scenarios. In this work, we propose PGFedSplit, a personalized federated learning framework that improves both personalization and global generalization under severe client heterogeneity. PGFedSplit adopts a split architecture and performs adaptive aggregation scheduling tailored to the roles of different model components, enabling stable knowledge sharing while maintaining client specific adaptation. Each client further leverages a mixture of locally extracted representations and synthetic representations generated from server side Gaussian statistics, improving robustness under label imbalance and missing class conditions. Extensive experiments on Fashion MNIST, CIFAR 10, CIFAR 100, and Tiny ImageNet demonstrate consistent improvements over state of the art PFL methods, with stable convergence and superior personalization in highly heterogeneous settings.

AI.tldr: Split-architecture personalized FL (PGFedSplit) with adaptive aggregation per model component and server-side Gaussian-statistic synthetic representations; improves personalization and global generalization under severe client heterogeneity; validated on multiple standard datasets with robust convergence.
AI.motivation: Client data heterogeneity in federated learning degrades global performance and over-constrains personalization. Existing PFL methods trade global sharing against local specialization, risking overfitting or underfitting. A method that stabilizes cross-client knowledge sharing while preserving client-specific adaptation is needed.
AI.method: Use a split neural network architecture with component-wise aggregation rules and adaptive scheduling. Each client combines locally extracted representations with synthetic representations generated from server-side Gaussian statistics, enabling robust performance under label imbalance and missing classes. The server coordinates adaptive aggregation across model components to balance global sharing and local adaptation.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on Fashion-MNIST, CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Tiny ImageNet shows consistent improvements over state-of-the-art PFL methods, with stable convergence and enhanced personalization in highly heterogeneous settings.
AI.conclusion: PGFedSplit effectively balances global generalization and client personalization under severe heterogeneity, exhibiting robustness to label imbalance and missing classes and demonstrating strong empirical performance across diverse datasets.

title: Gumbel Machine: Counterfactual Student Writing Generation via Gumbel Noise Steering
summary: An effective method of teaching across disciplines is to provide examples of high-quality work. However, an example may be significantly different from a student's current work, making it challenging for them to emulate. An ideal learning demonstration is a counterfactual version of the student work, an improved version that is still similar to their own. Existing automated approaches for counterfactual text generation using Large Language Models (LLMs) result in domain-specific systems that are difficult to translate into practical applications. We present the Gumbel Machine, a flexible, modular approach to generating counterfactuals that leverages LLM instruction-following capabilities while encouraging similarity to a reference factual text. Central to our approach is a novel, controlled decoding algorithm, $β$-Hindsight control, which uses latent randomness as a tunable similarity control mechanism during counterfactual generation. Experiments on datasets of student writing, scored on various criteria, demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach at generating counterfactuals both rubric-consistent and similar to a reference.

AI.tldr: Introduces Gumbel Machine with β-Hindsight control for generating counterfactual text that is both rubric-consistent and similar to a reference, addressing domain-specific limitations of prior LLM methods.
AI.motivation: To provide effective learning demonstrations by offering high-quality, comparable counterfactual exemplars; existing automated counterfactuals via LLMs are often domain-specific and hard to generalize.
AI.method: A modular framework that leverages LLM instruction-following and a novel decoding algorithm, β-Hindsight control, which uses latent randomness to tune similarity to a reference while improving rubric alignment.
AI.result: Experiments on student-writing datasets show counterfactuals that are both rubric-consistent and similar to the reference text, demonstrating the method's effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: The Gumbel Machine offers a flexible, transferable approach for automated counterfactual generation across disciplines, enabling practical educational tools with improved alignment to rubrics and preservation of reference similarity.

title: Memory-Distilled Selection for Noise-Robust Anomaly Detection
comment: Accepted by ICML2026. The code is available at https://github.com/SirojbekSafarov/MeDS
summary: Anomaly detection (AD) under data contamination is critical for deploying unsupervised defect detection in industrial environments, where curating perfectly clean training sets is impractical. However, existing methods are sensitive to contamination, suffering significant performance degradation as the noise ratio increases. In this paper, we propose Memory-Distilled Selection (MeDS), a training algorithm based on data selection. MeDS constructs an ensemble of partial memories via random subsampling, where the resulting sparsity acts as a low-pass filter that captures nominal patterns across a wide range of noise ratios, enabling coarse-level identification of contaminated samples. The aggregated distances to the bootstrapped memories are then distilled into a reconstruction score network, which is subsequently fine-tuned on clean data filtered using scores from the distilled model, enabling fine-grained localization of anomalies. MeDS is robust across a wide range of noise ratios without requiring noise-ratio-specific hyperparameter tuning, achieving 99.16\% image-level AUROC on MVTecAD at a 40\% noise ratio, and attaining state-of-the-art performance on both VisA and Real-IAD under noisy settings. We thoroughly verify the efficacy of MeDS on industrial AD benchmarks under noisy data scenarios, accompanied by in-depth empirical analyses.

AI.tldr: MeDS is a data-selection-based training algorithm for robust anomaly detection under data contamination. It uses an ensemble of partial memories from random subsampling to identify nominal patterns, then distills the results into a reconstruction score network that is fine-tuned on data filtered by the scores, enabling coarse-to-fine anomaly localization and achieving state-of-the-art performance under noisy conditions.
AI.motivation: Unsupervised anomaly detection suffers significant performance degradation when training data are contaminated. There is a need for methods that are robust to noise and do not require contamination-specific hyperparameters.
AI.method: Construct an ensemble of partial memories via random subsampling to create a sparsity-driven low-pass filter over nominal patterns. Compute aggregated distances from bootstrapped memories and distill these into a reconstruction score network. Fine-tune the network on clean data filtered by scores from the distilled model to enable fine-grained anomaly localization. The approach is designed to be robust across a wide range of noise ratios without noise-ratio-specific hyperparameter tuning.
AI.result: MeDS achieves robustness across varying noise ratios, with no contamination-specific tuning. It reports 99.16% image-level AUROC on MVTecAD at 40% noise and attains state-of-the-art performance on VisA and Real-IAD under noisy conditions, supported by extensive empirical analyses on industrial benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: MeDS provides a robust, noise-agnostic training paradigm for unsupervised AD under data contamination, enabling effective coarse-to-fine anomaly localization and achieving leading empirical performance.

title: Focal Reward: Balanced Reinforcement Learning under Rubric-Based Rewards
summary: The open-ended generation in LLMs usually requires multi-dimensional rubrics to adequately assess quality and guide the improvement of reinforcement learning. However, a critical dilemma inherent in this training paradigm is the imbalanced reward polarization along different rubric dimensions. Under this bottleneck, even if LLMs achieve relatively high rewards after training, they may still exhibit severe deficiencies in certain dimensions, leading to a direct deterioration in user experience. To address this problem, we propose Focal Reward, a novel objective to automatically balance the training of reinforcement learning under rubric-based rewards. Specifically, we first leverage an inverse reward projection mechanism to estimate the saturation degree of each criterion in the rubric, which forms the basis to calibrate the reward direction. Then, the final objective is designed with an automatically reweighting coefficient for each criterion to achieve the fine-grained balancing. Extensive experiments across three model scales and six benchmarks demonstrate that our Focal Reward method outperforms the strongest static aggregation baseline in all 18 model-benchmark comparisons. Rollout, mechanism, and ablation analyses further show that these gains arise from online, saturation-aware reallocation toward rubrics that still have room for improvement.

AI.tldr: Proposes Focal Reward, an online, saturation-aware reweighting of rubric-based rewards to balance multi-dimensional quality criteria in reinforcement learning with LLMs, improving alignment across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Open-ended LLM generation uses multi-dimensional rubrics; imbalanced reward polarization causes high overall rewards yet weaknesses in some criteria, degrading user experience. Need a method to balance training across rubric dimensions.
AI.method: Introduce inverse reward projection to estimate saturation (room for improvement) of each rubric criterion; use this to calibrate reward direction and apply an automatic reweighting coefficient per criterion for fine-grained balancing. Training is online and saturation-aware, reallocating focus toward under-optimized rubrics.
AI.result: Across three model scales and six benchmarks, Focal Reward outperforms the strongest static aggregation baseline in all 18 model-benchmark comparisons. Rollout, mechanism, and ablation analyses indicate gains arise from online reallocation toward rubrics with remaining improvement margins.
AI.conclusion: Focal Reward effectively mitigates rubric-level reward polarization in RLHF-style training, providing online, saturation-aware balancing that improves overall user experience and performance across diverse models and benchmarks.

title: SIA: Self Improving AI with Harness & Weight Updates
summary: Humans are the bottleneck in building and improving AI. Both the models and the agents that wrap them are written, tuned, and corrected by people. The long-horizon goal of an AI that can figure out how to improve itself remains open. Two largely disjoint research lines attack this bottleneck. The harness-update school has a meta-agent rewrite the scaffold of a task-specific agent (its tools, prompts, retry logic, and search procedure) while the model weights are held fixed. The test-time training school uses hand-written RL pipelines to update the model's own weights on task feedback while the harness is held fixed. These two silos operate in isolation. We propose SIA, a self-improving loop in which a language-model agent (the Feedback-Agent) updates both the harness and the weights of a task-specific agent. We evaluate across three contrasting domains: Chinese legal charge classification, low-level GPU kernel optimisation, and single-cell RNA denoising. Combining both levers outperforms scaffold iteration alone on all three benchmarks. The gains are 56.6% on LawBench, 91.9% runtime reduction on GPU kernels, and 502% on denoising over the initial baseline. Harness updates make the model agentic, shaping how it searches and acts, while weight updates build the domain intuition that no prompt or scaffold can instil.

AI.tldr: SIA: a self-improving loop where a Feedback-Agent updates both harness and weights to improve task-specific agents, outperforming scaffold-only methods across three domains.
AI.motivation: Human bottleneck in AI development; two siloed approaches (harness-update and test-time training) are insufficient; need integrated self-improvement.
AI.method: Introduce Feedback-Agent that rewrites scaffold (tools/prompts/retry/logic/search) and updates model weights via task feedback; evaluate on Chinese legal charge classification (LawBench), GPU kernel optimization, single-cell RNA denoising; compare to baselines.
AI.result: Significant gains: 56.6% on LawBench, 91.9% runtime reduction on GPU kernels, 502% improvement in denoising over initial baseline; combined leverage outperforms scaffold-only.
AI.conclusion: Joint harness and weight updates yield stronger performance; harness shapes search/acting, weight updates inject domain intuition beyond prompts and scaffolds.

title: DynFrame: Adaptive Reasoning-Driven Multimodal Framework with Dynamic Frame Augmentation for Complex Video Understanding
summary: Recent video multimodal large language models (MLLMs) increasingly couple step-by-step reasoning with on-demand visual evidence retrieval, allowing models to revisit relevant video segments during inference. However, two structural gaps remain in existing thinking-with-video systems. (i) Sampling density is not a learnable decision: existing methods may let the model decide where to look, but the per-window frame rate is largely fixed. As a result, fine-grained evidence is often recovered through repeated retrieval calls, which increases inference context length and training difficulty. (ii) Retrieval and answer generation are usually optimized with a single trajectory-level advantage, so the "where to look" tokens and the "how to answer" tokens receive the same credit even when one is correct and the other is not. To address these gaps, we present DynFrame, a framework that emits the temporal window and the sampling density as native tokens within a single autoregressive pass. This learnable span-density retrieval enables acquiring multi-granularity evidence with a single retrieval step. Based on the above tokenized retrieval interface, we further introduce Segment-Decoupled GRPO (SD-GRPO), which splits each rollout at the retrieval boundary and assigns role-specific token-level advantages, separately crediting the sampling decision and the answer. Trained on the curated DM-CoT-74k and DM-RL-45k, DynFrame-4B is competitive with strong 7B-8B baselines across six benchmarks (NExT-GQA, Charades-STA, ActivityNet-MR, Video-MME, MLVU, LVBench), and DynFrame-8B sets new state-of-the-art on most metrics. Code is available at https://github.com/zhangguanghao523/DynFrame.

AI.tldr: Proposes DynFrame: a video MLLM framework that learns sampling density for temporal windows via tokenized retrieval, plus Segment-Decoupled GRPO to credit retrieval and answer separately; achieves SOTA on multiple video QA/segmentation benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Address two gaps: (1) fixed per-window sampling density forcing repeated retrievals for fine-grained evidence and longer context; (2) single trajectory credit assignment dilutes learning signals between 'where to look' and 'how to answer'.
AI.method: Introduce a tokenized retrieval interface that emits temporal window and sampling density as native tokens within a single autoregressive pass. Develop Segment-Decoupled GRPO (SD-GRPO) that splits rollouts at the retrieval boundary and assigns role-specific token-level advantages to sampling vs answering. Train on DM-CoT-74k and DM-RL-45k. Evaluate on six benchmarks; release code.
AI.result: DynFrame-4B is competitive with strong 7B–8B baselines across six benchmarks (NExT-GQA, Charades-STA, ActivityNet-MR, Video-MME, MLVU, LVBench); DynFrame-8B achieves new state-of-the-art on most metrics.
AI.conclusion: Learning span-density retrieval and decoupled credit assignment improve efficiency and accuracy in video reasoning with LLMs; the approach offers a practical, scalable path for sampling-density control in multimodal reasoning.

title: On the Error-Correcting Effects of Stochasticity in Discrete Diffusion
summary: Discrete diffusion models achieve strong performance in text and image generation, but their inference remains slow and must inherently balance sampling efficiency and sample quality. In this work, we present a systematic study of how the \emph{degree of stochasticity} in Markov transitions governs the sampling tradeoff. We show that highly deterministic transitions converge rapidly but suffer from error accumulation, while more stochastic transitions converge more slowly yet can achieve higher final sample quality. Using an information-theoretic analysis, we identify the underlying mechanism as an error-correcting effect induced by \emph{redundant transitions} that symmetrically exchange mass between states, and show that these transitions can provably contract sampling errors. Motivated by this analysis, we propose \emph{Discrete Churn and Restart Sampling} (DCRS), a novel inference algorithm that injects controlled stochasticity by alternating between forward and reverse diffusion processes.
