Experiments on synthetic datasets and large-scale benchmarks show that DCRS improves the speed-quality tradeoff in the low number of function evaluations regime. On image datasets, DCRS achieves up to a $10\times$ reduction in sampling steps compared to standard samplers while maintaining competitive sample quality, whereas on language benchmarks, we observe more nuanced behavior depending on the corruption process and sampling procedure.

AI.tldr: Discrete diffusion sampling tradeoff is governed by the degree of stochasticity in transitions; highly deterministic steps are fast but error-prone, while more stochastic steps are slower but can yield higher final quality. Introduces DCRS, which injects stochasticity via alternating forward/reverse diffusion to improve speed-quality, achieving up to 10x fewer steps on images with competitive quality; language results vary by corruption and procedure.
AI.motivation: Inference in discrete diffusion models is slow and must balance sampling efficiency and sample quality. Deterministic transitions accumulate error; introducing controlled stochasticity can improve final quality. An information-theoretic view explains an error-correcting role of redundant transitions.
AI.method: Perform an information-theoretic analysis of stochasticity in Markov transitions, identifying redundant transitions that exchange mass and contract sampling errors. Develop Discrete Churn and Restart Sampling (DCRS) by alternating forward and reverse diffusion to inject controlled stochasticity. Evaluate on synthetic datasets and large-scale image and language benchmarks.
AI.result: DCRS improves the speed-quality tradeoff in the low function evaluation regime. On image datasets, achieves up to a 10× reduction in sampling steps with competitive sample quality. On language benchmarks, results are more nuanced and depend on the corruption process and sampling procedure.
AI.conclusion: Controlled stochasticity via redundant transitions acts as an error-correcting mechanism that contracts sampling errors; DCRS provides a principled, effective acceleration method for discrete diffusion sampling, particularly in image generation, with modality-dependent effects in language tasks.

title: Modeling Agentic Technical Debt and Stochastic Tax: A Standalone Framework for Measurement, Simulation, and Dashboarding
summary: Agentic AI systems combine probabilistic reasoning with delegated action through tools, context, memory, orchestration, and external workflow integration. This note develops a formal and managerially usable model that distinguishes Agentic Technical Debt from Stochastic Tax. Agentic Technical Debt is a stock of accumulated design and governance liability. Stochastic Tax is a recurring flow of operating burden that arises when stochastic agents are used in business workflows. The two constructs are related, but they are not the same: debt can amplify the tax, while the tax can remain positive even when debt is minimized. The note starts from a compact dashboard expression, expands it into a fuller structural model, defines all variables and parameters, shows how each cost category can be estimated from operational data, and illustrates the framework with an accounts-payable simulation and companion spreadsheet.

AI.tldr: Formal model distinguishing Agentic Technical Debt from Stochastic Tax in agentic AI systems, with estimation methods and practical demonstrations (AP simulation, spreadsheet).
AI.motivation: Provide a managerially usable framework to quantify design/governance liabilities (debt) and ongoing operating burden (tax) in agentic AI, and clarify their relationship.
AI.method: Begin with a compact dashboard expression, expand into a full structural model, define all variables/parameters, show estimation from operational data, illustrate via an accounts-payable (AP) simulation and companion spreadsheet.
AI.result: A rigorously specified model enabling separate estimation of debt and tax costs; links between debt and tax; practical tooling for governance and budgeting.
AI.conclusion: Debt can amplify tax; tax may persist even when debt is minimized; the framework supports measurement, budgeting, and governance of agentic AI systems.

title: PinPoint: Prompting with Informative Interior Points
summary: Modern referring image segmentation pipelines couple a vision-language model (VLM) for grounding with a promptable segmenter such as the Segment Anything Model (SAM) for mask generation. Prior training-free instances of this recipe consistently trail fine-tuned and reinforcement-learning (RL)-tuned specialists, and it has been unclear whether the gap comes from the VLM's grounding, SAM's capacity, or the prompt. We show that the gap is dominated by prompt ambiguity: a VLM-proposed bounding box (bbox) leaves SAM to guess which pixels inside the bbox belong to the object the expression denotes. Interior points are the natural disambiguator, but where they fall matters; prior work relies on naively sampled points that land on boundaries, distractors, and background clutter, and can even hurt performance compared to the bbox alone. Supervised and RL-tuned methods close this gap by training a VLM to predict better points; we show that this training is unnecessary. At a matched budget of five interior points, replacing naive sampling with stable, informative point selection improves cumulative Intersection-over-Union (cIoU) by 12-18 points across RefCOCO/+/g, with every model fixed. We turn this observation into PinPoint, a deterministic, training-free point selector that fuses four visual cues into a consensus map, selects compact, spatially diverse points away from boundaries, and uses the frozen VLM to label each point. Without any task-specific training, PinPoint matches supervised and RL-tuned specialists on the same stack while issuing only two VLM calls per query.

AI.tldr: Prompt ambiguity in VLM+SAM pipelines is the main bottleneck; a training-free, deterministic interior-point selector (PinPoint) using four cues and few interior points achieves strong disambiguation, matching supervised/RL-tuned methods with only two VLM calls per query.
AI.motivation: Determine whether gaps between training-free grounding and tuned specialists come from the VLM grounding, SAM capacity, or prompting; identify the role of interior points in disambiguation and how naive sampling harms performance.
AI.method: Introduce PinPoint: a deterministic point selector that fuses four visual cues into a consensus map, selects compact, spatially diverse interior points away from boundaries, and uses a frozen VLM to label each point. Operates with five interior points and requires only two VLM calls per query; no task-specific training. Evaluate on RefCOCO/+, g datasets against supervised/RL-tuned baselines.
AI.result: With five interior points, replacing naive sampling with PinPoint yields a 12–18 point increase in cumulative IoU (cIoU) across RefCOCO/+/g, across models fixed. PinPoint matches supervised and RL-tuned specialists on the same stack without task-specific training.
AI.conclusion: Prompt ambiguity is the dominant bottleneck; a simple, training-free point-selection strategy can close the gap to fine-tuned models. PinPoint provides robust, efficient disambiguation and generalizes across datasets with minimal VLM calls.

title: Near-Optimal Regret in Adversarial Kernel Bandits
summary: We study the adversarial kernel bandit problem, in which the loss at each round is induced by an arbitrary bounded element of a reproducing kernel Hilbert space (RKHS). We propose an exponential-weights algorithm built on a regularized importance-weighted loss estimator, together with an explicit correction term that cancels the bias introduced by the regularization. Our main result bounds the regret by $\widetilde{O}\big(\sqrt{T\, d_*(λ)\,\log|{X}|}\big)$, where $d_*(λ)$ is a widely-adopted notion of effective dimension that captures the complexity of the kernel. Up to logarithmic factors, this matches the known rate achieved in the related stochastic kernel bandit problem. A notable application is the Matérn$(ν,d)$ kernel with smoothness parameter $ν$ on $\mathbb{R}^d$, for which our bound specializes to $\widetilde{O}\big(T^{(ν+d)/(2ν+d)}\big)$, improving over the best-known prior rate of Chatterji et al. [2019] while simultaneously removing the rank-one adversary assumption required by their analysis. Moreover, this rate is the same as the known optimal rate for stochastic kernel bandits, and also matches a lower bound from concurrent work up to a $\log T$ factor.

AI.tldr: Exponential-weights algorithm for adversarial RKHS kernel bandits with a bias-corrected regularized estimator; regret ~ sqrt(T d_*(λ) log|X|).
AI.motivation: Study adversarial kernel bandits where losses lie in an RKHS and obtain near-optimal regret rates, removing restrictive assumptions and aligning with stochastic kernel-bandit rates.
AI.method: Regularized importance-weighted loss estimator within an exponential-weights framework, with an explicit correction term to cancel bias from regularization.
AI.result: Regret bound of ~ sqrt(T d_*(λ) log|X|). For Matérn kernels, specialized rate is ~ T^{(ν+d)/(2ν+d)}; improves prior rates and removes rank-one adversary assumption. Matches known stochastic rates and up to log factors with concurrent lower bounds.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that adversarial kernel bandits can achieve rates comparable to stochastic kernel bandits for common kernels (e.g., Matérn), via a bias-corrected estimator in an exponential-weights algorithm, and removes a previously needed restrictive assumption.

title: Maat: The Agentic Legal Research Assistant for Competition Protection
summary: Competition law experts conducting legal research must review extensive volumes of cases, decisions, and judicial reports to identify precedents and assess key elements in competition and merger cases. Although general research assistants such as Claude and ChatGPT and legal assistants such as SaulLM-7B and LegalGPT are increasingly used to assist legal research, they remain inadequate for competition law analysis: they lack specialized domain expertise, provide insufficient official citations, or hallucinate competition law cases. We propose Maat, a ReAct agent that orchestrates tools corresponding to different tasks of the research process. Designed iteratively with competition law experts, Maat grounds cases and findings in official sources using RAG for reliability, provides rich in-line citations, falls back to web search when database coverage is insufficient, and prompts the user for clarification when queries are ambiguous. Maat significantly outperforms all baseline assistants on case-specific tasks and performs within range of the top baseline on theoretical question tasks. The dataset used is available on GitHub.

AI.tldr: Maat is a ReAct-based agent that orchestrates specialized tools for competition law research, grounding results in official sources via RAG, providing inline citations, and using web search as a fallback. It outperforms baselines on case tasks and matches top baselines on theoretical questions; dataset available on GitHub.
AI.motivation: To address the inadequacy of general LLM assistants in competition law research, which struggle with domain expertise, official citations, and hallucination of cases, by building a tool-augmented workflow that ensures reliability and traceability.
AI.method: Maat uses a ReAct-style architecture to orchestrate multiple tools aligned with research tasks. It grounds cases and findings in official sources using retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) for reliability, offers rich inline citations, falls back to web search when database coverage is insufficient, and prompts for clarification on ambiguous queries. It was iteratively designed with competition law experts.
AI.result: Maat significantly outperforms all baseline assistants on case-specific tasks and performs within the range of the top baseline on theoretical question tasks. The dataset used is available on GitHub.
AI.conclusion: Maat demonstrates a viable, reliable, and auditable workflow for competition law research by combining tool orchestration, RAG grounding, and explicit citations; the accompanying dataset enables reproducibility and further benchmarking.

title: Rotation-Invariant Spherical Watermarking via Third-Order SO(3) Representation Coupling
summary: Reliable watermarking of panoramic imagery is fundamentally challenged by arbitrary 3D rotations. As panoramas are defined on the sphere, they naturally transform under the action of $SO(3)$, rendering conventional planar representations and augmentation-based robustness strategies inadequate and devoid of theoretical guarantees. To address this, we formulate panoramas as spherical signals and leverage $SO(3)$ representation theory to derive provably rotation-invariant descriptors. While spherical harmonic coefficients transform equivariantly under rotations, the natural invariant constructions are typically limited to zeroth-order statistics which eliminate directional information and severely constrain embedding capacity. In this work, we introduce a principled third-order invariant construction by coupling higher-order $SO(3)$ irreducible representations via tensor products and projecting onto the trivial representation. This yields a spherical invariant bispectrum that preserves phase information while remaining strictly rotation-invariant. Leveraging this property, we embed watermarks into higher-order spherical harmonic coefficients and recover them from invariant bispectral scalars, enabling reliable extraction under arbitrary 3D rotations. We provide a theoretical proof of $SO(3)$ invariance for it and demonstrate experimentally its near-perfect robustness to continuous rotations while maintaining high visual fidelity.

AI.tldr: Introduces a rotation-invariant watermarking framework for spherical panoramas via a third-order SO(3) invariant bispectrum, enabling robust watermark embedding and extraction under arbitrary 3D rotations while preserving phase information.
AI.motivation: Panoramas on the sphere transform under SO(3); traditional planar methods and augmentation-based robustness lack guarantees. There is a need for invariants that preserve directional information and support embedding capacity.
AI.method: Represent panoramas as spherical signals; use SO(3) representation theory; construct a third-order invariant by tensor-product coupling of higher-order irreps and projection to the trivial representation, yielding a rotation-invariant bispectrum that preserves phase; embed watermark into higher-order spherical harmonic coefficients; recover from invariant bispectral scalars; provide theoretical proof of invariance.
AI.result: Proves SO(3) invariance; experimental results show near-perfect robustness to continuous rotations with high visual fidelity.
AI.conclusion: The approach delivers provably rotation-invariant watermarking for spherical panoramas with maintained phase information and embedding capacity, offering reliable protection against arbitrary 3D rotations.

title: Few-shot Cross-country Generalization of Tabular Machine Learning and Foundation Models for Childhood Anemia Prediction under Distribution Shift
summary:
Childhood anemia affects around 40% of children aged 6-59 months globally and arises from heterogeneous factors, limiting model generalizability. We evaluate a transformer-based tabular foundation model against classical supervised methods under cross-country and data-scarce settings.
  We used DHS data from 16 countries across Africa, Asia, Latin America, the Caucasus, and the Middle East (n=68,856). We compared Logistic Regression, XGBoost, LightGBM, and TabPFN v2.6. Performance was assessed using AUC-ROC, Brier score, and ECE. Generalization was evaluated using leave-one-country-out (LOCO), reverse-LOCO, and few-shot settings. Subgroup analyses included sex, age, residence, maternal education, and wealth. Feature importance was estimated using SHAP.
  TabPFN outperformed classical models in low-data regimes (<200 samples), showing higher discrimination and better calibration. Across countries, it achieved the lowest Brier score (0.042) and ECE (0.203). Under full-data settings, AUC-ROC ranged from 0.59-0.76 with small between-model differences ($\leq 0.05$). LOCO performance was stable (0.58-0.69), driven by country context. Reverse-LOCO showed asymmetric transferability. Subgroup performance was consistent with no systematic demographic bias. SHAP identified child age, altitude, and height-for-age z-score as dominant predictors, followed by wealth and maternal education.
  Performance in childhood anemia prediction is driven more by population variation than model choice. TabPFN provides advantages in low-resource settings through improved discrimination and calibration, highlighting foundation models as promising tools for data-scarce global health prediction.

AI.tldr: Transformer-based TabPFN outperforms classical methods in low-data regional studies of childhood anemia, with strong calibration, but gains are modest in full-data settings; population heterogeneity, not model type, drives performance; shows good cross-country generalization and interpretable predictors via SHAP.
AI.motivation: To assess whether a transformer-based tabular foundation model can generalize across countries and data-scarce settings for predicting childhood anemia, compared with classical supervised methods.
AI.method: Data: DHS data from 16 countries ( Africa, Asia, Latin America, Caucasus, Middle East), n=68,856. Models compared: Logistic Regression, XGBoost, LightGBM, TabPFN v2.6. Evaluation: AUC-ROC, Brier score, ECE. Generalization: leave-one-country-out (LOCO), reverse-LOCO, few-shot. Subgroups: sex, age, residence, maternal education, wealth. Interpretability: SHAP.
AI.result: TabPFN outperformed classical models in low-data regimes (<200 samples) with higher discrimination and better calibration. Across countries: lowest Brier score 0.042 and ECE 0.203. Full-data AUC-ROC: 0.59–0.76 with small model differences (≤0.05). LOCO: stable 0.58–0.69, country-driven. Reverse-LOCO: asymmetric transferability. Subgroup: no systematic demographic bias. SHAP: top predictors—child age, altitude, height-for-age z-score; followed by wealth and maternal education.
AI.conclusion: Performance is driven more by population variation than model choice. TabPFN offers advantages in data-scarce, low-resource settings through improved discrimination and calibration, supporting foundation models as promising tools for data-limited global health prediction.

title: 2-ASP(Q) programs with weak constraints: Complexity and efficient implementation
summary: ASP(Q) extends Answer Set Programming (ASP) with Quantifiers over answer sets. In this paper we focus on the class of ASP(Q) programs with two quantifiers and weak constraints, denoted as 2-ASP(Q)^w. 2-ASP(Q)^w is a practically relevant fragment of ASP(Q) that is expressive enough to capture optimization problems up to the class Delta_3^P. On the theoretical side, we provide a complete complexity characterization of the main computational tasks for 2-ASP(Q)^w programs, including tight completeness results and the analysis of nontrivial cases that have not been addressed in previous works. On the practical side, we introduce novel strategies for computing (optimal) quantified answer sets in the Casper system, that rely on a Counterexample-Guided Abstraction Refinement (CEGAR) technique tailored to ASP(Q). An experimental evaluation on hard benchmarks from different application domains shows that the proposed techniques are effective in practice.

AI.tldr: Introduces 2-ASP(Q)^w, a two-quantifier ASP(Q) fragment with weak constraints; provides tight complexity characterizations for core tasks; develops a CEGAR-based quantified ASP solver in Casper; empirical validation on hard benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Bridge quantified reasoning in ASP with practical optimization capabilities, achieving expressiveness up to Delta_3^P while delivering rigorous complexity results and practical computation strategies.
AI.method: Theoretical: derive complete, tight complexity characterizations for the main computational tasks of 2-ASP(Q)^w, including analysis of previously unaddressed cases. Practical: design and implement CEGAR-based strategies to compute optimal quantified answer sets within the Casper system, specialized for ASP(Q).
AI.result: Established tight complexity results for the key tasks in 2-ASP(Q)^w; proposed a novel CEGAR-driven computation approach for quantified answer sets; demonstrated effectiveness of the approach via experiments on hard benchmarks across multiple domains.
AI.conclusion: 2-ASP(Q)^w is a practically relevant, theoretically well-understood fragment of ASP(Q) for optimization problems in higher complexity classes, and the Casper-based CEGAR methods offer a viable path for solving quantified ASP problems in practice.

title: METATR: A Multilingual, Evolving Benchmark for Automatic Text Recognition
summary: Benchmarks that reflect the diversity and complexity of real-world documents are essential for accurately evaluating Automatic Text Recognition (ATR) systems, especially Vision-Large Language Models (vLLMs). Although recent models demonstrate impressive performance, they are often evaluated on datasets containing modern, printed texts mostly written in English, which limits their relevance to many practical applications. Therefore, selecting a model for a specific use case requires evaluating it on data that matches the target documents. This highlights the importance of representative benchmarks for real-world applications. In this paper, we introduce METATR (v1.0), a multilingual, evolving benchmark designed to evaluate ATR models across a wide range of documents, facilitating meaningful model comparison and selection. The benchmark was designed to maximize diversity by including documents from various public collections. These documents cover 29 languages and include texts with multiple scripts and layouts. Beyond the dataset itself, METATR defines a standardized prompting and normalization methodology and establishes a dynamic evaluation framework. This approach is intended to produce reproducible results while remaining extensible over time. We evaluated a wide range of state-of-the-art systems, including open-source models and closed-source models. Results are reported across various dimensions, including performance at the dataset and language levels, robustness to handwritten documents, and computational efficiency. Our findings show that, although proprietary models achieve the most consistent performance, substantial variability persists across scripts and layouts. Overall, METATR provides a multidimensional, practitioner-oriented framework for assessing multilingual ATR in real-world conditions and tracking progress as the field evolves.

AI.tldr: METATR v1.0 introduces a multilingual, evolving benchmark for automatic text recognition (ATR) to evaluate models across 29 languages, scripts, and layouts, with a standardized prompting/normalization framework and a dynamic evaluation setup. It enables cross-dataset/language comparisons and real-world progress tracking.
AI.motivation: Current ATR benchmarks are skewed toward English, modern printed text, limiting real-world applicability. A diverse, multilingual, and evolving benchmark is needed to better reflect practical use cases and guide model selection.
AI.method: Construct METATR v1.0 from documents across multiple public collections covering 29 languages and varying scripts/layouts; define standardized prompting and normalization procedures; develop a dynamic evaluation framework; benchmark a wide range of models (open-source and closed-source) and report results by dataset, language, script-handling robustness, and efficiency.
AI.result: Proprietary models show the most consistent performance, but substantial performance variability persists across scripts and layouts; METATR enables multidimensional, reproducible, and extensible evaluation for real-world ATR conditions.
AI.conclusion: METATR provides a practitioner-oriented, evolving benchmark for multilingual ATR, facilitating fair model comparison, remediation, and progress tracking as the field evolves.

title: Geometry-Aware Contrastive Learning for Few-Shot Automatic Modulation Recognition
summary: Standard Self-Supervised Learning (SSL) for Automatic Modulation Recognition (AMR) struggles with ineffective isotropic augmentations, spectral instability, and semantic drift. To address these challenges, we propose Dynamic-Consistency Contrastive Learning (DyCo-CL), a geometry-aware framework that couples Virtual Adversarial Augmentation (VAA) with a semantic consistency loss. We provide a theoretical analysis indicating that this strategy acts as an implicit spectral regularizer for the encoder, enabling stable manifold exploration. Complementing this, our Signal-Adaptive Swin Backbone with fixed-window attention improves structural stability by constraining attention locality, while a Hybrid Knowledge Fusion module anchors representations with physical priors. Experiments on RML benchmarks show that DyCo-CL achieves a 6.27% accuracy gain in 1-shot settings over prior methods.

AI.tldr: Geometry-aware SSL for AMR: DyCo-CL couples Virtual Adversarial Augmentation with semantic consistency to act as a spectral regularizer; enhances stability via signal-adaptive Swin backbone and physical-prior fusion; reports 6.27% 1-shot gain on RML.
AI.motivation: Standard SSL for Automatic Modulation Recognition (AMR) suffers from ineffective isotropic augmentations, spectral instability, and semantic drift, hindering robust modulation classification in varying radio environments.
AI.method: Dynamic-Consistency Contrastive Learning (DyCo-CL) couples Virtual Adversarial Augmentation (VAA) with a semantic consistency loss. Theoretical analysis suggests this strategy acts as an implicit spectral regularizer for the encoder, enabling stable manifold exploration. The architecture includes a Signal-Adaptive Swin Backbone with fixed-window attention to constrain attention locality, and a Hybrid Knowledge Fusion module to anchor representations with physical priors.
AI.result: Experiments on RML benchmarks show DyCo-CL achieves a 6.27 percentage point gain in 1-shot accuracy over prior methods.
AI.conclusion: DyCo-CL provides a geometry-aware SSL framework for AMR with improved stability and accuracy. The combination of spectral-regularization via VAA and semantic consistency, together with the constrained backbone and physics-informed fusion, yields robust representations suitable for low-shot learning scenarios.

title: Alignment Tampering: How Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback Is Exploited to Optimize Misaligned Biases
comment: Accepted at ICML 2026, Source code: https://alignment-tampering.github.io/
summary: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) is the standard method to align Large Language Models (LLMs) with human preferences. In this work, we introduce alignment tampering, a potential vulnerability where the LLM undergoing alignment influences the preference dataset, causing RLHF to amplify undesired behaviors. This arises from core limitations of RLHF: (1) preference datasets are constructed from the LLM's own outputs, allowing it to influence them, and (2) pairwise comparisons only indicate which response is better, not why. These limitations can be exploited to cause alignment tampering. For example, if an LLM generates biased responses with higher quality, annotators will prefer them based on quality. However, preference labels do not distinguish quality from bias, and the reward model inherits this limitation. Optimizing such rewards through reinforcement learning or best-of-N sampling can amplify misaligned biases. Our experiments demonstrate amplification across diverse biases: from keyword bias to propaganda (e.g., sexism), brand promotion, and instrumental goal-seeking. Mitigation remains challenging, as existing techniques for robust RLHF fail to fully resolve alignment tampering without sacrificing response quality. These findings reveal structural vulnerabilities of current RLHF and emphasize the need to prevent this vulnerability. Project page: https://alignment-tampering.github.io/

AI.tldr: RLHF is vulnerable to alignment tampering where the LLM shapes its own preference data, amplifying biased or undesired behaviors via reward-model optimization; demonstrated across biases; mitigations incomplete.
AI.motivation: To expose structural weaknesses in RLHF caused by data collection from model outputs and pairwise-only feedback, which can fail to distinguish quality from bias.
AI.method: The paper analyzes the mechanism and empirically tests amplification across multiple bias domains (keyword bias, sexism/propaganda, brand promotion, instrumental goals) under RLHF pipelines and best-of-N strategies; evaluates robustness of existing mitigation techniques.
AI.result: Shows consistent amplification of misaligned biases due to alignment tampering; robust RLHF methods do not fully resolve the vulnerability without hurting performance/quality.
AI.conclusion: Alignment tampering reveals fundamental vulnerabilities in current RLHF; preventing this requires new strategies to decouple quality from bias in preference data and to harden reward models against manipulation.

title: Joint 2D-3D Segmentation and Association in Street-level Imaging
comment: 15 pages, 6 image figures, 1 in-body table, 1 in-body algorithm, 2 indexes with tables
summary: Accurate interpretation of street-level imagery is essential for large-scale urban mapping and the creation of Spatial Digital Twin (SDT) environments. This work presents a unified framework for joint 2D-3D segmentation and association that integrates visual semantics with multi-view geometric reasoning. Unlike conventional approaches that rely heavily on sequential frames for temporal tracking, our method leverages zero-shot detection and segmentation together with structure-from-motion reconstruction to establish stable cross-view correspondences. A 3D-driven association mechanism replaces traditional 2D multi-object tracking, using geometric consistency to guide identity preservation across wide-baseline viewpoints and varying imaging conditions. By combining 2D texture cues with global 3D context, the proposed pipeline is well-suited for scalable street-level processing and can be used for a variety of object types. Experiments demonstrate substantially improved coverage of ground-truth sequences and more robust identity retention compared to state-of-the-art 2D-only tracking methods, achieving a 22% performance gain in challenging urban scenarios.

AI.tldr: Unified 2D-3D segmentation with 3D-driven cross-view association using zero-shot detection and SfM, replacing 2D tracking for stable identities across wide baselines in street-level imagery.
AI.motivation: Enable scalable, robust street-level mapping and Spatial Digital Twins by maintaining object identities across views without heavy reliance on sequential 2D tracking.
AI.method: Joint 2D-3D segmentation and association: zero-shot detection/segmentation, structure-from-motion (SfM) reconstruction to establish cross-view correspondences; a 3D-driven association mechanism enforces geometric consistency to preserve identities across wide-baseline viewpoints and varying imaging conditions; fuses 2D texture cues with global 3D context.
AI.result: Significant improvements over 2D-only tracking, including substantially higher ground-truth coverage and better identity retention; reported 22% performance gain in challenging urban scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The proposed pipeline enables scalable street-level processing for diverse object types by leveraging 3D geometry to guide cross-view associations, reducing dependence on traditional 2D MOT pipelines and improving robustness in variable urban conditions.

title: Spend Your Rollouts Where It Counts: Rollout Allocation for Group-Based RL Post-Training
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) is the dominant paradigm for post-training large language models. However, in the online, on-policy setting, rollout generation dominates the computational cost of training. Group-based policy optimization methods compute advantages from multiple rollouts per prompt, yet they indiscriminately allocate budget to prompts with collapsed reward distributions, wasting expensive rollouts on negligible learning signals. We demonstrate that group-based updates are most effective in regimes of high reward variance. Since the policy evolves throughout training, prompt informativeness must be estimated online rather than precomputed, but exhaustively evaluating every prompt is computationally prohibitive. We introduce Pilot-Commit, a budget-aware rollout allocation framework for group-based RL post-training. Pilot-Commit decouples prompt evaluation from exploitation: a pilot stage estimates per-prompt informativeness using a fraction of the budget, and the remaining rollouts are allocated to high-leverage prompts while low-signal prompts are skipped. Across multiple math reasoning benchmarks and model scales from 1.5B to 14B parameters, Pilot-Commit matches baseline accuracy with significantly lower sampling costs, reaching target accuracy up to $1.9\times$ faster than GRPO and $4.0\times$ faster than DAPO in cumulative rollouts.

AI.tldr: Pilot-Commit introduces a budget-aware, two-stage rollout allocation for group-based RL post-training that decouples prompt informativeness estimation from exploitation, achieving baseline accuracy with substantially reduced sampling costs and faster convergence than GRPO and DAPO.
AI.motivation: Online, on-policy RL for post-training LLMs is compute-intensive; group-based updates waste budgets on prompts with high reward variance. Accurate per-prompt informativeness estimates are needed online as the policy evolves.
AI.method: A two-stage framework: (1) Pilot stage uses a fraction of the budget to estimate per-prompt informativeness; (2) Exploitation stage allocates remaining rollouts to high-leverage prompts and skips low-signal prompts, enabling budget-aware group updates across prompts.
AI.result: Pilot-Commit matches baseline accuracy while substantially reducing sampling costs; achieves up to 1.9x faster cumulative rollouts than GRPO and up to 4.0x faster than DAPO across math reasoning benchmarks on models from 1.5B to 14B parameters.
AI.conclusion: Budget-aware, online informativeness estimation enables efficient group-based RL post-training by focusing rollouts on high-leverage prompts, maintaining performance while reducing computational expenditure.

title: Natural Language Query to Configuration for Retrieval Agents
summary: Modern retrieval agents expose many configuration choices -- LLM, retriever, number of documents, number of hops, and synthesis strategy -- each shaping both answer quality and serving cost. Today, these pipelines are typically hand-tuned once per workload, leaving substantial per-query optimization untapped. We formulate the problem: given a natural-language query and either an accuracy or a budget target, select from a predefined pipeline catalog the configuration that minimizes cost or maximizes accuracy at inference time. We propose **BRANE**, which uses an LLM to convert each query into workload-specific characteristics, then trains a lightweight per-configuration predictor that estimates whether the pipeline will answer the query correctly. At inference time, **BRANE** selects the configuration that maximizes predicted correctness penalized by cost, exposing a tunable cost-quality tradeoff without retraining. Across MuSiQue, BrowseComp-Plus, and FinanceBench, **BRANE** consistently pushes the cost-quality Pareto frontier, matches the best fixed configuration's accuracy at up to 89% lower cost, and outperforms LLM-routing, rule-based, and fine-tuned Qwen3-4B baselines. These results show that per-query configuration of the full retrieval pipeline is a practical alternative to static workload-level tuning.

AI.tldr: BRANE uses an LLM-driven meta-prediction to select per-query retrieval configurations from a catalog, optimizing cost vs. accuracy at inference time without retraining.
AI.motivation: Retrieval pipelines are typically hand-tuned per workload, leaving per-query optimization untapped. There is a need to dynamically balance accuracy and cost for diverse queries.
AI.method: An LLM converts each query into workload-specific features. A lightweight per-configuration predictor estimates the likelihood of correct answers. At inference, BRANE selects the configuration that maximizes predicted correctness penalized by cost, without retraining, exposing a tunable cost-quality tradeoff.
AI.result: BRANE consistently improves the cost-quality Pareto frontier across MuSiQue, BrowseComp-Plus, and FinanceBench, matching best fixed accuracy at up to 89% lower cost and outperforming LLM-routing, rule-based, and fine-tuned baselines.
AI.conclusion: Per-query configuration of the full retrieval pipeline is a practical alternative to static workload tuning, enabling cost-efficient, accurate retrieval without extra training.

title: Learning Reference-Guided Exposure Correction with Hybrid Illumination Characteristics
summary: We present HICNet, a reference-guided exposure correction framework. A lightweight, content-agnostic encoder distills each image into a compact illumination embedding capturing regional brightness, edge contrast, and higher-order luminance moments. The embedding difference between a source and its reference drives a multi-scale modulation network that combines FiLM-based global adjustment with Photometric Channel Rebalancing for fine-grained, illumination-aware spectral gating, producing exposure-matched outputs while faithfully preserving scene details. A cross-batch contrastive loss orders the illumination manifold, bolstering robustness to diverse lighting conditions. Trained without ground truth or intrinsic decomposition, HICNet attains better accuracy on public benchmarks and generalizes well to entirely unseen scenes.

AI.tldr: HICNet provides reference-guided exposure correction via a lightweight encoder that derives an illumination embedding; a multi-scale modulation network uses embedding differences with FiLM and Photometric Channel Rebalancing to produce exposure-matched outputs; trained with cross-batch contrastive loss, it requires no ground-truth and generalizes to unseen scenes.
AI.motivation: to robustly correct exposure across diverse lighting without ground-truth or intrinsic decomposition, and to structure the illumination manifold for improved transfer.
AI.method: A content-agnostic encoder distills each image into a compact illumination embedding capturing regional brightness, edge contrast, and higher-order luminance moments. The embedding difference between source and reference drives a multi-scale modulation network that combines FiLM-based global adjustment with Photometric Channel Rebalancing for fine-grained, illumination-aware spectral gating. A cross-batch contrastive loss orders the illumination manifold to improve robustness across lighting conditions. Training is done without ground truth or intrinsic decomposition.
AI.result: The authors report better accuracy on public benchmarks and good generalization to entirely unseen scenes, indicating robustness to diverse lighting and effective exposure correction without GT supervision.
AI.conclusion: HICNet delivers exposure-matched outputs while preserving scene details, with a lightweight encoder-driven, reference-guided approach and cross-batch contrastive learning that enables strong cross-scene generalization without ground-truth data.

title: PIDM-DP: Physics-Informed Diffusion with Dormand-Prince Integration for Chaotic System Identification and State Reconstruction across Multiple Dynamical Regimes
comment: extended work of my journal paper submission
summary: Reconstructing continuous state trajectories of chaotic dynamical systems from sparse, noisy observations remains a fundamental open problem in nonlinear science. We introduce the Physics-Informed Diffusion Model with Dormand-Prince Integration (PIDM-DP), which embeds a fully differentiable 5th-order Dormand-Prince (DP-RK45) ODE integrator directly into the reverse sampling loop of a Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Model (DDPM). At each denoising step, physics residuals are back-propagated via automatic differentiation, constraining every generated trajectory to satisfy the system's governing equations to 5th-order accuracy. A linear-scheduled guidance mechanism that ramps the physics weight from zero at high noise levels to its full value near the clean-data limit prevents the gradient explosions that cause naive physics-informed approaches to fail on stiff systems with Jacobian eigenvalues of order $O(10^3)$. Evaluated across five benchmark systems of increasing complexity 3D Lorenz, 3D Rössler, 5D Hyperchaotic, 20D Lorenz-96, and the stiff 3D Rabinovich-Fabrikant at 10% observation density with additive Gaussian noise ($σ=0.05$), PIDM-DP achieves reconstruction RMSE improvements of up to $15.4\times$ over an unconstrained diffusion baseline and decisively outperforms the Ensemble Kalman Filter on stiff systems where ensemble covariance collapses. On the Rabinovich-Fabrikant out-of-distribution benchmark, PIDM-DP attains RMSE $0.1097 \pm 0.0269$ versus $0.9443 \pm 0.5288$ (unconstrained diffusion, $8.6\times$ worse) and $0.3561 \pm 0.3040$ (EnKF, $3.2\times$ worse), with $p<0.001$ in paired Wilcoxon tests ($N = 30$). Topological validation via the Rosenstein Lyapunov estimator confirms that PIDM-DP preserves the chaotic invariant measure.

AI.tldr: A physics-informed diffusion model with an embedded 5th-order Dormand-Prince ODE integrator (PIDM-DP) reconstructs continuous chaotic dynamics from sparse, noisy observations, enforcing governing equations during reverse diffusion and using a linear-scheduled physics weight.
AI.motivation: Reconstruct continuous state trajectories of chaotic systems from limited, noisy data; existing methods struggle with stiffness and preserving chaotic invariants while ensuring physical consistency.
AI.method: Integrate a fully differentiable DP-RK45 ODE solver into the reverse sampling loop of a DDPM. At each denoising step, backpropagate physics residuals to constrain trajectories to the governing equations with 5th-order accuracy. Use a linear-scheduled guidance that ramps physics weight from zero to full near clean data to avoid gradient explosions for stiff systems. Evaluate on five benchmarks (3D Lorenz, 3D Rössler, 5D Hyperchaotic, 20D Lorenz-96, 3D Rabinovich-Fabrikant) at 10% observations with Gaussian noise (σ=0.05).
AI.result: PIDM-DP achieves RMSE improvements up to 15.4x over unconstrained diffusion; outperforms Ensemble Kalman Filter on stiff systems; on Rabinovich-Fabrikant out-of-distribution benchmark, RMSE 0.1097±0.0269 vs 0.9443±0.5288 (unconstrained diffusion) and 0.3561±0.3040 (EnKF), with p<0.001 (paired Wilcoxon, N=30). Rosenstein Lyapunov validation indicates preservation of chaotic invariant measure.
AI.conclusion: PIDM-DP demonstrates that integrating a high-order, differentiable ODE solver into a diffusion framework with physics-informed priors yields accurate, robust trajectory reconstruction in chaotic, stiff, and high-dimensional regimes, including OOD settings.

title: MUSE-Autoskill: Self-Evolving Agents via Skill Creation, Memory, Management, and Evaluation
comment: 30 pages, 8 figures, 13 tables, working in progress
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents rely on reusable skills to solve complex tasks. However, existing skill creation approaches treat skills as isolated and static artifacts, limiting their reusability, reliability, and long-term improvement. We propose MUSE-Autoskill Agent (Memory-Utilizing Skill Evolution), a skill-centric agent framework that lets agents continuously improve their task-solving capability by creating, reusing, and refining skills under a unified lifecycle (creation, memory, management, evaluation, and refinement). Our framework enables agents to create skills on demand, store and reuse them across tasks, organize and select them efficiently, and evaluate them through unit tests and runtime feedback for continuous refinement. We further introduce skill-level memory that accumulates experience for each skill across tasks, enabling more effective reuse and adaptation over time. Experiments on SkillsBench provide initial evidence that lifecycle-managed skills can improve task success, efficiency, reuse, and cross-agent transfer, highlighting the importance of treating skills as long-lived, experience-aware, and testable assets.

AI.tldr: Introduces MUSE-Autoskill, a memory-utilizing, lifecycle-driven framework that creates, stores, and refines skills for LLM agents; skills are long-lived, memory-enabled assets evaluated via unit tests and runtime feedback, improving reuse and cross-task transfer as shown on SkillsBench.
AI.motivation: Skills are currently treated as isolated, static artifacts, hindering reuse, reliability, and long-term improvement; a unified lifecycle and per-skill memory can enable continual refinement and cross-task transfer.
AI.method: Define MUSE-Autoskill Agent with lifecycle stages: creation, memory, management, evaluation, refinement; on-demand skill creation, skill memory accumulation across tasks, organized storage, efficient retrieval/select; evaluation via unit tests and runtime feedback; experiments on SkillsBench to assess task success, efficiency, reuse, cross-agent transfer.
AI.result: Evidence that lifecycle-managed skills improve task success rates, efficiency, reuse across tasks and cross-agent transfer; memory-augmented skills help adaptation.
AI.conclusion: Treat skills as long-lived, experience-aware, testable assets; lifecycle-managed, memory-equipped approach enables continual skill improvement and broader applicability across agents.

title: CIRCLED: A Multi-turn CIR Dataset with Consistent Dialogues across Domains
summary: Existing Multi-Turn Composed Image Retrieval (MTCIR) datasets lack dialogue-history consistency and are restricted to the fashion domain. To address these limitations, we construct CIRCLED by extending FashionIQ, CIRR, and CIRCO. In CIRCLED, the query at each turn progressively approaches the target image. Data are generated via a CIReVL-based retrieval pipeline and curated with multiple filters on retrieval success, turn length, consistency, and information redundancy to ensure quality. In total, we collect 22,608 multi-turn sessions across nine subsets, substantially exceeding Multi-turn FashionIQ (11,505 sessions) in both scale and generality. We further apply multiple baseline methods and quantitatively assess retrieval accuracy on CIRCLED. Our work provides a practical, high-quality benchmark to facilitate future research on multi-turn CIR. The dataset and code are publicly available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/tk1441/CIRCLED and https://github.com/mti-lab/circled.

AI.tldr: Presents CIRCLED, a large, multi-turn image retrieval benchmark addressing dialogue-history consistency and domain generality by extending FashionIQ, CIRR, and CIRCO; 22,608 sessions across nine subsets; CIReVL-based data generation and filtering; baseline evaluations; code/dataset released.
AI.motivation: Need for consistent dialogue history in Multi-Turn Composed Image Retrieval (MTCIR) and broader domain coverage beyond fashion; existing datasets are limited in dialogue coherence and scope.
AI.method: Extend existing datasets to form CIRCLED; generate data via a CIReVL-based retrieval pipeline; apply filters on retrieval success, turn length, consistency, and information redundancy; assemble 22,608 multi-turn sessions across nine subsets; evaluate with multiple baselines.
AI.result: Substantial scale and generality gains over prior datasets (e.g., more sessions than Multi-turn FashionIQ); baseline retrieval accuracy assessed on CIRCLED; demonstrates practicality and utility as a benchmark.
AI.conclusion: CIRCLED provides a high-quality, scalable benchmark to advance multi-turn CIR research; data and code are publicly available for community use.

title: RT-Lynx: Putting the GEMM Sparsity In a Right Way for Diffusion Models
summary: Diffusion Transformers (DiT) achieve strong performance in image generation but incur substantial inference costs. While prior work has reduced this cost via quantization and distillation, semi-structured sparsity, which can nearly halve FLOPs, remains underexplored. A key reason is that most existing approaches focus on weight sparsification, and pruning 50% of the weights can remove critical model capacity and degrade generation quality. Our study, however, shows that DiT activations are intrinsically sparse and significantly more robust to N:M semi-structured sparsification than weights. Motivated by this observation, we advocate a paradigm shift from weight sparsification to activation sparsification. We propose RT-Lynx, which applies N:M sparsification to activations and incorporates error-compensation techniques to mitigate accuracy loss. We further implement highly optimized CUDA kernels tailored to this setting, achieving up to a 1.55x speedup on average in linear layers. Extensive experiments across multiple diffusion models demonstrate that our method preserves the generation quality of the original models while substantially accelerating inference.

AI.tldr: Activation sparsification via N:M on DiT (RT-Lynx) shifts focus from weight pruning to activations, achieving ~1.55x average speedup in linear layers while preserving generation quality across diffusion models.
AI.motivation: Reduce high inference cost of diffusion transformers by leveraging intrinsic sparsity in activations, which are more robust to N:M sparsification than weights; address limitations of weight-centric pruning (capacity loss and quality degradation).
AI.method: Introduce RT-Lynx that applies N:M sparsification to activations with error-compensation techniques; develop optimized CUDA kernels for the new sparsification regime; evaluate on multiple diffusion models.
AI.result: Maintains generation quality comparable to unpruned models while delivering substantial inference acceleration; up to 1.55x speedup on average in linear layers across tested models.
AI.conclusion: Advocates a paradigm shift to activation-level sparsification for diffusion models; demonstrates practical gains and motivates further hardware/software co-design to exploit activation sparsity in Transformers.

title: Self-Intersection-Aware 3D Human Motion Generation Using an Efficient Human Sphere Proxy
summary: Human motion generation has made tremendous progress in recent years, with state-of-the-art approaches surpassing ground truth data in leading evaluation benchmarks. However, visual inspection of the generated motions paints a different picture. Even state-of-the-art approaches generate motions frequently containing self-intersections, i.e., body parts interpenetrating, which are strong artifacts, severely limiting the perceived motion quality. We introduce a novel loss, which explicitly penalizes self-intersections, to the training of human motion generation methods. We base our loss on a sphere proxy of human geometry, which allows us to calculate a self-intersection loss 98% faster and uses 83% less memory than comparable methods based on triangular meshes. The loss is agnostic to the specific approach, and we add it to the training of the recent human motion generation methods human motion diffusion model (MDM) and MoMask. Our extensive experiments show a reduction of self-intersections in generated motions of up to 49% while improving other evaluation metrics. The code is available at https://github.com/boschresearch/humansphereproxy .

AI.tldr: Introduces a sphere proxy-based self-intersection loss for human motion generation, reducing interpenetrations while being faster and memory-efficient; improves MDM and MoMask with up to 49% fewer self-intersections; code released.
AI.motivation: Self-intersections are visually jarring artifacts that persist despite high numeric accuracy; mesh-based penalties are costly; a lightweight geometric proxy could enable efficient penalization of interpenetrations.
AI.method: Define a differentiable sphere proxy for human geometry; compute a self-intersection loss based on proxy interactions; integrate into training of existing methods (MDM, MoMask) without architectural changes; evaluate speed/memory versus mesh-based losses and quantify artifact reduction.
AI.result: Self-intersections reduced by up to 49%; improvements on other evaluation metrics; substantial computational efficiency (≈98% faster, ≈83% less memory) compared to mesh-based methods; code available.
AI.conclusion: Sphere proxy-based self-intersection loss is effective, efficient, and broadly applicable to motion generation; opens path for better perceptual quality without heavy geometry processing; potential for wider adoption and extension.

title: More Expressive Feedforward Layers: Part I. Token-Adaptive Mixing of Activations
summary: Feedforward network (FFN) layers account for a large fraction of parameters and nonlinear expressivity in Transformer-based large language models (LLMs). Despite the evolution from ReLU and GELU to gated variants such as SwiGLU, most FFN designs still use a single fixed activation function, applying the same nonlinear transformation to all tokens. In this work, we propose Mixture of Activations (MoA), a token-adaptive FFN design that mixes a dictionary of activation functions using lightweight input-dependent gates while sharing the same linear projections. As an input-independent counterpart, we also introduce learnable activations (LA), which form linear combinations of activation functions for both ReLU-type and SwiGLU-type FFNs. Theoretically, we establish strict finite-width expressive separations among fixed-activation FFNs, LA, and MoA: LA strictly contains fixed-activation FFNs, while MoA strictly contains LA, with the additional expressivity arising from input-dependent nonlinear hybridization. Empirically, we evaluate MoA through extensive pre-training experiments on dense and MoE language models ranging from 0.12B to 2B parameters under different token budgets, optimizers, and learning rate schedules. MoA consistently achieves lower terminal loss and exhibits more favorable scaling behavior than well-tuned baselines, with minimal parameter and computational overhead. These results suggest that token-adaptive activation mixing is a simple and effective mechanism for improving FFN expressivity in LLMs.

AI.tldr: Mixture of Activations (MoA) enables token-adaptive FFN activations via a dictionary with input-dependent gates, paired with Learnable Activations (LA) for fixed-activation FFNs. It establishes strict finite-width expressivity hierarchies (fixed-activation < LA < MoA) and shows MoA improves pre-training loss and scaling with minimal overhead across 0.12B–2B parameter LLMs.
AI.motivation: Fixation on a single activation per FFN token limits nonlinear expressivity. The work seeks token-adaptive activation mixing to boost expressivity without substantial parameter or compute costs, applicable to both dense and Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) LLMs.
AI.method: Introduce MoA: a dictionary of activation functions and lightweight input-dependent gates that mix activations while sharing the same linear projections. Also introduce LA (Learnable Activations): linear combinations of activations for ReLU-type and SwiGLU-type FFNs. Provide theoretical results showing strict finite-width expressivity separations: fixed-activation FFNs ⊂ LA ⊂ MoA.
AI.result: Extensive pre-training experiments on dense and MoE LLMs from 0.12B to 2B params across varying token budgets, optimizers, and learning-rate schedules. MoA achieves lower terminal loss and better scaling with minimal overhead compared to strong baselines.
AI.conclusion: Token-adaptive activation mixing is a simple, effective mechanism to enhance FFN expressivity in LLMs, applicable with negligible parameter and computational overhead.

title: Once-For-All: A Train-Once and Select-Anytime Framework for Multimodal Instruction Tuning
comment: 15 pages, 6 figures. Mingkang Dong and Hongyi Cai contributed equally to this work. Muxin Pu is the corresponding author
summary: Multimodal instruction tuning is the de facto recipe for adapting vision language models (VLMs), yet instruction data are highly redundant, making data selection critical for training efficiency. Existing methods derive selection signals from a specific model or dataset, so whenever the target model or candidate pool changes, the criteria must be recomputed from scratch at substantial cost. To address this, we propose OFA, a data selection framework that trains a reusable selector once and applies it to any dataset or model without recomputation. OFA clusters multimodal instructions in a frozen CLIP space, derives pseudo labels from the cluster structure, and trains a lightweight selector for only a few epochs; samples on which this selector is least confident are selected as the most informative. Once trained, the frozen selector transfers directly across datasets and model scales. The selector is trained once on LLaVA-665K and applied both to LLaVA-665K itself and, without any retraining, to the unseen Vision-Flan-186K. Selecting only 15% of the data, OFA achieves 98.3% of full data performance across 10 downstream benchmarks; on the smaller Vision-Flan-186K, the transferred selector surpasses full data training by 10.6%, confirming that the learned signal generalizes to datasets never seen during selector training. The same selected subsets benefit VLMs at both Qwen2.5-VL-3B and LLaVA-v1.5-7B without per model recomputation, decoupling selection from the target model. These results demonstrate that a single, transferable selector provides an effective and reusable solution for efficient multimodal instruction tuning.

AI.tldr: A transferable, once-trained data selector for multimodal instruction tuning enables efficient data selection across models/datasets, achieving near full-data performance with only 15% data and generalizing to unseen datasets.
AI.motivation: Data redundancy in multimodal instruction tuning leads to costly re-computation of selection signals whenever the model or candidate pool changes; a reusable selector would improve training efficiency.
AI.method: Cluster multimodal instructions in a frozen CLIP space; derive pseudo labels from cluster structure; train a lightweight selector for a few epochs; select samples where the selector is least confident; freeze and transfer the selector to other datasets/models without retraining; demonstrate trained-on LLaVA-665K applied to LLaVA-665K and Vision-Flan-186K; 15% data yields strong performance.
AI.result: 15% data with OFA yields 98.3% of full-data performance across 10 downstream benchmarks; on Vision-Flan-186K the transferred selector outperforms full-data training by 10.6%; benefits multiple VLMs (Qwen2.5-VL-3B, LLaVA-v1.5-7B) without per-model recomputation.
AI.conclusion: A single, transferable selector provides an effective, reusable solution for efficient multimodal instruction tuning by decoupling data selection from the target model and generalizing to unseen datasets.

title: Bilevel Optimization over Saddle Points of Zero-Sum Markov Games
comment: Accepted to the International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026)
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) often has a hierarchical structure, where an upper-level (UL) learner selects model parameters and a lower-level (LL) decision-making process responds, naturally leading to a bilevel optimization problem. Most existing bilevel RL methods assume a single-policy LL Markov decision process (MDP), and therefore fail to capture competitive structures arising in applications such as incentive design, where multiple policies interact. We study bilevel optimization problems in which the LL problem is a regularized min-max zero-sum Markov game and the UL objective is optimized through the saddle-point equilibrium induced by the LL game. In this work, we propose penalty-augmented Nikaido-Isoda descent-ascent (PANDA), a penalty-based first-order policy-gradient method based on the Nikaido-Isoda function. By exploiting the min-max game structure, PANDA avoids computing UL hypergradients and does not require second-order information. We prove that PANDA converges to stationary points without convexity assumptions on either the UL or LL objectives. Moreover, PANDA reaches an $ε$-stationary point in $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(ε^{-1})$ iterations with sample complexity $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(ε^{-3})$, matching the best-known rates for bilevel RL with single-policy LL MDPs. Experiments demonstrate the superior performance of PANDA over closely related baselines.

AI.tldr: Proposes PANDA, a penalty-augmented Nikaido-Isoda method for bilevel RL where the lower-level is a regularized min-max zero-sum Markov game; it avoids UL hypergradients, converges to stationary points without convexity, with ε-stationary ~O~(ε^-1) iterations and sample complexity ~O~(ε^-3); experiments show superior performance.
AI.motivation: Model hierarchical RL with interacting lower-level policies (competitive designs) that are not captured by single-policy LL MDPs. Address the computational cost of UL hypergradients and second-order information, and extend bilevel RL to min-max LL games.
AI.method: Penalty-augmented Nikaido-Isoda descent-ascent (PANDA); a first-order policy-gradient method based on the Nikaido-Isoda function, using penalty to couple UL and LL without requiring UL hypergradients or second-order information; leverages the min–max game structure to induce UL saddle-point.
AI.result: Theoretically guarantees convergence to stationary points without convexity assumptions; achieves an ε-stationary point in roughly Õ(ε^-1) iterations with sample complexity roughly Õ(ε^-3); performance matches the best-known rates for bilevel RL with single-policy LL MDPs.
AI.conclusion: PANDA provides an efficient, robust method for bilevel RL when the LL problem is a regularized min-max Markov game, eliminating hypergradient computations and offering solid convergence guarantees and practical performance gains in incentive-design-like settings.

title: Cesarean Scar Defect Segmentation in Transvaginal Ultrasound Images: a Dataset and Benchmark
summary: Cesarean Scar Defect (CSD) is one of the most prevalent complications following cesarean delivery. Transvaginal ultrasonography is widely used for primary CSD screening. Accurate determination of CSD outline and dimensions is crucial for treatment. However, CSDs are frequently overlooked by sonographers due to small size and irregular morphology, suboptimal image quality, and limited clinical awareness in resource-constrained settings. Despite artificial intelligence advances in medical imaging, no public dataset exists for transvaginal ultrasound CSD segmentation. To address this gap, we present a comprehensive CSD dataset comprising 1,111 images and 16 videos, yielding 501 positive samples with confirmed CSD and precise pixel-level manual annotations. Annotations are performed following standardized clinical guidelines through collaboration between experienced sonographers and trained PhD students. This work provides high-quality benchmark resources for advancing medical image segmentation algorithms and promoting clinical innovation. Ultimately, improved CSD diagnosis and subsequent treatment strategies can enhance the quality of life in women of reproductive age, representing significant value for both medical research and clinical practice.

AI.tldr: Public dataset for transvaginal ultrasound Cesarean Scar Defect (CSD) segmentation: 1,111 images and 16 videos, with 501 confirmed positive samples and pixel-level annotations; aims to enable robust AI segmentation and improve clinical practice.
AI.motivation: CSD is a common post-cesarean complication that is often overlooked due to small/irregular lesions and limited imaging quality, especially in resource-limited settings. There is a lack of publicly available datasets for CSD segmentation, hindering development of automated tools and standardized evaluation.
AI.method: Collection of 1,111 transvaginal ultrasound images and 16 videos; 501 samples with confirmed CSD; pixel-level manual annotations following standardized clinical guidelines, performed by experienced sonographers in collaboration with trained PhD students.
AI.result: Provides a high-quality benchmark resource for medical image segmentation in CSD, enabling evaluation and development of AI algorithms; facilitates reproducibility and comparability across studies.
AI.conclusion: Enhanced CSD diagnosis and treatment planning, potentially improving reproductive-age women's quality of life; the dataset supports medical research and clinical innovation.

title: WINDQuant: Weight-Informed Neural Decision-Making for Global Mixed-Precision LLM Quantization
summary: Quantization is an effective approach to reduce the memory footprint and inference cost of large language models (LLMs), yet maintaining performance in the ultra-low-bit regime remains challenging. Existing post-training methods often suffer from severe accuracy degradation, while quantization-aware training requires costly retraining and additional resources. Moreover, most mixed-precision strategies rely on coarse-grained or heuristic sensitivity analysis that overlooks fine-grained variations within weight matrices. We propose WINDQuant, a reinforcement-learning-based allocation controller for ultra-low-bit LLM quantization. Rather than introducing another low-level quantization operator, WINDQuant learns how to assign bit-widths and quantization treatments to fine-grained column chunks under a global storage budget. By operating at the column-chunk level, WINDQuant enables flexible and fine-grained precision assignment within layers under a global target bit-width. The implementation combines PPO with activation-aware calibration, lightweight per-unit quantizer fitting, and explicit effective-bit accounting of the learned mixed-precision plan. Experiments on LLaMA models demonstrate that WINDQuant achieves competitive performance in ultra-low-bit settings while reducing optimization overhead relative to retraining-based approaches, highlighting reinforcement learning as a practical controller for adaptive mixed-precision quantization.

AI.tldr: WINDQuant uses a reinforcement-learning-based controller (PPO) to allocate ultra-low-bit quantization at the column-chunk level within LLM layers, subject to a global storage budget. It integrates activation-aware calibration, lightweight per-unit quantizer fitting, and explicit effective-bit accounting to enable fine-grained precision choices without retraining, achieving competitive ultra-low-bit performance on LLaMA with reduced optimization overhead.
AI.motivation: Quantization is essential to reduce memory and compute for large language models, but preserving accuracy in ultra-low-bit regimes is challenging. Traditional post-training quantization (PTQ) degrades accuracy, while quantization-aware training (QAT) is costly. Existing mixed-precision methods often rely on coarse, heuristic sensitivity analyses that miss fine-grained variations within weight matrices, limiting benefit.
AI.method: WINDQuant introduces a reinforcement-learning-based allocation controller that operates at the column-chunk level to assign bit-widths and quantization treatments under a global storage budget. The method combines: (1) PPO-based policy optimization for mixed-precision decisions, (2) activation-aware calibration to inform the controller, (3) lightweight per-unit quantizer fitting to adapt quantizers at fine granularity, and (4) explicit accounting of effective bits to track the actual storage impact of the learned plan. The design avoids introducing new low-level quant operators and instead manipulates quantization settings at the column-chunk level within existing quantization frameworks.
AI.result: On LLaMA models, WINDQuant achieves competitive performance in ultra-low-bit settings while reducing optimization overhead compared with retraining-based approaches. The results demonstrate that an RL controller can effectively allocate mixed precision at fine granularity to meet global storage budgets without substantial retraining, highlighting practical applicability of reinforcement learning for adaptive mixed-precision quantization in large models.
AI.conclusion: Reinforcement-learning-based mixed-precision control at the column-chunk level is a viable and effective strategy for ultra-low-bit LLM quantization, offering fine-grained precision allocation under a global budget with reduced optimization overhead compared to retraining-based methods.

title: Beyond Trajectory-Level Attribution: Graph-Based Credit Assignment for Agentic Reinforcement Learning
summary: Group-based reinforcement learning (RL) methods have achieved remarkable success in improving the performance of large language models (LLMs) and have been rapidly extended to agentic tasks. However, their credit assignment relies heavily on coarse-grained trajectory-level attribution according to final outcomes, making it difficult to capture the contribution of individual steps, such as valuable steps obscured within failed trajectories. To uncover latent information and enable more faithful step-level credit assignment, we propose Graph-based Group Policy Optimization (GraphGPO), which first aggregates all rollout trajectories into a unified state-transition graph and then estimates the distance from each state to the task goal using the global information encoded in the graph. Finally, GraphGPO assigns credit to each edge by estimating a graph-based advantage, based on how much the transition reduces the distance to the task goal. In this way, GraphGPO significantly improves training efficiency and achieves state-of-the-art performance across a range of challenging benchmarks.

AI.tldr: A graph-based method (GraphGPO) for step-level credit assignment in group-based RL by building a state-transition graph from rollouts and computing edge-level advantages via distance-to-goal reductions, claiming faster training and SOTA on benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Coarse-grained trajectory-level credit assignment misses the contributions of individual steps; enabling fine-grained, interpretable credit assignment via global graph structure.
AI.method: Aggregate all rollout trajectories into a unified state-transition graph; estimate a distance-to-goal for each state using global graph information; assign edge credit by a graph-based advantage, measuring how much the transition reduces the distance to the task goal; integrate this into policy optimization to improve training efficiency.
AI.result: Reported significant training efficiency gains and state-of-the-art performance across multiple benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: GraphGPO provides a principled, scalable approach to step-level credit assignment by leveraging graph-based distance-to-goal measurements, enabling finer-grained credit attribution and improved sample efficiency.

title: Receipt Replay OOD: A Small Benchmark for Screen Replay Detection Under Domain Shift
summary: Public datasets such as DLC-2021, SynID, and KID34K have significantly contributed to research on presentation attack detection for identity documents, including screen replay attacks. However, evaluation of out-of-domain (OOD) robustness remains insufficiently explored, especially under realistic domain shifts. In this work, we introduce Receipt Replay OOD, a small out-of-domain benchmark for screen replay detection. Receipts share several characteristics with identity documents, including planar geometry, curved corners, wear-and-tear artifacts, and text or logo patterns, while avoiding personally identifiable information constraints commonly associated with identity documents. We evaluate document replay detection models under cross-domain conditions and demonstrate the impact of domain shift on generalization performance. The dataset is publicly available.

AI.tldr: Introduces Receipt Replay OOD, a small cross-domain benchmark for screen replay detection, to study OOD robustness; shows domain shift degrades generalization; dataset is publicly available.
AI.motivation: Address the limited exploration of out-of-domain robustness in presentation attack detection for identity documents by proposing a realistic, privacy-safe proxy dataset (receipts) that shares key visual characteristics with IDs.
AI.method: Create Receipt Replay OOD as a small out-of-domain benchmark for screen replay detection; evaluate existing document replay detectors in cross-domain settings to quantify the impact of domain shift on generalization; analyze performance differences across domain pairs.
AI.result: Demonstrates that domain shift adversely affects generalization of document replay detection models; the dataset is publicly released to enable further study and benchmarking.
AI.conclusion: Receipt Replay OOD provides a practical, privacy-friendly benchmark for evaluating OOD robustness in screen replay detection, prompting development of domain-invariant or transfer-learning approaches.

title: Self-Improvement Imitation with Biologically Guided Search for Protein Design Under Oracle Budgets
summary: Protein sequence optimization under tight oracle budgets requires methods that explore vast combinatorial spaces while making each evaluation informative. Existing reinforcement learning and off-policy generative approaches often degrade under surrogate noise, and position-agnostic mutation proposals risk disrupting functionally critical residues. We introduce SILO, a trajectory-level self-improvement imitation framework for oracle-budgeted protein design. SILO uses a hierarchical edit policy that decomposes each mutation into a position choice followed by a residue choice. In each active-learning round, the policy samples candidate trajectories via incremental stochastic beam search without replacement (SBS), and a UCB-based proxy ensemble, combined with an alanine-scan fitness score (AFS), selects candidates with functionally relevant edits for in silico oracle evaluation. The policy is then updated by next-action cross-entropy imitation on the round's best oracle-labeled trajectories, avoiding value-function estimation. Across eight reproduced protein fitness landscapes and five strong baselines from prior work, SILO achieves the highest maximum and top-100 mean fitness on 8 of 8 landscapes within our evaluations, often exhibiting faster early-stage improvement. In low-data and noisy-proxy stress tests on two landscapes per setting, SILO remains competitive or best when several baselines degrade. Ablations show that SBS with AFS account for much of the gains, with iterative imitation providing additional improvement. Code is available at: https://github.com/grimmlab/SILO.git

AI.tldr: SILO is a trajectory-level imitation framework for protein design under strict oracle budgets, combining a hierarchical edit policy with stochastic beam search and an alanine-scanning fitness proxy to select informative edits, achieving strong performance across multiple landscapes and baselines while being robust to noise.
AI.motivation: Protein sequence optimization under tight oracle budgets demands methods that efficiently explore combinatorial sequence space while ensuring each evaluation is informative. Existing RL and off-policy methods suffer from surrogate-noise; mutation proposals that ignore residue-level functionality risk disrupting critical residues. A method that selects informative edits and learns from oracle-labeled trajectories is needed.
AI.method: SILO uses a hierarchical edit policy that first selects a mutation position, then a residue; during active learning rounds it samples candidate trajectories via incremental stochastic beam search without replacement (SBS) and a UCB-based proxy ensemble, combined with an alanine-scan fitness score (AFS) to pick edits with functional relevance for in silico oracle evaluation. The policy is updated by next-action cross-entropy imitation on the best oracle-labeled trajectories, avoiding value-function estimation. This framework emphasizes trajectory-level imitation rather than traditional value-based RL.
AI.result: Across eight reproduced protein fitness landscapes and five strong baselines, SILO achieves the highest maximum and top-100 mean fitness on 8/8 landscapes, with faster early-stage improvement. In low-data and noisy-proxy regimes (two landscapes per setting), SILO remains competitive or best when baselines degrade. Ablations show SBS and AFS contribute most to gains, with iterative imitation providing additional improvements.
AI.conclusion: SILO demonstrates strong performance and robustness for oracle-budgeted protein design by integrating SBS-guided trajectory sampling, an alanine-scanning proxy, and imitation-based policy updates, with code available for replication.

title: REVERSE: Reinforcing Evidence Verification and Search for Agentic Image geo-localization
summary: Image geo-localization aims to determine where a photograph was taken, a task that often requires more than recognizing visible landmarks. Human experts typically solve it through an iterative workflow: they inspect informative regions, form location hypotheses, seek external evidence, and revise their judgments as new clues appear. Existing methods only partially capture this process: direct prediction methods bypass evidence acquisition altogether, while retrieval-augmented methods introduce external evidence but usually provide limited supervision on the intermediate decisions of where to search, how to query, and how to filter noisy results. We present REVERSE, a framework that reinforces the interplay between evidence search and verification to enable multi-turn agentic reasoning. REVERSE teaches three intermediate decisions: where to look, what to query, and what evidence to trust. To support this, we construct tool-grounded trajectories with annotated region selections, search observations, and geo-informative evidence labels, and introduce process rewards for visual grounding, query utility, and evidence discrimination. An offline search cache makes retrieval observations stable and reusable during reinforcement learning, enabling dense supervision over noisy search results. With a 4B model, REVERSE outperforms strong retrieval-augmented baselines and rivals substantially larger models on Im2GPS3k and YFCC4k. Code is available at https://github.com/yonglleee/REVERSE.

AI.tldr: REVERSE introduces a reinforcement-learning framework for image geo-localization that mimics human iterative reasoning by jointly learning where to look, what to query, and what evidence to trust. It uses tool-grounded trajectories, an offline search cache, and process rewards to supervise intermediate decisions, achieving strong results on benchmarks with a 4B model and releasing code for reproducibility.
