AI.motivation: Existing geo-localization methods either directly predict without evidence or rely on retrieval without explicit supervision of search/verification steps. The work aims to close this gap by enabling multi-turn, agentic reasoning with dense supervision over intermediate decisions.
AI.method: Train an agent to perform three intermediate decisions: where to look (region selection), what to query (search observations), and what evidence to trust (evidence labeling). Build tool-grounded trajectories with region selections, search observations, and geo-informative evidence labels. Use process rewards for visual grounding, query utility, and evidence discrimination. Employ an offline search cache to stabilize retrieval observations and provide dense supervision over noisy search results. Evaluate with a 4B-model and compare to retrieval-augmented baselines and larger models.
AI.result: REVERSE outperforms strong retrieval-augmented baselines and rivals substantially larger models on Im2GPS3k and YFCC4k datasets.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates that coupling iterative evidence search with verification, guided by dense intermediate supervision and caching, can yield strong geo-localization performance even with a comparatively small model; code is released for reproducibility.

title: Model Merging on Loss Landscape: A Geometry Perspective
comment: CVPR 2026 Findings Track. 18 pages, 4 figures, 6 tables
summary: Model merging offers a promising avenue for knowledge integration and parallel development without retraining. Yet, existing methods either ignore the geometry of the loss landscape or rely on intractable full-space Hessian approximations. We propose EpiMer, a framework that casts model merging as solving the Fréchet mean on a Riemannian manifold and restricts the computation to a low-rank subspace spanned by the task vectors. With the expected Hessian as the metric, we reveal a connection between local curvature and epistemic uncertainty of the parameters. Our theoretical analysis decomposes the merging error bound into the subspace Fréchet variance and the residual energy, and provides a closed-form characterization of when curvature-aware merging provably outperforms flat-geometry methods. In addition, our framework unifies both curvature-aware methods and recent spectral methods as special cases of the subspace Fréchet mean with different geometric metrics. Merging fine-tuned CLIP-ViT models on eight image classification tasks, Epistemic Merging strictly outperforms the baselines on all three CLIP-ViT backbones at matched rank, improving the across-task average accuracy and worst-task accuracy on every backbone.

AI.tldr: EpiMer frames model merging as a Fréchet mean on a Riemannian manifold, restricted to a low-rank subspace spanned by task vectors and using the expected Hessian as the metric; it provides a theory-based error decomposition and shows empirical gains on CLIP-ViT models across tasks.
AI.motivation: To enable knowledge integration via model merging without retraining, while incorporating the loss landscape geometry and avoiding intractable full-space Hessian approximations; and to unify curvature-aware methods with spectral approaches.
AI.method: Cast model merging as computing the Fréchet mean on a Riemannian manifold in a restricted low-rank subspace spanned by task vectors; use the expected Hessian as the metric; derive an error decomposition into subspace Fréchet variance and residual energy; provide closed-form conditions when curvature-aware merging outperforms flat-geometry methods; show that curvature-aware and spectral methods are special cases of the same subspace Fréchet-mean framework with different metrics.
AI.result: Empirically, Epistemic Merging outperforms baselines for merging fine-tuned CLIP-ViT models on eight image classification tasks across three backbones at matched rank, improving both average accuracy across tasks and worst-task accuracy.
AI.conclusion: A curvature-aware, subspace Fréchet-mean approach to model merging yields theoretical and empirical benefits, unifying curvature-aware and spectral methods and offering a practical path to knowledge integration without retraining.

title: RoadGIE: Towards A Global-Scale Aerial Benchmark for Generalizable Interactive Road Extraction
summary: Accurate road segmentation from aerial imagery is fundamental to many geospatial applications. However, existing datasets often suffer from limited scene diversity, low semantic granularity, and poor structural continuity, restricting their generalization across environments. To address these challenges, we introduce WorldRoadSeg-360K, the largest and most diverse road segmentation dataset to date, comprising 366,947 high-resolution images collected from 38 countries and 223 cities across various terrains and continents. WorldRoadSeg-360K serves as a comprehensive benchmark and reveals key challenges in handling diverse and structurally complex scenes. Automated approaches often struggle to preserve road connectivity, while current interactive methods lack efficient, topology-sensitive tools for real-world road editing. To this end, we present RoadGIE, establishing a novel interactive paradigm for road extraction in remote sensing. Unlike prior point- or box-based prompting strategies, RoadGIE supports connectivity-aware prompts, including clicks and scribbles, which inherently align with the topology of road networks. To improve structural consistency and mitigate performance degradation during iterative interactions, RoadGIE integrates an expert-guided prompting strategy and adapts the skeleton-based recall loss for interactive scenarios. RoadGIE achieves state-of-the-art performance in both segmentation accuracy and topological consistency on WorldRoadSeg-360K and other benchmarks, while maintaining efficient operation with only 3.7M parameters. The code are publicly available at: https://github.com/chaineypung/RoadGIE

AI.tldr: Largest diverse road segmentation dataset WorldRoadSeg-360K plus an interactive topology-aware road extraction model RoadGIE with prompts that preserve connectivity.
AI.motivation: Existing road datasets have limited scene diversity, low semantic granularity, and poor topology; current interactive methods lack topology-aware tools for real-world road editing.
AI.method: WorldRoadSeg-360K: dataset assembly from 38 countries, 223 cities; RoadGIE: connectivity-aware prompts (clicks, scribbles), expert-guided prompting strategy, skeleton-based recall loss; 3.7M parameters; code release.
AI.result: RoadGIE achieves state-of-the-art segmentation accuracy and topological consistency on WorldRoadSeg-360K and other benchmarks; efficient inference with 3.7M parameters.
AI.conclusion: The proposed dataset and interactive model push forward both dataset quality and topology-preserving road extraction; the approach offers practical tools for geospatial analysis and editing.

title: SL-BiLEM: Structured Learnable Behavior-in-the-Loop Epidemic Modeling for Forecasting and Policy Evaluation
summary: Epidemic forecasting faces a fundamental challenge: human behavior dynamically responds to disease spread, creating feedback loops that induce distribution shifts at policy intervention points. This renders data-driven models unreliable under distribution shift. We propose \textbf{SL-BiLEM} (Structured Learnable Behavior-in-the-Loop Epidemic Model), leveraging physical constraints as regularization for robust extrapolation. The framework decomposes effective transmission as $β_{\text{eff}}(t,g) = β_0(g) \times m_{\text{policy}}(t) \times m_{\text{media}}(t) \times m_{\text{comp}}(t,g)$, where monotonicity, smoothness, and bounded-jump constraints on the learned compliance function maintain predictive validity under novel policy regimes. Beyond forecasting, SL-BiLEM enables counterfactual analysis for intervention decision support. We validate forecasting on three real-world datasets (cruise ship, school influenza, and school-district COVID-19 surveillance) and evaluate counterfactual recovery on synthetic benchmarks with known ground truth. SL-BiLEM demonstrates: (1) 76\% improvement over neural-mechanistic baselines, with only 53\% OOD degradation versus 1142\% for neural baselines under policy-induced shift; (2) 100\% bootstrap CI coverage across 27 synthetic counterfactual experiments; and (3) Treatment Effect Accuracy exceeding 0.85. These results establish SL-BiLEM as an interpretable tool for public health decision-makers seeking accurate prediction and principled intervention planning.

AI.tldr: Structured Learnable Behavior-in-the-Loop Epidemic Model (SL-BiLEM) uses physics-inspired constraints to robustly forecast epidemics under behavior-driven distribution shifts and enables counterfactual intervention analysis.
AI.motivation: Human behavioral responses to disease create nonstationary dynamics; traditional data-driven models struggle under distribution shift at policy interventions.
AI.method: Decompose effective transmission β_eff(t,g) = β0(g) × m_policy(t) × m_media(t) × m_comp(t,g); enforce monotonicity, smoothness, and bounded-jump constraints on the learned compliance function to ensure validity under novel policy regimes; support counterfactual analysis for intervention planning; evaluate on real-world datasets (cruise ship, school influenza, school-district COVID-19) and synthetic benchmarks.
AI.result: (1) 76% improvement over neural-mechanistic baselines, with 53% OOD degradation vs 1142% for neural baselines under policy-induced shift; (2) 100% bootstrap CI coverage across 27 synthetic counterfactual experiments; (3) Treatment Effect Accuracy > 0.85.
AI.conclusion: SL-BiLEM offers an interpretable, robust forecasting framework with principled intervention planning suitable for public health decision-makers.

title: Natural Human Motion Recovery by Aligning High-Order Temporal Dynamics from Monocular Videos
comment: 13 pages, 6 figures. Accepted as an Oral presentation and Best Paper Candidate at CVPR 2026. Project page: https://zju3dv.github.io/htd-refine/
summary: Human motion recovered from monocular videos often appears overly smooth or dynamically inconsistent, even when joint positions are numerically accurate. We observe that this limitation stems from the absence of reliable high-order temporal cues -- velocity and acceleration -- which are essential for reconstructing motion that exhibits realistic momentum, timing, and high-frequency detail. We introduce HTD-Refine, a post-processing framework that augments existing Human Motion Recovery (HMR) pipelines using explicitly estimated high-order temporal dynamics. At the core of our system is PVA-Net, a temporal transformer that infers per-joint 2D positions, 3D velocities, and 3D accelerations directly from a monocular video. These predicted dynamics serve as soft yet informative constraints in a global optimization procedure that refines world-space trajectories, significantly reducing jitter, suppressing over-smoothing, and restoring physically plausible motion. Extensive experiments on challenging in-the-wild benchmarks show that HTD-Refine consistently improves state-of-the-art HMR methods, yielding more accurate global trajectories and substantially more natural motion dynamics. Our results highlight the critical role of high-order temporal modeling in advancing monocular human motion recovery.

AI.tldr: HTD-Refine uses high-order temporal cues estimated by PVA-Net to post-process monocular HMR outputs, improving trajectory quality and motion naturalness.
AI.motivation: Monocular HMR outputs are often overly smooth and temporally inconsistent due to the absence of high-frequency temporal cues like velocity and acceleration; incorporating these cues yields more realistic motion with correct momentum and timing.
AI.method: Introduce HTD-Refine framework; PVA-Net temporal transformer estimates per-joint 2D positions, 3D velocities, and 3D accelerations from video; use these as soft constraints in a global optimization to refine world-space trajectories.
AI.result: Significant reductions in jitter and over-smoothing; improved global trajectories; more natural motion dynamics; consistent improvements across state-of-the-art HMR baselines on challenging in-the-wild benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: High-order temporal dynamics are essential for realistic monocular human motion recovery, and HTD-Refine provides an effective post-processing approach that leverages these cues to enhance existing HMR pipelines.

title: Image Feature Fusion-based Federated Client Unlearning (FCU)
summary:
Major data protection regulations all mention the "right to be forgotten," and that's what pushed federated unlearning (FU) techniques forward. But one stubborn issue remains: catastrophic forgetting--you erase the target knowledge, yet somehow you also end up throwing out essential retained knowledge, which then hurts the model's global generalization.
  To get a better balance between unlearning effectiveness and generalization ability, we propose something called Image Feature Fusion-based Federated Client Unlearning (IFF-FCU). The idea is to bring in a linear Image Feature Fusion mechanism (Mixup) that dynamically creates mixed samples, bridging the gap between forget-distribution and retain-distribution. What this strategy does isn't just deleting a few discrete data points--it theoretically widens and regularizes the forgetting boundary.
  We ran extensive experiments on medical imaging benchmarks (RSNA-ICH and ISIC2018), and the results show that our approach achieves reasonably good unlearning. For instance, on the ICH dataset, IFF-FCU achieves a highly competitive Error deviation from the retrained gold standard, demonstrating robust improvements over existing baselines.

AI.tldr: IFF-FCU uses Mixup-based image feature fusion to enable federated unlearning by creating mixed samples that widen the forgetting boundary, achieving competitive unlearning while preserving generalization; validated on RSNA-ICH and ISIC2018.
AI.motivation: Address regulatory demand for the right to be forgotten in federated learning and mitigate catastrophic forgetting that degrades global generalization; seek a better balance between unlearning effectiveness and retained knowledge.
AI.method: Proposes Image Feature Fusion-based Federated Client Unlearning (IFF-FCU). Employs linear image feature fusion (Mixup) to dynamically synthesize mixed samples that bridge forget-distribution and retain-distribution, arguing that this regularizes the forgetting boundary; goes beyond deleting data points.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on medical imaging benchmarks RSNA-ICH and ISIC2018 shows reasonably good unlearning; on the ICH dataset, a highly competitive error deviation from the retrained gold standard, indicating robust improvements over baseline methods.
AI.conclusion: IFF-FCU offers a practical approach to balance unlearning and generalization in federated settings, with promising results on medical-imaging tasks; further work could extend analysis to more domains and provide deeper theoretical justification.

title: Small Object Detection in Industrial Recycling: A New Dataset and YOLO Performance Evaluation
summary: In this paper, we address the problem of detecting small, dense, and overlapping objects, a major challenge in computer vision. Our focus is on reviewing proposed methods based on deep learning supervised approaches. We provide a detailed comparison of these systems on a new dataset of more than 10k images and 120k instances, highlighting their performance, accuracy, and computational efficiency in the industrial recycling process use case. Through this comparative analysis, we identify the most reliable systems currently available and the specific challenges they are designed to tackle. Furthermore, we explore the benefits of data augmentation and synthetic images. Based on our analysis, we also propose potential future directions and innovative solutions that could enhance the effectiveness of small, dense and overlapped object detection systems. The scope of our investigations encompasses object detection, length measurement, and anomaly detection within the context of the recycling process. The anomaly detection strategy is robust against variations in image resolution and zoom levels, ensuring reliable performance in industrial applications. The repository of the proposed dataset, methods and evaluation codes can be found at: https://github.com/o-messai/SDOOD

AI.tldr: A survey of deep-learning methods for detecting small, dense, and overlapping objects in an industrial recycling setting, using a new large dataset (>10k images, ~120k instances). It compares methods on performance, accuracy, and efficiency; discusses data augmentation and synthetic data; analyzes object detection, length measurement, and anomaly detection; presents robust anomaly detection across resolution/zoom; provides dataset and code at GitHub; suggests future directions.
AI.motivation: Tackle the persistent challenge of small, densely packed, and overlapping objects in industrial environments, where accurate and efficient detection directly impacts process control and automation. The study aims to benchmark state-of-the-art supervised deep learning approaches, assess data augmentation and synthetic data benefits, and identify reliable systems for recycling applications.
AI.method: Comprehensive literature review of deep-learning supervised methods for small-object detection, construction or compilation of a new large-scale dataset (>10k images, 120k instances) from recycling imagery, systematic comparison of methods on the dataset focusing on detection accuracy and computational efficiency, exploration of data augmentation and synthetic data, and discussion of potential future directions. Includes analysis of three tasks: object detection, length measurement, and anomaly detection; proposes a robust anomaly-detection strategy across varying image resolutions and zoom levels; provides repository with dataset, methods, and evaluation codes.
AI.result: Identifies the most reliable small-object detectors for industrial recycling scenarios on the new dataset, reports on performance-computational trade-offs, and offers insights into when data augmentation and synthetic data are most beneficial. Highlights challenges unique to small/dense/overlapping objects and provides concrete recommendations and future research directions. Dataset and evaluation tools are released via GitHub (o-messai/SDOOD).
AI.conclusion: While effective methods exist for small, dense, and overlapped object detection in recycling contexts, performance hinges on dataset characteristics and augmentation strategies. The study suggests ongoing development of robust detectors, augmented with synthetic data, and integrated approaches that combine detection, measurement, and anomaly detection for reliable industrial deployment.

title: MTL-FNO: A Lightweight Multi-Task Fourier Neural Operator for Sparse Field Reconstruction
summary: Efficient onboard multi-field sparse reconstruction is essential for the autonomous operation of aerospace vehicles. While existing deep learning models exhibit promise for single-field reconstruction, deploying multiple independent models leads to prohibitive model size growth and fails to exploit cross-field correlations, particularly under few-shot conditions. To address these challenges, we first propose a lightweight multi-task Fourier neural operator (MTL-FNO), an end-to-end joint training framework based on hard parameter sharing. In each layer, the parameters are divided into shared and task-specific components to capture common features across fields while preserving task-specific characteristics. Moreover, the task-specific fine-tuning parameters are implemented as low-rank terms, achieving substantial model compression. Second, to address the difficulty of co-optimizing shared and task-specific parameters along with their real and imaginary parts, we revisit the FNO's spectral weight from a polar-form perspective and devise a physically meaningful decoupled optimization scheme. Specifically, we apply polar decomposition to slice-wise disentangle the spectral weight into a unitary tensor encoding phase information and a positive semi-definite tensor characterizing amplitude. By decoupling the optimization of phase and amplitude, our method can effectively mitigate tasks conflict. Meanwhile, to preserve unitary geometric fidelity during training, the Cayley transform is introduced to reparameterize the unitary tensor, converting the constrained optimization problem to an unconstrained one. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed method under few-shot conditions is validated on two representative engineering cases. Results show that MTL-FNO achieves accuracy comparable to or even surpassing that of standard FNO, while reducing total model size by 76% and 60%, respectively.

AI.tldr: A multi-task Fourier neural operator (MTL-FNO) for joint multi-field sparse reconstruction using hard parameter sharing, low-rank task-specific terms, and polar-decomposed spectral weight with Cayley reparameterization; achieves significant model compression (76% and 60%) while maintaining or improving accuracy under few-shot conditions.
AI.motivation: Reduce model size and exploit cross-field correlations for multi-field reconstruction, addressing the inefficiency of deploying separate models per field, especially in resource-constrained aerospace systems and limited data regimes.
AI.method: Hard parameter sharing across fields in Fourier neural operator layers; split parameters into shared and task-specific; task-specific parameters implemented as low-rank terms; spectral weight decomposed in polar form into phase (unitary) and amplitude (PSD); decoupled optimization of phase and amplitude; Cayley transform reparameterizes the unitary component to enforce unitary constraint; end-to-end training; validated on two engineering cases.
AI.result: Under few-shot settings, MTL-FNO achieves accuracy comparable to or surpassing standard FNO; total model size reduced by 76% and 60% in the two cases.
AI.conclusion: MTL-FNO effectively enables cross-field multi-task learning with substantial compression and robust performance, aided by decoupled optimization and stable unitary reparameterization; promising for efficient onboard inference in aerospace with scarce data.

title: SIMPC: Learning Self-Induced Mirror-Point Consistency for Unsupervised Point Cloud Denoising
comment: Accepted by ICML 2026. 17 pages, 8 figures, 8 tables
summary: In point clouds, noise directly perturbs point coordinates that encode both spatial location and geometry, making one-to-one correspondence construction more challenging than in images. Existing methods impose statistical mappings across noisy variants via noise or optimal transport, but suffer from correspondence ambiguity. In this work, we propose Self-Induced Mirror-Point Consistency (SIMPC) to learn deterministic correspondences between points and the underlying surface in an unsupervised manner. For each noisy point, SIMPC generates a mirror-point on the opposite side of the underlying surface, guided by geometric priors during the denoising process. By encouraging consistency between the denoising targets of the original point and its mirror counterpart, SIMPC effectively localizes the position of underlying surface. Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that SIMPC significantly outperforms state-of-the-art unsupervised methods and surpasses several strong supervised counterparts.

AI.tldr: Introduces SIMPC, an unsupervised framework for learning deterministic correspondences between noisy points and underlying surfaces in 3D point clouds by generating mirror-points and enforcing consistency during denoising, achieving strong results.
AI.motivation: Noise perturbs point coordinates and geometry in point clouds, making one-to-one correspondences difficult. Existing statistical mapping approaches suffer ambiguity; there is a need for deterministic, unsupervised surface-localization mechanisms.
AI.method: For each noisy point, generate a mirror-point on the opposite side of the underlying surface using geometric priors during denoising. Enforce consistency between the denoising targets of the original point and its mirror to localize the surface.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets show SIMPC significantly outperforms state-of-the-art unsupervised methods and surpasses several strong supervised counterparts.
AI.conclusion: SIMPC provides a robust unsupervised mechanism to learn point-to-surface correspondences and localize the underlying surface, highlighting a new direction for unsupervised point-cloud denoising via deterministic correspondences.

title: APEX: Amplitude Anchors and Phase Priors for Target-Scarce Higher-Frequency Wave Prediction
summary: Learning-based surrogates have become increasingly effective for wave-field prediction, and neural operators in particular have shown strong performance within observed frequency regimes. However, higher-frequency prediction under scarce target supervision remains comparatively underexplored, especially in wave problems where higher-frequency data are substantially more expensive to simulate or measure than lower-frequency data. A central difficulty is that cross-frequency transfer is inherently asymmetric: coarse amplitude structure remains relatively stable across frequencies, whereas phase-sensitive oscillatory structure deteriorates much more rapidly as frequency increases. Motivated by this asymmetry, we propose APEX, Amplitude-anchored and Phase-prior-guided Enhancement from eXtrapolated coarse predictions, a framework for target-scarce higher-frequency wave-field prediction. A lower-frequency neural operator first provides a coarse prediction in the target-frequency regime, from which we retain only the amplitude as a transferable structural anchor. A conditional flow-matching enhancer then reconstructs the target higher-frequency field under the guidance of a Green's-function-inspired phase prior. Experiments on SimpleWave, Helmholtz, and Maxwell benchmarks show that APEX consistently outperforms direct lower-to-higher extrapolation, target-adapted operator, and joint generative baselines under limited target-frequency supervision. Our results suggest that reliable higher-frequency prediction of oscillatory wave fields should not rely on direct end-to-end transfer of the full complex field, but instead on explicitly reusing transferable coarse structure while separately recovering the missing oscillatory detail.

AI.tldr: APEX uses amplitude-anchored coarse predictions and phase-prior-guided flow-matching to extrapolate high-frequency wave fields with limited target data, outperforming standard extrapolation and joint generative baselines.
AI.motivation: Cross-frequency transfer in wave problems is inherently asymmetric: coarse amplitude structure is relatively stable across frequencies, while phase-sensitive oscillatory content deteriorates rapidly with frequency. High-frequency data are expensive to obtain, motivating target-scarce learning.
AI.method: Pipeline: (1) use a lower-frequency neural operator to predict a coarse target-frequency field; (2) retain only the amplitude as a transferable structural anchor; (3) apply a conditional flow-matching enhancer guided by a Green's-function-inspired phase prior to reconstruct the high-frequency field.
AI.result: APEX consistently outperforms direct lower-to-higher extrapolation, target-adapted operator baselines, and joint generative baselines under limited target-frequency supervision on SimpleWave, Helmholtz, and Maxwell benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Reliable higher-frequency predictions should reuse transferable coarse amplitude structure and separately recover missing oscillatory detail; end-to-end transfer of the full complex field is suboptimal under target scarcity.

title: I2PRef: Image-Driven Point Completion with Iterative Refinement
summary: We present an image-conditioned point cloud completion approach that treats images as the primary geometric source rather than a secondary guide. To this end, we introduce an Image-to-Point (I2P) module that can reconstruct complete point clouds directly from a single RGB image, with no need for 3D inputs. Additionally, we introduce a transformer-based Point-to-Point (P2P) refinement module that uses self- and cross-attention between point tokens and image features to iteratively refine the coarse I2P output. The I2P module enables the image encoder to learn rich geometric representations, while the P2P module progressively recovers fine-grained details. Unlike existing multimodal methods that rely on auxiliary losses or fusion modules, our explicit I2P task provides a strong, geometry-aware prior based on images alone. Extensive experiments on ShapeNet-ViPC demonstrate state-of-the-art completion performance with a 12.3% relative Chamfer Distance improvement over prior methods. Code is available at: https://github.com/AzharSindhi/I2PRef.git

AI.tldr: Image-conditioned point cloud completion with an image-to-point (I2P) module and a transformer-based point-to-point (P2P) refinement, achieving state-of-the-art Chamfer Distance on ShapeNet-ViPC without 3D inputs.
AI.motivation: Give images a primary role as the geometric source for 3D completion, eliminating reliance on 3D inputs and heavy auxiliary losses, by learning geometry-aware representations directly from RGB images.
AI.method: Propose I2P module to reconstruct complete point clouds from a single RGB image (no 3D input). Introduce P2P refinement using self- and cross-attention between point tokens and image features to iteratively refine the coarse I2P output. Train end-to-end with image as sole geometric cue.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art completion performance on ShapeNet-ViPC with a 12.3% relative improvement in Chamfer Distance over prior methods.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that images can serve as a strong, geometry-aware prior for 3D completion through explicit image-to-point modeling and transformer-based refinement; releases code for reproducibility.

title: Revealing the core dimensions underlying representations in brains, behavior and AI
summary: The study of representations is widespread across fields, including neuroscience, psychology, and artificial intelligence. While representations are often studied and compared through similarities between stimuli, current methods provide only limited access to the dimensions that shape these representations and are often limited in interpretability. To overcome these challenges, here we introduce Similarity-Based Representation Factorization (SRF), a general computational method for recovering low-dimensional, non-negative, interpretable embeddings from similarity matrices derived from measured data. Across simulations and many neural, behavioral, and computational datasets, SRF recovers interpretable dimensions from diverse forms of representational data, even for very sparsely sampled, incomplete data. The dimensions derived from these datasets match those obtained by task-specific models, predict independent behavioral properties, improve exploratory analysis, and offer higher power for confirmatory hypothesis testing than comparing similarity matrices. Together, these results establish SRF as a general-purpose method with broad applications for uncovering, understanding, and leveraging the dimensions underlying representations.

AI.tldr: SRF is a general method to extract low-dimensional, non-negative, interpretable embeddings from representational similarity data, enabling robust, interpretable dimension discovery even with sparse data.
AI.motivation: Understanding representations is hampered by limited access to their true dimensions and interpretability; a general, data-driven method is needed to recover interpretable factors from similarity data across neuroscience, psychology, and AI.
AI.method: Similarity-Based Representation Factorization (SRF) recovers low-dimensional embeddings from similarity matrices derived from measured data, enforcing non-negativity to yield interpretable dimensions; demonstrated across simulations and neural/behavioral/computational datasets, including sparse/incomplete data.
AI.result: SRF recovers interpretable dimensions that align with task-specific models, predict independent behavioral properties, enhance exploratory analyses, and provide greater statistical power for confirmatory tests than direct similarity-matrix comparisons.
AI.conclusion: SRF constitutes a general-purpose framework for uncovering and leveraging the dimensions underlying representations with broad applicability across disciplines.

title: Localizing Memorized Regions in Diffusion Models via Coordinate-Wise Curvature Differences
summary: Diffusion models can unintentionally memorize training samples, raising concerns about privacy and copyright. While recent methods can detect memorization, they often rely on global or model-specific signals and provide limited insight into where memorization appears within a generated image. We provide a geometric characterization of local memorization as a coordinate-wise variance collapse. However, such collapse can also arise from intrinsic data constraints rather than overfitting. To isolate overfitting-driven memorization, we propose curvature-difference methods that subtract the curvature of an underfitted baseline, either the unconditional model or a less-trained version of itself. We further derive a score-difference proxy that provides a geometric explanation for the widely used score-difference-based detection metric. Experiments on Stable Diffusion, evaluated against ground-truth memorization masks, show that our method outperforms the prior attention-based localization method. Code is available at https://github.com/Gwangho99/mem-curv-diff.

AI.tldr: Proposes a geometric, local memorization analysis for diffusion models by viewing memorization as coordinate-wise variance collapse, and uses curvature-difference against a baseline to isolate overfitting-driven memorization; introduces a score-difference proxy and demonstrates improved localization over prior attention-based methods on Stable Diffusion, with code released.
AI.motivation: Address privacy and copyright concerns from diffusion-model memorization by not only detecting memorization but localizing where it occurs within generated images; existing methods rely on global or model-specific signals and lack local, actionable localization.
AI.method: Define local memorization as coordinate-wise variance collapse in generated images. To separate overfitting signals from intrinsic data constraints, use curvature-difference by subtracting the curvature of an underfitted baseline (unconditional model or weaker training). Derive a score-difference proxy to geometrically explain score-difference detection metrics. Evaluate using ground-truth memorization masks on Stable Diffusion and compare to attention-based localization; provide code.
AI.result: The proposed curvature-difference and score-difference framework yields improved localization of memorization compared with prior attention-based methods, as demonstrated on Stable Diffusion with ground-truth masks.
AI.conclusion: A geometrically grounded framework for local memorization detection—via variance collapse and curvature differences against baselines—offers more accurate localization and explains existing score-based detection metrics, with practical validation and available code.

title: Leveraging Text-to-Image Diffusion Models for Unsupervised Visual Object Tracking
comment: Accepted by IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence (TPAMI), 2026
summary: Unsupervised visual object tracking is a challenging task that requires following arbitrary targets in videos without training on ground-truth annotations. Despite considerable progress, existing state-of-the-art unsupervised trackers often struggle in scenarios that demand fine-grained understanding of semantic and visual structural information within video frames. Text-to-image diffusion models are well known for their ability to generate images that accurately reflect the semantics and structures described in the input prompt, demonstrating a strong grasp of visual semantics and structures. Building on this capability, we approach the unsupervised tracking from a new perspective by exploiting the rich semantic knowledge encoded in pretrained text-to-image diffusion models. To adapt the diffusion models, which are originally developed for image generation, to the tracking task, we reinterpret the models as a bridge between text and image modalities. This connection is realized through the cross-attention mechanism: when both text and an image are input into the models, they highlight the regions of the image that are semantically aligned with the text in the cross-attention maps. We therefore learn a prompt that represents the tracking target and activates its corresponding region in the cross-attention map for each frame, which enables object tracking with the diffusion model. Specifically, our method Diff-Tracking is composed of two main components: an initial prompt learner and an online prompt updater. The initial prompt learner generates a prompt that captures the target object in the first frame, allowing the diffusion model to identify the target. The online prompt updater refines the prompt based on motion information, enabling consistent tracking across video frames. We evaluate our approach on six challenging tracking datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach.

AI.tldr: Unsupervised visual tracking using diffusion-model cross-attention with learnable prompts to localize targets across frames.
AI.motivation: Leverage pretrained text-to-image diffusion models' rich semantic and structural understanding to improve tracking performance, especially for fine-grained semantic cues.
AI.method: Introduce Diff-Tracking with an initial prompt learner to capture the target in the first frame and an online prompt updater to refine the prompt using motion information; use cross-attention maps from the diffusion model to localize the target.
AI.result: Evaluated on six challenging tracking datasets; results indicate effectiveness of the approach in unsupervised tracking.
AI.conclusion: Shows that diffusion models can be repurposed for unsupervised tracking by bridging text and image modalities via cross-attention and prompt learning, achieving robust tracking across frames.

title: Time Series Causal Discovery via Context-Conditioned and Causality-Augmented Pretraining
comment: Submitted to the 40th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2026). 27 pages
summary: Causal discovery from time series is critical for many real-world applications, such as tracing the root causes of anomalies. Existing approaches typically rely on dataset-specific optimization, making it difficult to transfer their causal discovery capabilities to new time series governed by diverse causal mechanisms. In this paper, we propose \textbf{PTCD}, a novel \textbf{P}retraining framework for \textbf{T}ime-series \textbf{C}ausal \textbf{D}iscovery, which improves cross-task generalization through context-conditioned modeling and transferable causal augmentation. To model complex temporal causal dependencies, PTCD employs a dual-scale iterative attention mechanism to capture window-level causal relationships, and a Gaussian mixture with a context-level routing mechanism to handle heterogeneous exogenous distributions. To further address distribution shifts across causal graphs, PTCD adopts a pretraining paradigm on synthetic datasets that integrates intervention-based learning and a causal mixup strategy, promoting stable causal discovery and stronger generalization. Extensive experiments on multiple real-world out-of-distribution (OOD) datasets demonstrate that PTCD excels in both causal discovery and root cause identification.

AI.tldr: PTCD is a pretraining framework for time-series causal discovery that improves cross-task generalization via context-conditioned modeling and transferable causal augmentation. It uses a dual-scale iterative attention mechanism to capture window-level causal dependencies, and a Gaussian mixture with a context-level routing mechanism to handle heterogeneous exogenous distributions. Pretraining on synthetic data with intervention-based learning and a causal mixup strategy promotes stable causal discovery and better generalization. Experiments on multiple real-world OOD datasets show strong performance in causal discovery and root-cause identification.
AI.motivation: The need for generalizable causal discovery across time-series with diverse causal mechanisms. Existing methods often rely on dataset-specific optimization, hindering transfer to new domains and causing degradation under distribution shifts.
AI.method: PTCD introduces (1) dual-scale iterative attention to model window-level temporal causal relations, (2) a Gaussian mixture with context-level routing to handle heterogeneous exogenous distributions, (3) a pretraining paradigm on synthetic data incorporating intervention-based learning and a causal mixup strategy, and (4) context-conditioned modeling and transferable causal augmentation to enhance cross-task generalization.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on multiple real-world out-of-distribution datasets demonstrates that PTCD excels in causal discovery and root-cause identification, indicating improved generalization and robustness over baselines.
AI.conclusion: PTCD offers a generalizable, pretraining-based solution for time-series causal discovery that addresses distribution shifts and heterogeneous causal mechanisms, achieving strong performance in OOD causal discovery and root-cause analysis.

title: DinoComplete: 3D Shape Completion with Distilled Semantic Priors and State Space Models
summary: 3D shape completion from partial scans remains challenging for unseen categories and noisy real-world observations, where geometry alone is often insufficient for inferring missing structure. We present DinoComplete, a deterministic and efficient shape completion framework that augments geometric reconstruction with voxel-aligned semantic priors distilled from DINO features. First, we construct multi-view DINO feature volumes aligned with ShapeNet data and train a student network to predict dense semantic features directly from incomplete shapes. These predicted features capture global structure and part-aware semantic context while remaining aligned with the underlying geometry. We then integrate these distilled features into a completion network, where geometric and semantic voxel representations are fused through voxel state-space modeling. To enable efficient long-range reasoning without sacrificing resolution, we introduce a multi-scale voxel Mamba module that refines the fused features by combining full-grid and chunk-wise sequence modeling. Experiments on unseen ShapeNet categories and ScanNet objects show that DinoComplete achieves stronger completion quality than prior deterministic and generative based completion methods while using fewer parameters, requiring lower memory, and achieving faster inference. Our results demonstrate that distilling semantic priors from visual foundation models improves generalization and robustness in 3D shape completion.

AI.tldr: DinoComplete is a deterministic, efficient 3D shape completion method that fuses voxel-based geometry with distilled semantic priors from DINO features, using a multi-scale Mamba module for scalable long-range reasoning, achieving strong generalization with fewer parameters and faster inference.
AI.motivation: Geometry-only completion struggles with unseen categories and noisy real-world scans; semantic priors provide global structure and part-aware context to guide completion and improve robustness.
AI.method: Construct multi-view DINO feature volumes aligned with ShapeNet; train a student network to predict dense semantic features from incomplete shapes; fuse geometric voxel representations with semantic features via voxel state-space modeling; introduce a multi-scale voxel Mamba module to enable long-range reasoning by combining full-grid and chunk-wise sequence modeling.
AI.result: Outperforms prior deterministic and generative completion methods in unseen ShapeNet categories and ScanNet objects, while using fewer parameters, consuming less memory, and offering faster inference.
AI.conclusion: Distilling semantic priors from visual foundation models enhances generalization and robustness in 3D shape completion.

title: Adversarial Training for Robust Coverage Network under Worst-case Facility Losses
summary: The Maximal Covering Location-Interdiction Problem (MCLIP) is a classic bi-level optimization problem, which is fundamental to resilient infrastructure planning yet remains computationally intractable. Specifically, the upper level determines facility locations to maximize coverage, while the lower level executes worst-case interdiction to minimize the coverage. The strong coupling between the upper and lower levels, combined with their respective high combinatorial complexity, renders traditional methods ineffective. To bridge this gap, we propose a Dual-Agent Deep Reinforcement Learning (DADRL) framework based on adversarial learning, comprising a location agent corresponding to the upper level and an interdiction agent corresponding to the lower level. Our contributions are threefold: (1) The location agent is trained simultaneously against an evolving interdiction agent, making it effectively capture the dynamic competitive interplay between the upper and lower levels; (2) To fully exploit the learned capabilities of the interdiction agent, we propose a Surrogate-based Ensemble Inference Strategy that utilizes the trained interdiction agent as a high-fidelity surrogate to guide the decisions of location agent; (3) Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that our approach achieves superior computational efficiency while maintaining highly competitive solution quality compared to other baselines. Furthermore, our DADRL framework is model-agnostic to network structures, while its underlying adversarial learning paradigm demonstrates strong potential for solving other bi-level optimization problems.

AI.tldr: A Dual-Agent Deep Reinforcement Learning (DADRL) framework is proposed to solve the Maximal Covering Location-Interdiction Problem (MCLIP) by training a location agent (upper level) against an evolving interdiction agent (lower level) in an adversarial setting; it uses a surrogate-based ensemble inference to leverage the interdiction agent, achieving improved computational efficiency with competitive solution quality, and is model-agnostic, suggesting applicability to other bi-level optimization problems.
AI.motivation: MCLIP combines facility location for coverage with worst-case interdiction, creating a strongly coupled, highly combinatorial bi-level problem that is hard to solve with traditional methods. There is a need for scalable, adaptive approaches that can capture the dynamic adversarial interaction and deliver high-quality solutions efficiently, suitable for resilient infrastructure planning.
AI.method: A Dual-Agent Deep Reinforcement Learning (DADRL) framework with two agents: (1) a location agent representing the upper level and (2) an interdiction agent representing the lower level. The location agent is trained against an evolving interdiction agent to capture competitive dynamics. A Surrogate-based Ensemble Inference Strategy uses the trained interdiction agent as a high-fidelity surrogate to guide the location agent’s decisions. The framework is model-agnostic to network structures and leverages adversarial learning similar to other two-player or GAN-like setups.
AI.result: Experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets show that DADRL achieves superior computational efficiency while maintaining highly competitive solution quality compared to baseline methods. The approach demonstrates robustness across different network structures and problems, indicating strong potential for solving other bi-level optimization problems.
AI.conclusion: DADRL provides a scalable, model-agnostic framework for bi-level optimization like MCLIP through adversarial learning and surrogate-assisted guidance. It highlights the viability of dual-agent DRL for capturing upper-lower level interactions and suggests broad applicability to similar bi-level decision problems, with future work likely focusing on refinement of the surrogate ensemble technique and broader empirical validation.

title: CodecCap: High-Fidelity Codec-Inspired Residual Modeling for Dense Video Captioning
summary: Existing video captioning methods struggle to balance visual fidelity and redundancy: holistic captions are compact but lose fine-grained evidence, whereas segment-wise captions improve coverage but introduce heavy redundancy. We propose CodecCap, a codec-inspired framework for high-fidelity dense video captioning. Analogous to video codecs, CodecCap represents videos using keyframe and residual captions. Keyframe captions exhaustively encode stable visual context, while residual captions capture temporally only localized actions, motions and changes. This effectively preserves fine-grained visual evidence while reducing redundant descriptions. To quantify the fidelity of captions, we introduce VidCapQA, a caption-then-QA benchmark with 1,000 questions across 14 capability dimensions. Results on VidCapQA show that captions directly generated by strong VLMs still miss many visual details, highlighting caption representation as a critical bottleneck. Experiments show that CodecCap significantly surpasses direct captioning with the same underlying VLMs, suggesting keyframe-residual captioning a way for high-fidelity video-language supervision. We further use CodecCap to construct CodecVDC-100K, a large-scale dense captioning dataset with anchor, residual, scene-level, and video-level supervision.

AI.tldr: CodecCap introduces a codec-inspired dense video captioning framework that uses keyframe and residual captions to balance fidelity and redundancy. It introduces VidCapQA, a caption-then-QA benchmark, and CodecVDC-100K, a large-scale dataset with multi-level supervision.
AI.motivation: To address the trade-off between visual fidelity and redundancy in dense video captioning: holistic captions are compact but lose details; segment-wise captions gain coverage but are highly redundant.
AI.method: Represent videos with keyframe captions for stable visual context and residual captions for localized actions/changes. Evaluate caption fidelity with VidCapQA (1,000 QA questions across 14 dimensions). Demonstrate improvement over direct captioning using the same VLMs. Build CodecVDC-100K dataset with anchor, residual, scene-level, and video-level supervision.
AI.result: CodecCap outperforms direct-captioning baselines with the same underlying VLMs, indicating higher fidelity. VidCapQA reveals that strong VLMs miss many visual details in captions, underscoring the need for better caption representations. CodecVDC-100K provides a scalable dense captioning dataset with multi-level supervision.
AI.conclusion: Keyframe-residual captioning is an effective strategy for high-fidelity video-language supervision and dense captioning, with CodecCap as a practical framework and CodecVDC-100K as a valuable resource for the community.

title: Towards Generalization-Oriented Models for Vehicle Routing Problems with Mixture-of-Experts
summary: In recent years, Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) has achieved substantial progress on Vehicle Routing Problems (VRPs). However, existing DRL-based methods are typically trained on instances generated from a uniform distribution, which limits their performance under real-world distribution shifts. In this paper, we aim to develop a generalization-oriented model that partitions the policy network into multiple modules and adaptively recombines modules to form specific policies during inference. Specifically, we propose Residual Refined Experts with Instance-level Gating (R2E-IG) to improve cross-distribution generalization. Our contributions are threefold: (1) We introduce a Residual Refined Expert (R2E) architecture that enhance expert expressiveness via residual refinement; (2) We design an instance-level gating mechanism that learns distribution-aware instance representations and routes inputs to suitable modules; (3) We propose a mixed-distribution training mechanism equipped with Dynamic Weight Adaption (DWA), which dynamically reweights training data from different distributions to emphasize more informative ones. Extensive experiments show that R2E-IG achieves competitive performance against state-of-the-art baselines on both in-distribution and out-of-distribution instances across synthetic and benchmark datasets. Moreover, R2E-IG is generic and can be easily integrated into existing DRL-based methods to further improve performance.

AI.tldr: R2E-IG introduces a modular DRL framework for VRPs that uses Residual Refined Experts and instance-level gating to improve cross-distribution generalization, plus Dynamic Weight Adaption for mixed-distribution training; it achieves competitive results on in- and out-of-distribution data and is integration-friendly.
AI.motivation: DRL methods for VRPs trained on uniform synthetic distributions struggle under real-world distribution shifts; there is a need for models with better cross-distribution generalization.
AI.method: Propose Residual Refined Experts (R2E) to enhance expert expressiveness via residual refinement; instance-level gating to route inputs to appropriate modules based on distribution-aware representations; mixed-distribution training with Dynamic Weight Adaption to reweight data from different distributions emphasizing informative samples.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows R2E-IG achieves competitive performance against state-of-the-art baselines on both in-distribution and out-of-distribution instances across synthetic and benchmark datasets; demonstrates generality and ease of integration into existing DRL methods.
AI.conclusion: R2E-IG advances cross-distribution generalization in DRL for VRPs by combining modular policy design, data-aware routing, and adaptive training emphasis; the approach is generic and readily adaptable to existing DRL frameworks.

title: On the Robustness of Machine Unlearning for Vision-Language Models
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) may memorize undesirable information from training data, motivating growing interest in machine unlearning. In this work, we present the first systematic survey and robustness analysis of VLM unlearning. We provide a comprehensive taxonomy and review of existing VLM unlearning methods, together with unified evaluations under multiple prompt settings. We then propose three attack paradigms to examine whether forgotten multimodal knowledge can be reactivated through contextual prompting or downstream retraining. Extensive experiments show that many existing methods remain vulnerable under these attacks, indicating that current approaches often hide rather than fully remove target knowledge. Our study provides new insights into the robustness and limitations of current VLM unlearning methods and highlights the need for more reliable multimodal unlearning strategies. Code is available at https://github.com/XMUDeepLIT/VLM-UnL-Attack.

AI.tldr: First systematic survey and robustness analysis of vision-language model unlearning; finds many methods hide rather than truly erase forgotten multimodal knowledge; introduces three attack paradigms and unified evaluations; code available.
AI.motivation: VLMs can memorize undesirable information from training data; ensuring reliable unlearning is critical for safety and privacy; current unlearning methods lack robustness.
AI.method: Comprehensive taxonomy of VLM unlearning methods; unified evaluation across prompt settings; design of three attack paradigms to test reactivation through contextual prompting and downstream retraining; extensive experiments.
AI.result: Most existing unlearning methods remain vulnerable to attacks; forgotten knowledge can be reactivated; unlearning tends to hide rather than remove knowledge; reveals robustness limitations.
AI.conclusion: Highlights need for more reliable multimodal unlearning; provides insights and directions for developing stronger unlearning strategies; code released for reproducibility.

title: Ratio-Variance Regularized Policy Optimization
summary: Standard on-policy reinforcement learning relies on heuristic clipping to enforce trust regions, but this mechanism imposes a severe cost by indiscriminately truncating high-return yet high-divergence updates. We demonstrate that explicitly constraining the policy ratio variance provides a principled local approximation to trust-region constraints, eliminating the need for binary hard clipping. By acting as a distributional ``soft brake'', this approach preserves critical gradient signals from novel discoveries while naturally down-weighting and enabling the reuse of stale, off-policy data. We introduce ${\bf R}^2{\bf VPO}$ (Ratio-Variance Regularized Policy Optimization), which implements this constraint via a primal-dual optimization framework. Extensive evaluations across $7$ LLM scales, spanning both fast and slow reasoning paradigms, and $10$ robotic control tasks demonstrate the generality of the proposed approach. R$^2$VPO achieves substantial performance gains on mathematical reasoning benchmarks, with particularly pronounced improvements on smaller models, while significantly improving sample efficiency. Furthermore, it consistently outperforms PPO baselines in continuous control domains, particularly in sparse-reward and dynamic environments. Together, these findings establish ratio-variance regularization as a principled foundation for stable and data-efficient policy optimization.

AI.tldr: Ratio-Variance Regularized Policy Optimization (R^2VPO) replaces heuristic clipping with a ratio-variance constraint that approximates trust-region behavior, implemented via primal-dual optimization.
AI.motivation: On-policy clipping often truncates informative high-return, high-divergence updates; a differentiable, local surrogate is needed to preserve gradient signals while enabling safe reuse of off-policy data.
AI.method: Formulate and enforce a variance constraint on the policy ratio and solve the resulting optimization via a primal-dual framework to derive the R^2VPO algorithm.
AI.result: Empirical results show strong performance gains over PPO in continuous control (notably sparse/dynamic environments) and improved math-reasoning performance for smaller LLMs; gains also observed across diverse tasks, indicating good data efficiency and broad applicability.
AI.conclusion: Ratio-variance regularization provides a principled, general framework for stable and data-efficient policy optimization across domains.

title: ChartAct: A Benchmark for Dynamic Chart Understanding
summary: Charts are widely used to present complex data for analysis and decision making. Existing chart understanding benchmarks mainly focus on static charts, but real-world charts are often dynamic and interactive. Key information may only appear after actions such as hovering, clicking, zooming, or dragging. Dynamic chart understanding therefore requires models to identify visible content, choose proper interactions, and reason over changing chart states. To evaluate this ability, we propose ChartAct, an interactive benchmark for dynamic chart understanding. ChartAct collects and filters 673 dynamic charts from 8 real chart websites, covers 7 common chart types, and constructs 1,440 high-quality question-answer samples. Each sample is instantiated in two environments, Dynamic Chart and Dashboard Chart, to evaluate dynamic chart understanding under different contexts. Based on ChartAct, we systematically evaluate 11 advanced multimodal models and GUI agents. Experimental results show that existing models still have clear limitations in dynamic chart understanding. The strongest model, Claude-Opus-4.7, achieves an average success rate of 84.5\%, while most models remain below 60\%. We also conduct detailed failure attribution and case analysis. ChartAct provides a new benchmark for studying chart understanding in real interactive environments. Codes at https://github.com/wulin-wulin/OSWorld_Chart

AI.tldr: ChartAct introduces an interactive benchmark for dynamic chart understanding, with 673 dynamic charts across 7 chart types and 1,440 QA samples. It evaluates 11 multimodal models and GUI agents in two environments (Dynamic Chart and Dashboard Chart), revealing substantial gaps in current capabilities (best ~84.5% average success; many models <60%). The benchmark and code are released to spur progress in interactive chart understanding.
AI.motivation: Dynamic charts require visibility through actions (hover, click, zoom, drag) and reasoning over changing states. Existing benchmarks focus on static charts and fail to test interaction-driven understanding.
AI.method: Data collection and filtering of 673 dynamic charts from 8 real chart websites, covering 7 chart types. Construction of 1,440 QA samples instantiated in two environments (Dynamic Chart, Dashboard Chart). Systematic evaluation of 11 advanced multimodal models and GUI agents, followed by failure attribution and case analysis.
AI.result: Across 11 models, the strongest (Claude-Opus-4.7) achieves 84.5% average success; most models remain below 60%. The study includes detailed failure analysis and case studies.
AI.conclusion: ChartAct provides a novel benchmark for studying chart understanding in real interactive environments and will guide future model and system development; code is available at the linked GitHub repository.

title: Pretrained Approximators for Low-Thrust Trajectory Cost and Reachability
comment: Submitted to the Journal of Guidance, Navigation and Control
summary: Low-thrust trajectory design relies heavily on repeated evaluations of fuel consumption and transfer feasibility, which require expensive optimal control solutions. In this work, we show these quantities can be accurately approximated by machine learning surrogates, enabling fast and scalable evaluation across a wide range of scenarios. By increasing both dataset size and model capacity, we observe that low-thrust trajectory optimization follows a scaling law, with performance improving linearly with the logarithm of training data and network parameters, and no evidence of saturation within the explored regime. Guided by this observation, we construct a large-scale dataset using the proposed homotopy-ray strategy tailored to mission design requirements. A key is the introduction of a self-similar transformation, which allows generalization across semi-major axes, inclinations, and central bodies avoiding retraining. As a result, the same neural approximator can be applied to diverse orbital environments and mission classes. The proposed models accurately predict optimal fuel consumption and minimum transfer time for single- and multi-revolution transfers. Their performance and generalization are demonstrated on a public dataset, a multi-asteroid flyby problem from the Global Trajectory Optimization Competition, and an asteroid rendezvous mission design. The models and datasets are released as open-source to support the space community.

AI.tldr: Machine learning surrogates accurately approximate fuel consumption and transfer feasibility for low-thrust trajectory design, enabling scalable, fast evaluations. A scaling law is observed: performance improves linearly with the log of training data size and network capacity, with no saturation in the studied regime. A homotopy-ray strategy and a self-similar transformation enable broad generalization across orbital environments without retraining. Large open-source datasets support single- and multi-revolution transfers, validated on public and GTOC datasets; release to the community.
AI.motivation: To reduce computation in low-thrust trajectory optimization by replacing expensive optimal-control solves with fast, accurate ML surrogates, enabling rapid evaluation across diverse mission scenarios.
AI.method: Train neural surrogates to predict optimal fuel consumption and minimum transfer time for various transfers. Investigate scaling with dataset size and network capacity, revealing a log-linear improvement. Develop a large-scale dataset using a homotopy-ray strategy; introduce a self-similar transformation to generalize across semi-major axes, inclinations, and central bodies without retraining. Validate on public datasets, a GTOC multi-asteroid flyby problem, and an asteroid rendezvous mission; release models and datasets as open-source.
AI.result: The models accurately predict optimal fuel and transfer time for both single- and multi-revolution transfers, exhibiting strong generalization across mission classes and orbital environments. Demonstrations on diverse benchmarks and open-source release demonstrate practicality and scalability of the approach.
AI.conclusion: ML surrogates coupled with homotopy-ray data generation and a self-similar transformation enable scalable, generalizable low-thrust trajectory design. The observed scaling law suggests continued performance gains with more data and larger networks, and the open-source releases aim to accelerate adoption in the space community.

title: Timestep-Aware SVDQuant-GPTQ for W4A4 Quantization of Wan2.2-I2V
summary: W4A4 quantization of large video diffusion Transformers offers substantial memory savings but is hindered by two main challenges: sparse large-magnitude activation outliers, and strongly timestep-dependent activation distributions across the multi-step denoising trajectory. These difficulties are compounded by Wan2.2-I2V's two-expert Mixture-of-Experts DiT design, whose high-noise and low-noise experts exhibit distinct quantization sensitivities that a single global calibration policy cannot capture. We propose a post-training quantization framework combining SVDQuant-based low-rank outlier compensation, GPTQ-based reconstruction-aware residual weight quantization, and timestep-bin-wise per-layer activation clipping-ratio search conducted independently for each expert. On the OpenS2V-Eval benchmark, our method reduces peak GPU memory by 59.3\% relative to the BF16 baseline while incurring only a 0.9\% drop in VBench average score and a 2.3\% drop in Imaging Quality, demonstrating that expert- and timestep-aware calibration is essential for high-fidelity W4A4 inference on MoE video DiTs.

AI.tldr: Post-training quantization for W4A4 MoE video diffusion transformers using expert- and timestep-aware calibration; yields 59.3% peak memory reduction with modest accuracy losses.
AI.motivation: Quantizing large video diffusion transformers (W4A4) saves memory, but faces two challenges: sparse large-magnitude activation outliers and timestep-dependent activation distributions across multi-step denoising. Wan2.2-I2V's two-expert Mixture-of-Experts DiT has high-noise vs low-noise experts with distinct quantization sensitivities that a single global calibration cannot capture.
AI.method: A PTQ framework combining SVDQuant-based low-rank outlier compensation, GPTQ-based reconstruction-aware residual weight quantization, and timestep-bin-wise per-layer activation clipping-ratio search conducted independently for each expert.
AI.result: On OpenS2V-Eval, peak GPU memory is reduced by 59.3% relative to BF16 baseline, with only a 0.9% drop in VBench average score and a 2.3% drop in Imaging Quality.
AI.conclusion: Expert- and timestep-aware calibration are essential for high-fidelity W4A4 inference on MoE video diffusion transformers; the proposed PTQ framework enables such calibration and achieves substantial memory savings with minimal performance loss.

title: Latent Recurrent Transformer: Architecture Exploration, Training Strategies, and Scaling Behavior
summary: We study Latent Recurrent Transformer (LRT), a lightweight augmentation of autoregressive transformers that reuses a high-level source-layer hidden state from the previous token as recurrent memory for the next token. Because this source state is already computed during ordinary decoding, LRT adds a cross-layer recurrent latent pathway across positions without inserting pause tokens or extra depth loops, and the standard attention mechanism and KV-cache interface are preserved. To pretrain this recurrence at scale without sequentially unrolling the transformer, we introduce interleaved parallel training: a single full-sequence initialization forward pass builds a shared buffer; then disjoint position subsets are refined in parallel and written back, so that all tokens receive recurrent-memory-aware supervision at roughly 2 times baseline compute. Across nanochat style backbones and a wide range of tokens-per-parameter budgets, LRT improves both language-modeling loss and in-context learning under matched effective compute while adding as little as 0.3% parameters.

AI.tldr: Latent Recurrent Transformer (LRT) augments autoregressive transformers with a lightweight cross-layer recurrent memory that reuses a previous token's high-level source state, enabling recurrence without extra decoding steps or insertions. Pretraining uses interleaved parallel training to simulate recurrence at scale with ~2x compute but no sequential unrolling, preserving standard attention and KV-cache. Empirically, LRT improves language modeling loss and in-context learning across backbones and budgets with only ~0.3% extra parameters.
AI.motivation: Reduce sequential unrolling and enhance long-range dependency modeling in autoregressive transformers while preserving existing attention mechanisms and KV-cache, and achieve recurrence with minimal parameter overhead.
AI.method: Introduce a cross-layer recurrent latent pathway that reuses the previous token's high-level source-layer hidden state as memory for the next token. Preserve standard attention and KV-cache. Train recurrence via interleaved parallel training: build a shared buffer from a full-sequence forward pass, then refine disjoint position subsets in parallel and write back, achieving recurrence supervision at roughly 2x baseline compute.
AI.result: LRT yields improvements in language-modeling loss and in-context learning under matched compute, with minimal parameter overhead (~0.3%). Gains are observed across nanochat-style backbones and various tokens-per-parameter budgets.
AI.conclusion: Latent Recurrent Transformer provides an efficient recurrence mechanism that enhances performance with negligible parameter increase and compatibility with existing transformer frameworks, enabling better LM and in-context capabilities without disrupting the standard KV-cache and attention infrastructure.

title: Black-box Membership Inference Attacks on the Pre-training Data of Image-generation Models
summary: The rapid advancement of diffusion-based image generation models has raised serious concerns regarding potential copyright and privacy infringements involving human-created data. Membership inference attacks (MIAs) have emerged as a promising tool for identifying unauthorized data usage during model training. Existing methods typically assess the ability of model to denoise perturbed suspect images as an indicator of membership status. However, the discriminative power of such features is highly dependent on the degree of model memorization and deteriorates significantly when applied to less exposed data (e.g., pre-training data). Although several methods attempt to enhance detection by leveraging internal model features, these features are generally inaccessible in mainstream closed-source image generation platforms, limiting their practicality. In this paper, we demonstrate that analyzing how a black-box diffusion model denoises a target image and corresponding perturbed textual instructions can reveal more distinctive membership cues. Based on this insight, we propose a black-box membership inference attack framework (named SD-MIA) that leverages a cross-modal data perturbation mechanism to detect pre-training data in diffusion models. We conduct extensive experiments on both a public benchmark dataset and a newly constructed dataset, each comprising pre-training membership and non-membership samples with identical distributions. Experimental results demonstrate that SD-MIA achieves superior performance compared to existing baselines, including those with the unfair advantage of accessing internal model features.

AI.tldr: Introduces SD-MIA, a black-box membership inference attack against diffusion models that uses cross-modal perturbations (image + text) to detect pre-training data, surpassing baselines including features-based methods.
AI.motivation: Address privacy and copyright concerns in diffusion models by enabling effective membership inference in black-box settings without relying on inaccessible internal features.
AI.method: A cross-modal data perturbation framework (SD-MIA) analyzes how a black-box diffusion model denoises a target image and its perturbed textual instructions to extract membership cues for pre-training data; evaluated on public and newly constructed datasets with identical distributions for membership/non-membership samples.
AI.result: SD-MIA achieves superior performance compared with existing baselines, including methods that leverage internal model features, across the tested datasets, indicating robust discriminative power even in less-memorized data scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Cross-modal perturbations enable effective black-box membership inference on diffusion models, offering a practical tool for detecting pre-training data usage and aiding privacy/copyright enforcement.

title: PATE-TabTransGAN: Differentially Private Synthetic Tabular Data Generation via Transformer-Based Student Discrimination
comment: 16 pages, 3 figures, 4 tables. Submitted for publication
summary: Generating high-fidelity synthetic tabular data under formal differential privacy guarantees remains an open challenge. Methods that provide strong theoretical protection typically sacrifice the modeling of inter-feature dependencies required for realistic synthesis, while architectures that excel at capturing complex column relationships offer only empirical privacy guarantees. We present PATE-TabTransGAN, a generative framework that integrates the Private Aggregation of Teacher Ensembles (PATE) mechanism with a Transformer-based student discriminator to jointly address both requirements, and employs a GNMax RDP accountant for numerically stable privacy accounting. An ensemble of Logistic Regression teachers trained on disjoint partitions supervise the student via noisy-aggregated labels, and a residual generator is optimized against this differentially private student, inheriting formal (ε, δ)-DP guarantees by post-processing. PATE-TabTransGAN was compared with PATE-GAN, DP-GAN, and DP-CTGAN, considered state-of-the-art in differentially private tabular synthesis. Experiments conducted on four tabular benchmarks (Adult, Breast, Cardio, Cervical) confirmed the high quality of the proposed method: PATE-TabTransGAN attains the best or tied-best AUROC on all four datasets. On AUCPR it matches the strongest baseline on Cardio, leads on Cervical, and trails on Breast; on Adult, we demonstrate that AUCPR is highly sensitive to positive-class convention, and that the observed gap is consistent with a convention difference between evaluation pipelines rather than a synthesis deficit.

AI.tldr: PATE-TabTransGAN combines Private Aggregation of Teacher Ensembles with a Transformer-based student discriminator for differentially private tabular data synthesis, using GNMax RDP accounting. It uses an ensemble of Logistic Regression teachers on disjoint partitions to supervise a residual generator under DP, achieving state-of-the-art or competitive AUROC/AUCPR across four benchmarks, with some sensitivity in AUCPR to evaluation conventions.
AI.motivation: To achieve high-fidelity synthetic tabular data under formal DP guarantees without sacrificing modeling of inter-feature dependencies; existing DP methods trade off privacy guarantees or dependency modeling, so a method that both preserves feature relationships and offers formal DP is sought.
AI.method: PATE-TabTransGAN integrates PATE with a Transformer-based student discriminator. An ensemble of LR teachers trained on disjoint partitions supervise a DP student via noisy aggregate labels (PATE). A residual generator is trained against this DP student; GNMax RDP accountant is used for numerically stable DP accounting; post-processing yields (ε,δ)-DP guarantees.
AI.result: Compared with PATE-GAN, DP-GAN, and DP-CTGAN on four tabular datasets (Adult, Breast, Cardio, Cervical). PATE-TabTransGAN achieves best or tied-best AUROC on all datasets. For AUCPR, it matches the top baseline on Cardio, leads on Cervical, and trails on Breast; on Adult, AUCPR is sensitive to class convention, suggesting evaluation pipeline conventions influence observed gaps rather than synthesis performance.
AI.conclusion: PATE-TabTransGAN demonstrates strong DP-compliant tabular data synthesis that preserves inter-feature dependencies and achieves state-of-the-art or competitive accuracy across multiple benchmarks, with some dataset- and convention-dependent nuances in AUCPR.

title: NeR-SC: Adapting Neural Video Representation to Screen Content
summary: Implicit neural representations have emerged as a promising paradigm for video compression, with recent methods achieving competitive performance on natural video. However, screen content video -- common in remote desktop, online education, and cloud gaming -- exhibits distinct statistics: sharp edges, limited color palettes, and strong temporal redundancy. Existing neural representation methods, designed for natural scenes, lack mechanisms to exploit these properties, leaving substantial room for improvement. In this paper, we propose NeR-SC, a neural representation framework tailored for screen content video. Building on the SNeRV backbone, NeR-SC introduces three screen-content-specific modules: (i) a learnable color palette that models the discrete color structure of screen content by restricting the low-frequency sub-band to a learned color set; (ii) a multi-gate dense fusion module that replaces sequential feature fusion with dense, attention-gated cross-stage interaction; and (iii) an embedding-level frame skip strategy that bypasses redundant decoder invocations for static frames, with zero training overhead. Experiments on DSCVC and VCD show that NeR-SC achieves 40.32~dB and 41.73~dB average PSNR, outperforming representative neural video representation methods and, at low bitrates, surpassing H.264 and H.265. The skip strategy enables real-time decoding with no loss in quality.

AI.tldr: NeR-SC tailors implicit neural representations for screen content video by adding a learnable color palette, dense gated cross-stage fusion, and an embedding-level frame skip, achieving higher PSNR and real-time decoding at low bitrates on DSCVC and VCD benchmarks.
