AI.motivation: Screen content videos have sharp edges, limited color palettes, and strong temporal redundancy, which differ from natural scenes. Existing neural video representations struggle to exploit these properties, limiting performance.
AI.method: Extend the SNeRV backbone with three screen-content-specific modules: (i) a learnable color palette that models discrete color structure by constraining the low-frequency sub-band to a learned color set; (ii) a multi-gate dense fusion module enabling dense, attention-gated cross-stage interactions instead of sequential feature fusion; (iii) an embedding-level frame skip strategy that bypasses redundant decoder invocations for static frames with zero training overhead.
AI.result: On DSCVC and VCD benchmarks, NeR-SC achieves average PSNRs of 40.32 dB and 41.73 dB, respectively, outperforming representative neural video representation methods; at low bitrates, it surpasses H.264 and H.265. The embedding-level skip enables real-time decoding with no quality loss.
AI.conclusion: NeR-SC effectively leverages screen-content statistics through a learnable color palette, dense gated fusion, and frame-skipping to deliver higher fidelity at competitive bitrates while enabling real-time decoding.

title: Innovation: An Almost Characterization of Hallucination
summary: Hallucination is a central limitation of large language models (LLMs), and substantial effort has been devoted to understanding and mitigating it. Towards this, Kalai and Vempala (STOC 2024) introduced a probabilistic framework formalizing calibration and hallucination, and showed that, with high probability, calibrated LLMs hallucinate roughly at the rate of the "missing mass", a measure of how incomplete the training data is relative to its source. This raises two fundamental questions: (i) what property of a calibrated LLM makes hallucinations unavoidable? and (ii) can hallucinations be avoided by giving up calibration? We answer these questions by introducing a simpler property we call innovation that measures the tendency of a model to produce outputs outside the training data. We show that innovation is implied by the condition for hallucination identified by Kalai and Vempala, and, further, that it is an almost characterization of hallucination: hallucination implies innovation, and conversely, innovation implies hallucination with high probability. We also provide lower bounds on the hallucination rate based on the "innovation rate", and by relating innovation rate back to missing mass, we obtain new hallucination rate lower bounds based on missing mass that extend the results of Kalai and Vempala.

AI.tldr: Defines an 'innovation' property for LLM outputs as outputs outside training data, proving it closely tracks the calibration-based hallucination condition of Kalai and Vempala. It yields almost-equivalence: hallucination implies innovation and, with high probability, innovation implies hallucination. Uses this to derive lower bounds on hallucination rates via the innovation rate and missing-mass, extending Kalai–Vempala results.
AI.motivation: Hallucination in LLMs is a central limitation. The paper seeks to identify a property of calibrated models that makes hallucination unavoidable and to address whether abandoning calibration could avoid hallucination. It introduces 'innovation' as a simpler, intuitive driver of hallucination.
AI.method: Formalize 'innovation' as the propensity to output content outside the training data. Prove that the Kalai–Vempala calibration condition implies innovation; establish almost-iff relation: hallucination ⇔ innovation with high probability; derive lower bounds on hallucination rate using innovation rate; relate innovation rate to missing-mass to obtain new lower bounds extending Kalai–Vempala.
AI.result: Innovation is shown to be implied by the Kalai–Vempala condition and, conversely, to almost characterize hallucination (hallucination ⇒ innovation and innovation ⇒ hallucination with high probability). The authors derive lower bounds on hallucination rates in terms of innovation rate and tie these to missing mass, thereby extending the Kalai–Vempala results.
AI.conclusion: The paper offers a near-complete, intuitive characterization of hallucination via the 'innovation' property, linking model output novelty to training-data incompleteness. This clarifies when hallucination is unavoidable under calibration and provides quantitative lower bounds that connect to missing data analyses.

title: SCKAN: Structural Consensus-based KAN Prototype Learning for Semi-Supervised Pancreas Segmentation
comment: 10.5 pages, 5 figures, Medical Image Computing and Computer Assisted Intervention 2026
summary: Accurate pancreas segmentation is critical for early cancer diagnosis, where annotation scarcity necessitates Semi-Supervised Learning (SSL). However, due to significant inter-sample morphological variability, existing SSL methods face severe generalizability limitations under sparse supervision, leading to the Supervision Bias problem. To address this, we propose Structural Consensus-based KAN Prototype Learning (SCKAN), which constructs the first cross-sample structural consensus learning with Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs), to achieve more generalizable and accurate segmentation. Specifically, SCKAN contains two key designs: Structure-constrained Prototype Consistency Learning (SPCL), which prompts unbiased structural representation by enforcing cross-sample consistency via prototype-level contrastive optimization, and Consensus-based Kolmogorov-Arnold Fusion (CKaF), which reduces morphology-specific bias by aggregating stable consensus and filtering sample-wise noise via KAN's adaptive B-spline nonlinearity. Extensive experiments on two public pancreas datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of SCKAN. Code is at https://github.com/rhodaliu17/SCKAN.

AI.tldr: SCKAN is a semi-supervised pancreas segmentation method that uses cross-sample structural consensus via Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks to combat supervision bias under sparse labels, comprising SPCL (prototype-consistency with cross-sample structure) and CKaF (consensus-based fusion with adaptive nonlinearity). It yields improved generalization on two public datasets; code is released.
AI.motivation: Annotation scarcity and high inter-sample morphological variability in pancreas data cause poor generalization of SSL methods. Supervision bias arises when models overfit to the labeled samples’ structures, hindering performance on unseen variability.
AI.method: SCKAN introduces two components: (1) Structure-constrained Prototype Consistency Learning (SPCL) that enforces cross-sample structural consistency through prototype-level contrastive learning, promoting unbiased structural representations across samples. (2) Consensus-based Kolmogorov-Arnold Fusion (CKaF) that reduces morphology-specific bias by aggregating stable consensus across samples and filtering noise via Kolmogorov-Arnold Network’s adaptive B-spline nonlinearity.
AI.result: Experiments on two public pancreas datasets demonstrate that SCKAN improves segmentation accuracy and robustness under sparse supervision, outperforming baselines and validating the effectiveness of SPCL and CKaF. Code is provided at the project link.
AI.conclusion: SCKAN provides a more generalizable, accurate segmentation under limited annotations by mitigating supervision bias through cross-sample structural consensus and adaptive fusion. The approach shows promise for other organ segmentation tasks and underlines the value of structural-consensus prototypes in SSL.

title: The Kalman Evolve: Closing the Gap in Kalman Filtering via Interpretable Algorithm Discovery
summary: State estimation is a fundamental problem in control and signal processing, for which the Kalman Filter provides an optimal solution under linear dynamics, Gaussian noise, and known noise covariances. However, these assumptions often fail in realistic sensing settings such as Doppler radar and LiDAR. In these cases, the optimal estimator is inherently nonlinear, which leads to systematic performance degradation. This creates a performance gap that cannot be eliminated by tuning the noise covariance parameters (i.e., the process and measurement noise in the Kalman Filter) alone. To address this limitation, we propose Kalman Evolve, a framework for discovering improved filtering algorithms by jointly optimizing both noise parameters and the update structure. Our approach leverages large language models (LLMs) as a structured prior over program space, enabling the generation of interpretable, non-affine modifications to the classical Kalman filter while preserving its recursive form. We provide analytical results establishing the suboptimality of affine estimators under common nonlinear sensing models, motivating the need for structure-aware updates. Across a range of synthetic and real-world tracking benchmarks, including Doppler radar, LiDAR-based localization, and pedestrian tracking, the discovered algorithms consistently improve over strong baselines such as the Optimized Kalman Filter, achieving up to 12\% reduction in RMSE. These results suggest that optimizing the structure of the Kalman filter, rather than only its parameters, provides a practical and interpretable way to improve state estimation.

AI.tldr: Kalman Evolve uses an LLM-guided search to discover non-affine, structure-aware Kalman updates, jointly optimizing noise parameters and update rules, yielding up to 12% RMSE improvement over strong baselines in nonlinear sensing tasks.
AI.motivation: The classical Kalman filter is optimal only for linear Gaussian dynamics with known noise covariances. Real sensing (e.g., Doppler radar, LiDAR) is nonlinear, making affine estimators suboptimal and tuning noise covariances insufficient. A structure-aware update that preserves recursion while deviating from affine form can close the performance gap.
AI.method: Leverage large language models as a structured prior over program space to generate interpretable, non-affine modifications to the Kalman update while maintaining recursive form. Jointly optimize noise parameters and the update structure. Provide analytical results showing suboptimality of affine estimators under common nonlinear sensing models. Evaluate on synthetic and real benchmarks (Doppler radar, LiDAR localization, pedestrian tracking).
AI.result: The discovered algorithms consistently outperform baselines such as the Optimized Kalman Filter, achieving up to 12% RMSE reduction across tasks. The updates are interpretable and leverage structure-aware modifications rather than purely parametric tuning.
AI.conclusion: Optimizing the structure of the Kalman filter, not just its parameters, is practical and yields interpretable improvements. Kalman Evolve offers a framework to learn improved filtering algorithms via LLM-guided search, with potential applicability to other recursive estimators.

title: BEAT: Rhythm-Elastic Alignment for Agentic Music-guided Movie Trailer Generation
summary: Automatic movie trailer generation must select shots from a full-length film and synchronize them with background music. Existing methods either relegate music alignment to post-processing or enforce rigid one-to-one shot-music mappings, overlooking that professional editing rhythm is elastic: rapid cuts accompany high-energy passages while sustained shots span quieter bars. We introduce BEAT, a framework that addresses this gap with two core components: MuVA, a compact music-visual alignment encoder trained with Sinkhorn-regularized two-stage learning, and Bar-DP, an energy-adaptive dynamic programming algorithm that produces elastic many-to-one alignments following musical dynamics. These components are integrated into a five-phase agentic pipeline that grounds the core alignment in learned cross-modal features while coordinating higher-level creative decisions through structured text signals. To support comprehensive evaluation, we also introduce TrailerArena, a benchmark with 20+ metrics across four complementary dimensions. On TrailerArena, BEAT achieves state-of-the-art performance across shot selection, ordering, and perceptual quality, while producing fully composed trailers end-to-end.

AI.tldr: BEAT enables elastic, many-to-one music-to-video alignment for automatic trailer generation, combining MuVA (music-visual alignment encoder with Sinkhorn-regularized two-stage learning) and Bar-DP (energy-adaptive dynamic programming) within a five-phase agentic pipeline, and benchmarks on TrailerArena showing state-of-the-art performance across shot selection, ordering, and perceptual quality for end-to-end trailer generation.
AI.motivation: Existing trailer generation methods either separate music alignment into post-processing or enforce rigid one-to-one shot-music mappings, failing to capture the elastic rhythm of professional editing where fast cuts match high-energy portions and sustained shots span quieter sections.
AI.method: Introduce MuVA, a compact music-visual alignment encoder trained via Sinkhorn-regularized two-stage learning; Bar-DP, an energy-adaptive dynamic programming algorithm producing elastic many-to-one alignments guided by musical dynamics; integrate these into a five-phase agentic pipeline that grounds alignment in learned cross-modal features and coordinates high-level decisions through structured text signals.
AI.result: On TrailerArena, BEAT achieves state-of-the-art performance for shot selection, ordering, and perceptual quality, and can generate fully composed trailers end-to-end.
AI.conclusion: BEAT effectively models elastic music-video alignment for trailer generation, validated on TrailerArena with comprehensive, multi-faceted metrics, enabling end-to-end production of high-quality trailers; the approach highlights the value of elastic, many-to-one alignment combined with structured decision processes.

title: IPIBench: Evaluating Interactive Proactive Intelligence of MLLMs under Continuous Streams
summary: Recent multimodal large language models (MLLMs) achieve strong performance on reactive question answering, but real-world streaming assistants require proactive reasoning over continuous visual inputs. Existing benchmarks mainly study reactive or proactive interactions in isolated single-turn settings, overlooking dynamic multi-turn scenarios where users may add, modify, or cancel proactive requests alongside interleaved reactive queries. To address this gap, we introduce IPIBench, the first benchmark for evaluating Interactive Proactive Intelligence of MLLMs under streaming video settings. IPIBench covers proactive monitoring, proactive task management, and interleaved reactive-proactive requests. Evaluations on representative MLLMs reveal two major limitations: unstable proactive triggering and weak coordination between reactive and proactive behaviors. We further propose IPI-Agent, a training-free agentic framework with an interaction-control policy and a temporal-gating mechanism for stabilizing proactive triggering and coordinating multi-turn interactions. Experiments show that IPI-Agent consistently improves existing MLLMs across all benchmark settings.

AI.tldr: IPIBench introduces the first benchmark for Interactive Proactive Intelligence (IPI) in streaming video MLLMs, and IPI-Agent is a training-free framework to stabilize proactive triggering and coordinate multi-turn interactive behavior, showing improvements over representative MLLMs.
AI.motivation: Current MLLMs excel at reactive question answering or isolated proactive tasks but lack evaluation and tooling for dynamic, multi-turn, streaming scenarios where users add/modify/cancel proactive requests alongside reactive queries.
AI.method: IPIBench covers proactive monitoring, proactive task management, and interleaved reactive-proactive requests in streaming video. IPI-Agent employs an interaction-control policy and a temporal-gating mechanism to stabilize proactive triggering and coordinate multi-turn interactions, all without additional training.
AI.result: Evaluations identify unstable proactive triggering and weak reactive-proactive coordination as key issues in existing MLLMs. IPI-Agent consistently improves performance of existing MLLMs across all benchmark settings.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark and the training-free agentic framework jointly advance evaluation and operational capability for interactive proactive intelligence in streaming video, offering a practical path to more reliable proactive behavior in MLLMs.

title: MONA: Muon Optimizer with Nesterov Acceleration for Scalable Language Model Training
summary: The Muon optimizer has recently offered a promising alternative to AdamW for large language model training, leveraging matrix orthogonalization to produce geometry-aware updates. However, like all first-order methods, Muon can become trapped in sharp local minima. In this work, we present MONA, an optimizer that bridges Muon's orthogonalization framework with curvature-aware acceleration. MONA adds an acceleration term directly into Muon's gradient processing pipeline. This term is calculated from the exponential moving average of gradient differences. We provide a detailed convergence analysis for MONA, showing that the acceleration term enables escape from sharp minima while preserving Muon's spectral-norm regularization. Empirically, MONA achieves better convergence and downstream task performance compared to both Muon and AdamW across three scales of Mixture-of-Experts pretraining, spanning from 1B to 68B parameters, with the largest model trained on 1 trillion tokens. Furthermore, we conduct supervised fine-tuning on the MOE-68B-A3B model and evaluate it on general capability, mathematical reasoning, and code generation benchmarks, where MONA achieves SOTA performance.

AI.tldr: MONA adds a curvature-aware acceleration to Muon by incorporating an exponential moving average of gradient differences, improving convergence and achieving strong downstream performance on MOE-scale models and fine-tuning benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To overcome Muon's tendency to stall in sharp local minima and to leverage curvature information, while maintaining Muon's spectral-norm regularization.
AI.method: Integrates an acceleration term into Muon's gradient processing pipeline; computes the term from the exponential moving average of gradient differences; provides a convergence analysis; evaluated on Mixture-of-Experts pretraining from 1B to 68B parameters (up to 1 trillion tokens) and on supervised fine-tuning of MOE-68B-A3B.
AI.result: MONA yields better convergence and downstream performance than Muon and AdamW across three MOE scales; the largest model is trained on 1 trillion tokens; achieves state-of-the-art results on general capability, mathematical reasoning, and code generation benchmarks for MOE-68B-A3B.
AI.conclusion: MONA successfully integrates curvature-aware acceleration into Muon's orthogonalization framework, enabling escape from sharp minima while preserving spectral-norm regularization, leading to superior large-scale MOE pretraining and downstream fine-tuning performance.

title: SoftCap: Soft-Budget Control for Diffusion Transformer Acceleration
summary: Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) achieve strong visual quality, but their iterative denoising process requires many costly Transformer evaluations. Training-free acceleration methods reduce this cost by caching, forecasting, or verifying intermediate features, yet the runtime decision of when to execute a Full step is often driven by fixed schedules or hand-tuned thresholds. We propose \textbf{SoftCap}, a training-free control layer for cache-based DiT inference. SoftCap couples a Trajectory Drift Observer, which estimates local cache risk from lightweight hidden-state statistics, with a Soft-Budget PI Controller, which adjusts the Full-triggering threshold from realized compute relative to a fixed reference profile. The budget is a soft ceiling: it shapes the threshold but does not require a run to spend a prescribed number of Full evaluations. On FLUX.1-dev, SoftCap improves over SpeCa at a comparable middle-compute operating point, raising ImageReward from 0.967 to 0.981 and reducing LPIPS-Full from 0.518 to 0.498 at nearly identical FLOPs, while target-sweep diagnostics show the intended soft-ceiling behavior as the budget is relaxed.

AI.tldr: SoftCap is a training-free control layer for cache-based diffusion transformers that adaptively governs Full-step triggering via a Trajectory Drift Observer and a Soft-Budget PI Controller, enabling a soft compute budget and improving image quality at similar FLOPs.
AI.motivation: To address rigid runtime decisions for Full-step execution in diffusion-transformer inference and to enable training-free, adaptive acceleration beyond fixed schedules or hand-tuned thresholds.
AI.method: Introduce SoftCap, composed of a Trajectory Drift Observer that estimates local cache risk from lightweight hidden-state statistics and a Soft-Budget PI Controller that adjusts the Full-trigger threshold based on realized compute relative to a fixed reference profile. The budget is a soft ceiling that shapes the threshold without requiring a fixed number of Full evaluations.
AI.result: On FLUX.1-dev, SoftCap outperforms SpeCa at a comparable middle-compute point: ImageReward improves from 0.967 to 0.981 and LPIPS-Full decreases from 0.518 to 0.498, with nearly identical FLOPs; target-sweep diagnostics demonstrate the intended soft-ceiling behavior as the budget is relaxed.
AI.conclusion: SoftCap offers a training-free, adaptive mechanism for cache-based Diffusion Transformer inference, improving the compute–quality trade-off without enforcing a strict runtime quota; the soft-budget framing yields flexible resource management and stable performance gains.

title: Not All Disagreement Is Learnable: Token Teachability in On-Policy Distillation
summary: On-policy distillation (OPD) trains a student on its own rollouts with token-level teacher supervision. Recent selective OPD methods exploit the non-uniformity of OPD signals by prioritizing high-entropy or high-disagreement tokens. We revisit this principle and ask: which token-level teacher signals are actually learnable? Using a fixed-context diagnostic that measures same-context teacher-student KL reduction, we show that raw KL disagreement is a coarse proxy for learning value. It conflates learnable disagreement, where the teacher assigns corrective mass to the student's top-K candidates, with incompatible disagreement, where the teacher places mass mostly off the student's current support. We formalize this local compatibility as token teachability and show that it better predicts fixed-context improvement than raw KL alone. Motivated by this finding, we propose Teachability-Aware OPD (TA-OPD), a lightweight token-position selection method that applies OPD loss to high-teachability positions without reward models or verifiers. Across Qwen2.5 and Qwen 3 teacher-student settings, TA-OPD often surpasses full-token OPD with only 5% retained tokens and improves over entropy- and divergence-based baselines. Our results reframe selective OPD as selecting learnable teacher signals rather than merely salient tokens.

AI.tldr: Teachability-Aware On-Policy Distillation (TA-OPD) identifies learnable token-level teacher signals by a fixed-context KL reduction measure, defining token teachability as local compatibility of teacher corrections with the student’s current support. TA-OPD selectively applies OPD loss to high-teachability tokens, without reward models. It outperforms full-token OPD and entropy/divergence baselines using only ~5% of tokens in Qwen2.5/Qwen3 setups.
AI.motivation: On-policy distillation signals are non-uniform; not all high-entropy or high-disagreement tokens are learnable. Distinguishing learnable from incompatible teacher signals is crucial for effective selective OPD.
AI.method: Introduce a fixed-context diagnostic that measures same-context teacher-student KL reduction. Formalize token teachability as local compatibility between teacher mass allocation and the student’s current support. Propose Teachability-Aware OPD (TA-OPD): select high-teachability token positions and apply OPD loss without relying on reward models or verifiers.
AI.result: Empirically, token teachability better predicts fixed-context improvement than raw KL. TA-OPD often surpasses full-token OPD with only 5% retained tokens and outperforms entropy- and divergence-based baselines in Qwen2.5 and Qwen3 teacher-student settings.
AI.conclusion: Selective OPD should target learnable teacher signals; teachability provides a practical, lightweight criterion, enabling effective token-level supervision with substantial token efficiency.

title: Semi-Supervised Gaze Estimation via Disentangled Subspace Contrastive Learning
summary: Appearance-based gaze estimation always suffers from poor generalization due to limited annotated samples and insufficient dataset diversity. Leading approaches adopt weakly supervised learning to generate large-scale pseudo-labeled data from unconstrained real-world scenarios, aiming to mitigate the domain shifts. In this work, we devise a simple yet effective semi-supervised learning architecture that leverages unlabeled data to enhance domain generalization, thereby reducing reliance on labor-intensive manual annotations. Our key insight is to impose Jacobian regularization to disentangle feature representations into discriminative subspaces dedicated to specific gaze components, such as pitch and yaw angles. We further exploit the intrinsic ordinal ranking within each subspace for contrastive learning, enabling the model to learn robust gaze representations from a small set of labeled samples and an abundance of unlabeled ones. This ultimately yields our Disentangled Subspace Contrastive Learning (DSCL) framework. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks verify that the proposed DSCL is plug-and-play, achieving competitive performance using only 20\%, 10\%, and even 5\% of the annotated data under both in-domain and cross-domain evaluation settings. The public code is available at \href{https://github.com/da60266/DSCL}{https://github.com/da60266/DSCL}.

AI.tldr: DSCL: A semi-supervised framework for appearance-based gaze estimation that disentangles pitch and yaw into subspaces via Jacobian regularization and uses ordinal-contrastive learning, achieving competitive domain generalization with only 5–20% of labeled data.
AI.motivation: Appearance-based gaze estimation suffers from poor generalization due to limited annotated data and dataset diversity, prompting semi-supervised and disentangled representations to mitigate domain shifts.
AI.method: Introduce Jacobian regularization to disentangle features into discriminative subspaces for gaze components (e.g., pitch and yaw). Within each subspace, leverage intrinsic ordinal ranking for contrastive learning. Train in a semi-supervised setting using a small labeled set and abundant unlabeled data; DSCL is plug-and-play.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks show DSCL is plug-and-play and achieves competitive performance using 20%, 10%, and even 5% of annotated data under both in-domain and cross-domain evaluations; code available publicly.
AI.conclusion: Disentangled subspace representations combined with ordinal-contrastive learning improve domain generalization and label efficiency for gaze estimation, offering a practical, plug-and-play solution.

title: Learning Energy-Based Models from Stochastic Interpolants using Spatiotemporal Differences
summary: Learning an energy-based model from data samples is a central problem in machine learning. Many recent and popular methods, such as denoising score matching for training energy-based diffusion models, use stochastic interpolants to corrupt data samples at different noise levels indexed by a time variable. This defines a joint density over both the data space and time, and most methods learn its energy through either spatial or temporal differences. We identify distinct failure modes for both of these approaches. To solve them, we propose Spatiotemporal Noise-Contrastive Estimation (stNCE), a framework for learning the energy through joint spatiotemporal differences. stNCE unifies many existing methods and leads to new training objectives. Experiments on images and molecules demonstrate performance competitive with state-of-the-art density estimation methods.

AI.tldr: A unified spatiotemporal energy-based learning framework (stNCE) that addresses failure modes in using only spatial or temporal differences by learning energy via joint spatiotemporal differences, unifying existing methods and enabling competitive density estimation on images and molecules.
AI.motivation: Existing energy-based models trained with diffusion-like noise suffer from distinct failure modes when the energy is estimated using only spatial or only temporal differences. A joint spatiotemporal approach aims to fix these issues and improve density estimation.
AI.method: Introduce Spatiotemporal Noise-Contrastive Estimation (stNCE), a framework that learns energy through joint spatiotemporal differences in the data-time joint distribution. It unifies existing methods and yields new training objectives for EBMs.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations on image and molecular datasets show performance competitive with state-of-the-art density estimation methods, validating the effectiveness of the joint spatiotemporal approach.
AI.conclusion: stNCE provides a general, unifying energy-based learning framework that overcomes the shortcomings of purely spatial or purely temporal contrasts, offering competitive performance and new training objectives across domains.

title: Pop-Up Distractions Reveal Bag-of-Events Behavior in Video Large Language Models
summary: A key capability for video understanding is reliably linking subjects to events across time, yet whether Video Large Language Models (VideoLLMs) actually achieve this remains unclear. In this work, we introduce DistractionBench to evaluate whether VideoLLMs can robustly link subjects and events in the presence of unrelated video segments. Through controlled interventions, such as inserting short advertisement clips into longer videos, we show that VideoLLMs frequently hallucinate interactions between entities from different segments, incorrectly attributing actions from injected advertisements to subjects in the main video. We characterize this systematic hallucination as bag-of-events (BoE) behavior, where models process videos as collections of events rather than temporally structured sequences. Evaluating 11 popular VideoLLMs, we find that all models exhibit substantial BoE behavior. Our findings suggest that VideoLLMs lack reliable mechanisms for temporal grounding and motivate the development of models with more robust subject-event association.

AI.tldr: VideoLLMs fail to robustly link subjects and events under distraction, showing bag-of-events behavior with cross-segment hallucinations.
AI.motivation: Assess whether VideoLLMs can reliably associate subjects with events across time, especially when unrelated segments are inserted.
AI.method: Introduce DistractionBench with controlled interventions (e.g., inserting advertisements into longer videos); evaluate 11 VideoLLMs; analyze outputs for cross-segment attributions and identify bag-of-events behavior.
AI.result: All 11 models exhibit bag-of-events behavior, hallucinating interactions from injected ads and misattributing actions to main video subjects; weak temporal grounding is evident across models.
AI.conclusion: Temporal grounding and robust subject-event association are lacking in current VideoLLMs; DistractionBench reveals a need for architectures and training that enforce coherent event sequences and cross-segment consistency.

title: RAPNet: Accelerating Algebraic Multigrid with Learned Sparse Corrections
comment: Proceedings of the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning, Seoul, South Korea Code available at https://github.com/idoby/rapnet
summary: The scalable solution of large sparse linear systems is a bottleneck in scientific computing and graph analysis. While algebraic multigrid (AMG) offers optimal linear scaling, its performance is severely constrained by the trade-off between the sparsity and convergence quality of coarse-grid operators. Classical AMG heuristics struggle to balance these objectives, often sacrificing stability or performance for sparsity. We propose RAPNet, a graph neural network (GNN) framework that resolves this trade-off by learning to generate sparse, robust coarse operators directly from the sparse algebraic system. Key to our approach is a level-wise training strategy that enables learning from small subgraphs and generalization to million-node domains, bypassing the bottlenecks of prior neural AMG attempts. RAPNet executes exclusively during the solver setup phase, ensuring that the solve phase retains its favorable computational properties. We show that our method outperforms classical non-Galerkin baselines on diverse PDE discretizations and graph Laplacians, making it particularly effective for multi-query tasks such as eigenproblems, time-dependent simulations, and inverse or design problems.

AI.tldr: GNN-based framework RAPNet learns to generate sparse, robust coarse operators for algebraic multigrid, trained level-wise on small subgraphs to generalize to large domains, improving setup efficiency and performance over classical non-Galerkin baselines; applicable to multi-query tasks and does all learning during solver setup.
AI.motivation: Overcome the sparsity-convergence trade-off in AMG coarse operators and the scalability bottleneck of handcrafted heuristics; enable robust, sparse coarse operators learned from data that generalize to large-scale systems.
AI.method: Graph neural network that outputs coarse operators; level-wise training on subgraphs; training only during solver setup; ensures the solve phase remains efficient; applicable to PDE discretizations and graph Laplacians.
AI.result: Outperforms classical non-Galerkin baselines across diverse PDEs and graph Laplacians; improves robustness and scalability; benefits multi-query tasks like eigenproblems, time-dependent simulations, inverse/design problems.
AI.conclusion: RAPNet offers a scalable, learned AMG strategy that balances sparsity and convergence by leveraging subgraph-level learning and level-wise training, enabling robust coarse operators for large-scale problems.

title: JLT: Clean-Latent Prediction in Latent Diffusion Transformers
summary: Flow matching with clean-data prediction has shown that regressing the clean point can exploit low-dimensional structure more effectively than predicting an ambient noised quantity. We ask whether this principle remains useful after images are mapped into a learned latent space, where compression has already removed much of the raw pixel variability. We introduce JLT, a 130M latent diffusion Transformer over frozen FLUX.2 VAE codes, and compare clean-latent prediction with a matched velocity-prediction DiT under the same representation, backbone, and training settings. Although the three variables x, epsilon, and v are linearly convertible for a fixed corruption time, a local Gaussian analysis shows that velocity regression inherits an isotropic target-covariance floor and amplifies low-variance latent directions, while clean prediction damps them. On ImageNet 256 x 256, JLT-B/1 obtains FID-50K 2.50 with classifier-free guidance, with a large matched-target gap over velocity prediction. These results suggest that prediction targets in latent diffusion are representation-dependent geometric choices, rather than interchangeable algebraic parameterizations.

AI.tldr: In latent diffusion, predicting clean latent data outperforms velocity prediction, due to representation-dependent geometry in latent spaces; a 130M latent diffusion Transformer on FLUX.2 VAE codes demonstrates a significant FID gap favoring clean-latent prediction on ImageNet 256x256.
AI.motivation: To determine whether the advantage of clean-data prediction in flow-matching extends to latent spaces where dimensionality is reduced by a learned encoder, and to understand how target representations affect diffusion model performance.
AI.method: Train JLT, a 130M-parameter latent diffusion Transformer operating on frozen FLUX.2 VAE codes. Compare clean-latent prediction against velocity (epsilon) prediction (DiT) under identical representations, backbones, and training settings. Use a local Gaussian analysis to interpret target covariance and test on ImageNet 256x256 with classifier-free guidance (FID-50K metric).
AI.result: Empirical and theoretical analysis shows that velocity regression inherits an isotropic target-covariance floor and amplifies low-variance latent directions, while clean prediction dampens them. JLT-B/1 achieves FID-50K 2.50 with classifier-free guidance, with a substantial gap over velocity prediction under matched conditions.
AI.conclusion: Prediction targets in latent diffusion are geometry- rather than algebraically-driven choices; optimal targets depend on the representation, and clean latent prediction is preferable in the latent space for the studied setup.

title: Generalist Graph Anomaly Detection via Prototype-Based Distillation
summary: Driven by the pressing demand for graph anomaly detection (GAD) in high-stakes domains, the generalist GAD paradigm, which trains a single detector transferable across new graphs, has recently gained growing attention. However, existing methods often rely on scarce and costly annotations for training and sometimes even require few-shot support at inference, which limits their robustness to diverse and unseen anomaly patterns. To address this limitation, we introduce ProMoS, the first unsupervised generalist GAD framework, which detects anomalies by modeling the abundant normality in unlabeled data. ProMoS adopts a knowledge-distillation paradigm to distill normality priors from a frozen self-supervised graph neural network (GNN) teacher to a mixture-of-students model with shared global and lightweight personalized branches, enabling efficient and expressive normality modeling without learning from scratch. We further propose prototype-guided soft-label distillation to align teacher and student in a shared prototype space, enhancing cross-graph generalizability. During inference, ProMoS performs zero-shot anomaly detection on unseen graphs via distillation bias and prototype geometric deviation. Extensive experiments show the effectiveness and efficiency of ProMoS, charting a practical path toward label-free, zero-shot generalist GAD.

AI.tldr: ProMoS is an unsupervised generalist graph anomaly detector that distills normality from a frozen self-supervised GNN teacher into a mixture-of-students, with prototype-guided distillation enabling zero-shot anomaly detection on unseen graphs.
AI.motivation: Address the scarcity of labeled data and the need for robust generalization in graph anomaly detection by learning a transferable, label-free detector that effectively models abundant normality to detect unseen anomalies.
AI.method: Knowledge distillation from a frozen self-supervised GNN teacher to a mixture-of-students with shared global and lightweight personalized branches; prototype-guided soft-label distillation to align teacher and student in a shared prototype space; zero-shot inference on unseen graphs via distillation bias and prototype geometric deviation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness and efficiency of ProMoS, showing its capability for label-free, zero-shot generalist graph anomaly detection on unseen graphs.
AI.conclusion: ProMoS demonstrates that unsupervised, zero-shot generalist GAD is feasible by distilling normality priors and aligning through prototype spaces, offering a practical path toward robust, annotation-free anomaly detection across graphs.

title: COVD: Continual Open-Vocabulary Object Detection with Novel Concept Injection
summary: Open-vocabulary object detection (OVD) has made significant progress, enabling detectors to generalize from seen to unseen categories. However, real-world category spaces continually evolve, and existing OVD models still struggle with newly emerging concepts, while repeated full retraining is prohibitively expensive. To this end, we introduce a new task setting, termed Continual OVD with Novel Concept Injection (COVD), where models sequentially learn incoming novel concept groups while preserving prior concepts and original open-vocabulary knowledge, along with a new benchmark, Novel-114. Our key observation is that pretrained visual encoders often already perceive and represent many novel concepts, and the main bottleneck lies in the lack of stable semantic alignment between visual representations and textual concepts. Based on this, we propose NoIn-Det, an efficient continual injection framework without additional parameters. NoIn-Det freezes the visual encoder, preserves the text representation space using only texts of common concepts and previously injected concepts, and injects novel concepts by updating only a small subset of text-branch parameters beneficial to novel concept learning. Extensive experiments show that NoIn-Det effectively learns novel concepts, preserves old knowledge, and consistently outperforms existing continual learning methods for VLMs without introducing additional parameters.Novel-114 and the code will be released.

AI.tldr: NoIn-Det enables continual injection of novel concepts for open-vocabulary object detection without adding parameters, by freezing the vision encoder, preserving the text space, and updating only a small subset of text-branch parameters; introduces COVD and Novel-114 with strong empirical results.
AI.motivation: Real-world category spaces continually evolve and retraining OVD models is expensive. Although pretrained visual encoders can perceive novel concepts, aligning visual and textual representations is the bottleneck. A parameter-efficient continual learning approach is needed.
AI.method: NoIn-Det freezes the visual encoder; preserves a stable text representation space using texts of common and previously injected concepts; injects novel concepts by updating only a small subset of text-branch parameters, without adding new modules or parameters.
AI.result: Empirical results show effective learning of novel concepts, preservation of old knowledge, and superior performance over existing continual learning methods for vision-language models, without increasing parameter count. Novel-114 benchmark and code are provided.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a scalable, parameter-efficient workflow for continual open-vocabulary detection via selective text-branch adaptation, validating the COVD paradigm and its practical viability.

title: The Strongest Teacher Is Not Always the Best Teacher: Student-Centric Answer Selection
summary: LLM training increasingly relies on teacher-generated supervision, from synthetic responses to reasoning traces and tool-use demonstrations. Current practice often chooses the highest-performing teacher to generate student training data, implicitly treating teacher test performance as a proxy for teaching quality. We show that this assumption can fail: even when multiple teachers provide correct answers to the same question, the answer from the strongest teacher is not necessarily the best supervision for a given student. To address this gap, we propose Student-Centric Answer Sampling (SCAS), a framework that selects from verified teacher-generated answers according to their estimated student-centric learning cost. Motivated by a token-wise gradient decomposition, we derive an efficient forward-only proxy for this cost and use it to guide answer selection during training. Experiments across 30 teacher models, 6 student base models, and 8 tasks show that SCAS consistently improves student performance, suggesting that effective distillation should prioritize supervision matched to the current student rather than teacher strength alone.

AI.tldr: SCAS improves LLM distillation by selecting teacher-provided answers based on student-centric learning cost rather than teacher strength, using a forward-only proxy from token-level gradients to guide supervision, yielding consistent gains across many models and tasks.
AI.motivation: Traditional distillation relies on the strongest teacher as the source of supervision, assuming its performance generalizes to teaching quality; this can be suboptimal when teaching needs vary with the student’s state.
AI.method: Introduce Student-Centric Answer Sampling (SCAS) that chooses among verified teacher answers according to estimated student-centric learning cost. Derive an efficient forward-only proxy via token-wise gradient decomposition to approximate this cost and use it to steer answer selection during training.
AI.result: SCAS consistently improves student performance across 30 teacher models, 6 student base models, and 8 tasks, demonstrating the value of student-aligned supervision over raw teacher strength.
AI.conclusion: Effective distillation should prioritize supervision matched to the current student rather than relying on teacher strength alone; SCAS provides a practical, scalable approach to achieve this.

title: PILOT: A Data-Free Continual Learning Approach for Real-Time Semantic Segmentation via Boundary Guidance
summary: Real-time semantic segmentation models offer an excellent balance between accuracy and inference speed. However, deploying these models in dynamic real world environments often requires the ability to learn novel classes incrementally without retraining on the entire dataset. This capability is known as continual learning. In this regard, the standard fine-tuning methods in deep learning often fail due to catastrophic forgetting, where the model learns new information but forgets previously trained and learned classes. Contributing to this crucial domain, the current paper proposes a novel continual learning framework tailored for PIDNet, which is a widely cited state-of-the-art real-time semantic segmentation model. Our method, PILOT(Parallel Incremental Learning Over Time), introduces a real-time and lightweight strategy by implementing a parallel Derivative-branch (D-branch) designed to capture the high frequency boundary information of novel classes while freezing the trained parameters of the original segmentation network. This novel setup allows the model to adapt to new semantic categories while preserving the knowledge of previously learned classes. By using only data associated with the new class, our model significantly reduces training overhead. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach successfully segments new classes while maintaining high mean Intersection over Union (mIoU) on the original base classes, thereby comfortably outperforming all major continual learning approaches in this domain. Overall, PILOT is shown to effectively mitigate catastrophic forgetting with minimal impact on inference latency, thus maintaining real-time performance.

AI.tldr: PILOT is a real-time continual learning framework for semantic segmentation that adds a parallel Derivative-branch to a frozen base network to learn new classes with low overhead, preserving base-class performance.
AI.motivation: Address the need for incremental learning of new semantic classes in dynamic environments without full retraining, while avoiding catastrophic forgetting and maintaining real-time inference speeds.
AI.method: Introduce a parallel Derivative-branch (D-branch) that captures high-frequency boundary information of novel classes. The original segmentation network is frozen; the D-branch is trained only on data for the new class, enabling quick adaptation with minimal computational overhead.
AI.result: Experiments show successful segmentation of new classes while sustaining high mIoU on base classes and outperforming major continual learning baselines, with negligible impact on inference latency.
AI.conclusion: PILOT effectively mitigates catastrophic forgetting in real-time semantic segmentation by leveraging a lightweight parallel branch, enabling efficient continual learning with preserved base knowledge and speed.

title: Negligible in Size, Significant in Effect: On Scale Vectors in Large Language Models
summary: Normalization layers in modern large language models (LLMs) consist of a deterministic normalization operation and a learnable scale vector. While the normalization operation has been extensively studied, the scale vector remains poorly understood despite its ubiquitous use. In this work, we present a systematic study of scale vectors in LLMs from the perspectives of expressivity, optimization, and architectural structure. First, we show empirically that although scale vectors constitute only a negligible fraction of model parameters, removing them substantially degrades LLM pre-training. Our theory further shows that, in Pre-Norm architectures, scale vectors do not increase expressivity; instead, they improve optimization through a self-amplifying preconditioning effect on subsequent linear mappings. Second, we investigate the role of weight decay for scale vectors. By distinguishing Input-Norm and Output-Norm layers, we theoretically show that weight decay is beneficial for the former but harmful for the latter, due to their distinct roles in optimization and expressivity. Third, motivated by this understanding, we propose three lightweight and complementary improvements to scale vectors: branch-specific heterogeneity, improved placement around linear mappings, and magnitude-direction reparameterization. Both theory and experiments show that each improvement yields consistent gains. Finally, we combine these improvements into a unified scale-vector strategy and evaluate it through extensive LLM pre-training experiments on dense and mixture-of-experts models ranging from 0.12B to 2B parameters, across multiple optimizers and learning rate schedules, under industrial-scale token budgets. The unified strategy consistently achieves lower terminal loss than well-tuned baselines and exhibits more favorable scaling behavior, while adding negligible parameter and computational overhead.

AI.tldr: Scale vectors in normalization layers, though parameter-efficient, are essential for effective pre-training and optimization in LLMs; their role is more about preconditioning than expressivity in Pre-Norm; weight decay impacts Input-Norm and Output-Norm differently; three lightweight improvements plus a unified strategy yield consistent gains with negligible overhead.
AI.motivation: To resolve the underappreciated role of scale vectors in LLM normalization, separating their expressivity impact from optimization effects, and to propose practical improvements for better pre-training performance.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of Pre-Norm architectures; differentiating Input-Norm vs Output-Norm; derivation of weight-decay effects; proposal of three lightweight improvements (branch-specific heterogeneity, placement around linear mappings, magnitude–direction reparameterization); extensive pre-training experiments on dense and MoE LLMs from 0.12B to 2B parameters across multiple optimizers and LR schedules.
AI.result: Scale vectors contribute significantly to pre-training effectiveness despite their small parameter count. In Pre-Norm, they do not increase expressivity but improve optimization via self-amplifying preconditioning. Weight decay benefits Input-Norm but harms Output-Norm. The proposed improvements yield consistent gains, and the unified strategy achieves lower terminal loss with negligible overhead across model types and budgets.
AI.conclusion: A unified, lightweight scale-vector strategy improves LLM pre-training performance and scaling behavior. Scale vectors should be treated as optimization aids with architecture-aware regularization and placement, rather than purely expressive parameters.

title: SPHERE-JEPA: Spherical Prediction with Homogeneous Embeddings
summary: A fundamental open question in self-supervised learning (SSL) is the explicit characterization of the optimal geometry of the learned representations. Recently, LeJEPA identified isotropic Gaussian embeddings as optimal for minimizing downstream prediction risk in Euclidean spaces. However, the corresponding problem for distributions supported on lower-dimensional manifolds, such as the hypersphere, remains unexplored. In this work, we demonstrate that extending this minimax analysis to smooth distributions on Riemannian manifolds fundamentally changes the optimal solution. We show that, under a worst-case formulation, both k-nearest neighbors and kernel ridge regression induce hyperspherical uniformity. More precisely, we show that uniform distributions on manifolds are optimal for k-nearest neighbors, and that the uniform distribution on the sphere is optimal for kernel ridge regression with both the exponential dot-product kernel and the linear kernel. This theoretical insight reveals a fundamental limitation of Gaussian embeddings: their non-uniform density induces anisotropic k-NN neighborhoods, severely biasing the estimator. To correct this, we introduce SPHERE-JEPA, a theoretically grounded SSL framework. We adapt LeJEPA's Cram{é}r-Wold projection mechanism to enforce hyperspherical uniformity rather than a Gaussian prior. Empirically, SPHERE-JEPA yields significant improvements, boosting texture retrieval mAP by over 6%, while consistently matching or outperforming LeJEPA on standard benchmarks-including a +1.8% linear probing gain on ImageNet-1K (ViT-B/14).

AI.tldr: Minimizing downstream risk in SSL on manifolds favors hyperspherical uniform priors; Gaussian embeddings cause anisotropy; SPHERE-JEPA enforces hyperspherical uniformity, yielding practical gains over LeJEPA.
AI.motivation: Understand the optimal geometry of SSL representations beyond Euclidean space and identify the limitations of Gaussian embeddings; extend minimax analyses to smooth distributions on Riemannian manifolds, particularly spheres.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis showing worst-case minimax leads to uniform distributions on manifolds for kNN and kernel ridge regression; demonstrate that uniformity on the sphere is optimal for kernels (exponential dot-product and linear). Propose SPHERE-JEPA by adapting LeJEPA's Cramér-Wold projection to enforce hyperspherical uniformity. Empirical evaluation on texture retrieval and ImageNet-1K with ViT-B/14.
AI.result: Under worst-case formulation, kNN and kernel ridge regression induce hyperspherical uniformity. Uniform distributions on manifolds optimize kNN; uniform on the sphere optimizes kernel ridge for the studied kernels. Gaussian embeddings are limited due to non-uniform density causing anisotropic neighborhoods. SPHERE-JEPA achieves >6% mAP gain in texture retrieval and +1.8% linear probing on ImageNet-1K (ViT-B/14), matching or outperforming LeJEPA.
AI.conclusion: Extends SSL theory to manifold settings, identifying hyperspherical uniformity as the optimal prior under a minimax lens. Demonstrates a fundamental limitation of Gaussian embeddings for manifold SSL and provides a principled framework (SPHERE-JEPA) to obtain uniform hyperspherical representations with practical performance gains.

title: Image Thresholding: Understanding Bias of Evaluation Metrics towards Specific Evaluation Functions
summary: Multilevel image thresholding is widely used for segmentation in applications ranging from medical imaging to remote sensing. Classical objective functions, such as Otsu's between-class variance and Kapur's entropy, are often optimized using metaheuristic algorithms, with performance evaluated via metrics like Structural Similarity Index (SSIM) and Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR). These evaluations implicitly assume that SSIM and PSNR provide unbiased measures of segmentation quality. In this study, we examine this assumption by analyzing the correlation between thresholding objective functions and quality metrics across all possible thresholds for images in the BSDS500 dataset. Results show that Otsu's criterion consistently exhibits high correlation with both SSIM and PSNR, while Kapur's entropy demonstrates weaker and more variable correlation. Otsu outperforms Kapur in correlation with PSNR for all images and with SSIM for over 91%. Our findings reveal an inherent metric-objective-function bias. This work highlights the need for more neutral evaluation frameworks and motivates extending the analysis to additional thresholding criteria and domains. Source code of this paper can be found at https://w3id.org/met-dp/icpr26-95

AI.tldr: Otsu's thresholding objective correlates strongly with SSIM and PSNR across thresholds on BSDS500; Kapur's entropy shows weaker/variable correlation, indicating a bias where common quality metrics align with Otsu more than Kapur. Neutral evaluation frameworks are needed; extend analysis to more criteria and domains.
AI.motivation: Examine whether standard quality metrics (SSIM, PSNR) unbiasedly reflect segmentation quality and whether they align with popular thresholding objectives (Otsu, Kapur) across the full threshold spectrum.
AI.method: For each BSDS500 image, compute namesake thresholding objective values (Otsu's between-class variance and Kapur's entropy) for all possible grayscale thresholds. For each threshold, generate a binary segmentation and evaluate quality against the original image using SSIM and PSNR. Compute correlation (likely Pearson) between objective values and quality metrics across all thresholds, and compare across images.
AI.result: Otsu's criterion shows consistently high correlation with both SSIM and PSNR across the dataset. Kapur's entropy exhibits weaker and more variable correlations. Otsu outperforms Kapur in correlation with PSNR for all images and with SSIM for over 91% of images.
AI.conclusion: There exists an inherent bias between thresholding objective functions and quality metrics, suggesting that popular metrics may favor certain objectives. The study motivates developing neutral evaluation frameworks and extending the analysis to more thresholding criteria and domains; code is available at the provided URL.

title: EEG-FM-Audit: A Systematic Evaluation and Analysis Pipeline for EEG Foundation Models
summary: Large EEG Foundation Models (FMs) have shown great potential for decoding EEG signals across diverse cognitive tasks. However, existing EEG-FM studies exhibit three critical limitations: opaque supervised baseline tuning, unverified contributions of complex learning paradigms, and a lack of transparency in model decision-making. To address these, we propose EEG-FM-Audit, a comprehensive evaluation and analysis pipeline designed to systematize the assessment of EEG-FMs. EEG-FM-Audit consists of three primary components: (1) an ASHA-driven benchmarking protocol that ensures fair comparisons by transparently optimizing supervised baselines; (2) paradigm-level ablation studies to evaluate the effectiveness of learning paradigms in FMs; and (3) a neurophysiological probing (NPP) framework, which explores whether FMs leverage valid temporal, spatial, and spectral EEG properties. We apply EEG-FM-Audit to four state-of-the-art EEG-FMs and five representative supervised models across three public datasets. Our results reveal that properly tuned supervised baselines can match or outperform advanced FMs, despite requiring significantly fewer parameters. Furthermore, we find that the effectiveness of learning paradigms of FMs is highly dependent on dataset scale and architecture. Finally, NPP analysis demonstrates how FMs rely on specific physiological features, establishing a framework for more interpretable neural decoding.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive audit framework for EEG foundation models (EEG-FM-Audit) that reveals well-tuned supervised baselines can rival or surpass state-of-the-art FMs, and introduces paradigm-level ablations and neurophysiological probing to enhance transparency and interpretability.
AI.motivation: To address opacity of supervised baselines, unclear contributions of complex learning paradigms, and limited interpretability in EEG foundation models.
AI.method: Three components: (1) ASHA-driven benchmarking to transparently optimize supervised baselines; (2) paradigm-level ablations to assess the effectiveness of learning paradigms; (3) neurophysiological probing (NPP) to verify use of valid temporal, spatial, and spectral EEG features. Applied to four state-of-the-art EEG-FMs and five supervised models across three public datasets.
AI.result: Properly tuned supervised baselines can match or outperform advanced EEG-FMs with significantly fewer parameters. The effectiveness of learning paradigms is highly dependent on dataset scale and architecture. NPP shows that FMs rely on specific physiological features, enabling more interpretable neural decoding.
AI.conclusion: EEG-FM-Audit provides a systematic, transparent evaluation framework for EEG foundation models, highlighting the importance of careful baseline tuning, context-dependent paradigm effectiveness, and neurophysiological interpretability to guide future development.

title: Leveraging Visual Signals for Robust Token-Level Uncertainty in Vision-Language Generation
summary: Uncertainty quantification (UQ) remains a critical challenge in Large Vision Language Models (LVLMs) for reliable predictions and real-world deployment. However, most existing methods are adapted from the LLM literature and primarily focus on the language modality, leaving the contribution of visual information to LVLM uncertainty largely underexplored. In this paper, we investigate how LVLMs process visual information and whether this process can be used to improve uncertainty estimation. By analyzing hidden representations after the integration of visual features during the generation process, we observe that high-confidence predictions rely more heavily on visual content than uncertain ones. Building on this insight, we propose Visual-Grounded Token UQ (VIG-TUQ), a training-free framework that explicitly incorporates visual grounding into uncertainty estimation by weighting token-level language uncertainty with visual grounding scores. We evaluate VIG-TUQ on multiple datasets and across diverse LVLM architectures, including early-fusion, late-fusion, and native-fusion models. Results indicate that our method often improves upon existing token-level uncertainty approaches. Code and data will be made available upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: A training-free method (VIG-TUQ) leverages visual grounding scores to weight token-level uncertainty in LVLMs, improving uncertainty estimation across early/late/native fusion architectures; the approach rests on the observation that high-confidence outputs rely more on visual content, and expects to enhance reliability in real-world deployment.
AI.motivation: Uncertainty quantification in LVLMs is underdeveloped compared to LLMs due to the additional visual modality; existing UQ methods largely adapt LLM strategies and ignore the role of vision in uncertainty, hindering reliable LVLM deployment.
AI.method: Analyze hidden representations post-visual feature integration to show visual reliance for confident predictions, then introduce VIG-TUQ—training-free approach that multiplies/token-level language uncertainty by visual grounding scores to produce visually grounded uncertainty estimates across LVLM architectures (early, late, native fusion).
AI.result: Evaluations on multiple datasets and across diverse LVLM architectures show VIG-TUQ often improves upon existing token-level uncertainty baselines, indicating that incorporating visual grounding can enhance uncertainty estimation.
AI.conclusion: Visual grounding plays a meaningful role in LVLM uncertainty estimation. VIG-TUQ provides a simple, training-free mechanism to incorporate visual information into token-level UQ, with broad applicability across fusion strategies; code and data will be released after acceptance.

title: Agile Online Model Selection: Resolving Adaptation Lag via Safeguarded Large Learning Rates
summary: Maintaining predictive accuracy in non-stationary environments requires online model selection to adapt autonomously to unknown distribution shifts. However, existing tuning-free algorithms face a fundamental trade-off between robustness and agility. Specifically, to ensure dynamic regret bounds, they must restrict learning rates to small constants (e.g., $O(1)$). This restriction inevitably causes significant adaptation lag during abrupt changes. To resolve this, we propose a novel optimistic online mirror descent that utilizes safeguarded large learning rates up to $Θ(T)$, where $T$ is the number of rounds. Our key technical contribution is a post-hoc penalty mechanism that dynamically monitors unstable updates and excludes learning rates incurring excessive regret, eliminating the need for restrictive a priori constraints. We show that the cumulative penalty remains $O(\log T)$, allowing our algorithm to match near-optimal worst-case guarantees while achieving superior rates in benign cases. Empirical evaluations on synthetic and eleven diverse real-world datasets demonstrate that our approach reduces the adaptation lag from hundreds of rounds to a few rounds, consistently outperforming tuning-free baselines.

AI.tldr: Optimistic online mirror descent with a post-hoc penalty enables large learning rates up to Θ(T) while preserving regret guarantees, dramatically reducing adaptation lag.
AI.motivation: To maintain predictive accuracy under unknown and abrupt distribution shifts in online settings, tuning-free methods must balance robustness and agility; conventional small learning rates create long adaptation lags.
AI.method: Introduce an optimistic online mirror descent framework with safeguarded large learning rates and a post-hoc penalty that triggers when an update would incur excessive regret, excluding such learning rates from use. The cumulative penalty is proven to be O(log T) without requiring predefined restrictive learning rates.
AI.result: The method achieves near-optimal worst-case guarantees while delivering faster, benign-case improvements. Empirical results on synthetic data and eleven real-world datasets show adaptation lag shrinking from hundreds of rounds to a few rounds, outperforming tuning-free baselines.
AI.conclusion: Large, adaptive learning rates guided by a post-hoc penalty enable fast adaptation without sacrificing theoretical guarantees, offering a practical tuning-free solution for non-stationary online optimization.

title: Is an Image Also Worth 16x16=256 Superpixels? A Framework for Attentional Image Classification
summary: Superpixel-based image classification has traditionally leveraged graph neural networks (GNNs) for processing irregular image representations. Recent advances in computer vision, driven by Vision Transformers (ViTs), have introduced new paradigms in self-attentional models, surpassing convolutional neural networks (CNNs) in various tasks. However, a synergistic connection between GNNs, superpixels, and transformers remains unexplored. In this work, we propose Superpixel Transformers (SPT), a novel framework that unifies superpixel-based image classification and ViTs. SPT generalizes the Superpixel Image Classification with Graph Attention Networks (SICGAT) model and ViT to support arbitrary superpixel-based chunking strategies, connectivity graphs, and positional encodings. We introduce refinements including a multidimensional sine-cosine positional encoding and an enriched patch data structure that fully incorporates superpixel shape and color information. By testing SPT across datasets such as CIFAR10, FashionMNIST, and Imagenette, with various superpixel generation and graph connectivity strategies, we demonstrate that SPT achieves superior performance compared to previous superpixel-based GNN methods and remains competitive with ViTs. Notably, our approach addresses the limitations of SICGAT, such as information loss during pixel aggregation, and shows how constrained graph connectivity can enhance ViT performance. SPT bridges the gap between superpixel-based and transformer models, opening avenues for cross-domain generalization and future innovations in hybrid attentional frameworks, and showing that an image can also be worth $16\times16$ superpixels.

AI.tldr: A novel Superpixel Transformers (SPT) unifies superpixel-based GNNs with Vision Transformers, enabling arbitrary superpixel chunking, graphs, and encodings; introduces multidimensional positional encoding and enhanced patch data; achieves strong results and competitive ViT performance while addressing SICGAT limitations.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between graph-based, superpixel representations and self-attention transformers. SICGAT loses information during pixel aggregation and ViTs struggle with irregular superpixel graphs; a unified framework should preserve superpixel geometry and leverage attention.
AI.method: Generalizes SICGAT and ViT into SPT; introduces multidimensional sine-cosine positional encoding; enriched patch data structure capturing superpixel shape and color; supports arbitrary chunking strategies, connectivity graphs, and positional encodings; evaluates across CIFAR10, FashionMNIST, Imagenette with various superpixel methods and graph connectivities.
AI.result: SPT achieves superior performance over prior superpixel-based GNNs and remains competitive with ViTs; addresses pixel aggregation information loss seen in SICGAT; shows constrained graph connectivity can enhance ViT performance.
AI.conclusion: SPT bridges the gap between superpixel-based methods and transformers, enabling cross-domain generalization and future hybrid attentional models; demonstrates that an image can be effectively represented with 16×16 superpixels.

title: When Muon Optimizer Meets Adversarial Training: A Theoretical and Empirical Study
summary: Adversarial training (AT) remains one of the most reliable empirical defenses against adversarial attacks. Its robustness critically depends on how the underlying min-max objective is optimized. In practice, Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) optimizer remains the default optimization choice for AT, whereas adaptive optimizers often improve standard training but may yield inferior robustness. Recently, the Muon optimizer, which orthogonalizes matrix-valued updates via an approximate polar decomposition, has achieved notable success in large-scale training at a memory cost comparable to SGD. This raises a security-relevant question: \textit{can orthogonalized optimization improve AT under strong and heterogeneous threat models?} Focusing on this problem, we conduct a comprehensive theoretical and empirical study. Theoretically, we show that Muon imposes a spectral-norm stability ceiling on matrix updates, limiting uncontrolled spectral growth in the training dynamics without explicitly shrinking the learned weights. Empirically, across five architectures and three $\ell_p$ threat models ($\ell_\infty$, $\ell_1$, $\ell_2$) and their union, Muon is competitive with SGD on CNNs and substantially outperforms AdamW on both CNNs and ViTs. These results identify optimizer geometry as a security-relevant factor in adversarial training, while clarifying the empirical regimes in which orthogonalized updates are beneficial. Overall, our findings highlight optimizer design as a security-critical component of AT.

AI.tldr: Orthogonalized Muon optimizer adds matrix-valued, orthogonalized updates to adversarial training, imposing a spectral-norm stability bound; empirically competitive with SGD on CNNs and outperforming AdamW on CNNs and ViTs across multiple threat models, highlighting optimizer geometry as a security-relevant factor.
AI.motivation: Investigate whether optimizer geometry, specifically orthogonalized updates, can improve adversarial training robustness under strong and heterogeneous threat models, and establish its security relevance.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis showing that Muon enforces a spectral-norm stability ceiling on matrix updates; followed by empirical evaluation across five architectures and three l_p threat models (l_infty, l_1, l_2) and their union, comparing Muon to SGD and AdamW in adversarial training.
AI.result: Muon is competitive with SGD on CNNs and substantially outperforms AdamW on both CNNs and ViTs, with memory cost comparable to SGD; results support that optimizer geometry influences AT security.
AI.conclusion: Optimizer geometry, specifically orthogonalized updates as in Muon, is a security-relevant component of adversarial training, with regimes where such updates offer robust benefits; guides when to adopt orthogonalized optimizers in AT.

title: Chaos-SSL: An Attention-Based Self-Supervised Learning Framework with Chaotic Transformation for Medical Image Classification
summary: Self-Supervised Learning (SSL) has emerged as a powerful paradigm to mitigate the reliance on large, annotated datasets, a common bottleneck in medical image analysis. However, standard SSL methods, which rely on simple geometric and color augmentations, may fail to capture the fine-grained, complex textural details necessary for classifying subtle pathologies. This paper introduces Chaos-SSL, a novel two-stage framework for medical image classification. In the first stage, we propose a new self-supervised pre-training strategy that leverages 1D chaotic maps (Logistic, Tent, and Sine) as a complex, non-linear augmentation for contrastive learning. We hypothesize that these chaotic transformations create ``harder'' and more semantically-rich views, forcing a network to learn robust representations of fine-grained medical textures. In the second stage, we introduce an attention-based fusion model that dynamically combines the specialized features from our Chaos-SSL model with the general-purpose features of a larger, ImageNet-pre-trained model. We validate our method on two public datasets: ISIC 2018 (skin lesions) and APTOS 2019 (diabetic retinopathy). Our results demonstrate that the Chaos-SSL model pre-trained with a Tent map for 30 epochs, followed by attention fusion, achieves performance fully competitive with the state-of-the-art, yielding an accuracy of 0.9261 on ISIC 2018 and 0.8726 on APTOS 2019. This significantly outperforms existing SSL methods, including several recent approaches.

AI.tldr: Chaos-SSL: self-supervised pretraining with 1D chaotic maps as complex augmentations, followed by attention-based fusion with ImageNet features for medical image classification; achieves competitive accuracy on ISIC 2018 and APTOS 2019.
AI.motivation: Standard SSL uses simple augmentations that may not capture fine-grained textures crucial for subtle pathologies in medical images. The paper proposes chaotic transformations to create harder, semantically richer views, potentially leading to better texture representations. A second-stage attention fusion leverages complementary strengths of specialized SSL features and general ImageNet features.
AI.method: Two-stage approach: (1) self-supervised pretraining using 1D chaotic maps (Logistic, Tent, Sine) as non-linear augmentations for contrastive learning; (2) an attention-based fusion model that dynamically combines Chaos-SSL features with ImageNet-pretrained features. Evaluation on ISIC 2018 and APTOS 2019, with Tent map at 30 epochs used for reported results.
AI.result: Chaos-SSL with Tent map pretraining at 30 epochs followed by attention fusion achieves 0.9261 accuracy on ISIC 2018 and 0.8726 on APTOS 2019, beating several SSL baselines and remaining fully competitive with state-of-the-art methods on these datasets.
AI.conclusion: The proposed chaotic augmentation and feature fusion strategy yields strong performance for fine-grained medical image classification, indicating that chaotic, non-linear augmentations can enhance SSL representations, and that combining specialized SSL features with generic ImageNet features via attention is an effective integration scheme. Further evaluation across more datasets and ablations would clarify generalization and contribution of each component.

title: RLVR Datasets and Where to Find Them: Tracing Data Lineage for Better Training Data
summary: The proliferation of Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) datasets has exacerbated provenance collapse due to unclear lineage among existing datasets. To bridge this fragmented RLVR data landscape, we propose Atomic-source Tracing via Lineage-Aware Search (ATLAS), a systematic framework for tracing RLVR datasets back to their atomic sources, attributing over 99.7% of 1.45M instances to 20 atomic sources. Our analysis reveals that most RLVR datasets are variants of a small set of shared upstream sources, with few introducing genuinely new data, and many facing data contamination risks. These findings naturally motivate us to curate a new RLVR dataset, DAPO++, and to benchmark existing datasets from a lineage-aware perspective. To this end, we propose Source-level Counterfactual Attribution (SCA) as a guiding principle to curate a decontaminated training dataset with concentrated learning signals. Essentially, SCA measures a sample's marginal utility by comparing per-atomic-source RL checkpoints against a shared base model. Building upon these attribution signals, we further design a composite dataset quality score Q that strongly correlates with downstream RLVR performance. Experiments on Qwen3 series models verify that DAPO++ consistently improves performance on held-out benchmarks, while Q reliably predicts downstream RLVR training effectiveness. Our code and data is available at https://github.com/Celine-hxy/ATLAS.

AI.tldr: Proposes ATLAS to trace RLVR datasets to atomic sources, revealing most data originate from a small set of sources; introduces DAPO++ dataset and Source-level Counterfactual Attribution (SCA) plus a dataset quality score Q; experiments show improved performance and good predictive power for RLVR training effectiveness; code available.
AI.motivation: Address provenance collapse and data contamination risks in the rapidly growing RLVR data landscape by providing lineage-aware tracing, decontaminated training data, and measurable quality signals to guide dataset curation and model training.
AI.method: ATLAS traces RLVR datasets to ~20 atomic sources with >99.7% attribution over 1.45M instances. Introduces Source-level Counterfactual Attribution (SCA) to measure marginal utility by comparing per-atomic-source RL checkpoints against a shared base model. Proposes Q, a composite dataset quality score, and curates DAPO++ based on attribution signals.
AI.result: Found that most RLVR data are variants of a small set of sources; DAPO++ improves performance on held-out benchmarks (Qwen3 series models). The quality score Q correlates with downstream RLVR training effectiveness and can predict training outcomes. Code and data available at the provided URL.
AI.conclusion: A lineage-aware framework (ATLAS) can mitigate provenance collapse in RLVR data, enabling effective decontamination via SCA and a predictive quality score Q; DAPO++ is a practical, improved dataset for downstream RLVR tasks, with open-source resources to enable replication.
