title: Touch-R1: Reinforcing Touch Reasoning in MLLMs
comment: Our code and data will be made public on the https://laiyingxin2.github.io/Projects
summary: While rule-based reinforcement learning has recently catalyzed explicit reasoning in multimodal models, tactile reasoning remains largely underexplored. Existing tactile-language models primarily rely on supervised or contrastive objectives, which limits their capacity to ground predictions in physical evidence or rectify misleading visual priors. Tactile reasoning introduces two modality-specific challenges: the ordinal nature of physical attributes (e.g., hardness, roughness) and the cross-sensor distribution shifts inherent in optical tactile hardware. In this work, we introduce TouchReason-1M, a large-scale multimodal dataset comprising over 1M synchronized tactile pairs across four distinct sensors, and TouchReason-Bench, a rigorous framework for evaluating tactile perception and visual-tactile conflict resolution. Building upon these, we propose Touch-R1, a tactile reasoning MLLM based on Qwen2.5-VL-7B. Touch-R1 is trained via a tactile-grounded GRPO objective that combines ordinal-aware accuracy, cross-sensor physical consistency, structured-format control, and an input-side tactile grounding objective. Specifically, the tactile-use reward assigns credit only when authentic tactile inputs yield superior correctness relative to counterfactual controls where the tactile stream is removed, shuffled, or noise-masked. On TouchReason-Bench, Touch-R1-7B outperforms Octopi-13B by 18.4\% and GPT-4o by 24.7\% on average. Its structured reasoning traces reveal emergent behaviors of probing, comparison, and revision, demonstrating that R1-style reasoning can be effectively grounded in physical contact.

AI.tldr: TouchReason-1M and TouchReason-Bench enable robust tactile grounding in multimodal models, culminating in Touch-R1 (a Qwen2.5-VL-7B-based tactile reasoning MLLM) trained with tactile-grounded GRPO. It achieves strong gains over baselines and shows emergent structured reasoning behaviors grounded in physical contact.
AI.motivation: Addresses the gap in tactile reasoning for multimodal models, where existing tactile-language approaches rely on supervised/contrastive objectives and fail to ground predictions in physical evidence; tackles two modality-specific challenges—ordinal physical attributes and cross-sensor distribution shifts—and provides data/benchmarks to enable robust tactile grounding.
AI.method: Construct TouchReason-1M: over 1M synchronized tactile pairs across four sensors; develop TouchReason-Bench for evaluating tactile perception and visual–tactile conflict resolution; propose Touch-R1, a tactile reasoning MLLM based on Qwen2.5-VL-7B, trained with tactile-grounded GRPO objective: combines ordinal-aware accuracy, cross-sensor physical consistency, structured-format control, and an input-side tactile grounding objective; tactile-use reward credits correctness only when authentic tactile inputs improve outcomes relative to counterfactuals where tactile stream is removed, shuffled, or noise-masked.
AI.result: On TouchReason-Bench, Touch-R1-7B outperforms Octopi-13B by 18.4% and GPT-4o by 24.7% on average; its structured reasoning traces show emergent behaviors of probing, comparison, and revision, indicating R1-style reasoning can be grounded in physical contact.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the feasibility and value of tactile-grounded, R1-style reasoning in multimodal models; provides a large-scale tactile dataset and a rigorous benchmark to advance visually grounded tactile perception and cross-modal grounding.

title: Convergence of Spectral Descent for Non-smooth Optimization
summary: The Muon optimizer has recently demonstrated remarkable empirical success in training large language models. However, the theoretical understanding of its mechanisms remains limited. Current convergence guarantees for Muon rely heavily on smoothness assumptions, leaving its non-smooth convergence behavior largely unexplored. In this work, we take a step toward bridging this gap by investigating Spectral Descent (SD), a simplified variant of Muon, together with its truncated counterpart, Truncated Spectral Descent (TSD). Under convexity, Lipschitz continuity, and sharpness conditions, we establish global linear convergence for both SD and TSD in non-smooth convex formulations. We also study regularized variants equipped with decoupled weight decay and derive sublinear convergence guarantees through their connection with Frank-Wolfe methods. Finally, we apply our theoretical framework to robust low-rank matrix recovery under mixed sparse and dense noise regimes and provide rigorous recovery guarantees. Numerical experiments support the theoretical findings and demonstrate the effectiveness of Muon-type methods for non-smooth optimization.

AI.tldr: Non-smooth convergence of Muon-type methods analyzed via Spectral Descent (SD) and Truncated Spectral Descent (TSD); prove global linear convergence under convexity, Lipschitz continuity, and sharpness; regularized variants yield sublinear rates via Frank-Wolfe connections; apply to robust low-rank matrix recovery with guarantees; supported by experiments.
AI.motivation: Address the gap in theoretical understanding of Muon-type optimizers in non-smooth regimes and provide rigorous convergence guarantees and practical recovery guarantees for robust estimation tasks.
AI.method: Introduce SD and TSD as simplified Muon variants; establish global linear convergence under convexity, Lipschitz continuity, and sharpness for non-smooth convex objectives; study decoupled weight-decay regularized variants and derive sublinear rates through connections to Frank-Wolfe methods; apply the framework to robust low-rank matrix recovery under mixed sparse/dense noise; provide numerical experiments.
AI.result: Global linear convergence established for SD and TSD in non-smooth convex settings; regularized variants show sublinear convergence via Frank-Wolfe connections; rigorous recovery guarantees for robust low-rank matrix recovery under mixed noise; numerical experiments corroborate theory and efficacy.
AI.conclusion: SD/TSD extend Muon-type optimization theory into non-smooth convex domains, offering strong convergence guarantees and practical recovery results; results highlight potential and also underscore assumptions (convexity, sharpness, Lipschitz) required; invites future work to relax assumptions and address non-convex settings.

title: Semantic Robustness Probing via Inpainting: An Interactive Tool for Safety-Critical Object Detection
summary: Testing object detectors in safety-critical domains requires semantically meaningful probes beyond pixel-level corruptions. We present SemProbe, a tool for semantic robustness probing: users upload deployment images, create masks manually or automatically, select operational design domain-derived factors (or custom prompts), and run diffusion-based controlled inpainting. The system supports batch jobs, parallel seed/workflow variations, and configurable generation parameters. After each output, model inference runs automatically and displays annotated before/after comparisons with performance deltas. All probes are logged as structured artifacts, enabling traceable robustness evidence aligned with safety evaluation workflows. We demonstrate \textsc{SemProbe} on hand detection for dimension saws, targeting factors from insurance-oriented test criteria.

AI.tldr: SemProbe enables semantic robustness probing for object detectors via diffusion-based inpainting, with semantic masks and design-domain factors, batch processing, and structured provenance.
AI.motivation: Safety-critical deployments require probes that test semantic robustness beyond pixel-level corruptions and produce traceable evidence aligned with safety evaluation workflows.
AI.method: Users upload deployment images, create masks (manual/automatic), select operational design domain-derived factors or custom prompts, and run diffusion-based controlled inpainting. The system supports batch jobs, parallel seed/workflow variations, and configurable generation parameters. After each output, model inference runs automatically and displays annotated before/after comparisons with performance deltas. All probes are logged as structured artifacts for traceability.
AI.result: Demonstrated SemProbe on hand detection for dimension saws, targeting factors from insurance-oriented test criteria.
AI.conclusion: SemProbe offers a practical, reproducible framework for semantic robustness evaluation in safety-critical settings, enabling traceable evidence and integration with safety evaluation workflows.

title: TED: Related Party Transaction guided Tax Evasion Detection on Heterogeneous Graph
comment: Accepted by Data Mining and Knowledge Discovery (DMKD25)
summary: Tax evasion causes severe losses of government revenues and disturbs the economic order of fair competition. To help alleviate this problem, the latest tax evasion detection solutions utilize expert knowledge to extract features and then train classifiers to determine whether a company is suspected of tax evasion. However, existing solutions mainly focus on the statistical features of the company, but fail to exploit the rich interactive information in tax scenarios, which affect the detection performance. In this paper, we first model the tax scenario as a heterogeneous graph and study the tax evasion detection problem under the heterogeneous graph model. To improve the performance of tax evasion detection, a novel graph neural network model is proposed to extract the comprehensive information of heterogeneous graphs. Specifically, we use heterogeneous and complex related party transaction groups to filter low-level noise information. Moreover, a hierarchical attention mechanism is designed to capture the deeper structure and semantic information hidden in the related party transaction group. We apply our method to the real risk management system of the tax bureau, and evaluate it on two human-labeled real-world tax datasets. The results demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms the state-of-the-art in the tax evasion detection task.

AI.tldr: Heterogeneous graph neural network for tax evasion detection that leverages related party transaction groups and hierarchical attention to filter noise and capture deep structural/semantic information, achieving state-of-the-art results on real-world datasets.
AI.motivation: Tax evasion causes significant government revenue losses and distorts fair competition. Existing classifiers rely mainly on statistical feature engineering and fail to exploit rich interactive information in tax scenarios. There is a need to model tax scenarios as heterogeneous graphs to improve detection performance.
AI.method: Model the tax scenario as a heterogeneous graph. Use heterogeneous, complex related party transaction groups to filter low-level noise. Implement a hierarchical attention mechanism to capture deep structure and semantic information in related party transaction groups. Evaluate on real-world, human-labeled tax datasets within a tax bureau risk management system.
AI.result: The proposed method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in tax evasion detection on real-world datasets.
AI.conclusion: A novel GNN-based framework for tax evasion detection that effectively leverages heterogeneous graphs and hierarchical attention to improve detection performance, with demonstrated potential for real-world risk management.

title: Model discovery for dynamical systems with complex-valued product units
summary: Discovering the governing equations of a dynamical system from observed trajectories provides deeper insight into its structure than mere prediction of future states. We present a data-driven approach to model discovery based on complex-valued product-unit networks, in which each unit represents a complex monomial and the network output is a sparse linear combination of such monomials. In contrast to established library-based methods such as SINDy, our approach does not require a predefined set of candidate functions: the relevant monomials, including those with fractional or negative exponents, are learned directly from data. Across four chaotic benchmark systems (Lorenz63, Lorenz84, the Four-Wing attractor, and a fractional variant of Lorenz63), we recover the exact governing equations in 90% of trials for the first three systems, and in 70-90% of trials for the fractional case, using at least 3000 training points. Applied to real-world human-gait accelerometer signals, the model produced stable trajectories with bounded prediction errors, corresponding to an RMSE of approximately 12-14% of the signal amplitude range over a test horizon three times longer than the training interval, demonstrating its potential for high-dimensional systems in which analytic equations are unavailable.

AI.tldr: A data-driven discovery method using complex-valued product-unit networks learns monomials directly from data (including fractional/negative exponents) and yields sparse governing equations, achieving high recovery on chaotic benchmarks and stable real-world trajectory forecasts.
AI.motivation: Library-based system identification (e.g., SINDy) relies on predefined candidate function libraries. The goal is to learn governing equations directly from data, including non-integer/exponent terms, and to apply to chaotic and real-world high-dimensional signals without a fixed library.
AI.method: A complex-valued product-unit network where each unit represents a complex monomial; the network output is a sparse linear combination of such monomials. The method learns the relevant monomials (including fractional/negative exponents) from data rather than a predefined library. Evaluated on four chaotic systems (Lorenz63, Lorenz84, Four-Wing, fractional Lorenz63) with at least 3000 training points, and on real-world human-gait accelerometer signals.
AI.result: Exact governing equations recovered in 90% of trials for Lorenz63, Lorenz84, and Four-Wing; 70–90% for the fractional Lorenz63 variant. Real-world gait data yielded stable trajectories with RMSE ~12–14% of the signal amplitude range over a horizon three times longer than the training interval.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates a library-free, data-driven path to discovering governing equations, including fractional exponents, with strong performance on chaotic benchmarks and promising applicability to high-dimensional, real-world systems without analytic models.

title: Sampling Data with Chains of Forward-Backward Diffusion Steps
summary: Sampling from learned high-dimensional distributions is a foundational computational problem. We introduce U-turn chains: Markov chains obtained by iterating short forward-backward steps of a diffusion model, in which each step proposes a move that remains on the learned data manifold and, paired with a Metropolis-Hastings correction, samples from energy-modified targets. For synthetic languages, we show that minimal U-turn dynamics undergoes an ergodicity-breaking phase transition driven by fragmentation of the data manifold; ergodicity is restored at larger U-turn magnitude. In the non-ergodic regime, low-level features relax faster than high-level ones, an ordering that inverts only at sufficiently large U-turn magnitude. We test these predictions on natural language and natural images. In both modalities, minimal U-turns relax slowly, especially for high-level features approximated by deep representations in CNNs or LLMs. The layer-ordering inversion appears only at large noise when mixing is efficient -- signatures consistent with strongly constrained, weakly mixing local dynamics. We discuss the implications of these results for sampling with diffusion models.

AI.tldr: U-turn chains sample from diffusion-models by short forward-backward steps with Metropolis-Hastings correction, revealing ergodicity transitions driven by data-manifold fragmentation; non-ergodic regime shows differential feature relaxation and a layer-ordering inversion at high noise; tested on language and image data.
AI.motivation: Understand the dynamical properties and ergodicity of sampling with diffusion-based models by introducing small forward-backward moves that stay on the learned data manifold and are corrected via MH acceptance, probing how manifold structure and noise affect mixing and feature hierarchies.
AI.method: Define and analyze U-turn Markov chains that perform short forward-backward diffusion steps, with a Metropolis-Hastings correction to target energy-modified distributions. Investigate ergodicity and phase transitions as a function of U-turn magnitude. Empirically test predictions on synthetic languages, natural language (LLMs/CNN features), and natural images, examining relaxation rates of low-level vs high-level features and layer-ordering behavior under varying noise.
AI.result: Identified an ergodicity-breaking phase transition driven by fragmentation of the data manifold at minimal U-turns; ergodicity restored at larger U-turn magnitude. In the non-ergodic regime, low-level features relax faster than high-level features, an ordering that inverts only at sufficiently large U-turn magnitude. Minimal U-turns produce slow relaxation in both NLP and vision, with high-level features (deep representations) relaxing slowest. Layer-ordering inversion emerges at large noise when mixing is efficient, indicating strongly constrained, weakly mixing local dynamics. These findings align with diffusion-model sampling limitations and suggest regimes for efficient mixing and feature hierarchy control.
AI.conclusion: The study highlights dynamical regimes and phase transitions in diffusion-based sampling, with practical implications for selecting U-turn magnitude and noise parameters to balance ergodicity, mixing speed, and hierarchical feature relaxation when using diffusion models for sampling in language and vision tasks.

title: Generative Animations: A Multi-Model Pipeline for Prompt-Driven Motion Synthesis
summary: Animation elevates digital documents into immersive experiences, yet creating custom motion paths remains cumbersome, requiring designers to manually select presets, plot Bézier points, and configure timing properties. We introduce Generative Animations, a system that transforms natural language prompts into production-ready animations. By chaining Large Language Models (LLMs) for semantic parsing with the Segment Anything Model (SAM) for visual grounding, our pipeline automatically generates motion paths that respect scene geometry, handle depth-based occlusions, and honor 3D perspective transforms. We demonstrate the system through three use cases: contour-following trajectories, orbital animations with z-order awareness, and perspective-aligned motion on transformed objects.

AI.tldr: Generative Animations translates natural-language prompts into production-ready, geometry-aware motion paths by chaining LLM-based semantic parsing with SAM-based visual grounding, enabling scene-respecting animations with depth occlusions and perspective transforms.
AI.motivation: Traditional animation workflows require manual, time-consuming crafting of motion paths that must respect scene geometry, depth, and perspective; an automated, prompt-driven pipeline could dramatically speed up production while maintaining geometric coherence.
AI.method: A multi-stage pipeline that uses large language models to semantically parse prompts, and the Segment Anything Model (SAM) for visual grounding, to automatically generate motion paths that align with scene geometry, handle depth-based occlusions, and apply 3D perspective transforms; demonstrated on contour-following trajectories, z-order-aware orbital animations, and perspective-aligned motion on transformed objects.
AI.result: The system is demonstrated through three use cases showing automatic generation of motion paths: contour-following trajectories, orbital animations with z-order awareness, and perspective-aligned motion on transformed objects, validating geometry-aware and perspective-consistent animation generation.
AI.conclusion: Generative Animations offers a workflow to produce production-ready animations directly from natural language prompts by integrating semantic parsing and visual grounding, reducing manual workflow effort and enabling consistent geometric coherence across complex scenes; future work may explore broader scene types and quantitative evaluation.

title: SCENT: Aligning Mass Spectra with Molecular Structure for Olfactory Perception
summary: Predicting human olfactory perception from molecular structure has seen remarkable progress, yet these approaches require explicit chemical structure at inference, which is not available in practical sensing settings. We address this gap by exploring direct electron ionization mass spectrometry (EI-MS), a sensing technique that acquires chemically informative fragmentation fingerprints in seconds, as an alternative input modality for olfactory prediction. We contribute Spectrum-to-Chemical Embedding alignmeNT (SCENT), a multi-modal contrastive learning framework that aligns EI-MS representations with pretrained chemical structure embeddings, while requiring only mass spectra at inference. On the multi-label odor descriptor prediction task, SCENT significantly outperforms MS-only baselines and achieves performance comparable to structure-based models, despite requiring no explicit molecular structure at test time. The learned representations also better approximate continuous human perceptual ratings and generalize to real-world lab-measured spectra, suggesting that cross-modal alignment is an effective strategy for grounding analytical spectra in chemical semantics.

AI.tldr: SCENT uses contrastive alignment between EI-MS spectra and pretrained chemical embeddings to predict olfactory descriptors without explicit structures at test time; achieves MS-based performance comparable to structure-based models and generalizes to real spectra.
AI.motivation: Address the limitation of structure-based olfactory prediction requiring explicit molecular structures at inference; leverage fast EI-MS fragmentation fingerprints as a practical input modality for semantic odor prediction.
AI.method: Multi-modal contrastive learning framework (Spectrum-to-Chemical Embedding alignment) that aligns EI-MS representations with pretrained chemical structure embeddings; at test time only mass spectra are required; learns a shared latent space bridging spectra to chemical semantics; evaluated on multi-label odor descriptor tasks.
AI.result: SCENT outperforms MS-only baselines and approaches the performance of structure-based models on odor descriptor prediction; learned representations better approximate continuous perceptual ratings and generalize to real-world lab-measured spectra.
AI.conclusion: Cross-modal alignment grounding analytical spectra in chemical semantics enables accurate olfactory predictions without explicit molecular structures at test time; EI-MS serves as a practical input modality for perception-driven chemical inference.

title: MRT: Masked Region Transformer for Layered Image Generation and Editing at Scale
summary: Layered image generation and editing is a fundamental capability that enables layer-wise reuse, editing, and composition of generated visual content, analogous to word-level editing in natural language. Despite its importance, this remains an underexplored area at scale. To address this gap, we present MRT, a 20B-parameter masked region diffusion model tailored for multi-layer transparent image generation and editing, trained on over 10M multilingual design samples spanning diverse aspect ratios and textual prompts. To fully leverage this scale, we make two key technical contributions. First, we unify three complementary tasks including text-to-layers, image-to-layers, and layers-to-layers within a shared masked region diffusion framework, where selective token masking enables flexible layer-wise generation and editing. Second, to enable overflow layer generation, we introduce an overflow-aware canvas layer that handles boundary inconsistencies and supports semi-transparent background synthesis, enabling complete editable layers extending beyond visible canvas boundaries. Additionally, we apply diffusion distillation to achieve 8-step, real-time multi-layer generation with minimal quality degradation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our framework substantially outperforms prior state-of-the-art approaches, including various commercial systems, across all three tasks, establishing a new benchmark for multi-layer transparent image generation. Notably, our model significantly outperforms the concurrent Qwen-Image-Layered model in image-to-layers quality according to user-study results, while achieving 10-100\times faster inference and reducing activation GPU memory consumption by 50-90\% during image-to-layer inference.

AI.tldr: A 20B-parameter masked region diffusion model (MRT) enables multi-layer transparent image generation/editing, unifying text-, image-, and layer-to-layer tasks with an overflow-aware canvas and diffusion distillation for 8-step real-time generation; it outperforms SOTA including Qwen-Image-Layered, with faster inference and lower memory.
AI.motivation: Layered image editing at scale remains underexplored and lacks a unified, scalable framework for creating and editing multi-layer transparent content akin to word-level editing in NLP. The problem spans tail and multilingual prompts, diverse aspect ratios, and boundary handling for layers extending beyond the visible canvas.
AI.method: 1) A single masked region diffusion framework that unifies text-to-layers, image-to-layers, and layers-to-layers via selective token masking to control layer generation/editing. 2) An overflow-aware canvas layer to address boundary inconsistencies and enable semi-transparent backgrounds, allowing layers to extend beyond canvas bounds. 3) Diffusion distillation to reduce inference to 8 steps, enabling real-time generation with minimal quality loss. 4) Training on 10M multilingual design samples with varied aspect ratios and prompts.
AI.result: The MRT framework substantially outperforms prior state-of-the-art across all three tasks, including commercial systems. Notably, it surpasses the concurrent Qwen-Image-Layered model in image-to-layers quality per user studies, while delivering 10–100x faster inference and 50–90% lower GPU memory usage during image-to-layer inference.
AI.conclusion: MRT establishes a new benchmark for multi-layer transparent image generation and editing, demonstrating scalable, real-time, high-quality layer-based content creation and editing, with practical efficiency gains over existing approaches.

title: SQARL: A Size-Agnostic Reinforcement Learning approach for Circuit Allocation in Distributed Quantum Architectures
summary:
The scaling of quantum processors is currently limited by technical challenges such as decoherence and cross-talk. As the number of qubits grows, interference increases the computational noise. Distributed quantum computing addresses these limitations by interconnecting smaller, easier-to-handle quantum processors (cores), but it introduces the challenge of minimizing slow, error-prone inter-core communication. The task of distributing quantum circuits across cores while minimizing communication costs is known as the Qubit Allocation problem. This work focuses on developing a deep learning approach to this problem, emphasizing flexibility to quantum hardware topology and improving state-of-the-art performance.
  Heuristic and non-learning algorithms, such as the Hungarian Qubit Allocation (HQA), currently represent the state of the art. Reinforcement Learning (RL) approaches leverage learned allocation policies but often lack flexibility, requiring retraining when hardware configurations change, and they fall short of the solution quality achieved by non-learning methods. However, learning mechanisms could outperform human-crafted heuristics.
  To overcome these limitations, this work proposes a flexible, transformer-based architecture that can handle arbitrary numbers of qubits and cores without retraining. Results show that the trained policy consistently outperforms the previous RL state of the art and narrows the gap between RL and HQA for the most common circuits. It achieves a 33% reduction in allocation cost relative to the HQA for the Cuccaro Adder and 25% on average for random circuits. These findings show that learning-based approaches can effectively match the performance of hand-crafted heuristics, a crucial step towards their application in real-world scenarios.

AI.tldr: Transformer-based reinforcement learning for Qubit Allocation in distributed quantum computing; achieves 33% reduction over HQA on Cuccaro Adder and 25% on average for random circuits; flexible to arbitrary qubit/core counts without retraining, narrowing the gap with hand-crafted heuristics.
AI.motivation: Scalability limits in quantum processors due to decoherence and cross-talk; distributed quantum computing connects smaller cores but incurs costly inter-core communication. Existing Qubit Allocation methods (HQA, RL) either lack flexibility or do not reach heuristic performance; a topology-agnostic, retraining-free learning approach could combine performance with adaptability.
AI.method: Proposes a transformer-based architecture that scales to arbitrary numbers of qubits and cores without retraining. Trains a policy for qubit allocation and evaluates against Hungarian Qubit Allocation (HQA) and RL baselines on Cuccaro Adder and random circuits to assess performance and generalization across hardware topologies.
AI.result: The learned policy beats the previous RL state of the art and reduces allocation costs, narrowing the gap to HQA for common circuits. Specifically, there is a 33% reduction in allocation cost vs HQA for the Cuccaro Adder and about 25% on average for random circuits.
AI.conclusion: Learning-based approaches, particularly transformer-guided policies, can match or approach the performance of hand-crafted heuristics in Qubit Allocation without retraining when hardware topology changes, representing a crucial step toward practical deployment of distributed quantum computing.

title: Can Retrieval Heads See Images? Multimodal Retrieval Heads in Long-Context Vision-Language Models
summary: Large vision-language models increasingly rely on long-context modeling to reason over documents, hour-level videos, and long-horizon agent trajectories, requiring them to locate relevant evidence across interleaved text and images. Prior work has studied this behavior using retrieval heads in large language models, but its copy-based criterion does not directly apply when evidence appears in images. We introduce a multimodal retrieval head detection method that scores attention from question tokens to textual or visual evidence. With this method, we show that multimodal retrieval heads are sparse, intrinsic, and causally important: only 4.4-10.2% of attention heads account for 50% of the positive retrieval-score mass, and masking the top-5% selected heads drops MMLongBench-Doc from 48.2% to 5.7% and SlideVQA from 71.2% to 8.9%, while random-head masking is far less damaging. Further analysis shows that these heads are partly shared across modalities yet remain dynamic within each modality, with image retrieval heads changing more than text retrieval heads as context length and haystack modality change. Without further training, we find that these heads can also be used directly to rank visually rich documents: on MMDocIR, Qwen3-VL-8B selected-head scoring improves Recall@1 by 7.7/7.4 macro/micro points for page retrieval and 6.3/6.8 points for layout retrieval over the strongest reported baseline.

AI.tldr: A small subset of multimodal retrieval heads governs most evidence retrieval in long-context LMMs; these heads are sparse, intrinsic, and causally important, with cross-modal sharing and modality-specific dynamics. Masking top retrieval heads severely degrades multimodal tasks, while using head scores can improve document ranking without extra training.
AI.motivation: To understand how long-context multimodal models locate evidence across interleaved text and images, and whether retrieval mechanisms exist inside attention heads that can be characterized, pruned, or repurposed for efficient retrieval in practice.
AI.method: Analyze attention heads in multimodal LLMs across long-context tasks by defining a retrieval score from question tokens to textual or visual evidence. Quantify head sparsity (fraction accounting for half of retrieval-score mass), perform head-masking experiments (top X% vs random) to assess causality, analyze cross-modality sharing and modality-dynamic behavior, and evaluate head-based scoring for ranking visually rich documents (MMDocIR) without additional training.
AI.result: Only 4.4–10.2% of attention heads account for 50% of positive retrieval-score mass. Masking the top 5% heads drops MMLongBench-Doc from 48.2% to 5.7% and SlideVQA from 71.2% to 8.9%; random-head masking is far less damaging. Heads are partly shared across modalities but remain dynamic within each modality, with image retrieval heads changing more with context length and haystack modality. Without further training, top heads can rank visually rich documents, e.g., on MMDocIR Qwen3-VL-8B selected-head scoring improves Recall@1 by 7.7/7.4 macro/micro for page retrieval and 6.3/6.8 for layout retrieval over the strongest reported baseline.
AI.conclusion: Multimodal retrieval heads are sparse, intrinsic, and causally important for long-context multimodal reasoning. They offer pruning/efficiency opportunities, and their selected heads can directly rank visually rich documents without extra training. The heads exhibit partial cross-modal sharing but modality-specific dynamics, suggesting targeted pruning and dynamic adaptation as viable directions for efficient multimodal retrieval in LLMs.

title: Less is More: Early Stopping Rollout for On-Policy Distillation
summary:
On-policy distillation has recently emerged as a promising alternative to standard sequence-level imitation, training a student by scoring its own rollouts with a teacher model. However, we observe ``Off-policy Teacher Decay'' problem in this paradigm: for the later tokens, with student's earlier trajectory as context that is off-policy to the teacher, the teacher's ability to produce a corrective score would decay, and may fall back to token-completion behavior learned in the pre-training stage. We empirically verify this problem, and we propose Early Stopping Rollout (ESR) to fix it: a simple yet effective distillation strategy that simply restricts the rollout generation to the first
  response tokens. We show that ESR both surpasses the full rollout OPD performance across model size, family, tasks and training regime, and exhibit much higher GPU efficiency and training stability, especially under cross model family scenarios. We further investigate the mechanism behind this surprising performance and discovered "Cascading Alignment" and "Sub-mode Commitment" effect of ESR that may explain why it works effectively and even sometimes exceeding the teacher model performance. Besides, we show that this position-based token selection strategy cannot be fully explainable by KL divergence and entropy signals.

AI.tldr: Early Stopping Rollout (ESR) is proposed to mitigate Off-policy Teacher Decay in on-policy distillation by restricting rollouts to the initial tokens, yielding better performance and efficiency than full rollouts across model sizes, families, tasks, and training regimes. The authors identify mechanisms—Cascading Alignment and Sub-mode Commitment—that may explain ESR’s effectiveness, and show that the improvement cannot be fully explained by KL divergence or entropy signals.
AI.motivation: Off-policy Teacher Decay (OPD) undermines the quality of on-policy distillation: as the student's trajectory becomes more off-policy relative to the teacher, the teacher's corrective scores for later tokens degrade, leading to suboptimal guidance and potentially pretraining-like completion behavior.
AI.method: Introduce Early Stopping Rollout (ESR): constrain teacher scoring to the first response tokens during rollout-based distillation. Evaluate ESR against full-rollout OPD across model sizes, families, tasks, and training regimes. Analyze mechanisms (Cascading Alignment, Sub-mode Commitment) and probe whether token-selection signals align with KL/divergence and entropy traces.
AI.result: ESR consistently surpasses full-rollout OPD across model size, family, tasks, and training regime, with higher GPU efficiency and improved training stability, particularly in cross-model-family scenarios. The authors identify Cascading Alignment and Sub-mode Commitment as the underlying mechanisms. They also show that ESR’s performance cannot be fully explained by KL divergence or entropy signals.
AI.conclusion: ESR is an effective, robust distillation strategy for on-policy learning that mitigates OPD and can even exceed teacher performance in some settings. Its success appears to arise from cascading alignment and sub-mode commitment, indicating token-position strategies may rely on dynamics beyond simple KL/entropy explanations; invites further theoretical and empirical study.

title: A Dynamic Programming Framework for Discovering Count and Values of Multilevel Image Thresholding
summary: Multilevel Image thresholding is an important preprocessing algorithm in computer vision applications nowadays. Since most common thresholding methods take the desired count of thresholds as input by the user, thresholding methods that automatically determines a suitable count of thresholds from the input image itself are advantageous. In this article, a novel thresholding method based on a dynamic programming algorithm and a modification of Minimum Error Thresholding (MET) criterion is thoroughly presented. An empirical statistical study is performed to pinpoint why this proposed method is superior. Moreover, an extended comparison between this proposed method and other state-of-the-art methods is performed on a comprehensive set of natural, satellite and medical test images. The numerical results show that the proposed MET-DP method takes much less time than traditional dynamic programming thresholding methods when the number of thresholds is high. The proposed method can detect a suitable count of thresholds for most of tested images of different types. However, traditional methods that take the count of thresholds as input produce thresholded images of higher structural similarity index measure (SSIM) and peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR) values than MET-DP. Source code can be found on https://w3id.org/met-dp/article1-code

AI.tldr: A novel MET-DP thresholding method that automatically determines the number of thresholds for multilevel image thresholding by combining a modified Minimum Error Thresholding (MET) criterion with dynamic programming. It achieves faster performance for higher threshold counts and generalizes across natural, satellite, and medical images, though fixed-threshold methods may yield higher SSIM/PSNR.
AI.motivation: Automatic selection of the threshold count in multilevel thresholding to reduce user input and improve efficiency, addressing slow DP-based methods at higher threshold counts and the need for robust performance across diverse image types.
AI.method: Develop MET-DP by integrating a modified MET criterion into a dynamic programming framework to simultaneously decide threshold values and the number of thresholds. Compare empirically against state-of-the-art methods on diverse image sets; analyze time and accuracy.
AI.result: MET-DP achieves substantially faster runtimes than traditional DP thresholding when the number of thresholds is large; it can automatically determine a suitable threshold count for most tested images; however, methods that require an explicit threshold count often achieve higher SSIM and PSNR.
AI.conclusion: MET-DP offers an efficient automatic multilevel thresholding solution with broad applicability and competitive accuracy; while it may trade some image quality metrics for speed, its automatic threshold count determination and code availability make it a practical alternative.

title: Learning Dynamic Graph Representations through Timespan View Contrasts
summary: The rich information underlying graphs has inspired further investigation of unsupervised graph representation. Existing studies mainly depend on node features and topological properties within static graphs to create self-supervised signals, neglecting the temporal components carried by real-world graph data, such as timestamps of edges. To overcome this limitation, this paper explores how to model temporal evolution on dynamic graphs elegantly. Specifically, we introduce a new inductive bias, namely temporal translation invariance, which illustrates the tendency of the identical node to keep similar labels across different timespans. Based on this assumption, we develop a dynamic graph representation framework CLDG that encourages the node to maintain locally consistent temporal translation invariance through contrastive learning on different timespans. Except for standard CLDG which only considers explicit topological links, our further proposed CLDG++ additionally employs graph diffusion to uncover global contextual correlations between nodes, and designs a multi-scale contrastive learning objective composed of local-local, local-global, and global-global contrasts to enhance representation capabilities. Interestingly, by measuring the consistency between different timespans to shape anomaly indicators, CLDG and CLDG++ are seamlessly integrated with the task of spotting anomalies on dynamic graphs, which has broad applications in many high-impact domains, such as finance, cybersecurity, and healthcare. Experiments demonstrate that CLDG and CLDG++ both exhibit desirable performance in downstream tasks including node classification and dynamic graph anomaly detection. Moreover, CLDG significantly reduces time and space complexity by implicitly exploiting temporal cues instead of complicated sequence models.

AI.tldr: Temporal translation invariance-based dynamic graph representation via CLDG and CLDG++ using diffusion and multi-scale contrastive learning across timespans, enabling efficient node representations and anomaly detection.
AI.motivation: Real-world graphs are inherently dynamic; static/self-supervised methods ignore temporal evolution. A temporal invariant inductive bias can promote consistent representations of the same node across time, enabling effective unsupervised learning and anomaly detection in dynamic graphs.
AI.method: Introduce temporal translation invariance as an inductive bias. CLDG learns representations by contrastive learning across different timespans; CLDG++ extends CLDG with graph diffusion to capture global context. A multi-scale contrastive objective is used: local-local, local-global, and global-global contrasts. The framework can integrate anomaly indicators by measuring cross-timespan consistency. CLDG is computationally efficient by avoiding heavy sequence models; CLDG++ further leverages diffusion for richer context.
AI.result: Empirical results show that CLDG and CLDG++ achieve desirable performance on downstream tasks such as node classification and dynamic graph anomaly detection. CLDG, in particular, reduces time and space complexity by implicitly leveraging temporal cues instead of complex sequence models, while CLDG++ provides improved representations through global-context diffusion.
AI.conclusion: Temporal translation invariance is a promising inductive bias for dynamic graph representation. The CLDG family provides effective, scalable unsupervised learning for dynamic graphs and anomaly detection, with strong potential for applications in finance, cybersecurity, and healthcare.

title: Gemini Embedding 2: A Native Multimodal Embedding Model from Gemini
summary: We introduce Gemini Embedding 2, a native multimodal embedding model that allows embedding video, audio, image, and text modalities in a unified representation space. We leverage the multimodal capabilities of Gemini to produce embeddings for arbitrary combinations of interleaved inputs across all these modalities that generalize well across a wide variety of tasks. Applying large-scale contrastive learning in a multi-task multi-stage training setup, we achieve state-of-the-art performance on key embedding benchmarks including unimodal, cross-modal, and multimodal retrieval spanning a diverse set of tasks. We show that our embedding model demonstrates strong performance (with a score of 62.9 R@1 on MSCOCO, 68.8 NDCG@10 on Vatex, 69.9 on MTEB multilingual and 84.0 on MTEB Code) across a variety of tasks surpassing the performance of specialized models. These unified capabilities make Gemini Embedding 2 a promising candidate for downstream use cases such as RAG, recommendation and search. Furthermore, its robust zero-shot performance across distinct fields - from astronomy and bioscience to fine arts and the culinary arts - establishes it as a highly reliable, out-of-the-box representation even for specialized domains.

AI.tldr: Gemini Embedding 2 introduces a native, unified multimodal embedding model that encodes video, audio, image, and text into a single representation space. Trained with large-scale contrastive learning in a multi-task, multi-stage setup, it achieves state-of-the-art results across unimodal, cross-modal, and multimodal retrieval benchmarks and demonstrates strong zero-shot generalization across diverse domains, making it suitable for RAG, recommendation, and search.
AI.motivation: To create a single, versatile embedding space that supports all major modalities and their combinations, enabling flexible downstream tasks and strong zero-shot generalization without relying on modality-specific encoders.
AI.method: A native multimodal embedding model that embeds input from video, audio, image, and text into a unified space. It supports arbitrary combinations of interleaved inputs from all modalities. Training uses large-scale contrastive learning in a multi-task, multi-stage setup, leveraging Gemini's multimodal capabilities to align representations across modalities and tasks.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on key embedding benchmarks, reporting: 62.9 R@1 on MSCOCO, 68.8 NDCG@10 on Vatex, 69.9 on MTEB multilingual, and 84.0 on MTEB Code. Demonstrates robust zero-shot performance across diverse domains (e.g., astronomy, bioscience, fine arts, culinary), and outperforms specialized models on various tasks.
AI.conclusion: Gemini Embedding 2 provides a unified, robust multimodal representation suitable for downstream tasks such as retrieval, RAG, recommendation, and search, with strong cross-domain zero-shot capabilities that extend to specialized domains.

title: Learning to Orchestrate Agents under Uncertainty
summary: Adaptive orchestration of heterogeneous agents requires making sequential delegation decisions under uncertain and evolving agent behaviour, e.g., coordinating specialised AI models with varying reliability, cost, and response quality. While prior work on agent orchestration focuses on performance or cost, uncertainty in agent reliability and output distributions is typically not modelled explicitly at the orchestration level. In this work, we study the problem of adaptive orchestration of heterogeneous agents under uncertainty, where a meta-controller must decide when to delegate to an agent, accounting for reliability, cost, and uncertainty. We propose BOT-Orch, a lightweight framework that recasts orchestration as a bandit problem over agents, regularized by OT distances between agent output distributions and task-specific reference distributions. We show that the regularised orchestration enjoys $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{T})$ regret under standard assumptions, and provably induces preference ordering among agents with identical mean rewards but differing distributional alignment. Empirically, we demonstrate that BOT-Orch outperforms standard bandit and heuristic baselines in synthetic but adversarial task allocation settings with heterogeneous, non-i.i.d. agent behaviour.

AI.tldr: BOT-Orch recasts heterogeneous agent orchestration as a bandit problem regularized by OT distances between agent output distributions and task references, achieving sqrt(T) regret and enabling preference ordering by distributional alignment; empirically superior to standard bandits and heuristics in adversarial, non-i.i.d. settings.
AI.motivation: Uncertainty in agent reliability and output distributions is often ignored in orchestration, yet crucial for robust delegation when agents vary in cost, reliability, and behavior.
AI.method: Formulate as a bandit over agents with OT-based regularization; use reference output distributions per task; derive regret bound O(sqrt(T)) under standard assumptions; show that agents with identical mean rewards are ordered by how well their distributions align with task references.
AI.result: Theoretical: O(sqrt(T)) regret; distributional alignment induces preference ordering among similarly-mean agents. Empirical: BOT-Orch outperforms baseline bandits and heuristics in synthetic, adversarial, non-i.i.d. settings with heterogeneous agents.
AI.conclusion: Regularizing orchestration with OT distances yields robust, distribution-aware agent selection, enabling principled handling of uncertainty in agent behavior and improving allocation performance in challenging environments.

title: PlayClass: Automated Play Behaviour Classification in Poultry
summary: Automated monitoring of animal welfare has largely targeted negative indicators, leaving positive welfare behaviours such as play underexplored. To address this gap, we present PlayClass, a pipeline for play-behaviour classification in poultry from top-down pen video. The pipeline leverages long-duration tracking with SAM 3 via YOLO-guided chunk boundaries to minimise identity errors in point-based prompting, and frozen embeddings from image and video foundation models for play action classification. Although handcrafted motion features from tracked masks alone achieved competitive accuracy, V-JEPA 2.1 consistently outperformed all other backbones across model scales, reaching 77.0 macro-averaged F$_1$ when combined with handcrafted features. Despite this result, the dataset remains challenging due to play sub-types sharing similar kinematic profiles with non-play and inter-bird occlusion. Overall, our work provides encouraging evidence towards automated frameworks for play behaviour classification in poultry.

AI.tldr: PlayClass presents a pipeline for automated play-behavior classification in poultry from pen video, combining SAM-guided YOLO chunking and frozen embeddings with handcrafted motion features, achieving 77.0 macro-F1 for play classification, while noting persistent challenge from similar kinematics and occlusion.
AI.motivation: Addresses a gap in automated welfare monitoring by focusing on positive welfare behaviours (play) in poultry, which have been underexplored compared to negative indicators.
AI.method: Long-duration tracking with SAM 3 using YOLO-guided chunk boundaries to reduce identity errors; use of frozen embeddings from image/video foundation models for play-action classification; comparison with handcrafted motion features on tracked masks; evaluation across model backbones with V-JEPA 2.1 performing best; macro-F1 used as primary metric.
AI.result: V-JEPA 2.1 consistently outperforms other backbones across scales; 77.0 macro-F1 when combined with handcrafted features; data remains challenging due to play sub-types resembling non-play in motion and inter-bird occlusion.
AI.conclusion: The study provides encouraging evidence that automated play-behaviour classification in poultry is feasible using a hybrid approach that combines short-term handcrafted features with frozen representations from foundation models, though further work is needed to disambiguate play sub-types and handle occlusion.

title: Two Speeds of Learning: A Representation-Readout Decomposition of Grokking and Double Descent
summary: Training loss and accuracy are the standard signals used to monitor generalization during deep neural network training. Two well-documented phenomena complicate this picture: in grokking, train loss falls rapidly while test performance improves abruptly only after a long delay; in epoch-wise double descent, train loss decreases monotonically while test loss or error rises and falls. Existing accounts are often task-specific, and a task-agnostic analysis framework for diagnosing and explaining these phenomena across realistic tasks and architectures is missing. We address this challenge by analyzing two competing processes that underlie learning dynamics: representation learning in the encoder and readout calibration in the final classifier. Using tools from representational geometry, neural tangent kernels, and linear probing, we show that both processes are active throughout training, with the fluctuations of their relative speed giving rise to seemingly anomalous generalization dynamics. Applying the representation-readout decomposition to grokking across a wide range of tasks and architectures, we find that the readout is train-biased before grokking onset, and representation learning is gradual but not absent, contrary to the lazy-to-rich account. The framework further provides diagnostic signatures distinguishing spurious from genuine generalization: in a previously reported MNIST grokking example and an epoch-wise double descent example, apparent delayed or non-monotone generalization is shown to arise from representation degradation and readout misalignment induced by non-standard training recipes. Together, these results establish the representation-readout decomposition as a top-down framework for understanding learning dynamics and revealing underlying algorithms for interpretability research.

AI.tldr: A representation-readout decomposition explains grokking and epoch-wise double descent by analyzing encoder representations and final-readout calibration; both processes are active throughout training, with their relative speeds driving anomalous generalization; provides diagnostics to distinguish spurious from genuine generalization and a task-agnostic framework for interpretability.
AI.motivation: There is a need for a task-agnostic framework to diagnose and explain anomalous generalization dynamics (e.g., grokking, epoch-wise double descent) across realistic tasks and architectures.
AI.method: Decompose learning into two competing processes: representation learning in the encoder and readout calibration in the final classifier. Use tools from representational geometry, neural tangent kernels (NTK), and linear probing to quantify their dynamics. Apply the representation-readout decomposition across diverse tasks/architectures, including MNIST grokking and epoch-wise double descent under non-standard training recipes.
AI.result: Both representation learning and readout calibration are active throughout training. The relative speed fluctuations between them give rise to non-monotone generalization dynamics. Readout is train-biased before grokking; representation learning is gradual but non-negligible. Diagnostic signatures can distinguish spurious from genuine generalization; non-standard training can cause representation degradation and readout misalignment, explaining apparent delays or non-monotone generalization.
AI.conclusion: The representation-readout decomposition provides a top-down framework to understand learning dynamics and interpretability across tasks and architectures. It enables diagnosing genuine vs. spurious generalization and offers a path toward more robust training and clearer insights into underlying algorithms.

title: How and What to Imagine? Visual Thinking in Unified Multimodal Models for Cross-View Spatial Reasoning
summary: Cross-view spatial reasoning remains a weak spot for vision-language models (VLMs): they often reason in language and lose the fine-grained geometry needed for the task. Thinking with images aims to address this by generating an intermediate thinking image, but recent work shows that models often ignore the visual evidence in these traces. We therefore ask how to make visual thinking matter, and what kind of visual thinking works best. We study these questions in unified multimodal models (UMMs), which natively support interleaved image-text generation. For the first question, we propose View Dropout (VDrop), a training-time intervention that hides parts of one input view from the answer span while keeping them visible to the thinking-image tokens. This encourages the model to use the thinking image when answering, instead of relying only on the input views. Once the thinking image is used for answer prediction, we study which type of visual thinking is most effective. We frame this as a learnability-informativeness tradeoff and compare three thinking-image variants: top-down, panoramic, and point-matching renderings. Trained on synthetic scenes and evaluated on five real-world out-of-domain benchmarks, panoramic visual thinking with VDrop is the only configuration that is both informative and learnable, and it achieves the best out-of-domain generalization.

AI.tldr: Panoramic visual thinking with View Dropout (VDrop) is the only configuration that is both informative and learnable, yielding the strongest out-of-domain generalization in unified multimodal models for cross-view spatial reasoning.
AI.motivation: Cross-view spatial reasoning is a weak spot for vision-language models. Thinking with images provides an intermediate thinking image, but models often ignore visual evidence. The study investigates how to make visual thinking matter and which form of visual thinking is most effective within unified multimodal models (UMMs) that interleave image and text generation.
AI.method: Introduce View Dropout (VDrop), a training-time intervention hiding parts of the input view from the answer span but keeping them visible to thinking-image tokens. Compare three thinking-image renderings—top-down, panoramic, and point-matching—framed as a learnability–informativeness tradeoff. Trained on synthetic scenes; evaluated on five real-world out-of-domain benchmarks.
AI.result: Among the three thinking-image variants, panoramic thinking with VDrop is the only configuration that is both informative and learnable and achieves the best out-of-domain generalization across five benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Panoramic visual thinking combined with VDrop best promotes the use of visual thinking in answering within UMMs, improving generalization. Other variants (top-down, point-matching) underperform due to weaker learnability or informativeness.

title: Q-GeoMem: Question-Guided Geometric Memory for Video Spatial Reasoning
summary: Video spatial reasoning requires accumulating viewpoint-dependent evidence over time while retaining information useful to the question being asked. Existing spatial video-language models improve geometric perception and long-range context modeling, but often treat memory as a generic temporal cache, which can introduce redundant or irrelevant geometry and weaken long-horizon reasoning. We propose \textbf{\ours}, a question-guided geometric memory framework for video spatial reasoning. \ours injects camera-conditioned geometry into visual tokens and maintains two complementary memories: a Fine-Grained Context Bank for recent dense features and camera states, and a Semantic-Geometric Evidence Bank for compact long-range evidence. Each candidate frame is scored by the product of Q-Former-based question relevance and novelty with respect to the retained bank; this score is stored and reused during reading, while a capacity-based replacement rule keeps the bank compact. During reasoning, both memories are read before update and adaptively fused with the current frame representation. Experiments on VSI-Bench and VSTI-Bench show that \ours achieves state-of-the-art performance among evaluated spatial reasoning models, validating the effectiveness of question-guided geometric memory. Ablations further verify the contribution of the proposed evidence scoring mechanism.

AI.tldr: Proposes a question-guided geometric memory framework for video spatial reasoning with two memories: a Fine-Grained Context Bank for recent features and a Semantic-Geometric Evidence Bank for long-range evidence. Uses Q-Former-based question relevance and novelty scoring to evaluate candidate frames, stores scores for reuse, and employs capacity-based replacement. Reads memories before update and fuses them with the current frame during reasoning. Achieves state-of-the-art on VSI-Bench and VSTI-Bench; ablations validate the evidence scoring mechanism.
AI.motivation: Current spatial video-language models treat memory as a generic temporal cache, leading to redundant/irrelevant geometry and weak long-horizon reasoning. There is a need for explicit, question-guided memory that captures camera-conditioned geometry and balances short-term dense context with compact long-range evidence.
AI.method: Introduce two complementary memories: (1) Fine-Grained Context Bank (dense, recent features and camera states); (2) Semantic-Geometric Evidence Bank (compact, long-range evidence). For each candidate frame, compute a score as the product of Q-Former-based question relevance and novelty relative to the retained bank; store this score and reuse during reading; apply a capacity-based replacement to keep banks compact. During reasoning, read both memories, then adaptively fuse with the current frame representation. Evaluate on VSI-Bench and VSTI-Bench.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance among evaluated spatial reasoning models on VSI-Bench and VSTI-Bench; ablations show the proposed evidence scoring mechanism contributes to performance gains.
AI.conclusion: Question-guided geometric memory effectively enhances video spatial reasoning by maintaining two complementary memories and a novel scoring mechanism that selects relevant, non-redundant evidence; this improves long-horizon reasoning and efficiency.

title: ReMoE: Boosting Expert Reuse through Router Fine-Tuning in Memory-Constrained MoE LLM Inference
comment: Accepted at the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026)
summary:
Fine-grained Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models sparsely activate only a subset of experts per token, reducing activated computation while maintaining high model capacity. However, in memory-constrained inference scenarios, only a small set of experts can be cached. Experts not in the cache must be fetched from slow external storage (e.g., UFS), leading to frequent evictions and substantial I/O overhead.
  We propose ReMoE, a router fine-tuning framework designed to boost token-wise expert reuse. ReMoE biases the router toward recently selected experts, producing temporally stable routing that better matches cache locality constraints. By increasing short-horizon expert reuse, ReMoE reduces expert fetches from storage without adding inference-time computation.
  Experiments on DeepSeek and Qwen models show that ReMoE improves expert reuse by 26% while maintaining downstream task performance. Real-system evaluations further confirm these benefits, improving output throughput by 8.4% under vLLM GPU-CPU expert offloading and reducing TPOT by 43.6-49.8% under llama.cpp on Jetson Orin NX, corresponding to a 1.77-1.99$\times$ decode speedup across diverse workloads. Checkpoints and usage instructions are available at https://github.com/BUAA-OSCAR/ReMoE.

AI.tldr: ReMoE biases MoE routers toward recently used experts to improve cache locality and reduce expensive storage fetches in memory-constrained inference, achieving notable expert reuse and throughput gains without extra computation.
AI.motivation: In memory-constrained MoE inference, only a small subset of experts can be cached; non-cached experts incur slow I/O and evictions, causing bottlenecks.
AI.method: Fine-tune the router to prefer recently selected experts, creating temporally stable routing that increases short-horizon expert reuse while avoiding added inference-time computation.
AI.result: Measured 26% improvement in expert reuse on DeepSeek and Qwen models; real-system evaluations show 8.4% throughput gain under vLLM GPU-CPU offloading and 43.6-49.8% TPOT reduction with llama.cpp on Jetson Orin NX, yielding about 1.77-1.99x decode speedup across workloads; code and checks available at the provided GitHub repo.
AI.conclusion: ReMoE effectively increases token-wise expert reuse to align with cache locality constraints, reducing external storage fetches without adding compute and delivering practical deployment gains.

title: PARE: Pruning and Adaptive Routing for Efficient Video Generation
summary: Video Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) generate high-quality videos but demand substantial compute due to wide blocks, deep architectures, and iterative sampling. Recent methods reduce cost by compressing width, depth, or sampling steps, but typically commit to a fixed architecture that cannot adapt to individual inputs or denoising stages. We propose PARE (Pruning and Adaptive Routing for Efficient video generation), which jointly compresses width and depth with structure-aware pruning and input-adaptive routing. For width, we observe that attention heads specialize into spatial and temporal roles, and design importance scoring that accounts for this distinction to prevent motion-critical temporal heads from being pruned prematurely. For depth, we train a lightweight router conditioned on denoising timestep and visual content to dynamically select which blocks to execute at each step, enabling per-input compute adaptation rather than static block removal. A progressive pipeline first recovers width-pruned quality via distillation, then jointly optimizes the student and router to decouple the two learning objectives. Experiments on Wan2.1-14B for both image-to-video and text-to-video generation show that PARE substantially reduces per-step computation while preserving quality across VBench dimensions, and composes with step distillation for further acceleration.

AI.tldr: PARE combines width/depth pruning with structure-aware and input-adaptive routing to enable per-input, per-step compute in video diffusion transformers, maintaining quality while reducing computation; validated on Wan2.1-14B for image-to-video and text-to-video with potential further speedups via distillation.
AI.motivation: Reduce compute of video diffusion transformers without fixed architectures by enabling adaptive, input-dependent pruning and routing across denoising steps.
AI.method: - Structure-aware pruning of attention heads distinguishing spatial vs temporal roles, with tailored importance scores to avoid pruning motion-critical temporal heads. - A depth router conditioned on denoising timestep and visual content to dynamically select which blocks execute at each step. - Progressive training: recover width-pruned quality via distillation, then jointly optimize student and router to decouple width/depth objectives.
AI.result: Per-step computation is substantially reduced while preserving quality on VBench metrics across image-to-video and text-to-video tasks; compatible with step distillation for further acceleration; demonstrated on Wan2.1-14B.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive, input-aware pruning plus routing can drastically reduce compute for video diffusion transformers with minimal quality loss, and is compatible with distillation strategies for further gains.

title: Adversarial Dual On-Policy Distillation from Expressive Flow-based Teacher
summary: Learning from demonstrations in embodied control is often cast as behavioral cloning, and recent diffusion or flow-matching policies improve this paradigm by modeling multi-modal expert actions. Yet these methods remain offline supervised learners: the policy is trained only on expert states and receives no corrective signal on the states it actually visits. On-policy distillation (OPD) offers a natural remedy, but standard OPD assumes a strong fixed teacher, which is unavailable in demonstration-only control. We propose \textbf{FA-OPD}, an \emph{adversarial dual on-policy distillation} method in which a Flow Matching (FM) teacher is learned from demonstrations and co-trained with a lightweight MLP student. The teacher provides two complementary signals on student rollouts. The reward channel learns an expert-likeness objective over state-action pairs and drives online exploration through long-horizon policy optimization. The action channel supplies dense local targets at student-visited states, stabilizing exploitation. FA-OPD couples them so that reward distillation enables generalization beyond point-wise demonstrations, while action distillation keeps exploration anchored near expert-like behavior. Across six robot navigation, manipulation, and locomotion benchmarks, FA-OPD beats strong baselines and shows much stronger robustness under noisy or limited demonstrations.

AI.tldr: FA-OPD introduces adversarial dual on-policy distillation for demonstration-based control by jointly training a Flow Matching teacher from demonstrations and a lightweight MLP student. It uses a reward-based distillation signal to encourage expert-like state-action distributions and an action-based distal target to stabilize exploitation. The two signals are co-optimized online, enabling generalization beyond demonstrations while keeping exploration anchored near expert behavior.
AI.motivation: Offline demonstrations in embodied control fail to provide corrective signals for states the policy visits, limiting generalization. Existing on-policy distillation requires a fixed teacher, which is unavailable in demonstration-only settings. FA-OPD aims to enable online learning by jointly learning a teacher and student from demonstrations, balancing exploration and imitation.
AI.method: Train a Flow Matching (FM) teacher from demonstrations and co-train it with a lightweight MLP student. The teacher provides two signals during student rollouts: (1) a reward channel that distills expert-likeness over state-action pairs to drive long-horizon policy optimization; (2) an action channel that outputs dense local targets at states visited by the student to stabilize exploitation. The framework operates adversarially dual distillation online, allowing the teacher to adapt as the student explores.
AI.result: Empirically outperforms strong baselines across six robot navigation, manipulation, and locomotion benchmarks. FA-OPD exhibits improved robustness when demonstrations are noisy or limited, indicating better generalization and stable online learning.
AI.conclusion: Jointly learning a FM-based teacher and an MLP student through dual on-policy distillation provides stable, robust online imitation for embodied control, enabling generalization beyond pointwise demonstrations while maintaining exploration near expert-like behavior.

title: Towards Controllable Image Generation through Representation-Conditioned Diffusion Models
summary: Diffusion models have emerged as powerful tools for high-quality image generation and editing, but guiding these models to produce specific outputs remains a challenge. Conventional approaches rely on conditioning mechanisms, such as text prompts or semantic maps, which require extensively annotated datasets. In this preliminary work, we explore diffusion models conditioned on representations from a pre-trained self-supervised model. The self-conditioning mechanism not only improves the quality of unconditional image generation, but also provides a representation space that can be used to control the generation. We explore this conditioning space by identifying directions of variations, and demonstrate promising properties in terms of smoothness and disentanglement.

AI.tldr: Self-supervised conditioning improves diffusion model generation quality and enables controllable generation via the learned representation space, with observed smoothness and partial disentanglement in the induced variation directions.
AI.motivation: Reduce dependence on large labeled conditioning data by leveraging a pre-trained self-supervised representation space to guide diffusion-based image generation and editing.
AI.method: Incorporate representations from a pre-trained self-supervised model as conditioning signals to a diffusion model (self-conditioning). Analyze the conditioning space by identifying variation directions (e.g., linear directions in representation space) and assess properties such as smoothness and disentanglement of generated outputs.
AI.result: Unconditional generation quality is improved by self-conditioning; the representation space affords controllable generation, with observed smooth variation along identified directions and evidence of partial disentanglement of factors.
AI.conclusion: Self-supervised conditioning is a promising, annotation-efficient approach for guiding diffusion models, enabling controllable editing and generation through interpretable directions in the learned representation space; further work should quantify disentanglement and robustness across tasks.

title: Mildly Overparameterized ReLU Networks on Orthogonal Data: Incremental Learning and Implicit Bias
summary: The successful training of neural networks hinges on the use of first order optimization methods, yet the theoretical characterization of these methods remains incomplete. This is especially true in settings with mild overparameterization. In this work, we study the gradient flow dynamics of two-layer ReLU networks from small initialization with orthogonal training data. We prove the limiting flow converges to a saddle-to-saddle jump process as the initialization scale tends to zero, revealing an incremental learning phenomenon in which a new neuron activates at each saddle. This analysis recovers the known result of Dana et al. (2025, arXiv:2502.16977) that the network interpolates the training data with high probability as soon as $m \gtrsim \log(n)$, where $m$ is the network width and $n$ is the number of training samples. This incremental process characterization also allows us to derive a novel implicit bias result: the learned interpolator has a squared $\ell_2$-norm scaling as $\sqrt{n}$, which is within a constant factor of the minimal $\ell_2$-norm interpolator. More broadly, our work provides the first rigorous proof of an incremental learning process for ReLU networks, whilst suggesting mildly overparameterized networks can converge to interpolating solutions whose complexity is of the same order as that of the optimal interpolator.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes gradient flow of two-layer ReLU networks with small initialization and orthogonal data, showing an incremental learning dynamic where neurons activate sequentially via a saddle-to-saddle jump; it recovers that width m ≳ log n suffices to interpolate; derives an implicit bias with the learned interpolator's squared L2-norm scaling as sqrt(n) (within a constant factor of the minimum-norm interpolator); provides the first rigorous incremental-learning proof for ReLU networks, suggesting mildly overparameterized nets can achieve interpolating solutions with near-optimal complexity.
AI.motivation: Address gaps in theoretical understanding of first-order optimization for mildly overparameterized networks; characterize gradient-flow dynamics and connect to interpolation thresholds and implicit bias; establish incremental learning as a fundamental phenomenon.
AI.method: Rigorous analysis of gradient flow for two-layer ReLU networks at small initialization with orthogonal training data; asymptotic analysis as initialization scale → 0; prove convergence to a saddle-to-saddle jump process and an incremental activation of neurons; leverage and extend prior interpolation results (e.g., Dana et al.).
AI.result: Proves that the limiting gradient flow follows a saddle-to-saddle jump, yielding incremental learning where one neuron activates at each saddle; confirms interpolation occurs with high probability when width m grows like log n; derives an implicit bias: the interpolator's squared L2-norm scales as √n, within a constant factor of the minimal-norm interpolator; provides the first rigorous incremental-learning proof for ReLU networks.
AI.conclusion: Shows mildly overparameterized two-layer ReLU networks can converge to interpolating solutions with complexity on the same order as the optimal interpolator; introduces a rigorous incremental-learning framework and highlights the role of initialization scale and data orthogonality in shaping the optimization dynamics.

title: When Eyes Betray AI: Social Gaze Consistency as a Semantic Cue for AI-Generated Image Detection
summary: Recent generative models have largely closed the gap on low-level artifacts - pixel fingerprints, frequency anomalies, upsampling traces - particularly in person-centric and partial-edit settings where the manipulated region is small and surrounded by photometrically authentic content. We introduce Social Gaze Consistency, a high-level semantic cue defined as the mutual coherence of gaze direction, head-eye alignment, and pupil placement between interacting individuals, and show that it constitutes a previously underutilized detection axis orthogonal to existing low-level paradigms. We instantiate this insight through three coupled mechanisms: (i) a controlled diagnostic dataset with region-specific perturbations of gaze-consistent imagery, where strict pair-level grouping forecloses generator-fingerprint memorization as an optimization-time shortcut rather than relying on augmentation; (ii) Block-Compositional Caption Supervision, which holds a single 5-block reasoning skeleton invariant across 1,250 macro-combined captions, decoupling reasoning consistency from surface diversity; (iii) Cross-architecture validation showing the same supervision improves a vision-language backbone (FakeVLM) by +3.7 pp on the COCOAI Interaction subset (balanced accuracy 67.8 -> 71.5) and +1.3 pp on the COCOAI Person subset (83.0 -> 84.3), with consistent gains on a vision-only backbone (Effort), evidencing a backbone-agnostic cue. Real- and fake-class recalls rise simultaneously, ruling out a "predict-all-fake" artifact. A four-step mechanistic account - paired-edit shortcut blocking, hard-to-easy difficulty transfer, CLIP prior preservation, and diffusion-family shared spectral weakness in periocular structure - explains why training on a single inpainter (FLUX.1-Fill) transfers to multi-generator suites. We will release the code upon acceptance to facilitate reproducibility.

AI.tldr: Summary generation failed
AI.motivation: Motivation analysis unavailable
AI.method: Method extraction failed
AI.result: Result analysis unavailable
AI.conclusion: Conclusion extraction failed

title: High-Quality Synthetic Financial Time-Series using a GAN-Diffusion Framework
