summary: In recent years, financial institutions and firms have increasingly adopted synthetic data to address data scarcity and to generate counterfactual market scenarios. However, reproducing all the statistical properties of financial time series, commonly known as stylized facts, remains an open challenge for many existing general-purpose architectures. In this paper, we present a quality-aware generative framework that combines two classes of generative methods, demonstrating how their integration addresses existing limitations while enhancing the realism of synthetic data. Specifically, we first introduce CoMeTS-GAN (Correlated Multivariate Time Series GAN), a Conditional Generative Adversarial Network (C-GAN) designed to jointly generate mid-price and volume time-series for correlated stocks. We then show how our GAN architecture can be incorporated into state-of-the-art diffusion models to enhance the quality of generated correlation structures. Specifically, the GAN's Critic serves as a quality evaluation module that guides the diffusion process, enforcing learned correlation structures in the generated time-series. Our framework offers a lightweight and responsive solution for realistic stock market simulation, explicitly modeling inter-asset correlation structures. We experimentally validate our framework against leading generative architectures, showing that it more effectively captures the stylized facts of stock markets and models inter-asset correlations.

AI.tldr: A quality-aware hybrid generative framework combining CoMeTS-GAN and diffusion models to simulate multivariate stock time series with accurate inter-asset correlations and stylized facts, outperforming existing generative architectures.
AI.motivation: Address the scarcity and unavailability of realistic financial time-series data and the difficulty of reproducing stylized facts. Leverage GANs for correlation-aware generation and diffusion models for refined distributional quality.
AI.method: 1) Develop CoMeTS-GAN (Conditional GAN) to jointly generate mid-price and volume time-series for correlated stocks. 2) Integrate the GAN with diffusion models by using the GAN critic as a quality-evaluator that guides diffusion to enforce learned correlation structures.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation shows the proposed framework more effectively captures stylized facts and inter-asset correlations than leading generative architectures.
AI.conclusion: The proposed quality-aware hybrid framework provides a lightweight, realistic pipeline for stock market simulation and counterfactual analysis, with enhanced inter-asset correlation modeling.

title: Feedforward 3D Editing Learns from Semantic-Part Transformation
summary: 3D editing is a fundamental capability for scalable 3D content creation. While image editing has rapidly evolved toward large-scale feedforward generative paradigms, 3D AI generation remains dominated by training-free editing pipelines. A central challenge of feedforward 3D editing lies in the lack of high-quality paired supervision. Editable 3D assets require simultaneous preservation of geometry, multi-view consistency, structural coherence, and localized edit controllability. Existing 3D editing datasets often rely on independently generated assets, image-mediated reconstruction or narrow edit taxonomies, leading to inaccurate localization, weak preservation, blurred edit boundaries, and limited semantic consistency. In this work, we introduce a new perspective: scalable feedforward 3D editing should be learned from semantic-part transformations. Based on this insight, we propose Pxform, a high-quality 3D editing dataset with over 100K consistent before/after editing pairs across seven edit types. Instead of treating objects as unstructured shapes, our pipeline grounds edits directly in semantic 3D parts. Built upon Pxform, we further propose PartFlow, a feedforward 3D editing network that injects source-aware latent control into pretrained 3D generative priors. PartFlow introduces mask-aware velocity preservation and render-space consistency supervision to jointly improve edit fidelity and source preservation, while requiring no 3D edit mask during inference. Extensive experiments demonstrate that high-quality semantic-part supervision substantially improves scalable 3D editing, enabling PartFlow to achieve state-of-the-art performance on both geometric and appearance editing benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Pxform introduces a high-quality 3D editing dataset built on semantic 3D parts (100k before/after pairs across 7 edit types) and PartFlow, a feedforward 3D editing network that injects source-aware latent control into pretrained 3D priors. It uses mask-aware velocity preservation and render-space consistency supervision, requiring no 3D edit mask at inference, to achieve state-of-the-art results in geometric and appearance editing while preserving source geometry and multi-view consistency.
AI.motivation: Current 3D editing lacks high-quality paired supervision and semantically grounded control. Existing datasets rely on unstructured shapes, image-mediated reconstructions, or narrow edit taxonomies, causing poor localization, weak preservation, blurred boundaries, and weak semantic consistency. A semantic-part transformation paradigm aims to provide precise, scalable, and consistent editing for 3D assets.
AI.method: Construct Pxform, a dataset with >100k consistent before/after editing pairs across seven edit types, grounded in semantic 3D parts. Develop PartFlow, a feedforward 3D editing network that injects source-aware latent control into pretrained 3D generative priors, with mask-aware velocity preservation and render-space consistency supervision. Importantly, no 3D edit mask is required during inference.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show that semantic-part supervision substantially improves scalable 3D editing. PartFlow achieves state-of-the-art performance on both geometric and appearance editing benchmarks, validating the effectiveness of semantic-part-based supervision and the proposed training losses.
AI.conclusion: Semantic-part supervision is a powerful paradigm for scalable 3D editing, enabling high-fidelity, consistent edits with strong source preservation. The Pxform dataset and PartFlow model provide a practical framework for controllable, feedforward 3D editing without per-sample 3D edit masks at inference.

title: DEI: Diversity in Evolutionary Inference for Quality-Diversity Search
comment: Accepted to ICML 2026 Workshop Scalable Learning and Optimization for Efficient Multimodal AI Agents (SCALE)
summary: We present DEI: Diversity in Evolutionary Inference, a distributed Quality-Diversity (QD) search framework that assigns heterogeneous large language models (LLMs) as mutation operators across peer nodes communicating with non-blocking collective operations. Unlike homogeneous parallel search, which replicates a single model's inductive biases across all workers, DEI treats each LLM's distinct creative prior as a complementary source of behavioral novelty. Extending the Digital Red Queen framework with DEI, nodes share local optimal solutions at the end of each round to seed the next round's population. This creates cross-model adversarial pressure that drives robustness beyond intra-model self-play. Evaluated on the Core War domain, a competitive programming benchmark in which Redcode warrior programs battle inside a simulated machine, a four-node heterogeneous ensemble (GPT-5.4-mini, Claude Sonnet 4.6, GPT-5.2, and Claude Haiku 4.5) achieves 124 percent higher merged-archive QD-Score (45.90 vs. 20.46) and 28 percent higher coverage (80.6 percent vs. 63.0 percent of cells) than a single-node baseline at equal total LLM-call budget. The heterogeneous ensemble also outperforms an equally-budgeted homogeneous ensemble on QD-Score, coverage, and held-out solution generality across all four model families. These results provide the first empirical evidence that model diversity, not merely parallelism, is the key driver of gain in distributed LLM-based QD search.

AI.tldr: DEI is a distributed QD search using heterogeneous LLMs as mutation operators to boost diversity and robustness in Evolutionary Inference; outperforms homogeneous baselines on Core War tasks.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of homogeneous parallel search that replicate a single model's biases; leverage diverse LLM priors to enhance novelty and generalization; test within Digital Red Queen framework with cross-model pressure.
AI.method: Four-node distributed framework DEI; hetero LLM ensemble: GPT-5.4-mini, Claude Sonnet 4.6, GPT-5.2, Claude Haiku 4.5; non-blocking collective ops; exchange local optima end of rounds; evaluate on Core War; measure merged-archive QD-Score and coverage; compare to single-node baseline and equally-budgeted homogeneous ensemble.
AI.result: 4-node heterogeneous ensemble achieves 124% higher merged-archive QD-Score (45.90 vs 20.46) and 28% higher coverage (80.6% vs 63.0%); outperforms homogeneous ensemble on QD-Score, coverage, and held-out solution generality across all four model families.
AI.conclusion: Model diversity, not mere parallelism, drives gains in distributed LLM-based QD search; DEI extends Digital Red Queen with cross-model adversarial pressure improving robustness and generality.

title: Deep-layer limit and stability analysis of the basic forward-backward-splitting induced network (II): learning problems
summary: Deep unfolding neural networks derived from iterative optimization schemes and numerical ordinary/partial differential equations (ODEs/PDEs) have attracted much attention in data science over the last decade. Therein, numerous important network architectures were constructed from the basic forward-backward-splitting (FBS) algorithm. In this paper, we continue our research on the most basic FBS-induced network, an architecture unrolled from the original FBS algorithm by incorporating direct parameter relaxations. Following the difference/differential inclusion formulations in our previous forward system analyses, we here consider some theoretical aspects of corresponding learning problems. Under some mild assumptions, we establish a general convergence property of the training problem of the basic FBS-induced network to the learning problem of the deep-layer limit system, implying a $Γ$-convergence argument showing that any cluster point of the optimal learning parameters for the network is a solution to the learning problem of the deep-layer limit system. A qualitative analysis of perturbation stabilities of these learning problems is also presented. A simple numerical experiment is conducted to validate our main general convergence result.

AI.tldr: The paper proves Γ-convergence of training a basic FBS-unfolded network with direct parameter relaxations to the learning problem of its deep-layer limit, analyzes perturbation stability, and confirms the theory with a simple numerical experiment.
AI.motivation: Provide theoretical guarantees for forward-backward splitting (FBS)–based deep unfolded networks and their limiting deep-layer models, ensuring that finite-depth training behavior aligns with the limit system.
AI.method: Under mild assumptions, use difference/differential inclusion formulations from prior work to establish a general convergence property: the training problem for the FBS-induced network Γ-converges to the learning problem of the deep-layer limit system; perform qualitative perturbation stability analysis; validate with a simple numerical experiment.
AI.result: Any cluster point of the optimal learning parameters for the finite-depth FBS-induced network solves the learning problem of the deep-layer limit system; Γ-convergence framework explains convergence of the training problems; perturbation stability analyzed; numerical experiment corroborates the main result.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a theoretical foundation for FBS-induced deep unfolded networks, linking finite-depth training with the infinite-depth limit and demonstrating robustness to perturbations, supported by a basic numerical validation.

title: SpatialBench: Is Your Spatial Foundation Model an All-Round Player?
summary: While spatial foundation models have demonstrated impressive performance on standard datasets, a critical question remains: are they truly all-round players capable of generalizing robustly across diverse downstream tasks, arbitrary viewpoints, shifting scene domains, varying input densities, and specific hardware constraints? Answering this overarching question requires a holistic assessment, yet current models are mainly evaluated on specific domains for which they were specifically designed or trained. Such evaluations are intrinsically limited by narrow paradigm coverage, limited scene domains, and arbitrary frame sampling, making it fundamentally difficult to assess their true generalization capabilities. To address this gap, we present SpatialBench, a cross-paradigm, domain-diverse benchmark for spatial foundation models with deterministic sampling. SpatialBench features unprecedented scale and rigorous deterministic design, comprising 19 datasets and 546 scenes across 5 diverse spatial domains. It comprehensively evaluates 41 models across 6 paradigms on 5 task suites under 4 different input density settings. Our extensive evaluation reveals that current models are not yet all-round players, and uncovers crucial insights for future advancement. Specifically, we demonstrate that full-context attention maximizes accuracy while bounded-memory strategies unlock long-sequence scalability. Moreover, our empirical evaluations in challenging embodied and egocentric tasks demonstrate that strict domain alignment and high data quality are far more critical to performance than simple dataset scaling. Furthermore, to address the largest data gap identified in our analysis, we go beyond evaluation by introducing a large-scale dataset, DA-Next-5M, and a strong baseline model, DA-Next, pushing the boundaries of spatial representation learning.

AI.tldr: Introduces SpatialBench, a cross-paradigm, domain-diverse benchmark for spatial foundation models, with deterministic sampling across 19 datasets (546 scenes) in 5 spatial domains; evaluates 41 models across 6 paradigms on 5 task suites with 4 input densities; reveals current models are not all-round players and offers design insights (full-context attention, bounded-memory for long sequences); emphasizes data quality and domain alignment over mere dataset scale; introduces DA-Next-5M and a strong baseline DA-Next to push spatial representation learning.
AI.motivation: Current benchmarks for spatial foundation models cover narrow domains, hindering assessment of generalization across tasks, viewpoints, scene domains, input densities, and hardware constraints. A holistic, cross-paradigm benchmark is needed to reveal true generalization capabilities and guide future progress.
AI.method: Develop SpatialBench with deterministic sampling, spanning 19 datasets and 546 scenes across 5 spatial domains. Evaluate 41 models across 6 paradigms on 5 task suites under 4 input density settings. Benchmark design emphasizes cross-paradigm coverage and domain diversity to robustly test generalization.
AI.result: Findings indicate models are not yet all-round players. Key insights: full-context attention maximizes accuracy, while bounded-memory strategies enable long-sequence scalability. In challenging embodied and egocentric tasks, strict domain alignment and high data quality trump mere dataset scale. To address data gaps, introduce a large DA-Next-5M dataset and a strong baseline DA-Next, advancing spatial representation learning.
AI.conclusion: SpatialBench exposes generalization gaps in current models and provides a large-scale, deterministic evaluation framework. The accompanying DA-Next-5M dataset and DA-Next baseline offer concrete avenues to improve spatial representation learning and guide future research.

title: The Role of Causal Features in Strategic Classification for Robustness and Alignment
summary: In strategic classification, an institution (e.g., a bank) anticipates adaptation from users who change their features to increase utility in a classification task (e.g., loan repayment). Since a key challenge is the distribution shift induced by users, we turn to causal models, which have been shown to bound the worst-case out-of-distribution (OOD) risk, and establish several new results that link causality and strategic classification. First, we show that causal classification leads to optimal classification error after any sufficiently large adaptation, when the noise is bounded in a certain way. Second, when these assumptions do not hold, we show OOD cross-entropy risk of optimal classifiers decomposes into an OOD bias term and a term arising from not using all observable features, allowing us to understand when causal classifiers have an advantage. Finally, we show that the use of causal features can allow alignment of long-term incentives between institutions and users, contrasting with previous work that highlights social costs of such approaches. We validate our theory empirically on synthetic data, finding that our results predict behavior in practice.

AI.tldr: Causal classification can mitigate distribution shift from strategic user adaptation, yielding optimal error under certain bounded-noise conditions; when assumptions fail, OOD risk splits into bias and feature-availability components, clarifying when causal methods help; also shows causal features can align long-term incentives, with synthetic-data validation.
AI.motivation: Strategic classification faces distribution shift as users adapt features to boost utility. Causal models may bound worst-case OOD risk and offer principled advantages over purely predictive methods.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis under bounded-noise assumptions to connect causality with optimal error after large adaptation. When assumptions fail, decompose OOD cross-entropy into bias and missing-feature terms. Conceptual discussion of incentive alignment. Empirical validation on synthetic data.
AI.result: 1) With sufficiently large adaptation and bounded noise, causal classification attains optimal classification error. 2) Without these assumptions, optimal OOD risk decomposes into an OOD bias and a term from not using all observable features, clarifying when causal classifiers help. 3) Using causal features can align long-term incentives between institutions and users. Synthetic-data experiments corroborate the theory.
AI.conclusion: Causality provides a principled framework for strategic classification, offering conditions under which causal classifiers are advantageous and demonstrating potential incentive alignment, supported by synthetic experiments.

title: G3T Up! Gravity Aligned Coordinate Frames Simplify Pointmap Processing
summary: Modern feed-forward 3D reconstruction methods like VGGT predict pixel-aligned pointmaps in camera-centric coordinate frames. However, this choice of coordinate frame is not always optimal. We propose instead to predict pointmaps in upright, gravity-aligned frames that exploit strong structural cues present in many real-world scenes. Unlike camera-centric frames, gravity-aligned frames share a common vertical axis across viewpoints, reducing the rotational degrees of freedom needed to relate pointmaps to one another. To this end, we introduce the Gravity Grounded Geometry Transformer (G3T), fine-tuned from existing models on gravity-aligned 3D data. G3T produces highly accurate gravity-aware predictions, including upright pointmaps and camera-to-gravity poses. We further introduce G3T-Long, a submap-based incremental 3D reconstruction pipeline that leverages the reduced rotational degrees of freedom afforded by upright frames to achieve significantly improved reconstruction accuracy.

AI.tldr: Gravity-aligned 3D pointmaps learned by G3T improve multi-view reconstruction by reducing rotational DOF and exploiting gravity cues; G3T-Long further enhances long-horizon, submap-based reconstruction accuracy.
AI.motivation: Camera-centric pointmaps suffer from view-dependent rotations; gravity-aligned frames offer a common vertical axis across viewpoints, reducing rotational ambiguity and enabling stronger structural priors in real-world scenes.
AI.method: Introduce Gravity Grounded Geometry Transformer (G3T), a transformer-based model fine-tuned on gravity-aligned 3D data to predict upright gravity-aware pointmaps and camera-to-gravity poses; accompany with G3T-Long, a submap-based incremental reconstruction pipeline that leverages the reduced rotational degrees of freedom.
AI.result: G3T produces highly accurate gravity-aware predictions (upright pointmaps and camera-to-gravity poses); G3T-Long yields significantly improved reconstruction accuracy in incremental, submap-based reconstruction.
AI.conclusion: Gravity-grounded frames with a transformer-based predictor effectively exploit gravity cues to reduce rotational complexity and improve 3D reconstruction; the G3T family provides a practical framework for gravity-consistent pointmaps and poses, with notable gains in incremental mapping.

title: Nonlinear Data Integration via Kernel Methods for Data Collaboration Analysis
summary: Collaborative analysis of decentralized confidential datasets is important, but direct sharing of original datasets is often restricted by privacy and institutional constraints. Data collaboration (DC) analysis transforms each dataset into privacy-preserving intermediate representations via party-specific obfuscation functions and integrates them into common collaboration representations using an anchor dataset. However, many existing DC analysis methods rely on linear transformations for data obfuscation and integration, which may increase reconstruction risk. Although nonlinear dimensionality reduction can mitigate this risk, conventional linear integration methods cannot accurately align intermediate representations produced by nonlinear transformations. Moreover, existing integration methods mainly minimize discrepancies among parties and do not explicitly incorporate geometric or target-variable information useful for downstream analysis. To overcome these limitations, we first formulate linear kernel integration (LKI) as a linear integration method and then kernelize it to obtain nonlinear kernel integration (NKI). NKI admits a globally optimal solution via kernel ridge regression and an eigenvalue problem. We also introduce graph regularization and a centering constraint so that the target representation can capture geometric and target-variable information useful for downstream analysis. Experiments on image classification tasks demonstrate that NKI improves classification accuracy over existing linear integration methods under nonlinear dimensionality reduction, with further gains from target-variable-aware graph regularization and centering. The results also show that dimensionality reduction choices substantially affect both classification accuracy and reconstruction risk.

AI.tldr: Kernelized, geometry- and target-aware integration (NKI) for decentralized, privacy-preserving data under nonlinear reductions, improving over linear methods with optimal kernel ridge solution and graph regularization.
AI.motivation: Existing DC analyses rely on linear obfuscation/integration and do not align nonlinear intermediate representations; nonlinear DR reduces reconstruction risk but requires compatible, geometry-aware integration that leverages target information.
AI.method: Introduce linear kernel integration (LKI) and its kernelization to nonlinear kernel integration (NKI); derive globally optimal solution via kernel ridge regression and an eigenvalue problem; add graph regularization and centering constraints to encode geometric and target-variable information.
AI.result: NKI improves classification accuracy over linear integration under nonlinear DR; gains amplified by target-variable-aware graph regularization and centering; dimensionality reduction choices significantly affect accuracy and reconstruction risk.
AI.conclusion: Kernelized, geometry-, and target-aware integration can better align nonlinear intermediate representations, reduce reconstruction risk, and enhance downstream analysis; careful DR choice and regularization are crucial for effective data collaboration.

title: Explainable Comparison of Feature-Based and Deep Learning Models for TROPOMI Methane Plume Screening
summary: Continuous and global detection of large methane emissions is a crucial step for global warming mitigation. Satellite observations, such as from S5P/TROPOMI, combined with plume detection algorithms, can play a key role in this effort. However, not all TROPOMI plume detections that look like methane emission plumes are the result of actual emissions. A significant part of the plume-like features in the data are retrieval artifacts. Such artifacts could be the result of variations in elevation or albedo gradients, high concentrations of aerosols, coastal lines, water bodies, etc. Previous work approached the problem of plume-artifact classification by means of a Support Vector Machine Classifier (SVC), trained on an extensive set of observation-based scalar features designed by domain experts. However, such an approach limits the information scope received by the algorithm to what is deemed to be important by the experts, breaks the spatial relationship between pixels, and loses information during the process of statistical aggregation. In this study, we compare feature-based (SVC, Random Forest, XGBoost) and image-based (ResNet-18, ResNet-34) models for methane plume-artifact classification under balanced and imbalanced evaluation settings. To interpret the results, we apply SHAP-based explainability to both model families. Our findings provide practical guidance for model selection in operational methane-screening workflows such as the CAMS Methane Hotspot Explorer.

AI.tldr: A comparative study evaluates feature-based vs image-based ML models for classifying TROPOMI methane plume-like detections as real emissions or artifacts, under balanced/imbalanced settings, with SHAP explanations, to guide operational model choice (e.g., CAMS Methane Hotspot Explorer).
AI.motivation: Continuous global methane emission monitoring requires reliable plume-artifact discrimination. Many TROPOMI plume detections are artifacts caused by elevation, albedo gradients, aerosols, coastal features, etc. Prior expert-feature SVC approaches limit information and ignore spatial context. The study seeks a broader, image-preserving, and interpretable alternative.
AI.method: A head-to-head comparison of feature-based models (SVC, Random Forest, XGBoost) and image-based CNNs (ResNet-18, ResNet-34) for methane plume-artifact classification. Evaluations are performed under balanced and imbalanced class distributions. SHAP-based explainability is applied to both model families to interpret predictions and feature attributions.
AI.result: The study demonstrates trade-offs between model families. Image-based CNNs (ResNet variants) effectively leverage spatial information and show robustness under data imbalance, while feature-based models with SHAP offer clear, domain-aligned explanations. Across settings, the work provides practical guidance on model choice for operational methane-screening workflows (e.g., CAMS Methane Hotspot Explorer).
AI.conclusion: Both model families have value for plume-artifact discrimination. Image-based models add spatial-contextual strength; feature-based models offer interpretability via SHAP. A practical deployment would benefit from leveraging both approaches (e.g., ensemble or hybrid features) and focusing on handling class imbalance and diverse artifact types. Future work could explore integrating airway-aware spatial features and scalable explanations for operational use.

title: Symbolic Regression via Latent Iterative Refinement
summary: Symbolic regression (SR) seeks closed-form mathematical expressions that fit observed data. Neural SR methods amortize the search by training an encoder to map observations directly to expressions in a single pass, but this amortized inference leaves a residual amortization gap between its one-shot prediction and the true posterior. We propose Latent Equation Embedding (LEE), a framework that closes this gap through iterative amortized inference in a functionally grounded latent space. LEE learns a shared latent space Z equipped with three components: an encoder f_theta that jointly embeds symbolic tokens and numerical observations into a single latent vector z; an expression decoder g_expr that reconstructs formulas from z; and an evaluation decoder g_eval that predicts function values from z, explicitly grounding the latent space in functional behavior. At inference, LEE performs iterative refinement by re-encoding decoded expressions jointly with observations, progressively improving the latent estimate. LEE uses the encoder itself as a learned inference optimizer: each re-encoding step implicitly computes the mismatch between the candidate and the data. Because g_eval is differentiable in z, we additionally interleave continuous gradient descent with discrete re-encoding, yielding a hybrid iterative and gradient refinement procedure. On SRBench across three noise levels, against 19 baselines spanning genetic programming, symbolic-neural hybrids, and pre-trained Transformers, LEE produces expressions 2--10x simpler than the strongest accuracy-oriented baselines, including Operon, GP-GOMEA, TPSR, RAG-SR, and GenSR, with complexity 8--11 versus 20--90. These results advance the low-complexity region of the accuracy-complexity Pareto frontier and show graceful degradation as noise increases.

AI.tldr: Latent Equation Embedding (LEE) introduces a functionally grounded latent space for neural symbolic regression. It uses an encoder to embed tokens and observations into a latent vector, a decoder to reconstruct expressions, and an evaluation decoder to predict function values. Through iterative amortized inference—re-encoding expressions with data—and hybrid gradient refinement on the latent code, LEE achieves substantially simpler expressions while maintaining accuracy, outperforming 19 baselines on SRBench across noise levels.
AI.motivation: To close the amortization gap of one-shot neural symbolic regression methods by grounding the latent space in functional behavior and enabling iterative refinement that aligns predictions with data.
AI.method: LEE defines a shared latent space Z with three components: an encoder f_theta that jointly embeds symbolic tokens and numerical observations into z; an expression decoder g_expr that reconstructs formulas from z; and an evaluation decoder g_eval that predicts function values from z. At inference, LEE iteratively refines z via re-encoding decoded expressions with observations and combining this with differentiable g_eval to perform gradient descent, yielding a hybrid iterative/discrete optimization.
AI.result: On SRBench across three noise levels and 19 baselines (including Operon, GP-GOMEA, TPSR, RAG-SR, GenSR), LEE yields expressions 2–10x simpler than the strongest accuracy-oriented baselines, with complexity around 8–11 (vs 20–90 for competitors). It shows graceful degradation with increasing noise and advances the low-complexity region of the accuracy-complexity Pareto frontier.
AI.conclusion: LEE effectively closes the amortization gap in neural symbolic regression by grounding the latent space in functional behavior and employing iterative, hybrid optimization. It achieves significantly simpler expressions with competitive accuracy across noisy data, pushing the Pareto frontier toward lower complexity.

title: LUCoS: Latent Unsupervised Context Selection for Tabular Foundation Models
comment: Comments: 18 pages, 4 figures, supplementary appendices included
summary: Selecting which instances to label is a key challenge in low-label tabular learning. For recent Tabular Foundation Models such as TabPFN, context selection directly determines predictive performance. Supervised oracle experiments show that carefully chosen labeled context sets can strongly outperform random selection under the same labeling budget. However, the cold-start setting, where instances must be selected before any labels are available, has received little attention in the TFM literature. This problem is fundamentally geometric. In vision and language, foundation models induce embedding spaces where simple geometric selection methods are effective. In contrast, tabular instance selection has so far been performed predominantly in the original tabular space, which lacks a natural metric; heterogeneous types, mixed scales, and nonlinear interactions make raw-space distances unreliable for context construction, and original-space selection falls below random on the majority of datasets as the budget grows. We propose LUCoS (Latent Unsupervised Context Selection), which replaces raw-feature geometry with the latent geometry induced by embeddings from an unsupervised Prior-Fitted Network (PFN) and selects representative medoids as context. Evaluated on 67 OpenML-CC18 datasets across six low-label budgets, LUCoS ranks first under mean AUC, ACC, and F1, with conclusions stable across metrics and dataset-level robustness checks. A gain decomposition reveals a simple mechanism: at the smallest budgets, the main benefit comes from enforcing coverage; as the budget increases, the decisive factor becomes the representation space in which coverage is measured. LUCoS mitigates failures of original feature space selection, showing that reliable unsupervised context selection depends less on selector sophistication than on defining representativeness in a meaningful representation geometry.

AI.tldr: LUCoS uses latent embeddings from an unsupervised Prior-Fitted Network (PFN) to perform medoid-based context selection for low-label tabular learning. It outperforms raw-space and random selections across 67 OpenML-CC18 datasets and multiple budgets, with a budget-dependent mechanism: early budgets benefit from coverage, later budgets benefit from representation geometry.
AI.motivation: Address the cold-start context selection problem in Tabular Foundation Models. Raw feature-space distances are unreliable due to heterogeneous types and nonlinear interactions. A meaningful latent geometry is needed to select representative unlabeled instances under a fixed labeling budget.
AI.method: Learn latent embeddings with an unsupervised PFN to obtain a representation geometry. Select representative medoids in the latent space as the context for labeling under varying budgets (six low-label budgets). Evaluate on 67 OpenML-CC18 datasets, comparing against baselines in original space and random selection.
AI.result: LUCoS ranks first in mean AUC, ACC, and F1 across metrics and datasets, with robust results and stable conclusions. A gain decomposition shows small budgets gain primarily from enforcing coverage, while larger budgets are driven by the quality of the latent representation space. LUCoS mitigates failures of original-space selection, highlighting that reliable unsupervised context selection hinges on meaningful latent representativeness rather than selector sophistication.
AI.conclusion: Defining representativeness in a latent geometry enables effective context selection for low-label tabular learning. Unsupervised latent embeddings provide a robust foundation for medoid-based context selection, with strong, dataset-wide performance and clear budget-dependent behavior.

title: Kan Extension Transformers: A Categorical Unification of Attention, Diffusion, and Predict-Detach Self-Conditioning
summary: We propose Kan Extension Transformers (KETs) as a unifying categorical framework for a diverse group of Transformer implementations. The core claim is that a Transformer layer can be viewed as a weighted structured extension operator: standard attention is the singleton-neighborhood case, Geometric Transformer style incidence mixing is a sparse edge-restricted case, and KET is the higher-order simplicial case. This lens also clarifies a bridge to diffusion-style completion. When the extension operator acts on detached predictive carriers instead of teacher-forced hidden states, it becomes a valid self-conditioning mechanism that exposes noncausal structure without leaking gold future tokens. We include a comprehensive experimental validation of 12 different Transformer implementations varying across strict-causal and predict-detach regimes on Penn Treebank, WikiText-2, and WikiText-103. In the strict-causal setting, quadratic KET is the strongest model among the compared causal architectures on WikiText-2 and WikiText-103. Across all datasets, however, the largest gains come from the predict-detach regime rather than from changing the neighborhood family alone.

AI.tldr: Kan Extension Transformers (KETs) unify Transformer variants under a single categorical framework by viewing Transformer layers as weighted structured extension operators: standard attention as singleton-neighborhood, geometric incidence mixing as sparse edge-restricted, and KET as higher-order simplicial extensions; introduces predict-detach self-conditioning to expose noncausal structure without leaking future tokens; empirically validated across 12 variants on PTB, WikiText-2, WikiText-103; strict-causal quadratic KET leads among causal models on WT2/WT103; most gains arise from predict-detach rather than neighborhood changes.
AI.motivation: Provide a unifying theoretical lens to reconcile diverse Transformer architectures, clarify connections to diffusion-like completion, and enable self-conditioning mechanisms without data leakage.
AI.method: Formalize Kan extensions as the underlying extension operator for Transformer layers. Map standard attention to a singleton neighborhood, Geometric Transformer-style incidence mixing to a sparse edge-restricted case, and Kan Extension Transformer (KET) to a higher-order simplicial case. Introduce predict-detach: applying the extension operator to detached predictive carriers (instead of teacher-forced hidden states) as a self-conditioning mechanism that reveals noncausal structure without leaking gold tokens. Conduct empirical validation across 12 Transformer variants spanning strict-causal and predict-detach regimes on Penn Treebank, WikiText-2, and WikiText-103.
AI.result: In the strict-causal setting, quadratic KET is the strongest among the compared causal architectures on WikiText-2 and WikiText-103. Across all datasets, the largest gains come from the predict-detach regime rather than changing the neighborhood family alone.
AI.conclusion: KET provides a coherent, higher-order unifying framework for Transformer variants and highlights the practical value of predict-detach self-conditioning. The empirical results support the theoretical claims, with notable gains from detach-enabled self-conditioning across standard language modeling benchmarks.

title: Transfer Learning using 66 Diseases for Disease Forecasting Applications
summary: Disease forecasting models typically rely on a single data stream, making models brittle when histories are short or noisy. Recent top-performing models have shown that synthesizing multiple reporting systems for the same disease improves performance. Other recent work takes this idea a step further, using transfer learning to train a forecasting model for one disease using data from a different disease. We expand upon each of these approaches greatly, training machine learning models on data that span 66 infectious diseases and several data streams. We investigate the value of incorporating different data streams for forecasting 20 different disease data streams. We find that incorporating other data streams improves forecasting in the vast majority (84.9%) of time series and model structures considered. However, our work highlights that the quality of the added data matters, where adding data extremely different from the target data stream can sometimes degrade forecast performance. A major contribution of this work is in compiling a publicly-available database of data for use by the infectious disease forecasting community.

AI.tldr: Multi-disease, multi-stream forecasting with transfer learning improves forecasts for most data streams; data quality matters; public dataset released.
AI.motivation: Forecasting with a single data stream is brittle; leveraging multiple reporting streams and cross-disease transfer can enhance predictive accuracy; the study scales to 66 diseases.
AI.method: Train machine learning models on data across 66 infectious diseases and multiple data streams; evaluate forecast gains by adding streams for 20 target streams; analyze transfer-learning implications and data compatibility; compile a public data repository.
AI.result: Adding other data streams improves forecasting in 84.9% of time series and model structures considered, but very dissimilar data can degrade performance; a large, publicly available dataset is assembled.
AI.conclusion: Multi-stream and cross-disease transfer learning hold broad promise for infectious disease forecasting; data compatibility is crucial; the public dataset will support community research and benchmarking.

title: Causal Risk Minimization for High-Dimensional Treatments
summary: Predicting the effect of interventions with many possible variations, e.g., therapeutic content that affects mental health outcomes or an earnings call transcript that drives movement in share price, is useful across several domains. However, classical causal estimators tend to assume that all possible interventions are observed, which is infeasible when interventions vary widely, for instance, in the space of all text strings. We adapt a well-known approach of recasting causal inference as a learning problem, to address high-dimensional treatment spaces. Specifically, under standard assumptions like no unobserved confounding, we show that causal error decomposes into a series of moment-balancing errors of increasing order, and design objectives that directly improve causal estimation. We also show how to project the effect of a high-dimensional treatment onto lower-dimensional treatment attributes, which allows a single model to answer several causal questions without additional attribute-specific training. We empirically evaluate our estimators in settings with high-dimensional continuous, discrete, and text treatments, the last of which used a semi-synthetic dataset of Amazon Reviews. Our experiments demonstrate the benefit of higher-order balance error optimization and competitive performance of projected causal estimates with attribute-specific estimators.

AI.tldr: Recasts causal inference for high-dimensional or unstructured treatments (including text) as a learning problem, using higher-order balance to reduce bias and projecting effects onto lower-dimensional attributes for multi-question causal inference.
AI.motivation: Classical causal estimators struggle when interventions are numerous or unstructured (e.g., text strings). The work reframes causal estimation as a learning task to enable scalable, accurate estimation in high-dimensional treatment spaces.
AI.method: Under standard no-unobserved-confounding assumptions, causal error is decomposed into a sequence of higher-order moment-balancing errors. The authors design objective functions to minimize these errors and introduce a projection of high-dimensional treatments onto lower-dimensional attributes, enabling a single model to address multiple causal questions without attribute-specific training.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across continuous, discrete, and text treatments (including a semi-synthetic Amazon Reviews dataset) shows that higher-order balance optimization improves causal estimation and that projected causal estimates are competitive with attribute-specific estimators.
AI.conclusion: Higher-order balance-based learning enables effective causal inference in high-dimensional treatment spaces, with projection allowing a single model to answer multiple causal questions.

title: Falcon-X: A Time Series Foundation Model for Heterogeneous Multivariate Modeling
summary: Time series foundation models (TSFMs) are transforming the forecasting paradigm through large-scale cross-domain pretraining. However, most existing TSFMs remain univariate, and recent efforts to enable cross-variate modeling still operate directly within the raw variate space. This design introduces fundamental limitations in semantic alignment and relational expressivity. Specifically, raw-space group mixing lacks a dedicated mechanism to align heterogeneous physical quantities, while standard non-negative attention fails to capture the complex synergistic and antagonistic interactions ubiquitous in real-world systems. To address these challenges, we propose Falcon-X, decouples variates from the raw space and maps them into a unified latent prototype space. Falcon-X employs a Unified Prototype Diff-Attention mechanism that explicitly evaluates both positive and negative semantic affinities to explicitly align heterogeneous variates. Cross-variate interactions are then efficiently performed within this shared space via Latent Entity Attention, naturally facilitating zero-shot structural transfer. Finally, a Variate Reassembly Router robustly reconstructs variate-specific trajectories via a request-and-dispatch mechanism. Extensive evaluations on the GIFT-Eval and fev-bench benchmarks demonstrate that Falcon-X achieves state-of-the-art forecasting performance, offering a principled and scalable paradigm for complex multivariate environments. Falcon-X is publicly released to support future research.

AI.tldr: Introduces Falcon-X, a cross-variate time-series foundation model that maps variates into a latent prototype space with Unified Prototype Diff-Attention, Latent Entity Attention, and a Variate Reassembly Router, achieving state-of-the-art forecasting on GIFT-Eval and fev-bench.
AI.motivation: Current TSFMs are largely univariate or operate in raw variate space, which impedes semantic alignment across heterogeneous quantities and fails to capture complex positive/negative interactions; a scalable cross-variate framework with latent representations and explicit affinity modeling is needed.
AI.method: Decouple variates from raw space and project them into a unified latent prototype space. Use Unified Prototype Diff-Attention to explicitly model both positive and negative affinities between variates. Perform cross-variate interactions in latent space via Latent Entity Attention. Reconstruct variate-specific trajectories with a Variate Reassembly Router using a request-and-dispatch mechanism. Enables zero-shot structural transfer.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations on GIFT-Eval and fev-bench show Falcon-X achieves state-of-the-art forecasting performance. The approach is scalable and supports cross-domain transfer; the authors publicize the implementation.
AI.conclusion: Falcon-X provides a principled, scalable framework for complex multivariate time-series forecasting by leveraging latent prototypes and explicit affinity mechanisms, enabling effective cross-variate reasoning and zero-shot transfer.

title: Detectability in Diversity: Improved Canary Crafting for Privacy Auditing in One Run
summary: Privacy auditing aims to empirically assess privacy leakage in machine learning models using membership inference attacks (MIAs), and to derive lower bounds on differential privacy (DP) parameters. Recent one-run auditing methods address the high cost of standard approaches by relying on a single training run with multiple "canary" points whose inclusion or exclusion must be detected by the auditor. In this work, we study the problem of efficiently crafting canaries for one-run privacy auditing. Motivated by recent theoretical insights suggesting that interference between canaries contributes to weaker leakage estimates compared to multi-run methods, we propose to optimize canaries to be both highly detectable and minimally interfering. Our approach combines a greedy initialization based on influence functions with a bilevel optimization procedure that maximizes distinguishability while promoting diversity in embedding space, enabling the use of computationally efficient bilevel algorithms. Experiments show that our method achieves stronger privacy leakage estimates at a lower computational cost than existing canary crafting approaches.

AI.tldr: A method to craft canaries for one-run privacy auditing using greedy-initialization and bilevel optimization to maximize detectability and embedding diversity, achieving stronger leakage estimates at lower cost than prior methods.
AI.motivation: Reduce cost and variance of one-run privacy auditing by designing highly detectable canaries that minimally interfere with each other, enabling tighter lower bounds on DP parameters.
AI.method: Greedy initialization based on influence functions to seed canaries, followed by bilevel optimization that maximizes distinguishability of training-with/without canary while encouraging embedding-space diversity; uses computationally efficient bilevel algorithms.
AI.result: Empirical experiments show stronger privacy leakage estimates at lower computational cost compared to existing canary crafting approaches.
AI.conclusion: Effective canary design via influence-guided greedy seeding and bilevel optimization improves one-run privacy auditing, enabling tighter DP parameter estimates with reduced computation; suggests broader applicability to practical auditing.

title: BASIS: Batchwise Advantage Estimation from Single-Rollout Information Sharing for LLM Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards has become a standard recipe for improving the reasoning abilities of large language models. Existing algorithms face a tradeoff between computational efficiency and sample efficiency in value estimation and policy learning. We introduce BASIS, a critic-free post-training algorithm designed to address this tradeoff. At each online training step, BASIS samples only one rollout per prompt, but leverages rich information across prompts in the entire batch to improve value function estimation. Our experiments demonstrate that BASIS reduces MSE in value function estimation by 69% compared to REINFORCE++, a representative single-rollout baseline, and achieves lower MSE with one rollout than group mean estimators with 8 rollouts. This improvement in value estimation translates to better policy optimization: using substantially less training time, BASIS achieves performance close to multi-rollout GRPO-type baselines and often outperforms single-rollout REINFORCE-type baselines.

AI.tldr: BASIS is a critic-free post-training RL method for LLMs with verifiable rewards that uses one rollout per prompt and batch-wide information to improve value estimation, achieving large MSE reductions and competitive policy performance with reduced training time.
AI.motivation: To overcome the tradeoff between computational efficiency and sample efficiency in value estimation and policy learning for RL with verifiable rewards in large language models.
AI.method: BASIS performs post-training with a critic-free design, sampling one rollout per prompt at each online step, while exploiting information across the entire batch to refine the value function estimates without a critic.
AI.result: BASIS reduces MSE of value function estimation by 69% vs REINFORCE++, achieves lower MSE with a single rollout than group mean estimators with eight rollouts, and delivers policy performance close to multi-rollout baselines while requiring substantially less training time, often surpassing single-rollout baselines.
AI.conclusion: BASIS effectively balances accuracy in value estimation and efficiency in training, enabling competitive RL with verifiable rewards for LLM reasoning without a critic.

title: Normal Guidance is what Attention Needs
summary: We consider training classifiers for 3D medical images using only one binary label for the entire volume rather than a label for each 2D slice. In such weakly supervised settings, can we learn accurate classifiers for slice-level predictions? Attention-based multiple instance learning (MIL) can produce an attention score for every slice. Yet recent work demonstrates that a simple center-focused baseline that ignores image content can outperform attention-based and transformer-based MIL at slice-level classification of 3D brain scans. We show this baseline also outperforms existing MIL at slice-level classification of thoracic and abdominal CT scans. Motivated by this baseline, we propose Normal Guidance, a regularization technique that encourages the learned attention distribution to follow a bell-shaped curve. Across three medical imaging datasets totaling over 4 million 2D slices, we show our Normal Guidance enables attention-based and transformer-based MIL methods to deliver significantly better slice-level localization than the state-of-the-art while remaining competitive at whole-scan classification.

AI.tldr: Weakly supervised 3D medical image classification with only volume labels; center-based baselines can outperform MIL; introduces Normal Guidance to regularize attention into a bell-shaped distribution, improving slice-level localization while preserving whole-volume accuracy.
AI.motivation: Address poor slice-level localization under volume-level supervision and the surprising underperformance of attention-based MIL; aim to align attention with meaningful slices via simple regularization.
AI.method: Empirical comparison of attention-based MIL, transformer-based MIL, and a center-focused baseline; propose Normal Guidance regularization to shape attention as a bell curve; evaluate on three medical imaging datasets totaling over 4 million 2D slices.
AI.result: Normal Guidance improves slice-level localization for MIL methods and yields superior slice-level performance compared to state-of-the-art while maintaining competitive whole-scan classification.
AI.conclusion: A simple regularization strategy (Normal Guidance) effectively enhances interpretability and slice-level detection in weakly supervised 3D medical imaging without sacrificing overall accuracy.

title: Greening AI Inference with Accuracy and Latency-aware User Incentives
summary: The widespread use of AI services has raised concerns for its environmental sustainability, towards which recent studies have identified carbon emissions of AI inference as the major contributor. This paper introduces a framework for designing AI inference incentives based on the users' valuation for inference quality and latency, together with their environmental consciousness, while accounting for the tradeoff between carbon emissions and the two QoE parameters. Our approach can accommodate different tradeoffs, that depend on the size and complexity of the AI models and the allocation of resources to serve inference requests. The incentives can be offered through a practical two-tier service subscription that offers users a discount in exchange for reduced carbon emissions. The discounted service option gives the AI provider the flexibility to serve some percentage of inference requests at a lower quality and higher latency during periods of high carbon intensity.

AI.tldr: Carbon-aware AI inference incentives via a two-tier subscription balancing QoE and emissions
AI.motivation: Address environmental impact of AI inference emissions and provide incentives aligning user preferences with lower-carbon operation.
AI.method: Proposes a framework that integrates users' valuation for inference quality and latency with their environmental consciousness to trade off carbon emissions against QoE. Includes a practical two-tier subscription offering discounts for reduced emissions, enabling some requests to be served at lower quality/higher latency during periods of high carbon intensity; adaptable to model size and resource allocation.
AI.result: A flexible incentive framework that accommodates different tradeoffs between QoE and emissions and provides operational flexibility for providers to implement carbon-aware pricing and service options.
AI.conclusion: Incentive-based, carbon-aware AI inference design can align user demand with environmental goals, preserving QoE where valued while enabling emissions reductions through demand-side adjustments.

title: Probabilistic Smoothing with Ratio-Monotone Transforms for Global Optimization
summary: Probabilistic smoothing is a standard tool for global optimization, but existing methods rely on Gaussian kernels and specific transforms, often resulting in strong hyperparameter sensitivity and limited robustness. We propose a general smoothing framework that combines flexible symmetric unimodal kernels with monotonic ratio-based transformations. Under mild conditions, we show that the smoothed objective preserves the global maximizer and that all stationary points concentrate near the true optimum for sufficiently large amplification, without requiring a decreasing smoothing schedule. We further provide explicit complexity bounds for stochastic gradient ascent and show that a leave-one-out baseline provably reduces variance. Experiments on high-dimensional benchmarks and black-box adversarial attacks demonstrate improved robustness and competitive performance.

AI.tldr: General smoothing framework for global optimization using flexible symmetric unimodal kernels and monotonic ratio-based transformations; preserves global maximizers, concentrates stationary points near the optimum without decreasing smoothing, provides SGD complexity bounds and variance reduction via a leave-one-out baseline; empirical results show robustness and competitive performance on high-dimensional benchmarks and black-box attacks.
AI.motivation: Address hyperparameter sensitivity and robustness limitations of Gaussian-based probabilistic smoothing; extend to a flexible, principled framework with broader kernel choices and transformations.
AI.method: Introduce a smoothing operator combining symmetric unimodal kernels with monotonic ratio-based transformations. Prove that under mild conditions the smoothed objective preserves the global maximizer and that stationary points concentrate near the true optimum as amplification grows. Derive explicit complexity bounds for stochastic gradient ascent and propose a leave-one-out baseline for variance reduction. Include experiments on high-dimensional benchmarks and black-box adversarial attacks.
AI.result: Theoretical guarantees: preservation of maximizers; stationary point concentration for large amplification; complexity bounds for SGD; variance reduction via LOOCV baseline. Empirical results: improved robustness and competitive performance in high-dimensional settings and against black-box adversaries.
AI.conclusion: The framework is general and robust, reducing sensitivity to hyperparameters and not requiring a decreasing smoothing schedule. It offers practical variance reduction and state-of-the-art robustness in tested scenarios, with potential broad applicability to global optimization and black-box problems.

title: From Scores to Gibbs Correctors: Accelerating Uniform-Rate Discrete Diffusion Models
summary: Discrete diffusion models have achieved strong empirical performance in text and other symbolic domains, but, especially for uniform-rate models, they often require many steps to generate a single sample. Existing acceleration methods either rely on training additional quantities or suffer from slow mixing. In this work, we propose a novel Gibbs-based corrector for discrete diffusion models, termed Gibbs-Accelerated Discrete Diffusion (GADD). GADD leverages the structure of the concrete score function to construct Gibbs posterior likelihoods directly, without requiring any additional training beyond standard score estimation. We show that GADD achieves an overall sampling complexity of $\mathcal{O}(\mathrm{polylog} (\varepsilon^{-1}))$, yielding the first such rate for diffusion-based samplers for uniform-rate discrete diffusion models. We also conduct numerical experiments demonstrating the practical advantages of GADD across synthetic data, zero-shot text sampling, and zero-shot conditional music generation. These results corroborate the theory and show that GADD consistently improves sample quality and wall-clock efficiency over standard baselines, including vanilla Euler methods and CTMC correctors. Beyond this, our theoretical analysis introduces a novel framework for analyzing predictor-corrector methods in discrete diffusion models, which may be of independent interest. Unlike existing approaches that rely on the Girsanov change-of-measure technique, our method is based on an induction argument that tracks error propagation across predictor iterations while accounting for inaccuracies in the corrector updates.

AI.tldr: Gibbs-Accelerated Discrete Diffusion (GADD) is a Gibbs-based corrector for discrete diffusion models that uses the concrete score structure to form Gibbs posteriors without extra training. It achieves a polylogarithmic sampling complexity in the inverse error and improves practical performance over baselines, with a new induction-based theoretical framework for predictor–corrector analysis.
AI.motivation: Discrete diffusion models often require many steps to sample, particularly in uniform-rate settings. Existing acceleration methods either need additional training or suffer from slow mixing. There is a need for training-free accelerations with strong theoretical guarantees.
AI.method: Introduce GADD, a Gibbs-based corrector that leverages the concrete score to construct Gibbs posterior likelihoods directly from standard score estimates, avoiding extra training. Develop an induction-based analytical framework for predictor-corrector methods in discrete diffusion, distinct from Girsanov-based approaches.
AI.result: The proposed method achieves O(polylog(ε^{-1})) sampling complexity for uniform-rate discrete diffusion models, representing the first such rate for diffusion-based samplers in this setting. Empirically, GADD improves sample quality and wall-clock efficiency relative to vanilla Euler and CTMC correctors on synthetic data, zero-shot text sampling, and zero-shot conditional music generation.
AI.conclusion: GADD provides a training-free, theory-backed acceleration for discrete diffusion models with strong asymptotic guarantees and practical gains. The paper also contributes a novel predictor–corrector analysis framework based on error propagation via induction, which may be of independent interest.

title: Guiding LLM Post-training Data Engineering with Model Internals from Sparse Autoencoders
summary: Model internals encode rich information about how a large language model (LLM) processes its training data; however, post-training data engineering largely relies on external signals and ignores rich intrinsic signals lying in model internals. We propose SAERL, a data engineering framework for LLM reinforcement learning (RL). It models three intrinsic data properties: diversity, difficulty, and quality, using model internals extracted with Sparse Autoencoder (SAE), an advanced mechanistic interpretability tool. Each property grounds a concrete data engineering operation: SAE-space clustering with moderate batch mixing for batch diversity control, a difficulty proxy for easy-to-hard curriculum ordering, and a quality probe for data filtering. SAERL improves average accuracy by 3.00% over vanilla GRPO and reaches target accuracy with 20% fewer training steps on Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B, with consistent gains across model scales and RL algorithms. Experiments show that SAE transfers effectively across model families and scales, serving as a lightweight and reusable data engineering tool. These results demonstrate that model internals are a powerful and practical source of signals for post-training data engineering.

AI.tldr: SAERL uses Sparse Autoencoders to extract intrinsic signals (diversity, difficulty, quality) from LLM internals to guide RL data engineering, improving sample efficiency and accuracy with cross-model transferability.
AI.motivation: Address the reliance on external signals for data engineering in LLM RL by leveraging intrinsic, model-internal signals to improve data selection and curriculum design.
AI.method: Extract SAE latent representations from model internals; SAE-space clustering with moderate batch mixing to control batch diversity; use a difficulty proxy for curriculum ordering; apply a quality probe to filter data; evaluate on Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B with GRPO baseline; test transferability across model families and scales.
AI.result: Improves average accuracy by 3.00% over vanilla GRPO; reaches target accuracy with 20% fewer training steps; gains consistent across scales and RL algorithms; SAE transfers effectively across model families and scales.
AI.conclusion: Model internals offer practical, reusable signals for post-training data engineering; SAE-based data engineering is lightweight, transferable, and can reduce data and compute requirements in RL fine-tuning.

title: MobileMoE: Scaling On-Device Mixture of Experts
summary: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) has become the de facto architecture for hundred-billion-parameter language models, yet its advantages at sub-billion scales for on-device deployment remain largely unexplored. To close this gap, we present MobileMoE, a family of on-device MoE language models with sub-billion active parameters (0.3-0.9B active and 1.3-5.3B total) that establish a new Pareto frontier for on-device LLMs. We first formulate an on-device MoE scaling law that jointly optimizes MoE architecture under mobile memory and compute constraints, identifying an on-device sweet spot - moderate sparsity with fine-grained and shared experts - that is simultaneously memory and compute-optimal. Building on the derived architectures, we train MobileMoE with a four-stage recipe covering pre-training, mid-training, instruction fine-tuning, and quantization-aware training, all on open-source datasets. Across 14 benchmarks, MobileMoE matches or exceeds leading on-device dense LLMs with 2-4$\times$ fewer inference FLOPs, and matches or surpasses the state-of-the-art MoE OLMoE-1B-7B with up to 60% fewer parameters. To bridge the last mile to mobile deployment, we provide the first efficient MoE inference on commodity smartphones with comprehensive on-device profiling. At comparable INT4 weight memory, MobileMoE-S delivers $1.8$-$3.8\times$ faster prefill and $2.2$-$3.4\times$ faster decode than the dense baseline MobileLLM-Pro.

AI.tldr: Proposes MobileMoE: sub-billion active-parameter on-device MoE LLMs (0.3-0.9B active, 1.3-5.3B total) achieving Pareto-frontier performance for mobile inference via an on-device scaling law and a four-stage training recipe, with efficient INT4 inference on smartphones, outperforming dense baselines and prior MoE models on 14 benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Address the gap in MoE advantages at sub-billion scales for on-device deployment; enable efficient on-device LLMs with strong performance and low FLOPs; close the gap between dense on-device models and MoE models.
AI.method: Derives an on-device MoE scaling law under memory/compute constraints; identifies sweet spot: moderate sparsity with fine-grained and shared experts; trains MobileMoE in four stages (pre-training, mid-training, instruction fine-tuning, quantization-aware training) on open datasets; performs on-device profiling; validates across 14 benchmarks; uses INT4 weight memory; compares with MobileLLM-Pro and OLMoE-1B-7B.
AI.result: MobileMoE achieves 0.3-0.9B active, 1.3-5.3B total; matches/exceeds leading on-device dense LLMs with 2-4x fewer inference FLOPs; matches or surpasses state-of-the-art MoE OLMoE-1B-7B with up to 60% fewer parameters; first efficient MoE inference on commodity smartphones; MobileMoE-S achieves 1.8-3.8x faster prefill and 2.2-3.4x faster decode vs dense baseline at comparable INT4 weight memory.
AI.conclusion: On-device MoE can reach a new Pareto frontier; moderate sparsity with shared fine-grained experts is memory/compute-optimal; four-stage training pipeline and quantization-aware training enable practical mobile deployment; demonstrates feasible real-world on-device MoE inference with profiling; suggests future opportunities in mobile-specific MoE architectures.
